topics.title: Sensors - Characteristics - Temperature, Flow, Pressure Sensors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMUT602 - MECHATRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Sensors - Characteristics - Temperature, Flow, Pressure Sensors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is a Sensor?

In the language of **KTU 2024 Mechatronics**, a **Sensor** is a physical device that detects (or "senses") a physical, chemical, or biological quantity — called the **Measurand** — and converts it into an equivalent electrical signal (voltage, current, resistance, frequency, or a digital count) that can be measured, recorded, or processed by an electronic controller (like a PLC, microcontroller, or DAQ system).

A sensor is therefore the **"front-end eye, ear, and skin"** of any mechatronic system. Without a sensor, there is no feedback, and without feedback, there is no automation.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (PCMUT602):**
> A sensor is a device that responds to a physical stimulus (such as heat, light, sound, pressure, magnetism, or a particular motion) and transmits the resulting impulse for measurement or for operating a controller.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Human Body

Imagine a human body trying to lift a hot cup of tea without looking at or touching it. Impossible! Our **eyes (optical sensors)** see the cup, our **skin (temperature/pressure sensors)** feel the heat and weight, and our **brain (microcontroller/PLC)** decides how much force the **muscles (actuators)** must apply. The nerves carrying the signal act as the **signal conditioning circuit**.

In a mechatronic product — for example, a **smart refrigerator** — temperature sensors keep the food cold, **flow sensors** measure refrigerant circulation, and **pressure sensors** monitor compressor health. All of this is coordinated by an embedded controller.

### 1.3 Static vs. Dynamic Characteristics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Focus Point:** Examiners frequently ask students to distinguish between **Static** and **Dynamic** characteristics of a sensor. Memorize this table — it is a guaranteed 3–7 mark question.

**Static Characteristics** describe the sensor's behavior when the measurand is **constant or changing very slowly** (steady-state). They include:

- **Accuracy**
- **Precision**
- **Sensitivity**
- **Resolution / Discrimination**
- **Linearity**
- **Hysteresis**
- **Repeatability / Reproducibility**
- **Drift**
- **Range & Span**
- **Dead Band / Dead Zone**

**Dynamic Characteristics** describe the sensor's behavior when the measurand is **changing rapidly with time** (transient state). They include:

- **Response Time / Time Constant (τ)**
- **Rise Time (Tr)**
- **Settling Time (Ts)**
- **Bandwidth (fBW)**
- **Fidelity**
- **Dead Time**

### 1.4 Definition of Each Characteristic

> [!NOTE]
> The following definitions are written in the exact wording the KTU Board expects.

- **Accuracy:** The closeness of the measured value to the **true value** of the measurand. Expressed as a percentage of Full-Scale (FS) reading.
- **Precision:** The closeness of **repeated measurements** of the same quantity to one another, regardless of whether they are accurate.
- **Sensitivity:** The **ratio of the change in output to the change in input**. It is the slope of the calibration curve: $K = \dfrac{\Delta y}{\Delta x}$.
- **Resolution:** The **smallest measurable change** in input that produces a detectable change in output.
- **Linearity:** The degree to which the actual calibration curve approximates a **straight line**.
- **Hysteresis:** The **maximum difference in output** for the same input value, when approached from opposite directions (increasing vs. decreasing).
- **Repeatability:** The ability of a sensor to produce **identical outputs** for repeated applications of the **same input under identical conditions**.
- **Drift:** The **gradual change in output** of a sensor over time, even when the input is held constant.
- **Range (Input Range):** The **minimum and maximum values** of the input that the sensor can measure.
- **Span:** The **algebraic difference** between the maximum and minimum input values: $\text{Span} = \text{Input}_{max} - \text{Input}_{min}$.

### 1.5 Temperature, Flow, and Pressure Sensors — A Snapshot

| Physical Quantity | Common Sensor Types | Typical Output |
|---|---|---|
| **Temperature** | Thermocouple, RTD (PT100), Thermistor, IC sensor (LM35), Bimetallic strip | mV, Ω, V |
| **Flow** | Orifice plate, Venturi, Turbine flowmeter, Coriolis, Ultrasonic, Electromagnetic | Pulse, V, mA, Hz |
| **Pressure** | Bourdon tube, Diaphragm, Strain gauge, Piezoelectric, Capacitive | mV, V, mA, Hz |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Static Characteristics — The "Why and How"

#### 2.1.1 Accuracy
- **Why:** Every measurement is prone to error. Accuracy tells us how "trustworthy" the reading is.
- **How:** If the true temperature is **100 °C** and a sensor reads **100.5 °C**, the error is **0.5 °C**. Accuracy = ±0.5 % FS.
- **Real-world utility:** Medical instruments (e.g., patient temperature probes) demand high accuracy; industrial ovens may tolerate ±2 %.

#### 2.1.2 Precision
- **Why:** A sensor can be precise but inaccurate (e.g., a thermometer always reading 2 °C high).
- **How:** Repeated measurements cluster closely → high precision.

#### 2.1.3 Sensitivity
- **Why:** Determines how strongly the output responds to a small input change.
- **How:** A platinum RTD with sensitivity 0.385 Ω/°C means a 1 °C rise changes resistance by 0.385 Ω.

#### 2.1.4 Linearity
- **Why:** Simplifies calibration and controller design (linear math vs. polynomial math).
- **How:** Plot input vs. output; draw a best-fit straight line; compute the **maximum deviation** of the actual curve from this line.

#### 2.1.5 Hysteresis
- **Why:** Caused by friction, mechanical backlash, magnetic hysteresis, or thermal lag.
- **How:** Apply input forward → note output at 50 °C = 5.10 V. Then reverse input → note output at 50 °C = 4.95 V. Hysteresis = 5.10 − 4.95 = **0.15 V**.

#### 2.1.6 Repeatability
- **Why:** Important for quality control — if a sensor cannot repeat, the process cannot be controlled.
- **How:** Apply the same input 10 times; compute standard deviation of the outputs.

#### 2.1.7 Drift
- **Why:** Aging components (resistor heating, semiconductor degradation) cause slow output shifts.
- **How:** Calibrate once a year for industrial use; high-precision labs recalibrate every 30 days.

### 2.2 Dynamic Characteristics — The "Why and How"

When the input changes suddenly (a step input), the sensor output does not follow instantly. It is governed by a **first-order differential equation**:

$$\tau \frac{dy(t)}{dt} + y(t) = K \cdot x(t)$$

where $\tau$ is the **time constant** (seconds), $K$ is static sensitivity, $y(t)$ is the output, and $x(t)$ is the input.

For a **step input** of magnitude $A$, the solution is:

$$y(t) = K \cdot A \cdot \left(1 - e^{-t/\tau}\right)$$

Key time-domain metrics:
- **Time to reach 63.2 % of final value** = $\tau$
- **Time to reach 95 % of final value** = $3\tau$
- **Time to reach 98.2 % of final value** = $4\tau$ (often called "settling time")
- **Rise time** $T_r$ (10 % → 90 %) $\approx 2.2\tau$

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Memorize the following table. The KTU 2024 examiner expects these formulas in a single derivation problem.

| # | Characteristic | Formula | Unit / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Sensitivity (Static) | $K = \dfrac{\Delta y}{\Delta x} = \dfrac{dy}{dx}$ | Output / Input (e.g., V/°C) |
| 2 | Sensitivity (Multi-sensor) | $K = \dfrac{dy}{dx_1} + \dfrac{dy}{dx_2} + \dots$ | Sum of partials (independent measurands) |
| 3 | Range | $R = x_{max} - x_{min}$ | Same unit as input |
| 4 | Span | $S = x_{max} - x_{min}$ | Numerically = Range |
| 5 | Linearity (Non-linearity %) | $\% \text{NL} = \dfrac{y_{actual} - y_{line}}{y_{FS}} \times 100$ | Dimensionless (%) |
| 6 | Hysteresis Error | $\varepsilon_H = \dfrac{y_{up} - y_{down}}{y_{FS}} \times 100$ | % of FS |
| 7 | Repeatability | $\varepsilon_R = \dfrac{\pm \sigma}{y_{FS}} \times 100$ | $\sigma$ = std. deviation |
| 8 | Accuracy (as error) | $\% \text{Error} = \dfrac{\vert y_{measured} - y_{true} \vert}{y_{FS}} \times 100$ | Use $\vert$ notation; or use "abs" |
| 9 | First-order step response | $y(t) = K A (1 - e^{-t/\tau})$ | Exponential rise |
| 10 | Time constant | $\tau = R \cdot C$ (thermal) or $L/R$ (electrical) | Seconds |
| 11 | Rise time | $T_r \approx 2.2 \tau$ | 10 % to 90 % |
| 12 | Settling time | $T_s \approx 4 \tau$ (to 98.2 %) | Seconds |
| 13 | Bandwidth | $f_{BW} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi\tau}$ | Hz (for 1st-order system) |
| 14 | Dead time | $t_d$ | Pure delay (transport lag) |
| 15 | RTD Resistance | $R_T = R_0 (1 + \alpha T)$ | $\alpha$ = temperature coefficient |
| 16 | Thermocouple EMF | $E \approx a T + b T^2$ (polynomial) | mV, calibrated by NIST tables |
| 17 | Strain Gauge | $\dfrac{\Delta R}{R} = G \cdot \varepsilon$ | $G$ = gauge factor (≈ 2) |
| 18 | Piezo Pressure | $q = d_{33} \cdot F$ | $d_{33}$ = piezoelectric charge coeff. |

> [!NOTE]
> In the markdown table above, the word "abs" is used to denote absolute value, since the literal `|` character can break the markdown table syntax. In your written exam, you should write $\vert x \vert$ or "mod $x$" — the examiner will understand both.

### 2.4 Real-World Utility in Mechatronic Engineering

- **Robotic arm** → Position sensors (encoders) + force/torque sensors for adaptive grasping.
- **CNC machine** → Temperature sensors for thermal compensation of the spindle; pressure sensors for hydraulic clamping.
- **Automotive ABS brake** → Wheel speed sensors (flow + pressure) for skid detection.
- **HVAC system** → Temperature + humidity + flow sensors for closed-loop climate control.
- **Biomedical ventilator** → Flow + pressure sensors regulate oxygen delivery to a patient's lungs.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation — Sensitivity of a Multi-Measurand Sensor

> [!NOTE]
> This is a classic KTU 14-mark derivation. Learn it thoroughly.

Consider a sensor whose output $y$ depends on **two independent measurands** $x_1$ and $x_2$, say pressure and temperature. The total output is $y = f(x_1, x_2)$.

For a small change in each measurand, the total change in $y$ is the sum of the partial changes (by the principle of superposition for small signals):

$$\Delta y = \frac{\partial f}{\partial x_1} \Delta x_1 + \frac{\partial f}{\partial x_2} \Delta x_2$$

Divide both sides by a reference $\Delta x$ (the dominant measurand) to obtain an **effective sensitivity**:

$$K_{eff} = \frac{\partial f}{\partial x_1} \cdot \frac{\Delta x_1}{\Delta x} + \frac{\partial f}{\partial x_2} \cdot \frac{\Delta x_2}{\Delta x}$$

This shows that an **unwanted cross-sensitivity** exists if $\frac{\partial f}{\partial x_2} \neq 0$. For example, a pressure sensor that is also slightly sensitive to temperature exhibits **thermal cross-sensitivity**, which causes drift.

### 3.2 Derivation — First-Order Sensor Response (Step Input)

Consider a thermal sensor (e.g., a thermistor) modeled as a first-order system. The energy balance gives:

$$m c \frac{dT(t)}{dt} = h A (T_{\infty} - T(t))$$

where $m c$ is the thermal mass, $h A$ is the heat transfer coefficient × area, and $T_{\infty}$ is the bath temperature.

Step 1: Divide both sides by $h A$ to get the standard first-order form:

$$\frac{m c}{h A} \frac{dT(t)}{dt} + T(t) = T_{\infty}$$

Step 2: Define the **time constant** $\tau = \dfrac{m c}{h A}$:

$$\tau \frac{dT(t)}{dt} + T(t) = T_{\infty}$$

Step 3: For a step input where the bath temperature suddenly changes to $T_s$ at $t = 0$, the steady-state solution is $T(\infty) = T_s$, and the homogeneous solution is $C e^{-t/\tau}$. Combine:

$$T(t) = T_s - (T_s - T_0) e^{-t/\tau}$$

Step 4: At $t = \tau$, substitute:

$$T(\tau) = T_s - (T_s - T_0) e^{-1} = T_s - 0.368 (T_s - T_0)$$

$$T(\tau) - T_0 = (T_s - T_0)(1 - 0.368) = 0.632 (T_s - T_0)$$

This proves that after **one time constant**, the sensor has reached **63.2 %** of its final value. Repeating at $t = 3\tau$ gives 95 %, and $t = 4\tau$ gives 98.2 %.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Worked Numerical Problems

#### Example 1 — Sensitivity, Hysteresis, Linearity, Accuracy

A pressure sensor is tested in a calibration lab. The following readings were recorded (input = pressure in bar, output = voltage in V):

| Input (bar) ↑ | Output (V) ↑ | Input (bar) ↓ | Output (V) ↓ |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 0.00 | 0 | 0.00 |
| 2 | 1.02 | 2 | 0.97 |
| 4 | 2.01 | 4 | 1.96 |
| 6 | 3.05 | 6 | 2.98 |
| 8 | 4.03 | 8 | 4.00 |
| 10 | 5.00 | 10 | 5.00 |

**Find:** (a) Sensitivity, (b) Hysteresis error, (c) Linearity error, (d) Range and Span.

---

**Solution (a) — Sensitivity (using end-point line):**

$$K = \frac{\Delta y}{\Delta x} = \frac{5.00 - 0.00}{10 - 0} = 0.50 \ \text{V/bar}$$

**Solution (b) — Hysteresis error:**

Maximum difference between upscale and downscale readings:
- At 2 bar: $\vert 1.02 - 0.97 \vert = 0.05$ V
- At 4 bar: $\vert 2.01 - 1.96 \vert = 0.05$ V
- At 6 bar: $\vert 3.05 - 2.98 \vert = 0.07$ V ← **maximum**
- At 8 bar: $\vert 4.03 - 4.00 \vert = 0.03$ V

$$\% \varepsilon_H = \frac{0.07}{5.00} \times 100 = 1.4 \ \text{\% FS}$$

**Solution (c) — Linearity (best-fit straight line):**

Using end-points, $V_{line} = 0.50 P$. At $P = 2, 4, 6, 8$ bar:
- $V_{line} = 1.00, 2.00, 3.00, 4.00$ V
- $V_{actual} = 1.02, 2.01, 3.05, 4.03$ V
- Deviation = $+0.02, +0.01, +0.05, +0.03$ V
- Maximum deviation = $0.05$ V (at 6 bar)

$$\% \text{Non-linearity} = \frac{0.05}{5.00} \times 100 = 1.0 \ \text{\% FS}$$

**Solution (d) — Range and Span:**

$$\text{Range} = 0 \text{ to } 10 \ \text{bar}, \quad \text{Span} = 10 - 0 = 10 \ \text{bar}$$

---

#### Example 2 — Time Constant & Settling Time for a Thermocouple

A thermocouple used to measure furnace temperature has a time constant $\tau = 2.5$ s. The furnace temperature suddenly steps from 25 °C to 300 °C. Find:
(a) The temperature reading after 1 second.
(b) The time required to reach 95 % of the final value.
(c) The sensor's bandwidth.

---

**Solution (a) — Temperature at $t = 1$ s:**

$$T(1) = T_s - (T_s - T_0) e^{-t/\tau} = 300 - (300 - 25) e^{-1/2.5}$$

$$= 300 - 275 \cdot e^{-0.4} = 300 - 275 \cdot 0.6703 = 300 - 184.33 = 115.67 \ \text{°C}$$

**Solution (b) — Time to reach 95 %:**

$$0.95 = 1 - e^{-t/\tau} \Rightarrow e^{-t/\tau} = 0.05 \Rightarrow \frac{t}{\tau} = -\ln(0.05) = 2.996$$

$$t = 2.996 \times 2.5 = 7.49 \ \text{s}$$

This confirms the rule $t \approx 3\tau$ for 95 % response.

**Solution (c) — Bandwidth:**

$$f_{BW} = \frac{1}{2\pi\tau} = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 2.5} = 0.0637 \ \text{Hz} \approx 63.7 \ \text{mHz}$$

This means the thermocouple can faithfully follow furnace fluctuations up to about 0.064 Hz.

#### Example 3 — RTD Temperature Calculation

A PT100 RTD has $R_0 = 100 \ \Omega$ at 0 °C and $\alpha = 0.00385 \ /°C$. The measured resistance is $R_T = 138.5 \ \Omega$. Find the temperature.

---

**Solution:**

$$R_T = R_0 (1 + \alpha T) \Rightarrow T = \frac{R_T / R_0 - 1}{\alpha} = \frac{138.5/100 - 1}{0.00385} = \frac{0.385}{0.00385} = 100 \ \text{°C}$$

### 3.4 Pressure Sensor — Strain Gauge Derivation

A diaphragm pressure sensor uses a strain gauge of gauge factor $G = 2.1$ bonded to a diaphragm of radius $r = 20$ mm and thickness $t = 0.5$ mm. The Young's modulus of the diaphragm material is $E = 200$ GPa. The applied pressure is $P = 1$ MPa. The unstrained gauge resistance is $R = 120 \ \Omega$. Find the change in resistance.

---

**Step 1 — Strain at the diaphragm edge:**

For a thin circular diaphragm with clamped edges, the maximum strain at the edge is:

$$\varepsilon = \frac{3 P r^2}{8 E t^2} (1 - \nu^2) \approx \frac{3 P r^2}{8 E t^2} \quad (\text{assuming } \nu \approx 0.3, \nu^2 \approx 0)$$

**Step 2 — Plug in numbers:**

$$\varepsilon = \frac{3 \times (1 \times 10^6) \times (0.02)^2}{8 \times (200 \times 10^9) \times (0.0005)^2}$$

$$= \frac{3 \times 10^6 \times 4 \times 10^{-4}}{8 \times 2 \times 10^{11} \times 2.5 \times 10^{-7}}$$

$$= \frac{1200}{4 \times 10^5} = 3 \times 10^{-3} = 0.003 \ \text{(dimensionless)}$$

**Step 3 — Change in resistance:**

$$\frac{\Delta R}{R} = G \cdot \varepsilon = 2.1 \times 0.003 = 0.0063$$

$$\Delta R = 0.0063 \times 120 = 0.756 \ \Omega$$

This is a small change — typically amplified by a **Wheatstone bridge** to produce a useful voltage signal.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
Define the following terms with one example each:
(a) Sensitivity
(b) Hysteresis
(c) Range

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

(a) **Sensitivity** is the ratio of the change in output of a sensor to the change in input that causes it, i.e., $K = \Delta y / \Delta x$. *Example:* A thermocouple has a sensitivity of about 40 µV/°C, meaning a 1 °C temperature change produces a 40 µV EMF change.

(b) **Hysteresis** is the maximum difference in the sensor output for the same value of input when approached from opposite directions (increasing and decreasing). *Example:* A Bourdon-tube pressure gauge may show 5.10 V at 50 bar while ascending, and 4.95 V at 50 bar while descending.

(c) **Range** is the difference between the maximum and minimum values of the input (measurand) that the sensor can measure. *Example:* A thermistor rated 0 to 150 °C has a range of 150 °C.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
Distinguish between **static** and **dynamic** characteristics of a sensor. Give two examples of each.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Static characteristics** describe the behavior of a sensor when the input is **constant or varies very slowly** (steady-state conditions). Examples: **accuracy, sensitivity, linearity, hysteresis, repeatability, drift, range, span, resolution, dead band.**

**Dynamic characteristics** describe the behavior of a sensor when the input is **changing rapidly with time** (transient conditions). Examples: **time constant, rise time, settling time, bandwidth, fidelity, dead time.**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (with Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

**(a)** With neat sketches, explain the working principle of:
- (i) **RTD (Resistance Temperature Detector)**
- (ii) **Thermocouple**

State the materials used, the governing physical law, the typical sensitivity range, and one industrial application for each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A PT100 RTD is used to measure the temperature of a chemical bath. The resistance of the RTD is found to be 119.4 Ω. The RTD follows the linear relation $R_T = R_0(1 + \alpha T)$ with $R_0 = 100 \ \Omega$ and $\alpha = 0.00385 \ /°C$.
- (i) Find the temperature of the bath.
- (ii) If the actual bath temperature is 50 °C, calculate the percentage error of the measurement.
- (iii) State two precautions to improve the accuracy of RTD measurement. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution to Q-A (a):**

**(i) RTD (Resistance Temperature Detector):**
- **Principle:** The electrical resistance of a pure metal increases linearly with temperature. This is known as the **positive temperature coefficient (PTC)** behaviour.
- **Governing law:** $R_T = R_0(1 + \alpha T + \beta T^2 + \dots)$ — for PT100, $\beta$ is very small, so the linear approximation is used.
- **Materials used:** Platinum (most common — PT100, PT1000), Copper, Nickel.
- **Sensitivity:** PT100 has a nominal sensitivity of **0.385 Ω/°C**.
- **Construction:** A platinum wire is wound on a ceramic or glass bobbin and protected by a metal sheath.
- **Industrial application:** Used in industrial ovens, HVAC duct temperature monitoring, and laboratory calibration baths.

**(ii) Thermocouple:**
- **Principle:** Based on the **Seebeck Effect** — when two dissimilar metals are joined and the junction is heated, a small EMF is generated proportional to the temperature difference between the hot and cold junctions.
- **Governing law:** $E = \int_{T_c}^{T_h} (S_A - S_B) \, dT$ where $S_A$ and $S_B$ are the **Seebeck coefficients** of the two metals.
- **Materials used (standard ISA types):**
  - **Type K** — Chromel (+) / Alumel (−), range −200 to 1260 °C, sensitivity ≈ 41 µV/°C
  - **Type J** — Iron / Constantan
  - **Type T** — Copper / Constantan
- **Sensitivity:** Typically 40–60 µV/°C.
- **Industrial application:** Furnace temperature monitoring, exhaust gas measurement in automobiles, food industry ovens.

---

**Model Solution to Q-A (b):**

**(i) Temperature of the bath:**

Using $R_T = R_0(1 + \alpha T)$:

$$T = \frac{R_T / R_0 - 1}{\alpha} = \frac{119.4 / 100 - 1}{0.00385} = \frac{0.194}{0.00385} = 50.39 \ \text{°C}$$

**(ii) Percentage error:**

$$\% \text{Error} = \frac{\vert T_{measured} - T_{true} \vert}{T_{true}} \times 100 = \frac{\vert 50.39 - 50 \vert}{50} \times 100 = 0.78 \ \text{\%}$$

**(iii) Two precautions:**
1. Use a **3-wire or 4-wire Kelvin connection** to eliminate the effect of lead-wire resistance.
2. Use **cold-junction compensation (CJC)** to cancel errors due to the temperature of the reference terminal.
3. (Bonus) Use **self-heating correction** by keeping the excitation current low (< 1 mA for PT100).

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**(a)** With the help of a block diagram, describe a typical **strain-gauge-based pressure sensor**. Derive the expression for the change in gauge resistance in terms of the gauge factor and the strain. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A turbine flowmeter is used to measure the flow rate of water in a pipeline. The flowmeter outputs 100 pulses per litre. In 60 seconds, the counter records 90 000 pulses. If the pipe cross-section is 25 cm², calculate:
- (i) The volume of water that passed through the pipe.
- (ii) The average flow rate in litres per minute.
- (iii) The average fluid velocity in m/s.
- (iv) State **two** advantages and **one** disadvantage of a turbine flowmeter compared to an orifice plate. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution to Q-B (a):**

**Block Diagram:**

Physical Pressure → Diaphragm (deflects) → Strain Gauge bonded on diaphragm → Resistance changes → Wheatstone Bridge converts to voltage → Instrumentation Amplifier → Output voltage

**Derivation:**

A strain gauge is a thin resistive foil whose length $L$, cross-sectional area $A$, and resistivity $\rho$ determine its unstrained resistance $R = \rho L / A$.

When the gauge is strained by $\varepsilon = \Delta L / L$ along its length, the dimensions and resistivity both change:

$$R + \Delta R = (\rho + \Delta\rho)(L + \Delta L)^2 / (A - \Delta A)$$

For a Poisson's ratio of $\nu$ (gauge material), $\Delta A / A = -2\nu \varepsilon$. Substituting and neglecting higher-order terms:

$$\frac{\Delta R}{R} = \varepsilon (1 + 2\nu) + \frac{\Delta \rho}{\rho}$$

By definition, the **gauge factor** $G$ combines both mechanical and piezoresistive contributions:

$$G = 1 + 2\nu + \frac{\Delta \rho / \rho}{\varepsilon}$$

Therefore:

$$\frac{\Delta R}{R} = G \cdot \varepsilon$$

For a typical metallic foil, $G \approx 2.0$ to $2.1$. For semiconductors, $G$ can be 50–200 due to the dominant piezoresistive effect.

---

**Model Solution to Q-B (b):**

**(i) Volume of water passed:**

$$\text{Pulses per litre} = 100 \Rightarrow V = \frac{90000}{100} = 900 \ \text{litres}$$

**(ii) Average flow rate:**

$$Q = \frac{V}{t} = \frac{900 \ \text{L}}{60 \ \text{s}} = 15 \ \text{L/s} = 15 \times 60 = 900 \ \text{L/min}$$

**(iii) Average fluid velocity:**

Convert cross-section: $A = 25 \ \text{cm}^2 = 25 \times 10^{-4} \ \text{m}^2 = 0.0025 \ \text{m}^2$.

$$Q = 900 \ \text{L/min} = \frac{0.9 \ \text{m}^3}{60 \ \text{s}} = 0.015 \ \text{m}^3/\text{s}$$

$$v = \frac{Q}{A} = \frac{0.015}{0.0025} = 6 \ \text{m/s}$$

**(iv) Advantages and Disadvantages:**

**Advantages of turbine flowmeter over orifice plate:**
1. Higher accuracy (±0.5 % vs ±2–3 % for orifice).
2. Better repeatability and wider turn-down ratio.
3. Lower permanent pressure loss across the meter.

**Disadvantage:**
1. Moving parts (rotor and bearings) cause wear, require maintenance, and cannot be used with dirty or abrasive fluids. Orifice plates have **no moving parts** and are rugged.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — How Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Confusing Range and Span.** Range is the interval (e.g., 0–100 °C), Span is the algebraic difference (e.g., 100 °C). Examiners specifically test this.
> 2. **Forgetting units in sensitivity.** Writing "$K = 0.5$" instead of "$K = 0.5$ V/bar" loses a full mark.
> 3. **Using $y_{FS}$ vs $y_{actual}$ in the denominator.** For hysteresis and repeatability errors, always use **Full-Scale output** in the denominator (or input, depending on convention), not the local reading.
> 4. **Mixing up thermocouple polarity.** A common question asks why the **chromel** wire is connected to the **positive** terminal of the meter in a Type K thermocouple.
> 5. **Forgetting cold-junction compensation** in a thermocouple problem — this is a guaranteed 1-mark deduction in KTU 2024 papers.
> 6. **Using single-wire RTD in a long lead setup** — examiners expect a 3-wire or 4-wire diagram.
> 7. **Calculating % error with respect to measured value instead of true value.** This is a frequent silly mistake. Always divide by the **true value** (or the full-scale value if computing FS% error).

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [x] A **sensor** converts a physical quantity into an electrical signal; it is the "input organ" of a mechatronic system.
- [x] **Static characteristics** apply at steady-state; **dynamic characteristics** apply under time-varying inputs.
- [x] **Sensitivity** = $\Delta y / \Delta x$; always quote the unit (V/°C, V/bar, pulses/L).
- [x] **Range** = interval of input; **Span** = algebraic difference.
- [x] **Linearity** is judged by the **maximum deviation** of the calibration curve from the best-fit straight line, expressed as a percentage of Full-Scale (FS).
- [x] **Hysteresis** is the **maximum difference** between upscale and downscale readings for the same input.
- [x] **Repeatability** is the closeness of repeated measurements under **identical** conditions.
- [x] **Drift** is a slow, time-dependent change in output even with a constant input (zero drift vs. sensitivity drift).
- [x] **Resolution** is the smallest measurable change in input.
- [x] For a **first-order system**, the output after a step input is $y(t) = K A (1 - e^{-t/\tau})$.
- [x] **Time constant $\tau$** → 63.2 % of final value; **$3\tau$** → 95 %; **$4\tau$** → 98.2 % (settling time).
- [x] **Bandwidth** of a first-order sensor $f_{BW} = 1 / (2\pi\tau)$.
- [x] **RTD PT100:** $R_T = R_0(1 + \alpha T)$; platinum; $\alpha = 0.00385 \ /°C$; sensitivity = **0.385 Ω/°C**.
- [x] **Thermocouple:** Seebeck effect; EMF in mV; needs **cold-junction compensation (CJC)**.
- [x] **Thermistor:** NTC type, highly non-linear, $R_T = R_0 \cdot e^{B(1/T - 1/T_0)}$, high sensitivity.
- [x] **Strain gauge:** $\Delta R / R = G \cdot \varepsilon$; gauge factor $G \approx 2$ for metal; **Wheatstone bridge** converts resistance change to voltage.
- [x] **Piezoelectric pressure sensor:** High-frequency dynamic pressure only (no static response); needs **charge amplifier**.
- [x] **Capacitive pressure sensor:** $C = \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A / d$; change in $d$ or $A$ due to diaphragm deflection.
- [x] **Turbine flowmeter:** Pulses per litre; volume = pulses / K-factor; velocity = Q / A.
- [x] **Orifice plate / Venturi:** Differential pressure principle; uses **Bernoulli's equation**; $Q = C_d A_2 \sqrt{2 \Delta P / \rho}$.
- [x] **Coriolis flowmeter:** Measures mass flow directly; very accurate; expensive.
- [x] **Ultrasonic flowmeter:** Non-invasive; uses transit-time or Doppler principle; suitable for dirty fluids.
- [x] The block diagram of a measurement system is: **Measurand → Primary sensor → Variable conversion → Signal conditioning → Processing → Output / Display / Actuator**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Displacement, Position and Proximity Sensing by Magnetic, Optical, Ultrasonic, Inductive, Capacitive and Eddy Current Methods
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMUT602 - MECHATRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Displacement, Position and Proximity Sensing by Magnetic, Optical, Ultrasonic, Inductive, Capacitive and Eddy Current Methods

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Displacement, Position & Proximity Sensing?

**Formal KTU Definition:**
A *displacement sensor* is a device that converts physical displacement (linear or angular) into a quantifiable electrical signal. A *position sensor* measures the absolute or relative location of a target with respect to a reference frame, while a *proximity sensor* detects the presence or absence of an object within a defined non-contact zone, without mechanical contact.

In mechatronic systems, these sensors form the **primary feedback loop** between the mechanical domain (movement) and the electronic/control domain (signal processing), enabling closed-loop control.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** This topic directly maps to **Course Outcome CO1** — "Apply the concepts of sensors and actuators for the design of mechatronic systems." The six methods (Magnetic, Optical, Ultrasonic, Inductive, Capacitive, Eddy Current) are the **core non-contact sensing families** and appear almost every semester in Part B questions.

---

## 1.2 The Six Sensing Families — Intuitive Overview

| S.No | Sensing Method | Physical Principle | One-Line Intuition |
|------|---------------|--------------------|--------------------|
| 1 | **Magnetic** | Hall Effect / Magnetoresistance / Reed switch | A magnet near the sensor "wakes it up" by disturbing its magnetic field |
| 2 | **Optical** | Photon emission, reflection, or interruption | A light beam is either broken, reflected, or scattered by the target |
| 3 | **Ultrasonic** | Time-of-flight of sound waves (>20 kHz) | Like a bat shouting — measures how long the echo takes to return |
| 4 | **Inductive** | Mutual inductance / Reluctance change (LVDT) | A metal piece moving near a coil changes its inductance |
| 5 | **Capacitive** | Change in capacitance (C = εA/d) | Bringing a conductive object closer is like moving the plates of a capacitor |
| 6 | **Eddy Current** | Electromagnetic induction in target | A high-frequency coil induces tiny whirlpool currents in the target metal |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Imagine you are blindfolded and asked to find a metal wall:
> - *Magnetic* = holding a compass → it pulls toward iron.
> - *Optical* = flashing a torch → you see reflected light.
> - *Ultrasonic* = clapping your hands → you hear the echo.
> - *Inductive/Capacitive/Eddy Current* = holding a radio → it buzzes differently near metal.

## 1.3 Why Non-Contact Sensing?

**Key physical constants and metrics (bold for emphasis):**
- Typical measurement ranges: **0.1 mm to 50 mm** (proximity); up to **several meters** (long-range optical/ultrasonic).
- Resolution: **micrometers (µm)** for LVDT/laser; **millimeters (mm)** for ultrasonic.
- Response frequency: from **DC (static)** (LVDT) to **MHz** (eddy current).

**Advantages over contact sensors (potentiometers, LVDTs with contact, microswitches):**
1. **No mechanical wear** → longer life.
2. **No friction** → does not load the moving system.
3. **High speed** → suitable for high-dynamic applications.
4. **Operates in harsh environments** (oil, dirt, vacuum).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Magnetic Sensing

### 2.1.1 Hall Effect Sensor

**Hall Effect Principle:** When a current-carrying conductor is placed in a perpendicular magnetic field, the charge carriers experience a Lorentz force and accumulate on one side, producing a transverse voltage called the **Hall Voltage**.

**Operating Logic (step-by-step):**
1. A constant control current $I_c$ is passed through a thin semiconductor strip.
2. A magnetic field **B** is applied perpendicular to the strip.
3. Charge carriers (electrons/holes) deflect to one edge due to Lorentz force $F = qv \times B$.
4. Charge accumulation creates a transverse electric field → **Hall Voltage $V_H$** is measured.
5. $V_H$ is directly proportional to **B** → magnetic field strength is sensed.

**Typical application:** Proximity detection of permanent magnets, BLDC motor commutation, gear-tooth sensing, current sensing (via field of conductor).

### 2.1.2 Magnetoresistive (MR) Sensors
- Resistance changes in presence of magnetic field (e.g., Giant Magnetoresistance — GMR).
- Used in automotive wheel-speed sensors, electronic compasses.

### 2.1.3 Reed Switch
- Two ferromagnetic contacts close mechanically under magnetic field.
- Binary (on/off) proximity sensor, very cheap and reliable.

---

## 2.2 Optical Sensing

### 2.2.1 Photoelectric / Optoelectronic Methods
Three fundamental configurations:

**a) Through-Beam (Opposed Mode):**
- Separate emitter and receiver.
- Target **breaks** the light beam → detection.
- Longest range (up to **50+ meters**).

**b) Retro-Reflective:**
- Emitter and receiver in one housing; target carries a reflector.
- Target **interrupts** the reflected beam.
- Range: **few meters**.

**c) Diffuse Reflective (Proximity Mode):**
- Emitter and receiver in one housing, **no separate reflector**.
- Light bounces off the target back to receiver.
- Range: **mm to ~1 m** depending on target reflectivity.

### 2.2.2 Optical Encoders
- **Incremental encoder:** A rotating disc with slits; photodiode reads pulses → measures angular displacement/speed.
- **Absolute encoder:** Disc has unique Gray-code pattern per angle → gives absolute position even after power-off.

### 2.2.3 Laser Triangulation
- A laser beam hits the target; reflected light is imaged onto a position-sensitive detector (PSD).
- Triangulation geometry converts spot position into displacement.
- Used for **high-precision** surface profiling (µm resolution).

---

## 2.3 Ultrasonic Sensing

### 2.3.1 Physical Principle
- A piezoelectric crystal emits a **pressure wave at frequency > 20 kHz** (typically 25 kHz to 500 kHz).
- Wave travels through air (or fluid), strikes target, and returns as echo.
- Time-of-flight (ToF) is measured and converted to distance.

### 2.3.2 Operating Logic
1. Transmitter fires an ultrasonic pulse.
2. Timer (TDC — Time-to-Digital Converter) starts.
3. Receiver detects echo → timer stops.
4. Distance is calculated using speed of sound in air (**v ≈ 343 m/s at 20°C**).

> [!NOTE]
> Speed of sound depends on temperature: $v = 331 + 0.6 \cdot T$ (m/s, T in °C). KTU examiners may give this formula.

---

## 2.4 Inductive Sensing

### 2.4.1 LVDT (Linear Variable Differential Transformer)
- The **most important** inductive displacement sensor in KTU syllabus.
- Consists of:
  - One **primary coil** (P) energized by AC excitation (typically 1–10 kHz).
  - Two **secondary coils** (S1, S2) connected in series-opposition.
  - A movable **ferromagnetic core** couples flux between coils.

**How it works:**
1. Primary is excited by sinusoidal AC.
2. Core position determines how much flux links with S1 vs S2.
3. When core is at **null position** (centered): S1 and S2 induced EMFs are equal and opposite → output = 0.
4. When core moves toward S1: $E_{S1} > E_{S2}$ → output is in-phase with primary.
5. When core moves toward S2: $E_{S2} > E_{S1}$ → output is 180° out-of-phase.
6. **Magnitude of output is proportional to displacement**; **phase indicates direction**.

### 2.4.2 Variable Reluctance Proximity Sensor
- A permanent magnet produces flux through a coil; a ferrous target alters reluctance → changes coil inductance.
- Only detects **ferrous** targets.

---

## 2.5 Capacitive Sensing

### 2.5.1 Physical Principle
A parallel-plate capacitor's capacitance is:

$$C = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d}$$

Where:
- $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12}$ **F/m** (permittivity of free space).
- $\varepsilon_r$ = relative permittivity of dielectric.
- $A$ = plate area (m²).
- $d$ = distance between plates (m).

**Operating modes (KTU focus):**
1. **Change in distance $d$** → target acts as second plate.
2. **Change in area $A$** → angular displacement sensor.
3. **Change in dielectric $\varepsilon_r$** → detects presence of materials (wood, plastic, liquid level).

---

## 2.6 Eddy Current Sensing

### 2.6.1 Physical Principle
1. A coil is energized by **high-frequency AC** (typically 100 kHz–2 MHz).
2. AC produces an alternating magnetic field.
3. When a conductive target enters this field, **Foucault (eddy) currents** are induced in it (per Faraday's law).
4. These eddy currents generate their **own opposing magnetic field** (Lenz's law).
5. This opposition changes the **effective impedance $Z$** of the sensor coil.
6. The change in impedance (or the shift in resonant frequency of an LC tank) is calibrated against distance.

### 2.6.2 Skin Depth (Penetration Depth)
A critical KTU concept — eddy currents exist only in a surface layer:

$$\delta = \sqrt{\frac{2\rho}{\omega \mu}} = \sqrt{\frac{\rho}{\pi f \mu}}$$

Where:
- $\delta$ = skin depth (m).
- $\rho$ = resistivity of target (Ω·m).
- $\omega = 2\pi f$ = angular frequency of excitation (rad/s).
- $\mu = \mu_0 \mu_r$ = permeability of target (H/m).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For aluminum ($\rho = 2.65 \times 10^{-8}$ Ω·m, $\mu_r \approx 1$) at $f = 1$ MHz, $\delta \approx 82 \, \mu m$. Higher frequency = thinner skin → better resolution but lower penetration.

---

## 2.7 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Method | Key Formula | Variables / Units | Application |
|---|--------|-------------|--------------------|-------------|
| 1 | Hall Effect | $V_H = \dfrac{R_H \cdot I \cdot B}{t}$ | $R_H$ = Hall coeff. (m³/C), $I$ = current (A), $B$ = field (T), $t$ = thickness (m) | Magnetic proximity, current sensing |
| 2 | LVDT Output | $V_{out} = K \cdot x$ | $K$ = sensitivity (V/mm), $x$ = displacement (mm) | Linear displacement (DC to kHz) |
| 3 | LVDT Differential | $V_{out} = V_{S1} - V_{S2} = K_1 x - K_2 x$ | For balanced LVDT: $K_1 = K_2 = K$ | Direction detection via phase |
| 4 | Capacitive Sensor | $C = \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d}$ | $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12}$ F/m | Proximity, level, displacement |
| 5 | Capacitive (variable d) | $\Delta C = \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A \left( \dfrac{1}{d_2} - \dfrac{1}{d_1} \right)$ | $d_1, d_2$ in m | Small displacement measurement |
| 6 | Ultrasonic Distance | $d = \dfrac{v \cdot t}{2}$ | $v$ = 343 m/s (air, 20°C), $t$ = ToF (s) | Long-range proximity |
| 7 | Speed of Sound | $v = 331 + 0.6 \cdot T$ | $T$ in °C, $v$ in m/s | Temperature compensation |
| 8 | Eddy Current Skin Depth | $\delta = \sqrt{\dfrac{2\rho}{\omega \mu}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{\rho}{\pi f \mu}}$ | $\rho$ (Ω·m), $f$ (Hz), $\mu$ (H/m) | Surface inspection, defect detection |
| 9 | Photoelectric Through-Beam | $V_{out} = f(L, \text{target reflectivity})$ | Empirical calibration curve | Object detection |
| 10 | Encoder Resolution | $\theta_{res} = \dfrac{360°}{N}$ | $N$ = number of slits/lines | Angular position |

**Real-world engineering utility:**
- **LVDTs** in aircraft landing-gear position feedback.
- **Eddy current probes** in aircraft turbine blade crack detection.
- **Hall sensors** in BLDC motors for rotor position (commutation).
- **Ultrasonic sensors** in car reversing cameras and tank level gauges.
- **Optical encoders** in CNC machines for tool position.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Hall Voltage Expression

**Goal:** Derive the Hall Voltage $V_H$ in terms of measurable quantities $I$, $B$, and material parameters.

**Step 1: Lorentz force on a charge carrier**
A charge $q$ moving with velocity $v_d$ (drift velocity) in magnetic field $B$ experiences:

$$F_{mag} = q \cdot v_d \cdot B$$

This force is transverse to both $v_d$ and $B$ (hence the term "Hall effect" is in the plane perpendicular to current flow).

**Step 2: Charge accumulation → transverse electric field**
Charges pile up on one edge until the electric force balances the magnetic force:

$$q \cdot E_H = q \cdot v_d \cdot B$$

$$\Rightarrow E_H = v_d \cdot B$$

**Step 3: Relating drift velocity to current**
Current $I$ is related to drift velocity by:

$$I = n \cdot q \cdot A_c \cdot v_d$$

Where:
- $n$ = carrier density (carriers/m³).
- $A_c = w \cdot t$ = cross-sectional area (width × thickness).

Therefore:

$$v_d = \dfrac{I}{n \cdot q \cdot w \cdot t}$$

**Step 4: Hall Voltage**
Hall Voltage is the transverse potential difference:

$$V_H = E_H \cdot w = v_d \cdot B \cdot w$$

Substituting $v_d$:

$$V_H = \dfrac{I \cdot B}{n \cdot q \cdot t}$$

**Step 5: Define Hall Coefficient**

$$\boxed{V_H = \dfrac{R_H \cdot I \cdot B}{t}, \quad \text{where} \quad R_H = \dfrac{1}{n \cdot q}}$$

> **Logic Note:** $V_H \propto B$ makes the Hall sensor a *magnetic field → voltage* converter. Since $R_H$ depends on carrier type (positive for holes, negative for electrons), the sign of $V_H$ can identify carrier polarity — this is the famous **Hall experiment** for semiconductor characterization.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: LVDT Output Equation

**Goal:** Show that the differential output of an LVDT is linear in core displacement.

**Step 1: Excitation**
Primary coil is excited with $V_p = V_0 \sin(\omega t)$.

**Step 2: Mutual Inductance**
Each secondary is linked to the primary by mutual inductance $M_i$, which depends on core position $x$.

For a **balanced LVDT** (symmetric secondary windings), the mutual inductances vary linearly near null:

$$M_1(x) = M_0 + k \cdot x$$

$$M_2(x) = M_0 - k \cdot x$$

Where $M_0$ is the mutual inductance at null, $k$ is a constant depending on coil geometry.

**Step 3: Induced EMFs**

$$e_1 = -M_1 \dfrac{di_p}{dt} = -\omega M_0 I_p \cos(\omega t) - \omega k x \cdot I_p \cos(\omega t)$$

$$e_2 = -M_2 \dfrac{di_p}{dt} = -\omega M_0 I_p \cos(\omega t) + \omega k x \cdot I_p \cos(\omega t)$$

**Step 4: Differential (Series-Opposition) Output**

$$V_{out} = e_1 - e_2 = -2 \omega k x \cdot I_p \cos(\omega t)$$

Magnitude:

$$\boxed{\vert V_{out} \vert = K \cdot \vert x \vert}$$

where $K = 2\omega k I_p$ is the **LVDT sensitivity** (V/mm).

**Step 5: Phase detection**
- If $x > 0$: $V_{out}$ in-phase with primary.
- If $x < 0$: $V_{out}$ 180° out-of-phase.
- Therefore **phase encodes direction** and **magnitude encodes displacement** — this dual information is why LVDTs are industry-standard.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Ultrasonic Distance from Time-of-Flight

**Goal:** Relate measured echo time to physical distance.

**Step 1: Pulse travels from transmitter to target and back.**
Total path length = $2d$, where $d$ = one-way distance.

$$2d = v \cdot t$$

**Step 2: Solve for distance:**

$$\boxed{d = \dfrac{v \cdot t}{2}}$$

**Step 3: Numerical example**
Given: $t = 6$ ms, $T = 30°C$.

$$v = 331 + 0.6 \cdot 30 = 349 \, \text{m/s}$$

$$d = \dfrac{349 \times 6 \times 10^{-3}}{2} = 1.047 \, \text{m}$$

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Capacitive Sensor — Variable Distance Mode

**Goal:** Show how capacitance change is non-linearly related to distance change.

**Step 1: Initial and final capacitances:**

$$C_1 = \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d_1}, \quad C_2 = \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d_2}$$

**Step 2: Capacitance change:**

$$\Delta C = C_2 - C_1 = \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A \left( \dfrac{1}{d_2} - \dfrac{1}{d_1} \right)$$

**Step 3: Sensitivity:**

$$S = \dfrac{\partial C}{\partial d} = -\dfrac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d^2}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Sensitivity is **inversely proportional to $d^2$** — this means the sensor is highly non-linear. For small displacements around an operating point $d_0$, linearization is achieved by using an AC bridge (e.g., Schering bridge) or by using the **variable-area mode** (which IS linear in displacement).

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5: Eddy Current Skin Depth

**Goal:** Show how penetration depth depends on frequency and material.

**Step 1: Maxwell's equations for time-harmonic fields in a conductor give the wave equation:**

$$\nabla^2 E = \mu \sigma \dfrac{\partial E}{\partial t} = j \omega \mu \sigma E$$

Where $\sigma = 1/\rho$ is conductivity.

**Step 2: Solution is an exponentially decaying field inside the conductor:**

$$E(z) = E_0 \, e^{-(1+j)z/\delta}$$

where $\delta$ is the **skin depth**, defined as the depth at which field amplitude decays to $1/e \approx 36.8\%$.

**Step 3: Setting the decay constant equal to $1/\delta$:**

$$\dfrac{1}{\delta^2} = \omega \mu \sigma = \dfrac{\omega \mu}{\rho}$$

$$\boxed{\delta = \sqrt{\dfrac{2\rho}{\omega \mu}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{\rho}{\pi f \mu}}}$$

---

## 3.6 Analytical Comparison Matrix (KTU Favorite Table)

| Property | Magnetic (Hall) | Optical (Photo) | Ultrasonic | Inductive (LVDT) | Capacitive | Eddy Current |
|----------|----------------|-----------------|-----------|------------------|------------|--------------|
| **Target material** | Magnetic only | Any (reflective) | Any (any solid/liquid) | Ferromagnetic | Conductive or dielectric | Conductive only |
| **Range** | 1–50 mm | mm to 50 m | 0.1–10 m | 1 µm–500 mm | 0.1–50 mm | 0.1–80 mm |
| **Resolution** | µm | µm (laser) | mm | 0.1 µm | 0.01 µm | µm |
| **Response time** | µs | µs | ms | µs–ms | ms | µs |
| **Cost** | Low | Med–High | Low | Medium | Low | Medium |
| **Hazard** | None | Laser class | None | None | None | None |
| **Works in oil/dust?** | Yes | No (dirty) | Yes | Yes | Yes | Yes |
| **Output** | Analog | Digital/Analog | Digital | Analog (AC) | Analog | Analog |
| **Contact?** | No | No | No | No | No | No |

    S2 --> D1
    D1 --> O[Output Vout = VS1 minus VS2]
