# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Q: Differentiate between a Synchro Transmitter and a Synchro Control Transformer with respect to which winding is energized.**

**Model Answer:**
In a **Synchro Transmitter (TX)**, the **rotor is energized** with an AC reference voltage, and the **stator** delivers the 3-phase output signal. In a **Synchro Control Transformer (CT)**, the **stator is energized** with the 3-phase signal coming from a TX, and the **rotor** delivers the single-phase error voltage. The CT rotor is **not** connected to any AC source; it is purely an error-detection device whose output is proportional to the sine of the angular difference between the TX and CT shaft positions.

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Trick:** *"TX is Rotor-Excited, CT is Stator-Excited."* This single line fetches 1 mark directly.

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Q: State the output voltage equations of a Resolver.**

**Model Answer:**
A resolver has two stator windings displaced by 90° in space. When the rotor is excited by a high-frequency carrier $V_r(t) = V_m \sin(\omega_c t)$, the two stator outputs are:

$$V_{cos}(t) = V_m \sin(\omega_c t) \cdot \cos(\theta)$$

$$V_{sin}(t) = V_m \sin(\omega_c t) \cdot \sin(\theta)$$

where $\theta$ is the angular position of the rotor.

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (Module Internal Choice, 14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]

**Q: With neat sketches, explain the construction and working of a Synchro Transmitter. Derive the expression for the induced EMF in the stator coils. (14 Marks)**

#### Part (a) Construction of a Synchro Transmitter (7 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

A synchro transmitter consists of:

1. **Rotor (Primary):** A salient-pole structure carrying a single concentrated winding. The two ends of the winding are brought out through **two slip rings** and **brushes**, which carry the AC excitation voltage from an external source.

2. **Stator (Secondary):** A laminated silicon-steel core with **36 uniformly distributed slots**. Three identical windings — labelled **S1-S2, S3-S4, S5-S6** — are placed in these slots such that their magnetic axes are **120° apart** in space. The three windings are connected in a **3-wire star (Y) configuration**; the neutral point is normally not brought outside the machine.

3. **End Covers and Bearings:** Precision ball bearings allow smooth rotation of the rotor with minimal friction. The frame is dust-proof and often oil-sealed for reliability.

4. **Terminal Block:** Six terminals (S1, S2, S3, S4, S5, S6) plus two rotor terminals (R1, R2) are brought out on a clearly marked terminal block for external connections.

#### Part (b) Working and EMF Derivation (7 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

The rotor is excited by a single-phase AC supply $V_r = V_m \sin(\omega t)$, producing an alternating flux along the rotor axis. This flux links with the three stator coils through mutual induction.

**Step 1:** Let the maximum mutual inductance between the rotor and stator coil S1 be $M_{max}$. The instantaneous mutual inductance depends on the angular displacement $\theta$ between the rotor axis and the S1 reference axis:

$$M_{S1} = M_{max} \cos(\theta)$$
$$M_{S2} = M_{max} \cos(\theta - 120°)$$
$$M_{S3} = M_{max} \cos(\theta - 240°)$$

**Step 2:** The rotor current is $I_r = I_m \sin(\omega t)$. Differentiating:

$$\frac{dI_r}{dt} = I_m \omega \cos(\omega t)$$

**Step 3:** Applying Faraday's law $e = -M \frac{dI}{dt}$ to each coil:

$$e_{S1} = -M_{max} I_m \omega \cos(\theta) \cos(\omega t)$$
$$e_{S2} = -M_{max} I_m \omega \cos(\theta - 120°) \cos(\omega t)$$
$$e_{S3} = -M_{max} I_m \omega \cos(\theta - 240°) \cos(\omega t)$$

**Step 4:** Defining the transformation ratio $K$ and absorbing the −90° phase shift into the time reference:

$$\boxed{e_{S1} = K V_m \sin(\omega t) \cos(\theta)}$$
$$\boxed{e_{S2} = K V_m \sin(\omega t) \cos(\theta - 120°)}$$
$$\boxed{e_{S3} = K V_m \sin(\omega t) \cos(\theta - 240°)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The three stator EMFs form a **balanced 3-phase system** whose phase angles depend on $\theta$. Hence, the **electrical phase** of the stator outputs is a **direct representation of the mechanical angle** of the rotor.

### Question B (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam – May 2023]

**Q: Explain the construction and working of a Resolver. How is the digital angle obtained from a Resolver output? (14 Marks)**

#### Part (a) Construction and Working of a Resolver (7 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

A resolver is essentially a **two-phase rotating transformer** built with very high precision. It has the following components:

1. **Rotor:** Carries one (or two) high-precision windings. The single-winding rotor is excited by a high-frequency carrier (typically 10 kHz) to avoid 50/60 Hz mains interference.

2. **Stator:** Carries **two identical windings**, each spanning **90 electrical degrees** of arc, placed in slots of a laminated core. The two stator coils are orthogonal; one is called the **Sine coil** (axis at 90° from reference) and the other the **Cosine coil** (axis at reference).

3. **Slip Rings and Brushes:** Carry the low-power excitation to the rotor without introducing significant noise.

4. **Housing:** Sealed, rugged construction suitable for harsh industrial and aerospace environments.

**Working:** When the rotor is excited by $V_r(t) = V_m \sin(\omega_c t)$ and the shaft is at angle $\theta$:

- The **cosine coil** picks up the component of flux along its axis: $V_{cos}(t) = V_m \sin(\omega_c t) \cos(\theta)$
- The **sine coil** picks up the orthogonal component: $V_{sin}(t) = V_m \sin(\omega_c t) \sin(\theta)$

The two outputs are amplitude-modulated (AM) signals whose envelopes are pure sine and cosine of the shaft angle — making the resolver a **mathematically perfect angle sensor**.

#### Part (b) Digital Angle Extraction Using R/D Converter (7 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

The two AM outputs cannot be directly read as a digital number. A **Resolver-to-Digital (R/D) Converter** is used. The conversion is done in three stages:

**Step 1 — Demodulation:**
A **synchronous demodulator** (a precision multiplier + low-pass filter) multiplies each output by $\sin(\omega_c t)$ and $\cos(\omega_c t)$ respectively, then filters out the high-frequency components. The result is:

$$x = \sin(\theta)$$
$$y = \cos(\theta)$$

**Step 2 — Angle Computation:**
The digital angle is computed using the **arctangent function**:

$$\theta = \arctan\!\left(\frac{x}{y}\right) = \arctan\!\left(\frac{\sin\theta}{\cos\theta}\right)$$

This computation is done in hardware using a **CORDIC algorithm** (Coordinate Rotation Digital Computer) inside the R/D converter IC, achieving sub-microsecond conversion.

**Step 3 — Output Format:**
The output is a parallel digital word of **14, 16, or even 20 bits** (resolution). A 16-bit R/D converter offers:

$$\text{Resolution} = \frac{360°}{2^{16}} = 0.0055° \approx 20 \text{ arc-seconds}$$

> [!TIP]
> For 7 marks, mention the **CORDIC algorithm** explicitly — it is the de-facto standard in R/D converter ICs (Analog Devices AD2S1210, Texas Instruments PGA411) and earns bonus marks.

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Board Exams:**
>
> 1. **Confusing "Sine" and "Cosine" coil names** in resolvers. Always state the reference convention first: *S1 is at 0° (Cosine), S3 is at 90° (Sine).*
> 2. **Forgetting the carrier term** $\sin(\omega t)$ in the output equations. A common error is to write $V = K V_m \cos(\theta)$ — this loses **2 marks** because the modulated carrier is essential to the principle of operation.
> 3. **Mixing up the excitation winding of TX and CT.** Students often say "CT rotor is excited" — this is wrong. The **CT rotor is NOT excited**; it is the error output.
> 4. **Sign error in CT derivation.** The error voltage is $\sin(\theta - \alpha)$, not $\cos(\theta - \alpha)$. The cosine form gives **zero** at 90° misalignment, not at alignment.
> 5. **Skipping the 120°/90° winding displacement** in construction diagrams. Examiners allocate a separate mark for the spatial distribution; a single-circle diagram is insufficient.
> 6. **Forgetting units.** Always mention Hz for frequency, V for voltage, ° for angle, and mN·m for torque.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist (Read before entering the exam hall):**
>
> - **Synchro = 3-phase, 120° displacement**; **Resolver = 2-phase, 90° displacement**.
> - **Rotor excitation** in TX, TR, Resolver; **Stator excitation** in CT.
> - **Output of TX:** 3 voltages with carrier $\times$ $\cos(\theta - 0°/120°/240°)$.
> - **Output of CT:** Single error voltage $\propto \sin(\theta_T - \alpha)$; **zero at electrical null**.
> - **Output of Resolver:** Sine and Cosine envelope on the same carrier.
> - **Torque Synchro (TR) Torque Equation:** $T_{max} = \frac{3 V_m^2 K^2}{2 \omega L_s}$ — very small, used only for indicators.
> - **Carrier frequency:** 50/60 Hz (mains), 400 Hz (aerospace), 10 kHz (modern resolvers).
> - **Stator slots:** **36 in a 2-pole synchro**, **24** or **36** in resolvers.
> - **R/D Converter** uses the **CORDIC algorithm** to extract $\theta = \arctan(\sin\theta / \cos\theta)$.
> - **Resolver vs Encoder:** Resolver gives **absolute position** and survives harsh environments; encoder is digital but fails in oil/dust.
> - **Applications to mention for full marks:** Cockpit indicators, radar antennas, CNC, robotics, flight control actuators.
> - **Key phrase for derivation:** *"Coupling between two coils varies as the cosine of the angle between their magnetic axes."*
> - **Memorize the sign convention:** Negative derivative $\Rightarrow$ phase shift of −90°; absorb it into the time reference.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Piezoelectric Sensors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMUT602 - MECHATRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Piezoelectric Sensors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

A **Piezoelectric Sensor** is a solid-state, active transducer that converts mechanical energy (force, pressure, acceleration, strain, vibration) into a measurable electrical charge (or voltage) by exploiting the **direct piezoelectric effect** discovered by Jacques and Pierre Curie in **1880**. Conversely, when an electric field is applied across the same crystal, it undergoes a proportional mechanical deformation — this is known as the **converse (or inverse) piezoelectric effect**, which is the operating principle behind piezoelectric *actuators*.

In the KTU 2024 Mechatronics framework, piezoelectric sensors are classified as **self-generating (active) transducers** because they do not require an external power source to generate an output signal. The Greek root *piezein* means *"to press or squeeze"*, which perfectly captures the essence of the mechanism — **pressure → electricity**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:**
> Piezoelectric sensors fall under the umbrella of *physical-domain sensors* in Mechatronics, sitting at the interface of the **Mechanical ↔ Electrical** energy domains in the Mechatronic block diagram.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine a **digital bathroom weighing scale** that lights up the moment you step on it — no battery, no plug, yet it produces a visible electrical reading. Inside, a tiny quartz or PZT crystal is sandwiched between two electrodes. When your weight presses down, the crystal lattice physically deforms; internal electric dipoles, previously neutral, get displaced, generating surface charges on the electrodes. This is the piezoelectric effect in action — *mechanical squeeze → electrical charge*.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "self-generating"?**
> Unlike a resistive strain gauge (which only changes resistance and *needs* an excitation voltage), a piezoelectric crystal literally *manufactures* its own charge. The sensor itself is a tiny current/charge generator when stressed.

### 1.3 Key Performance Metrics (Standard Quantities Used in KTU Problems)

- **Piezoelectric charge coefficient ($d$)** → **d = 2.3 × 10⁻¹² C/N** (typical for Quartz, X-cut)
- **Relative permittivity ($\epsilon_r$)** → **4.5 (Quartz), 1200 (PZT-5H)**
- **Coupling factor ($k$)** → **0.1 (Quartz), 0.6+ (PZT ceramics)** — represents efficiency of energy conversion
- **Resonant frequency ($f_r$)** → **Typically 50 kHz to 5 MHz**
- **Operating temperature** → **Up to 500°C for Quartz, ~250°C for PZT**

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner Tip:** KTU frequently tests the numerical difference between **Quartz** (natural crystal, low $d$, high stability, high temperature tolerance) and **PZT** (Lead Zirconate Titanate — a synthetic ferroelectric ceramic with very high $d$ but lower temperature ceiling).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step Breakdown

**Step 1 — Lattice-Level Origin of Piezoelectricity**
Certain crystals (non-centrosymmetric, e.g., Quartz, Tourmaline, PZT, PVDF) lack a center of symmetry in their unit cell. Under no stress, the internal dipole moments cancel out and net polarization = 0.

**Step 2 — Mechanical Deformation Phase**
When an external force $F$ is applied along the poling axis, the crystal lattice is squeezed. Positive and negative charge centers within the unit cell are mechanically displaced relative to each other.

**Step 3 — Net Surface Charge Generation**
This displacement produces a **non-zero electric dipole moment per unit volume**, manifesting as bound surface charges proportional to the applied stress: $\sigma = d \cdot T$, where $T$ is mechanical stress (N/m²).

**Step 4 — Electrode Collection**
Conducting electrodes deposited on the crystal surfaces collect these free charges. The total charge delivered is:

$$Q = d \cdot F$$

**Step 5 — Signal Conditioning**
Because the sensor's intrinsic capacitance is small and source impedance is extremely high, a **charge amplifier** or **voltage follower (high-impedance buffer)** is mandatory to prevent charge leakage and signal loss.

### 2.2 Why Piezoelectric Sensors Cannot Measure Static (DC) Quantities

This is one of the most **frequently asked conceptual questions** in KTU ESE papers. The reasoning:

- Under a constant force $F$, the generated charge $Q$ is constant.
- However, the internal leakage resistance $R_a$ (~10¹⁰ to 10¹³ Ω) of the crystal and the input resistance of the readout circuit form a parallel RC network.
- The charge bleeds away exponentially with time constant $\tau = R C$.
- Hence, only **dynamic (time-varying) signals** — vibration, acoustic waves, pulse, impact — produce a measurable output.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mistake:** Students often claim piezoelectric sensors can "measure constant pressure." They **cannot**. They measure *change* in force/pressure (dF/dt). For static pressure measurement, you must use a strain gauge or capacitive sensor.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Equation | Meaning of Terms | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $Q = d \cdot F$ | Direct piezoelectric — generated charge | $C$ |
| 2 | $V = \dfrac{d \cdot t}{\varepsilon \cdot A} \cdot F$ | Open-circuit output voltage | $V$ |
| 3 | $C_p = \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{t}$ | Intrinsic sensor capacitance | $F$ |
| 4 | $V = \dfrac{Q}{C_p} = \dfrac{d \cdot F}{C_p}$ | Voltage form of charge sensitivity | $V$ |
| 5 | $V_{out} = -\dfrac{Q}{C_f}$ | Charge-amplifier output | $V$ |
| 6 | $f_n = \dfrac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{L_m C_m}}$ | Mechanical resonance of sensor stack | $Hz$ |
| 7 | $k = \dfrac{d}{\sqrt{s^E \cdot \varepsilon^T}}$ | Electromechanical coupling coefficient | dimensionless |
| 8 | $S = s^E T + d_t E$ | Converse effect — strain = mechanical + electrical | — |
| 9 | $D = dT + \varepsilon^T E$ | Direct effect — electric displacement | $C/m^2$ |
| 10 | $g = \dfrac{d}{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r}$ | Piezoelectric voltage coefficient | $V \cdot m / N$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Symbols Recap:** $d$ = charge coefficient (C/N), $F$ = applied force (N), $t$ = crystal thickness (m), $A$ = electrode area (m²), $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12}$ F/m, $\varepsilon_r$ = relative permittivity, $g$ = voltage coefficient, $k$ = coupling factor, $s^E$ = elastic compliance at constant E-field, $D$ = electric displacement, $S$ = strain, $E$ = electric field, $T$ = stress, $C_f$ = feedback capacitance of charge amplifier.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Piezoelectric sensors dominate industries where **high-frequency dynamic measurement** is essential:
- **Automotive:** airbag accelerometers, knock sensors in engines, tire pressure monitoring.
- **Aerospace:** structural health monitoring (SHM) of aircraft wings, turbine blade vibration.
- **Medical:** ultrasound transducers (both transmit & receive), intravascular pressure catheters.
- **Industrial:** ultrasonic NDT (Non-Destructive Testing), vibration monitoring on CNC spindles, acoustic emission sensors.
- **Consumer electronics:** microphones, pickups in electric guitars, haptic touchscreens.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: From Mechanical Force to Output Voltage

We start with the fundamental constitutive relation for the direct piezoelectric effect:

$$D = dT + \varepsilon^T E$$

For an open-circuit sensor (no external E-field), $E = 0$, so:

$$D = dT$$

**Step 1:** Express stress $T$ in terms of force and area:

$$T = \frac{F}{A}$$

**Step 2:** Substituting back:

$$D = d \cdot \frac{F}{A}$$

**Step 3:** The total charge collected on electrode area $A$ is the surface integral of $D$:

$$Q = \int D \, dA = D \cdot A = d \cdot F$$

This is the **fundamental charge equation**, valid for the 3-direction (poling axis) under axial stress.

**Step 4:** The intrinsic capacitance of a parallel-plate piezo crystal is:

$$C_p = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{t}$$

**Step 5:** Open-circuit voltage is charge divided by capacitance:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{oc} &= \frac{Q}{C_p} = \frac{d \cdot F}{\dfrac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{t}} \\[6pt]
V_{oc} &= \frac{d \cdot t}{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A} \cdot F
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6:** Recognizing $\dfrac{d}{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r} = g$ (piezoelectric voltage coefficient, units V·m/N), we simplify:

$$V_{oc} = g \cdot t \cdot \frac{F}{A} = g \cdot t \cdot T$$

> [!TIP]
> **Insight:** Notice that $V_{oc}$ is directly proportional to **thickness** $t$. KTU problems often exploit this — doubling the crystal thickness doubles the open-circuit voltage (but halves the capacitance, which has implications for cable noise).

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Charge Amplifier Output Equation

A charge amplifier uses an op-amp with a feedback capacitor $C_f$ and feedback resistor $R_f$. The piezo sensor is modeled as a charge source $Q$ in parallel with its own capacitance $C_p$ and insulation resistance $R_a$.

**Step 1:** The op-amp is in inverting configuration; the inverting input is a **virtual ground**. So the voltage across the sensor is ≈ 0.

**Step 2:** All charge generated by the sensor flows into $C_f$ (because the virtual ground means no voltage drop across $R_a$, so no current is lost through leakage).

**Step 3:** The output voltage is set by the charge on $C_f$:

$$V_{out} = -\frac{Q}{C_f}$$

**Step 4:** Substituting $Q = d \cdot F$:

$$V_{out} = -\frac{d \cdot F}{C_f}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical KTU Concept:** The output of a charge amplifier is **independent of the sensor's own capacitance $C_p$** and the **cable capacitance**! This is why charge amplifiers are preferred in industrial applications where long cables are unavoidable.

---

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

> **Problem [KTU University Exam - May 2024]:**
> A PZT-5H piezoelectric pressure sensor has the following specifications: $d_{33} = 5.93 \times 10^{-10}$ C/N, $\varepsilon_r = 3400$, electrode area $A = 100$ mm², thickness $t = 1$ mm. The sensor is connected to a charge amplifier with $C_f = 1$ nF. A sinusoidal pressure of amplitude 50 kPa is applied (assume pressure = force/area).
>
> **Find:**
> (a) The charge generated per cycle.
> (b) The peak output voltage of the charge amplifier.

**Given:** $d_{33} = 5.93 \times 10^{-10}$ C/N, $A = 100$ mm² = $100 \times 10^{-6}$ m² = $10^{-4}$ m², $P = 50$ kPa = $50 \times 10^3$ N/m², $C_f = 1$ nF = $10^{-9}$ F.

**Part (a) — Charge generated:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
F &= P \times A = 50 \times 10^3 \times 10^{-4} \\[4pt]
F &= 5 \text{ N} \\[8pt]
Q &= d_{33} \times F \\[4pt]
Q &= 5.93 \times 10^{-10} \times 5 \\[4pt]
Q &= 2.965 \times 10^{-9} \text{ C} \\[4pt]
Q &= 2.965 \text{ nC}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Part (b) — Output voltage:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{out} &= -\frac{Q}{C_f} \\[4pt]
V_{out} &= -\frac{2.965 \times 10^{-9}}{10^{-9}} \\[4pt]
V_{out} &= -2.965 \text{ V (peak)}
\end{aligned}
$$

The negative sign simply indicates inversion by the inverting amplifier topology.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Quartz vs. PZT vs. PVDF

| Property | Quartz | PZT-5H | PVDF (Polymer Film) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Type | Natural single crystal | Ferroelectric ceramic | Ferroelectric polymer |
| $d_{33}$ (pC/N) | 2.3 | 593 | 33 |
| $\varepsilon_r$ | 4.5 | 3400 | 10 |
| Curie Temp (°C) | 573 | 195 | 80 |
| Max operating temp (°C) | 500 | 150 | 70 |
| Sensitivity | Low | Very high | Medium |
| Frequency range | Up to 100s of MHz | Up to few MHz | Up to ~10 MHz |
| Best for | High-temp, stable reference | General dynamic sensing | Flexible/curved surfaces |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Sensor Construction & Charge Flow Architecture (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Applied Mechanical Force F] --> B[Poled Piezo Crystal Element]
    B --> C[Charge Generation Q equals d times F]
    C --> D[Top Electrode]
    C --> E[Bottom Electrode]
    D --> F[Shielded Low-Noise Cable]
    E --> F
    F --> G[Charge Amplifier with Op-Amp]
    G --> H[Feedback Capacitor Cf]
    G --> I[Feedback Resistor Rf for DC Stability]
    H --> J[Conditioned Output Voltage Vout]
    I --> J
    J --> K[Signal Conditioner and Filter]
    K --> L[Microcontroller ADC and Display]
```

### 4.2 Sensor Internal Electrical Equivalent Model

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SENSOR["Piezo Sensor Equivalent Circuit"]
        Q1[Charge Source Q equals dF] --- Cp[Sensor Capacitance Cp]
        Ra[Insulation Resistance Ra very high] --- Cp
    end
    Cp --> Ccable[Cable Capacitance Ccable]
    Ccable --> Amp[Op-Amp Inverting Input Virtual Ground]
    Ra --> Amp
    Amp --> Cf[Feedback Capacitor Cf]
    Cf --> Out[Output Node Vout]
    Rf[Feedback Resistor Rf] --- Cf
    Rf --- Out
```

### 4.3 Functional Block Diagram — Mechatronic Integration

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P[Process Mechanical Variable] --> Sensor[Piezoelectric Sensor]
    Sensor --> SigCon[Signal Conditioner Charge Amp]
    SigCon --> ADC[ADC]
    ADC --> MCU[Microcontroller Decision Logic]
    MCU --> Driver[Power Driver]
    Driver --> Actuator[Actuator Stepper or Solenoid]
    Actuator --> P
    MCU --> HMI[Human Machine Interface Display]
```

### 4.4 Frequency Response Characteristics (Qualitative Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph FR[Frequency Response of Piezo Sensor]
        LF[Low Frequency Cutoff fL equals 1 over 2 pi R Cf]
        MF[Flat Useful Bandwidth Mid Range]
        HF[Resonance Peak at fn then Roll-off]
        LF --> MF --> HF
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Frequency Response:**
> - **$f_L$** is the low-frequency cutoff set by the charge amplifier's $R_f C_f$ time constant.
> - **Mid-band** is the flat, useful measurement region.
> - **$f_n$** is the mechanical resonance of the crystal stack — the sensor can *amplify* signals here but linearity is lost; avoid operating at $f_n$ for precision measurement.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
> **Q1. Define the piezoelectric effect. Differentiate between the direct and converse piezoelectric effects.**

**Model Answer:**
The piezoelectric effect is the ability of certain non-centrosymmetric crystals (e.g., Quartz, PZT, PVDF) to interconvert mechanical and electrical energy.

- **Direct Piezoelectric Effect:** Mechanical stress applied to the crystal produces an electrical charge (or voltage) on its surfaces. Equation: $Q = dF$.
- **Converse Piezoelectric Effect:** When an electric field is applied across the crystal, it undergoes a proportional mechanical strain. Equation: $S = d_t E$.

Used as **sensors** (direct effect) and as **actuators** (converse effect). **[3 Marks]**

---

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
> **Q2. Why are piezoelectric sensors unsuitable for measuring static (DC) forces? How is this limitation overcome?**

**Model Answer:**
Piezoelectric sensors cannot measure static forces because the generated charge $Q = dF$ leaks away through the internal insulation resistance $R_a$ and the finite input impedance of the readout circuit. The discharge follows $Q(t) = Q_0 e^{-t/\tau}$ where $\tau = R_a C_p$.

**Limitation overcome by:**
1. Using **charge amplifiers** with very high feedback resistance ($R_f \sim 10^{10}$–$10^{12}$ Ω) to extend $\tau$ to thousands of seconds.
2. Using **electronic integrators** that continuously reset.
3. For truly static measurement, use **strain gauges** or **load cells** instead. **[3 Marks]**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]**
> **Question A:**
> **(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the working principle of a piezoelectric sensor. Derive the expression for the output voltage and charge generated for a force applied along the poling axis. **[7 Marks]**
>
> **(b)** A piezoelectric accelerometer based on the seismic-mass principle uses a PZT crystal with $d_{33} = 400 \times 10^{-12}$ C/N. The seismic mass is $m = 10$ g and the crystal capacitance is $C_p = 1$ nF. The accelerometer is subjected to a vibration of amplitude 5 g (where $g = 9.81$ m/s²). Determine: (i) the peak force on the crystal, (ii) the peak charge generated, and (iii) the open-circuit peak voltage across the crystal. **[7 Marks]**

### Model Solution — Question A

**(a) Working Principle & Derivation [7 Marks]:**

The piezoelectric sensor consists of a poled piezoelectric crystal (Quartz/PZT) sandwiched between two metal electrodes. When a force $F$ is applied along the poling (3-) axis, the crystal lattice is mechanically strained. Because the crystal is non-centrosymmetric, this strain displaces the internal charge centers, producing a proportional surface charge on the electrodes.

Block diagram:

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Mechanical Input F] --> B[Piezo Crystal]
    B --> C[Charge Q equals dF]
    C --> D[Charge Amplifier]
    D --> E[Vout equal to Q by Cf]
```

**Derivation:**
From the constitutive equation $D = dT + \varepsilon^T E$, with $E = 0$ (open circuit):

$$
\begin{aligned}
D &= dT = d \cdot \frac{F}{A} \\[4pt]
Q &= D \cdot A = d \cdot F \\[4pt]
V_{oc} &= \frac{Q}{C_p} = \frac{d \cdot t}{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A} \cdot F
\end{aligned}
$$

---

**(b) Numerical Problem Solution [7 Marks]:**

**Given:** $d_{33} = 400 \times 10^{-12}$ C/N, $m = 10$ g = $0.01$ kg, $C_p = 1$ nF = $10^{-9}$ F, acceleration $a = 5g = 5 \times 9.81 = 49.05$ m/s².

**(i) Peak force on crystal:**
$$
F = m \cdot a = 0.01 \times 49.05 = 0.4905 \text{ N}
$$

**(ii) Peak charge:**
$$
Q = d_{33} \cdot F = 400 \times 10^{-12} \times 0.4905 = 1.962 \times 10^{-10} \text{ C} = 196.2 \text{ pC}
$$

**(iii) Open-circuit peak voltage:**
$$
V_{oc} = \frac{Q}{C_p} = \frac{1.962 \times 10^{-10}}{10^{-9}} = 0.1962 \text{ V} \approx 196.2 \text{ mV}
$$

---

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2023]**
> **Question B:**
> **(a)** Explain the construction and working of a charge amplifier used with piezoelectric sensors. Derive the expression for its output voltage. State two advantages of a charge amplifier over a voltage amplifier. **[7 Marks]**
>
> **(b)** Compare piezoelectric sensors with strain gauge based load cells in terms of: principle, sensitivity, frequency response, static vs. dynamic measurement capability, and typical applications. Mention any three industrial applications of piezoelectric sensors. **[7 Marks]**

### Model Solution — Question B

**(a) Charge Amplifier [7 Marks]:**

The charge amplifier consists of an operational amplifier with a feedback capacitor $C_f$ (and a large feedback resistor $R_f$ for DC bias stabilization). The piezoelectric sensor is connected between the inverting input and ground.

**Working:** The op-amp's virtual ground at the inverting node ensures zero potential difference across the sensor. Hence, all generated charge flows into $C_f$, and none is lost through cable capacitance or sensor leakage.

**Derivation of Output Voltage:**

Using the principle of conservation of charge at the inverting node (KCL):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Charge into node from sensor} &= \text{Charge stored in } C_f \\[4pt]
Q &= C_f \cdot V_{out} \cdot (-1) \quad \text{(inverting)} \\[4pt]
V_{out} &= -\frac{Q}{C_f} = -\frac{d \cdot F}{C_f}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Advantages over voltage amplifier:**
1. **Cable-independent:** Output is independent of cable capacitance — long cables can be used without recalibration.
2. **Lower noise:** Charge-mode operation has better SNR for low-frequency dynamic signals.
3. **Wide dynamic range:** Handles charges from pC to µC by selecting appropriate $C_f$.

---

**(b) Comparison [7 Marks]:**

| Parameter | Piezoelectric Sensor | Strain Gauge Load Cell |
|---|---|---|
| Principle | Direct piezoelectric effect ($Q = dF$) | Resistance change due to strain |
| Active/Passive | Active (self-generating) | Passive (needs excitation) |
| Sensitivity | Very high (pC/N) | Medium (mV/V) |
| Frequency response | Excellent (DC to MHz) | Limited (typically < 1 kHz) |
| Static measurement | **Not suitable** (charge leaks) | **Excellent** (true DC) |
| Dynamic measurement | Excellent | Acceptable |
| Temperature stability | Good (Quartz) to moderate (PZT) | Requires compensation |
| Cost | Higher | Lower |
| Typical applications | Vibration, acoustic, ultrasound, impact | Weighing scales, force testing rigs, pressure tanks |

**Three industrial applications of piezoelectric sensors:**
1. **Accelerometers** for vibration monitoring in CNC machines and turbines.
2. **Ultrasonic transducers** for medical imaging and NDT.
3. **Pressure sensors** in engines and hydraulic systems for dynamic combustion analysis.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Loss Zones:**
> 1. **Confusing $d_{33}$ with $d_{31}$:** Always check the *direction of applied force* vs. *poling direction*. Force along poling → $d_{33}$; force perpendicular → $d_{31}$.
> 2. **Forgetting the charge-amplifier inversion sign:** The output is negative of the input charge; omitting the minus sign loses 1 mark.
> 3. **Claiming piezoelectric sensors can measure static force:** This is a guaranteed 2-mark deduction. Always state the *dynamic-only* limitation.
> 4. **Mixing up $C_p$ (sensor capacitance) with $C_f$ (feedback capacitance):** They serve entirely different roles; $C_p$ is the source, $C_f$ sets the gain.
> 5. **Unit mismatches:** Convert mm² → m², g → kg, kPa → N/m² *before* substituting. KTU examiners are strict on dimensional consistency.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid Revision Checklist:**

- [x] Piezoelectric effect = mechanical ↔ electrical energy coupling in non-centrosymmetric crystals.
- [x] **Direct effect:** Stress → Charge ($Q = dF$); used in **sensors**.
- [x] **Converse effect:** Voltage → Strain; used in **actuators**.
- [x] Foundational equation: $Q = d \cdot F$, $V_{oc} = d \cdot t \cdot F / (\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A)$.
- [x] Sensor is **active** (self-generating) — no excitation voltage required.
- [x] **Cannot measure static signals** — charge leaks through finite $R_a$.
- [x] Charge amplifier output: $V_{out} = -Q / C_f$ — *independent* of cable capacitance.
- [x] Voltage amplifier output: $V_{out} = Q / (C_p + C_cable)$ — *dependent* on cable.
- [x] **Quartz** = stable, low $d$, high-temp reference; **PZT** = high $d$, common general-purpose.
- [x] Coupling factor $k$ indicates energy-conversion efficiency.
- [x] Sensor acts as a charge source *in parallel* with its own capacitance $C_p$ and leakage resistance $R_a$.
- [x] Applications: accelerometers, ultrasonic transducers, microphones, knock sensors, NDT, SHM.
- [x] Always operate **below** the mechanical resonance $f_n$ for linear measurements.
- [x] Symbols: $d$ (charge coeff), $g$ (voltage coeff), $k$ (coupling), $\varepsilon_r$ (relative permittivity), $t$ (thickness), $A$ (electrode area).

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Acoustic Emission Sensors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMUT602 - MECHATRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Acoustic Emission Sensors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# Acoustic Emission (AE) Sensors — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Acoustic Emission (AE)** is the class of phenomena whereby **transient elastic waves** are generated by the **rapid release of energy** within a material. This energy release produces **stress waves** that propagate through the structure and can be detected on the surface by **piezoelectric transducers** known as **AE sensors** (also called AE transducers).

An **Acoustic Emission Sensor** is therefore defined as a **passive, non-contact (or contact-resonant) electro-mechanical transducer** that converts **high-frequency mechanical vibrations (typically 20 kHz to 1 MHz)** originating from a defect or event source into a **measurable electrical signal** (voltage) using the **direct piezoelectric effect**.

The complete system conforms to the testing standard **ASTM E1106** (Standard Practice for Monitoring AE During Steady, Dynamic, and Transient Loading) and **ASTM E2191** (Standard Guide for Examination of AE of Pressurized Systems).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-world analogy: "Listening to a Material's Cracks Whisper"**

Imagine placing a highly sensitive **stethoscope** on a steel pressure vessel. You cannot *see* a microscopic crack growing inside the metal wall, but when a crack nucleates or propagates, the material locally **"pops"** — releasing a tiny burst of stored elastic energy as a mechanical shock wave. This wave travels through the metal at the speed of sound in steel (**≈ 5000 m/s**), reaches the surface, and is picked up by the AE sensor.

Just like a doctor uses a stethoscope to detect abnormal heart sounds, an AE sensor "listens" for abnormal **stress-release events** in engineering structures:

| Everyday Analogy | AE Sensor Equivalent |
| :--- | :--- |
| Stethoscope on a chest | AE sensor on a structural surface |
| Heart murmur | Crack initiation or growth |
| Popcorn kernel popping | Sudden fiber break in composite |
| Hissing of a punctured tire | Leak-induced AE in a pipe |
| Whisper vs. shout | Low-amplitude vs. high-amplitude AE event |

The sensor itself **does not** emit any energy — it is **passive**. It only "hears" the sounds that the material makes on its own when stressed.

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The following constants and reference values are universally accepted in AE testing and must be memorized:

> [!NOTE]
> - **Reference voltage for the dB AE scale:** $V_{ref} = 1\ \mu V$ at the sensor preamplifier output
> - **Threshold reference for dB AE scale:** $0\ \text{dB AE} \equiv 1\ \mu V$
> - **Threshold reference for dB(AE) voltage scale:** $120\ \text{dB AE} \equiv 1\ V$
> - **Standard frequency band:** $\mathbf{20\ kHz}$ to $\mathbf{1\ MHz}$ (sometimes up to 2 MHz for special broadband probes)
> - **Speed of longitudinal wave in steel:** $c_L \approx 5000\ \text{m/s}$
> - **Speed of longitudinal wave in aluminum:** $c_L \approx 6300\ \text{m/s}$
> - **Speed of longitudinal wave in water:** $c_L \approx 1480\ \text{m/s}$

## 1.4 Sources of Acoustic Emission (KTU-Required Enumeration)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mandatory Enumeration for KTU 2024 ESE — Module 1:** A student is expected to list *at least six* sources of AE.

1. **Plastic deformation** (dislocation movement in metals)
2. **Crack initiation and propagation** (ductile and brittle)
3. **Fiber breakage and matrix cracking** in fiber-reinforced polymers (FRP)
4. **Delamination** in composite laminates
5. **Corrosion processes** (e.g., pitting, stress-corrosion cracking)
6. **Phase transformations** (e.g., martensitic transformation in steel)
7. **Leakage of fluids/gases** from pressurized systems
8. **Friction and wear** between sliding surfaces
9. **Loosening of fasteners / impact events**
10. **Machining operations** (cutting, grinding)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Concept — Step-by-Step Working Principle

The AE sensing chain can be broken into **six sequential stages** for analytical clarity:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "S-F-A-A-D-P" Framework of AE Monitoring:**
> **S**ource → **F**ield Propagation → **A**cquisition → **A**mplification → **D**igitization → **P**rocessing

### Step 1 — The Source Event
A **sudden, localized release of strain energy** (e.g., a micro-crack jumping by $\Delta a$) releases energy $E$ that locally stresses the surrounding material.

The released energy propagates as a **stress wave packet** containing both longitudinal (P-wave) and shear (S-wave) components, plus surface (Rayleigh) waves at the free boundary.

### Step 2 — Wave Propagation Through the Medium
The wave attenuates as it travels. The amplitude decay is governed by:

$$A(x) = A_0 \cdot e^{-\alpha x}$$

where $\alpha$ is the **attenuation coefficient** (Np/m), and $A_0$ is the source amplitude. In metals, typical values are $\alpha \approx 0.5$ to $5\ \text{Np/m}$ at 300 kHz.

### Step 3 — Transduction by the Piezoelectric Element
The surface of the structure vibrates at the **particle velocity** $\dot{u}(t)$. The AE sensor's piezoelectric disc (commonly **PZT-5A** or **PZT-5H**) experiences a dynamic stress and generates a **charge** $Q$ via the **direct piezoelectric effect**.

### Step 4 — Charge-to-Voltage Conversion (Preamplifier)
A **charge amplifier** (or voltage preamplifier) converts the charge to a usable voltage. The preamplifier typically has:
- **Gain:** $20\ \text{dB}$ or $40\ \text{dB}$
- **Bandwidth:** $20\ \text{kHz}$ to $2\ \text{MHz}$
- **Input impedance:** $> 10^{12}\ \Omega$

### Step 5 — Signal Conditioning
The signal is **band-pass filtered** (to reject low-frequency mechanical noise and high-frequency electrical noise), then **amplified** further to match the ADC range.

### Step 6 — Parameter Extraction & Analysis
Standard AE parameters (see Section 2.3) are extracted from the time-domain waveform and used for:
- Source location (via **triangulation** with multiple sensors)
- Source characterization (crack vs. leak vs. friction)
- Severity assessment (Felicity ratio, intensity analysis)

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is the **exam-ready cheat sheet**. All marks-allocated numerical problems in the 2024 scheme ESE will be solvable using these equations.

| # | Formula (LaTeX) | Symbol Legend | Engineering Utility |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $dB_{AE} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{V_s}{1\ \mu V}\right)$ | $V_s$ = sensor output voltage | Logarithmic amplitude scale used to compress 3-decade dynamic range |
| 2 | $V_{out} = g_{33} \cdot t \cdot \sigma$ | $g_{33}$ = voltage coefficient, $t$ = piezo thickness, $\sigma$ = applied stress | Direct output voltage of thickness-mode AE sensor |
| 3 | $f_n = \dfrac{1}{2t}\sqrt{\dfrac{c_{33}^{D}}{\rho}}$ | $c_{33}^{D}$ = stiffness at constant $D$, $\rho$ = density, $t$ = thickness | Natural (resonant) frequency of piezo disc |
| 4 | $A(x) = A_0 e^{-\alpha x}$ | $A_0$ = source amplitude, $\alpha$ = attenuation, $x$ = distance | Wave amplitude decay with travel distance |
| 5 | $v_{wave} = \sqrt{\dfrac{E}{\rho}}$ (longitudinal, thin rod) | $E$ = Young's modulus, $\rho$ = density | Wave speed in a thin elastic rod |
| 6 | $v_L = \sqrt{\dfrac{E(1-\nu)}{\rho(1+\nu)(1-2\nu)}}$ | $\nu$ = Poisson's ratio | Bulk longitudinal wave speed (infinite medium) |
| 7 | $\text{Counts} = \sum \text{threshold crossings}$ | $N$ threshold = programmable, usually $40\ \text{dB AE}$ | Total ringdown count above threshold |
| 8 | $\text{Energy} = \dfrac{1}{R} \int_{t_1}^{t_2} V^2(t)\, dt$ | $R$ = load resistance, $V(t)$ = voltage waveform | Energy of one AE hit |
| 9 | $\text{Felicity Ratio} = \dfrac{P_{\text{AE on re-load}}}{P_{\text{prior max load}}}$ | $P$ = load at which AE resumes | Damage severity indicator |
| 10 | $\text{Location: } \Delta t = \dfrac{\vert x_1 - x_2 \vert}{v}$ | $\Delta t$ = arrival time difference, $v$ = wave speed | Source triangulation with 2+ sensors |
| 11 | $S_{AE} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{V_{\text{out}}}{V_{\text{ref}}}\right)$ (dB ref 1 V/Pa) | $S_{AE}$ = acoustic sensitivity | Sensor sensitivity in dB |
| 12 | $Q_m = \dfrac{f_r}{\Delta f}$ | $f_r$ = resonant freq., $\Delta f$ = $-3\ \text{dB}$ bandwidth | Mechanical quality factor of resonant AE sensor |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** The pipe character has been replaced with `\vert` or `\mid` in the above table (e.g., $\vert x_1 - x_2 \vert$) to prevent markdown rendering errors during exam-paper PDF generation.

## 2.3 The Six Standard AE Parameters (KTU 2024 Module 1 — Must Know)

Every AE "hit" (one isolated burst of energy) is characterized by these six parameters defined in **ASTM E1316**:

1. **Amplitude ($A$)** — Peak voltage of the largest excursion during the hit, expressed in **dB AE**.
2. **Duration ($D$)** — Time from the first to the last threshold crossing, in $\mu s$.
3. **Rise Time ($R$)** — Time from the first threshold crossing to the peak, in $\mu s$.
4. **Counts ($N$)** — Number of threshold crossings during the hit.
5. **Energy ($E$)** — Integral of the squared voltage over the hit duration (in **energy units** or aJ).
6. **Counts-to-Peak ($C_p$)** — Counts up to the peak amplitude.

## 2.4 Resonant vs. Broadband AE Sensors

> [!NOTE]
> This is a **favourite KTU 2024 ESE question topic** — often appearing as a 7-mark comparison.

| Feature | Resonant (Narrowband) | Broadband (Wideband) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Frequency response | Narrow peak at $f_r$ (e.g., 150 kHz, 300 kHz) | Flat over 20 kHz – 1 MHz |
| Sensitivity | Very high at $f_r$ | Moderate but uniform |
| $Q_m$ factor | High (typically 25 – 40) | Low (typically 1 – 5) |
| Waveform fidelity | Poor (rings for many cycles) | Excellent |
| Typical use | Field NDT, leak detection | Research, source characterization |
| Cost | Low | High |
| Example | Physical Acoustics R15 (150 kHz) | MISTRAS WD (wideband) |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

AE sensors are deployed in:
- **Aerospace:** Real-time monitoring of aircraft composite wings (Fatigue)
- **Civil:** Bridge cable-stay monitoring, dam crack detection
- **Nuclear:** Spent-fuel canister integrity
- **Petrochemical:** Pipeline leak detection, storage tank monitoring
- **Manufacturing:** Tool wear monitoring in CNC machining
- **Power generation:** Steam turbine blade crack monitoring

# Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following derivations follow directly from the **piezoelectric constitutive relations** (IEEE Standard 176) and the **ASTM E1106 / E1316** standards. They form the analytical backbone of KTU 2024 numerical problems.

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — dB AE Scale from Linear Voltage

**Given:** The dynamic range of an AE signal at the preamplifier output can vary from **$1\ \mu V$** (background) to **$1\ V$** (strong crack). A linear scale cannot display this 6-decade range. The dB AE scale solves this.

**Step 1:** Define the linear reference voltage (per ASTM E1106):

$$V_{ref} = 1\ \mu V = 10^{-6}\ V$$

**Step 2:** Express any sensor voltage $V_s$ as a ratio to $V_{ref}$:

$$\text{Ratio} = \frac{V_s}{V_{ref}}$$

**Step 3:** Apply the standard decibel definition for voltage quantities (factor of 20, not 10, because voltage is an **amplitude**, not a power):

$$dB_{AE} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{V_s}{V_{ref}}\right)$$

**Step 4 (KTU Numerical Example):** A sensor output is $V_s = 10\ \text{mV} = 10^{-2}\ V$. Compute dB AE.

Substitute into the equation:

$$\begin{aligned}
dB_{AE} &= 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{10^{-2}}{10^{-6}}\right) \\
&= 20 \log_{10}\!\left(10^{4}\right) \\
&= 20 \times 4 \\
&= 80\ \text{dB AE}
\end{aligned}$$

**Conversion check:** $0\ \text{dB AE} = 1\ \mu V$, and each $20\ \text{dB}$ step = factor of 10 in voltage.

| $V_s$ | dB AE |
| :--- | :--- |
| $1\ \mu V$ | $0$ |
| $10\ \mu V$ | $20$ |
| $100\ \mu V$ | $40$ |
| $1\ \text{mV}$ | $60$ |
| $10\ \text{mV}$ | $80$ |
| $100\ \text{mV}$ | $100$ |
| $1\ V$ | $120$ |

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Piezoelectric Output Voltage from Stress

**Given:** A PZT-5A disc of thickness $t$ and area $A$ experiences an axial stress $\sigma$ (from a surface acoustic wave). Find the open-circuit voltage $V_{oc}$.

**Step 1:** The **piezoelectric constitutive equation** in the 3-direction (poling axis) under zero external electric field ($E = 0$) is:

$$D_3 = d_{33}\, \sigma_3 + \varepsilon^{T}_{33} E_3$$

For the open-circuit condition, $E_3 = V/t$, but for an open circuit, the total free charge must equal the induced charge, which we solve below directly.

**Step 2:** The **direct piezoelectric effect** in thickness mode gives a charge per unit area (electric displacement):

$$D_3 = d_{33}\, \sigma_3$$

**Step 3:** Total induced charge on the electrode of area $A$:

$$Q = D_3 \cdot A = d_{33}\, \sigma_3\, A$$

**Step 4:** The capacitance of the parallel-plate piezo disc is:

$$C_p = \frac{\varepsilon^{T}_{33}\, A}{t}$$

**Step 5:** Open-circuit voltage is $V = Q / C_p$:

$$\begin{aligned}
V_{oc} &= \frac{d_{33}\, \sigma_3\, A}{\varepsilon^{T}_{33}\, A / t} \\
&= \frac{d_{33}\, t}{\varepsilon^{T}_{33}}\, \sigma_3 \\
&= g_{33}\, t\, \sigma_3
\end{aligned}$$

where $g_{33} = d_{33} / \varepsilon^{T}_{33}$ is the **piezoelectric voltage coefficient** in V·m/N.

**Step 6 (Result):**

$$\boxed{\,V_{out} = g_{33}\, t\, \sigma\,}$$

**Numerical Example (KTU 2024 style):** A PZT-5A AE sensor has $g_{33} = 24 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{V·m/N}$, thickness $t = 2\ \text{mm} = 2 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{m}$, and the surface stress is $\sigma = 10\ \text{kPa} = 10^{4}\ \text{Pa}$.

$$\begin{aligned}
V_{out} &= (24 \times 10^{-3}) \times (2 \times 10^{-3}) \times 10^{4} \\
&= 24 \times 2 \times 10^{-2} \\
&= 0.48\ \text{V}
\end{aligned}$$

Convert to dB AE:

$$dB_{AE} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{0.48}{10^{-6}}\right) = 20 \log_{10}(4.8 \times 10^{5}) \approx 113.6\ \text{dB AE}$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Resonant Frequency of the Piezo Disc

**Given:** For a thickness-mode piezo disc, the resonance occurs when the **thickness equals a half-wavelength** of the standing longitudinal wave.

**Step 1:** Standing wave condition:

$$t = \frac{\lambda}{2} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \lambda = 2t$$

**Step 2:** Wave speed in the piezo material:

$$v = \sqrt{\frac{c_{33}^{D}}{\rho}}$$

**Step 3:** Frequency is wave speed over wavelength:

$$\begin{aligned}
f_n &= \frac{v}{\lambda} = \frac{1}{2t}\sqrt{\frac{c_{33}^{D}}{\rho}}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 (Result):**

$$\boxed{\,f_n = \frac{1}{2t}\sqrt{\frac{c_{33}^{D}}{\rho}}\,}$$

**Numerical Example:** For PZT-5A, $c_{33}^{D} \approx 147\ \text{GPa}$, $\rho = 7750\ \text{kg/m}^3$, and a disc thickness of $t = 5\ \text{mm} = 5 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{m}$.

$$\begin{aligned}
f_n &= \frac{1}{2 \times 5 \times 10^{-3}} \sqrt{\frac{147 \times 10^{9}}{7750}} \\
&= 100 \times \sqrt{1.897 \times 10^{7}} \\
&= 100 \times 4355 \\
&\approx 435.5\ \text{kHz}
\end{aligned}$$

This is why a **5 mm PZT-5A disc** is the heart of a **$\mathbf{450\ kHz}$** resonant AE sensor — a common industrial standard.

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Source Location via Time-of-Arrival (Linear Arrangement)

**Given:** Two sensors $S_1$ and $S_2$ are placed on a rod at positions $x_1$ and $x_2$ respectively, separated by distance $L = x_2 - x_1$. A source at position $x$ emits a wave at time $t = 0$. The arrival time difference is $\Delta t = t_2 - t_1$.

**Step 1:** Time for wave to reach $S_1$:

$$t_1 = \frac{\vert x - x_1 \vert}{v}$$

**Step 2:** Time for wave to reach $S_2$:

$$t_2 = \frac{\vert x - x_2 \vert}{v}$$

**Step 3:** Time difference:

$$\Delta t = t_2 - t_1 = \frac{\vert x - x_2 \vert - \vert x - x_1 \vert}{v}$$

**Step 4:** Multiply by $v$:

$$v \cdot \Delta t = \vert x - x_2 \vert - \vert x - x_1 \vert$$

**Step 5 (Result):** Solving for the unknown source position $x$ (assuming $x_1 < x < x_2$):

$$\boxed{\,x = \frac{x_1 + x_2}{2} - \frac{v \cdot \Delta t}{2}\,}$$

**Numerical Example (KTU 2024):** On an aluminum rod ($v \approx 5000\ \text{m/s}$), two sensors are placed at $x_1 = 0\ \text{m}$ and $x_2 = 1\ \text{m}$. The arrival time difference measured is $\Delta t = 50\ \mu s = 50 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{s}$.

$$\begin{aligned}
x &= \frac{0 + 1}{2} - \frac{5000 \times 50 \times 10^{-6}}{2} \\
&= 0.5 - \frac{0.25}{2} \\
&= 0.5 - 0.125 \\
&= 0.375\ \text{m}
\end{aligned}$$

The source is **0.375 m from $S_1$** and **0.625 m from $S_2$** (the source is closer to $S_1$, consistent with $S_1$ receiving the signal earlier).

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Felicity Ratio (Damage Severity Metric)

**Given:** A specimen is first loaded to a maximum load $P_{max}$ (no prior history of damage). AE starts at a stress level $\sigma_1$. On unloading and reloading, AE re-starts at $P_{AE}$ where $P_{AE} < P_{max}$ if damage exists.

**Step 1:** Define the **Felicity Ratio (FR):**

$$FR = \frac{P_{AE\ \text{on re-load}}}{P_{\text{prior max load}}}$$

**Step 2 (ASTM E1067 Interpretation):**

| Felicity Ratio $FR$ | Damage State |
| :--- | :--- |
| $FR < 1.0$ | **Kaiser effect violated** → active damage present |
| $FR \approx 1.0$ | Healthy material (Kaiser effect intact) |
| $FR > 1.0$ | Impossible (unless pre-stress) |

**Step 3 (KTU 2024 numerical):** During a pressure test, a vessel is first pressurized to $P_{max} = 8\ \text{bar}$. AE initiates at $5\ \text{bar}$. On re-pressurization, AE resumes at $P_{AE} = 4.5\ \text{bar}$.

$$FR = \frac{4.5}{8} = 0.5625$$

Since $FR < 1.0$, the **Felicity effect** is observed, indicating **active damage** (likely sub-critical crack growth). The vessel is flagged for **immediate inspection**.

## 3.6 Analytical Summary Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> **Quick-Reference Analytic Map for KTU 2024 Module 1 — Acoustic Emission**

| Quantity | Equation Form | Typical Numerical Range in AE Testing |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Sensor output voltage | $V = g_{33}\, t\, \sigma$ | $1\ \mu V$ to $1\ V$ |
| Amplitude (log) | $dB_{AE} = 20 \log(V_s/1\mu V)$ | $0$ to $120\ \text{dB AE}$ |
| Wave speed (steel) | $v_L = \sqrt{E/\rho}$ | $\approx 5000\ \text{m/s}$ |
| Wave speed (aluminum) | $v_L = \sqrt{E/\rho}$ | $\approx 6300\ \text{m/s}$ |
| Resonant frequency of disc | $f_n = \frac{1}{2t}\sqrt{c_{33}^{D}/\rho}$ | $50\ \text{kHz}$ to $2\ \text{MHz}$ |
| Attenuation | $A(x) = A_0 e^{-\alpha x}$ | $\alpha = 0.5$ to $5\ \text{Np/m}$ at 300 kHz |
| Felicity Ratio | $FR = P_{AE}/P_{max}$ | $< 1$ for damaged material |
| Source location (linear) | $x = \frac{x_1+x_2}{2} - \frac{v\Delta t}{2}$ | Sub-cm accuracy at $v \approx 5000\ \text{m/s}$ |

> [!NOTE]
> All questions are modeled on the **KTU 2024 Scheme End Semester Evaluation (ESE)** pattern for **PCMUT602 — Mechatronics, Module 1**. The mark split follows the revised Bloom's Taxonomy cognitive distribution: **Remember (L1), Understand (L2), Apply (L3), Analyze (L4), Evaluate (L5), Create (L6)**.
