## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — Model 2024 Scheme]

> **"Define Acoustic Emission. List any four sources of acoustic emission in engineering materials."**
> **[3 Marks] — Bloom Level: Remember (L1)**

**Model Answer (Examiner's Key):**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (1.5 Marks):** Acoustic Emission (AE) is the phenomenon of generation of **transient elastic waves** in a material caused by the **rapid release of energy** from a localized source such as a crack, corrosion event, or phase transformation. These waves propagate through the material and can be detected on the surface using piezoelectric transducers known as **AE sensors**.

**Any four sources of AE (4 × 0.5 = 2 Marks):**

1. **Crack initiation and propagation** in metals and alloys
2. **Fiber breakage and delamination** in composite materials
3. **Plastic deformation** due to dislocation motion
4. **Leakage** of pressurized fluids or gases from pipelines and vessels
5. **Corrosion** — particularly pitting and stress-corrosion cracking
6. **Friction and wear** between moving mechanical components
7. **Phase transformations** such as martensitic transformation in steels

**Allocation:** Definition = 1.5 marks; Four sources (0.5 each) = 2 marks; Total = **3 marks**.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Model 2024 Scheme]

> **"Differentiate between resonant and broadband acoustic emission sensors. State one application of each."**
> **[3 Marks] — Bloom Level: Understand (L2)**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Resonant AE Sensor | Broadband AE Sensor |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Frequency response** | Narrow band peaked at a specific frequency (e.g., 150 kHz) | Flat response over 20 kHz to 1 MHz |
| **Sensitivity** | Very high at the resonant frequency | Uniform but moderate |
| **Waveform fidelity** | Poor — the sensor rings for many cycles | Excellent — preserves true waveform shape |
| **Typical application** | Field NDT, leak detection, weld monitoring | Research, source mechanism studies, modal AE |
| **Cost** | Lower | Higher |

**Application examples (1 mark):**
- **Resonant:** Leak detection in a chemical plant pipeline.
- **Broadband:** Source mechanism characterization in a composite coupon test.

**Allocation:** Three comparison points (1 mark each) = 3 marks. *(If only table given, marks awarded for table rows, not for the application.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module 1 ESE Pattern:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks** and is divided into two sub-parts of **7 marks each**. Students must answer **ONE full question** out of a choice of two (OR pattern). The split is typically **(a) 7 marks — descriptive/derivation** and **(b) 7 marks — analytical/numerical**.

---

### Question A [KTU University Exam — Model 2024 Scheme — 14 Marks]

> **(a) Explain the working principle of an Acoustic Emission sensor with a neat block diagram. Describe the function of the piezoelectric element and the preamplifier. (7 Marks)**
>
> **(b) An AE sensor output voltage is $V_s = 50\ \text{mV}$. Compute its amplitude in dB AE. If the sensor has $g_{33} = 25 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{V·m/N}$ and thickness $t = 2\ \text{mm}$, find the surface stress that produced this output. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer to (a):**

The Acoustic Emission sensor operates on the **direct piezoelectric effect**. When a transient elastic wave reaches the surface of the structure under test, the **piezoelectric disc** (commonly PZT-5A or PZT-5H) housed inside the AE sensor experiences a **dynamic mechanical stress**. This stress generates a proportional **electric charge** on the disc's electrodes.

**Block diagram (drawn by student):**
$$\text{AE Source} \rightarrow \text{Wave Propagation} \rightarrow \text{Sensor Face} \rightarrow \text{Piezoelectric Disc} \rightarrow \text{Preamplifier} \rightarrow \text{Filter} \rightarrow \text{ADC} \rightarrow \text{Processor} \rightarrow \text{Display}$$

**Function of the Piezoelectric Element (3 marks):**
- Converts mechanical stress from the acoustic wave into an electric charge.
- Operates at its **resonant frequency** (for resonant sensors) to maximize sensitivity.
- Poled in the thickness direction; the relevant coefficient is $d_{33}$ or $g_{33}$.

**Function of the Preamplifier (3 marks):**
- Converts the high-impedance charge signal into a low-impedance voltage signal.
- Provides fixed gain (typically **20 dB or 40 dB**) to overcome cable noise.
- Has very high input impedance ($> 10^{12}\ \Omega$) to avoid loading the piezo element.
- Has fixed bandpass response (typically **20 kHz to 2 MHz**).

**Marks split:** Block diagram = 1 mark; Piezo function = 3 marks; Preamplifier function = 3 marks. **Total = 7 marks.**

**Model Answer to (b):**

**Part (i) — Compute dB AE (3 marks):**

Using the standard formula:

$$dB_{AE} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{V_s}{1\ \mu V}\right)$$

Substitute $V_s = 50\ \text{mV} = 50 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{V}$:

$$\begin{aligned}
dB_{AE} &= 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{50 \times 10^{-3}}{10^{-6}}\right) \\
&= 20 \log_{10}(5 \times 10^{4}) \\
&= 20 \times [\log_{10}(5) + 4] \\
&= 20 \times [0.6990 + 4] \\
&= 20 \times 4.6990 \\
&\approx 93.98\ \text{dB AE}
\end{aligned}$$

**Part (ii) — Compute surface stress (4 marks):**

Using the derived constitutive relation:

$$V_{out} = g_{33} \cdot t \cdot \sigma$$

Solve for $\sigma$:

$$\sigma = \frac{V_{out}}{g_{33} \cdot t}$$

Substitute $V_{out} = 50 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{V}$, $g_{33} = 25 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{V·m/N}$, $t = 2\ \text{mm} = 2 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{m}$:

$$\begin{aligned}
\sigma &= \frac{50 \times 10^{-3}}{25 \times 10^{-3} \times 2 \times 10^{-3}} \\
&= \frac{50 \times 10^{-3}}{50 \times 10^{-6}} \\
&= 1000\ \text{Pa} = 1\ \text{kPa}
\end{aligned}$$

**Marks split:** Formula + substitution for dB = 3 marks; Stress derivation + answer = 4 marks. **Total = 7 marks.**

---

### Question B [KTU University Exam — Model 2024 Scheme — 14 Marks — Alternative Choice]

> **(a) Describe the application of Acoustic Emission sensors in non-destructive testing of pressure vessels. What is the Kaiser effect, and how is the Felicity ratio used as a damage indicator? (7 Marks)**
>
> **(b) In a linear AE source-location test on a steel pipe, two sensors are placed at $x_1 = 0\ \text{m}$ and $x_2 = 2\ \text{m}$. The wave speed in steel is $v = 5000\ \text{m/s}$. The arrival time difference is $\Delta t = 100\ \mu s$. Determine the source location. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer to (a):**

**Application in pressure vessel NDT (4 marks):**

Acoustic Emission testing is one of the most powerful NDT methods for **in-service pressure vessels** (per **ASME Section V, Article 12** and **ASTM E2191**). The procedure is:

1. **Instrumentation:** Multiple AE sensors (typically 4 to 32) are mounted on the vessel surface using a couplant (grease, gel, or tape) and held by magnetic clamps.
2. **Pressurization:** The vessel is pressurized in controlled steps (e.g., 0.25 × Design Pressure increments) while the AE system continuously records hits.
3. **Source location:** Arrival-time differences are processed by the triangulation algorithm to plot the source on a 2D map of the vessel.
4. **Severity assessment:** The Felicity ratio is computed at each load step.
5. **Decision:** A vessel with $FR < 1.0$ at the design pressure is **flagged for repair**.

**Kaiser Effect (1.5 marks):**
The **Kaiser effect** states that a material, when reloaded to a previously applied stress level, will **not produce significant AE** until the **previous maximum stress** is exceeded. It is a signature of **undamaged** elastic-plastic behavior. Discovered by Joseph Kaiser in 1950.

**Felicity Ratio (1.5 marks):**
The **Felicity Ratio (FR)** is defined as:

$$FR = \frac{\text{Load at which AE resumes on re-load}}{\text{Prior maximum load applied}}$$

- $FR = 1.0$ → Material is healthy (Kaiser effect intact)
- $FR < 1.0$ → **Felicity effect** observed → **active damage** (sub-critical crack growth, delamination, or corrosion)
- Lower the FR, the more severe the damage.

**Marks split:** NDT procedure = 4 marks; Kaiser = 1.5 marks; Felicity = 1.5 marks. **Total = 7 marks.**

**Model Answer to (b):**

**Step 1 — Identify the formula (2 marks):**

$$x = \frac{x_1 + x_2}{2} - \frac{v \cdot \Delta t}{2}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the values (2 marks):**

$$\begin{aligned}
x &= \frac{0 + 2}{2} - \frac{5000 \times 100 \times 10^{-6}}{2} \\
&= 1 - \frac{0.5}{2} \\
&= 1 - 0.25 \\
&= 0.75\ \text{m}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Interpret the result (3 marks):**
- The AE source is located **0.75 m from sensor 1** (at $x_1 = 0$) and **1.25 m from sensor 2** (at $x_2 = 2\ \text{m}$).
- The **negative sign** in the equation (we had $\Delta t = +100\ \mu s$, meaning $S_2$ received the wave later) implies the source is **closer to $S_1$**, which is consistent with our computed $x = 0.75\ \text{m}$.
- If $\Delta t$ were negative, we would conclude the source is on the other side of the midpoint, closer to $S_2$.
- If $\Delta t = 0$, the source is exactly at the **midpoint** of the two sensors.

**Marks split:** Formula selection = 2 marks; Substitution + arithmetic = 2 marks; Interpretation = 3 marks. **Total = 7 marks.**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls (Lose Marks Here!):**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of 20 in dB:** Some students wrongly use $10 \log(V/V_{ref})$ (which is for power, not voltage). The AE scale uses voltage, so it is **20 log**, not 10 log. **Penalty: 1 mark per occurrence.**
>
> 2. **Mixing up the dB AE reference voltage:** Students sometimes use $V_{ref} = 1\ \text{V}$ or $V_{ref} = 1\ \text{mV}$ for the dB AE scale. The correct reference is **$1\ \mu V$** at the preamplifier output (ASTM E1106). **Penalty: 1 mark.**
>
> 3. **Forgetting to convert units:** A common error is substituting $t = 2$ (in mm) directly into $V = g_{33} t \sigma$ without converting to meters. **Penalty: 0.5 mark.**
>
> 4. **Drawing a circuit diagram instead of a block diagram for Part B (a):** The question asks for a block diagram, not a circuit. Drawing a Wheatstone bridge or op-amp schematic is **0 marks for the diagram**. **Penalty: up to 1 mark.**
>
> 5. **Confusing Kaiser and Felicity effects:** A frequently tested KTU pitfall. The **Kaiser effect** = NO AE on reload until previous max is exceeded. The **Felicity effect** = AE on reload at a load LOWER than previous max (sign of damage). **Penalty: 1–2 marks.**
>
> 6. **Source-location sign error:** The expression $x = \frac{x_1+x_2}{2} - \frac{v\Delta t}{2}$ assumes $S_1$ is the **closer** sensor. If $\Delta t < 0$, the sign of the second term flips. **Penalty: 1 mark.**
>
> 7. **Omitting the units in the final answer:** Always state dB AE, kPa, m, etc. **Penalty: 0.5 mark per missing unit.**

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist — Acoustic Emission Sensors (Module 1)**

### Core Concept
- **Acoustic Emission** = transient elastic waves from a sudden, localized release of energy in a material.
- AE sensors are **passive** (no transmitted energy).
- Detection is by **direct piezoelectric effect** in a PZT disc.

### Operating Frequency Band
- Standard: **20 kHz to 1 MHz**.
- Resonant sensors tuned to a single frequency (e.g., 150 kHz, 300 kHz, 450 kHz).
- Broadband sensors: flat response over the full band.

### Sensor Construction
- **Wear plate** (protects the disc and improves coupling).
- **PZT disc** (the active element).
- **Backing material** (tungsten-epoxy, damps back-radiation).
- **Preamp inside** (some sensors are "integral preamp" type).

### Key Equations
- $dB_{AE} = 20 \log(V_s / 1\ \mu V)$
- $V_{out} = g_{33} \cdot t \cdot \sigma$
- $f_n = \frac{1}{2t}\sqrt{c_{33}^{D}/\rho}$
- $A(x) = A_0 e^{-\alpha x}$
- $FR = P_{AE} / P_{max}$

### Six AE Parameters
1. Amplitude (dB AE)
2. Duration ($\mu s$)
3. Rise Time ($\mu s$)
4. Counts
5. Energy
6. Counts-to-Peak

### Material Effects
- **Kaiser Effect:** No AE on reload until previous max stress exceeded. Indicates **healthy** material.
- **Felicity Effect:** AE on reload at lower stress than previous max. Indicates **damage**.

### Standards to Remember
- **ASTM E1106** — Monitoring AE during loading.
- **ASTM E1316** — AE terminology.
- **ASTM E2191** — Examination of AE of pressurized systems.
- **ASTM E1067** — Felicity ratio.
- **ASME Section V Article 12** — AE in-service inspection.
- **IEEE 176** — Piezoelectric constitutive equations.

### Practical Tips
- **Couplant** is mandatory (grease, gel, or tape) — no air gap between sensor and surface.
- **Threshold** is usually set at **40 dB AE** (some applications use 30 or 50 dB).
- **Source location accuracy** improves with more sensors and a known, constant wave speed.
- **Attenuation** increases with frequency and travel distance — limit sensor spacing to **< 1 m** for steel and **< 0.5 m** for aluminum at 300 kHz.

### Common Applications
- Pressure vessel inspection
- Pipeline leak detection
- Bridge and structural health monitoring
- Composite damage monitoring (aerospace)
- Tool wear monitoring
- Weld quality monitoring

### One-Line Memory Hook
> *"AE is the material's own voice when it fractures, and the piezoelectric sensor is the microphone that translates that voice into dB AE for the engineer to hear."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Principle and Types of Vibration Sensors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMUT602 - MECHATRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Principle and Types of Vibration Sensors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Vibration Sensor** is a transducer that converts mechanical oscillatory motion (displacement, velocity, or acceleration of a vibrating body) into a measurable electrical signal proportional to the vibration parameter being monitored. The vast majority of contact-type vibration sensors operate on the **Seismic Mass Principle**, in which a known inertial mass constrained by a spring and damper system experiences relative motion against the vibrating housing.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** In Module 1 of PCMUT602, the principle of vibration measurement is rooted in a **single degree-of-freedom (SDOF) mass-spring-damper** model. Understanding this model is mandatory, as it forms the foundation for deriving the response curves for displacement, velocity, and acceleration sensors.

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine holding a cup of water while walking. The water inside sloshes around due to your footsteps — that sloshing motion is the *relative motion of an inertial mass* (water) with respect to the *moving container* (your hand). 

A vibration sensor does the **exact same thing** in miniature:
- The **cup** = sensor housing (mounted on vibrating machine)
- The **water** = precisely engineered seismic mass (proof mass)
- The **sloshing** = the measured signal (how much the mass lags, oscillates, or deflects)
- The **cup's rim** = the spring/damper restoring mechanism

By cleverly converting that sloshing (relative motion) into voltage, current, or charge, the sensor tells us *how violently the machine is shaking*.

### 1.3 Why Vibration Measurement Matters

| Application Domain | Vibration Parameter Tracked | Why It Matters |
|---|---|---|
| **Rotating Machinery** (turbines, motors) | Velocity (mm/s RMS) | ISO 10816 / ISO 20816 compliance |
| **Structural Health Monitoring** (bridges, buildings) | Acceleration (g, m/s²) | Earthquake response, modal analysis |
| **Automotive NVH** | Acceleration + Frequency | Ride comfort, suspension tuning |
| **Aerospace / Turbomachinery** | Displacement (μm pk-pk) | Blade tip clearance, bearing health |
| **MEMS / Consumer Electronics** | Acceleration (3-axis, g) | Screen rotation, step counting |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Reference Metric:** Vibration severity in industrial machinery is universally expressed in **mm/s RMS (Root Mean Square velocity)** as per **ISO 10816-3**, with critical zones at **4.5 mm/s** (good), **7.1 mm/s** (acceptable), and **11.2 mm/s** (unacceptable). KTU exam questions frequently test these zone boundaries.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Universal Seismic Mass Model (SDOF)

Every displacement, velocity, and acceleration sensor is fundamentally governed by the equation of motion of a damped harmonic oscillator:

$$m\ddot{x}(t) + c\dot{x}(t) + kx(t) = F(t)$$

Where the symbols are defined as:

- $m$ = **seismic (proof) mass** in **kg**
- $c$ = **viscous damping coefficient** in **N·s/m**
- $k$ = **spring constant** in **N/m**
- $x(t)$ = **relative displacement** of mass with respect to housing (m)
- $F(t)$ = **external forcing / inertial load** (N)
- $t$ = time in **seconds (s)**

### 2.2 Key Derived Parameters

| Parameter | Formula | Physical Meaning | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Natural Frequency** | $\omega_n = \sqrt{\dfrac{k}{m}}$ | Frequency at which the sensor resonates freely | rad/s |
| **Natural Frequency (Hz)** | $f_n = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{k}{m}}$ | Same as above in cycles per second | Hz |
| **Damping Ratio** | $\zeta = \dfrac{c}{2\sqrt{km}} = \dfrac{c}{2m\omega_n}$ | Ratio of actual to critical damping | dimensionless |
| **Critical Damping** | $c_c = 2\sqrt{km}$ | Minimum damping to prevent oscillation | N·s/m |
| **Frequency Ratio** | $r = \dfrac{\omega}{\omega_n}$ | Ratio of forcing freq. to natural freq. | dimensionless |

### 2.3 Operating Regions — Why Sensor Type Depends on $r$

The *type* of vibration sensor you choose is entirely dictated by the **frequency ratio** $r = \omega/\omega_n$:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Golden Rule of Vibration Sensors (Board Favourite!):**
> - For $\omega \gg \omega_n$ (i.e., $r \gg 1$, typically $r > 3$): The relative displacement $x \approx -\ddot{y}/\omega_n^2$ → **ACCELEROMETER** region.
> - For $\omega \approx \omega_n$ (i.e., $r \approx 1$): The relative displacement is amplified by **quality factor $Q$** → **VELOCIMETER** (damped at $\zeta \approx 0.7$).
> - For $\omega \ll \omega_n$ (i.e., $r \ll 1$): The relative displacement $x \approx y$ → **DISPLACEMENT SENSOR** (seismometer / geophone).

### 2.4 Frequency Response Magnitudes

The transfer function $|H(j\omega)|$ for the relative displacement $x$ versus the base motion $y$ in a SDOF system:

$$\left| \frac{X(j\omega)}{Y(j\omega)} \right| = \dfrac{r^2}{\sqrt{(1-r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2}}$$

Where:
- $X$ = relative displacement amplitude
- $Y$ = base (housing) displacement amplitude

### 2.5 Why Piezoelectric Sensitivity Works Mathematically

The piezoelectric constitutive equation (direct effect) used in accelerometers:

$$q = d_{33} \cdot F$$

Where:
- $q$ = generated electric charge in **Coulombs (C)**
- $d_{33}$ = piezoelectric charge constant in **C/N (or pC/N)**
- $F$ = applied compressive force in **Newtons (N)**

Typical quartz: $d_{33} \approx 2.3$ pC/N; PZT ceramic: $d_{33} \approx 250$–500 pC/N.

### 2.6 Capacitive Sensor Equation (MEMS)

For a parallel-plate capacitive accelerometer:

$$C = \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d}$$

A small displacement $\Delta d$ of the seismic mass produces:

$$\dfrac{\Delta C}{C} \approx \dfrac{\Delta d}{d}$$

Where:
- $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12}$ F/m (permittivity of free space)
- $\varepsilon_r$ = relative permittivity of dielectric
- $A$ = plate overlap area in **m²**
- $d$ = gap between plates in **m**

### 2.7 Eddy Current Displacement Sensor

An eddy current probe generates a high-frequency alternating magnetic field (typically **1–3 MHz**) using an excited coil. When a conductive target approaches, eddy currents are induced, reducing the coil inductance. The resulting impedance change is linearized to yield displacement:

$$\Delta L \propto \dfrac{1}{x}$$

Where $x$ = distance between probe tip and target surface. Operating range: typically **0.5–5 mm**.

### 2.8 Laser Doppler Vibrometer (LDV)

Based on the optical Doppler shift of a back-scattered laser beam:

$$f_D = \dfrac{2 v}{\lambda}$$

Where:
- $f_D$ = Doppler frequency shift in **Hz**
- $v$ = instantaneous velocity of vibrating target surface in **m/s**
- $\lambda$ = wavelength of laser (commonly **632.8 nm** for He–Ne or **1550 nm** for fiber-coupled)

### 2.9 Engineering Utility

| Industry | Sensor Used | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Power plant turbines | Piezoelectric accelerometer | High temperature, broadband |
| Turbo-generator bearings | Eddy current probe | Non-contact, sub-mm measurement |
| Wind turbine blades | MEMS accelerometer | Wireless, low cost |
| MEMS gyroscope (smartphone) | Coriolis-effect MEMS | Angular rate sensing |
| Bridges / Civil | Geophone + GPS | Long-period seismic response |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: SDOF Seismic Mass Equation of Motion

Consider a proof mass $m$ suspended inside a vibrating housing. Let the **absolute displacement** of the housing be $y(t)$ and the absolute displacement of the mass be $z(t)$. Then the **relative displacement** is:

$$x(t) = z(t) - y(t)$$

**Step 1 — Forces acting on the seismic mass:**
The mass experiences three forces:
1. Spring restoring force: $-k \cdot x = -k(z - y)$
2. Damper viscous force: $-c \cdot \dot{x} = -c(\dot{z} - \dot{y})$
3. Inertial reaction: $m \ddot{z}$

**Step 2 — Newton's Second Law application:**

$$m \ddot{z} + c(\dot{z} - \dot{y}) + k(z - y) = 0$$

**Step 3 — Substituting $x = z - y$ (so $\dot{x} = \dot{z} - \dot{y}$ and $\ddot{x} = \ddot{z} - \ddot{y}$):**

$$m(\ddot{x} + \ddot{y}) + c\dot{x} + kx = 0$$

**Step 4 — Rearranging into standard form:**

$$\boxed{m\ddot{x} + c\dot{x} + kx = -m\ddot{y}}$$

This is the **master equation of vibration sensing**. The right-hand side is the inertial force arising from the housing acceleration $\ddot{y}$. This is the precise reason an accelerometer measures $\ddot{y}$ directly when $r \gg 1$.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Accelerometer Behaviour at High Frequency ($r \gg 1$)

Assume the housing vibrates harmonically: $y(t) = Y\sin(\omega t)$, so $\ddot{y} = -\omega^2 Y\sin(\omega t)$.

**Step 1 — Substitute steady-state sinusoidal solution:**

Let $x(t) = X\sin(\omega t - \phi)$. Substituting into the master equation:

$$m(-\omega^2 X\sin\theta) + c(\omega X\cos\theta) + k(X\sin\theta) = -m(-\omega^2 Y\sin\theta)$$

where $\theta = \omega t - \phi$.

**Step 2 — Collect sine terms (after eliminating phase lag by taking magnitudes):**

$$X(k - m\omega^2)^2 + (c\omega)^2 = (m\omega^2 Y)^2$$

**Step 3 — Solve for amplitude ratio:**

$$\dfrac{X}{Y} = \dfrac{(m\omega^2)}{\sqrt{(k - m\omega^2)^2 + (c\omega)^2}}$$

**Step 4 — Divide numerator and denominator by $m\omega_n^2 = k$:**

$$\dfrac{X}{Y} = \dfrac{r^2}{\sqrt{(1 - r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2}}$$

**Step 5 — Apply the high-frequency limit $r \gg 1$:**

When $r \gg 1$, the term $(1 - r^2)^2 \approx r^4$ dominates, and $(2\zeta r)^2$ becomes negligible:

$$\dfrac{X}{Y} \approx \dfrac{r^2}{r^2 \sqrt{1 + (2\zeta/r)^2}} \approx 1$$

But the **inertial force** on the right side of the master equation yields:

$$X \approx \dfrac{-m\ddot{y}}{k} = \dfrac{-m\ddot{y}}{m\omega_n^2} = \dfrac{-\ddot{y}}{\omega_n^2}$$

**Step 6 — Final accelerometer sensitivity:**

$$\boxed{X = \dfrac{\ddot{y}}{\omega_n^2}}$$

This proves that the **relative displacement of the seismic mass is directly proportional to the housing acceleration**, with the proportionality constant $1/\omega_n^2$. This is exactly why piezoelectric accelerometers are designed with **very high natural frequencies** (e.g., 30 kHz to 100 kHz) so that operating frequencies remain in the $r \gg 1$ plateau.

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Velocimeter Behaviour Near Resonance ($r = 1$, $\zeta = 0.7$)

The velocity of the seismic mass relative to housing is $\dot{x} = j\omega X$. The velocity transfer function magnitude is:

$$\left| \frac{\dot{X}}{Y} \right| = \dfrac{\omega \cdot r^2}{\sqrt{(1-r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2}}$$

At $r = 1$ and $\zeta = 0.707$ (optimal damping for flat velocity response), the denominator equals $\sqrt{0 + 2} = \sqrt{2}$, giving:

$$\left| \frac{\dot{X}}{Y} \right|_{r=1} = \dfrac{\omega}{\sqrt{2}} = \dfrac{\omega_n}{\sqrt{2}}$$

This constant amplitude ratio over a broad band ($0.5 < r < 3$) is why geophones with $\zeta \approx 0.7$ function as **velocity sensors**.

---

### 3.4 Derivation: Piezoelectric Charge to Voltage Conversion

The piezoelectric accelerometer generates a charge $q$ that must be converted to a usable voltage $V$ via a charge amplifier:

$$V_{out} = -\dfrac{q}{C_f}$$

Where $C_f$ is the feedback capacitance of the op-amp circuit (typically **1 nF to 10 nF**). Sensitivity of an accelerometer is therefore expressed as:

$$S = \dfrac{V_{out}}{a_{input}} = \dfrac{q/a}{C_f} = \dfrac{d_{33} \cdot m}{C_f} \quad \left[\dfrac{V}{g}\right]$$

Where $a$ = acceleration in g (where $1\,g = 9.81\,\text{m/s}^2$).

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked Example Trace (typical KTU problem):** A piezoelectric accelerometer has a seismic mass of $m = 20$ g, uses PZT with $d_{33} = 400$ pC/N, and a charge amplifier with $C_f = 1$ nF. Find its sensitivity in mV/g.
>
> Solution: $S = \dfrac{400 \times 10^{-12} \cdot 0.020}{1 \times 10^{-9}} = 8 \times 10^{-3}$ V/g = **8 mV/g**.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Sensor Type | Measured Quantity | Frequency Range | Output | Contact? | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Piezoelectric Accelerometer** | Acceleration | 1 Hz – 50 kHz | Charge / Voltage | Yes | General vibration, modal analysis |
| **Piezoresistive Accelerometer** | Acceleration | DC – 5 kHz | Resistance change | Yes | Crash testing, low freq. |
| **Capacitive (MEMS) Accelerometer** | Acceleration | DC – 10 kHz | Capacitance change | Yes | Consumer electronics, IoT |
| **Eddy Current Probe** | Displacement | DC – 10 kHz | Voltage | No (proximity) | Shaft vibration in turbomachinery |
| **Laser Doppler Vibrometer** | Velocity | DC – 20 MHz | Doppler frequency | No (optical) | Precision labs, MEMS testing |
| **Geophone / Seismometer** | Velocity | 0.1 Hz – 500 Hz | Voltage | Yes | Earthquakes, structural |
| **MEMS Gyroscope** | Angular rate | DC – 10 kHz | Voltage | Yes | Angular vibration, stabilization |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Working Principle of a Vibration Sensor

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Vibrating Machine Surface] --> B[Sensor Housing Mounted to Surface]
    B --> C[Spring k and Damper c Constraint]
    C --> D[Seismic Proof Mass m]
    D --> E[Relative Motion x t Between Mass and Housing]
    E --> F[Transduction Mechanism]
    F --> G1[Piezoelectric Charge q]
    F --> G2[Capacitance Change Delta C]
    F --> G3[Inductance Change Delta L]
    F --> G4[Doppler Frequency fD]
    G1 --> H[Signal Conditioning Amplifier]
    G2 --> H
    G3 --> H
    G4 --> H
    H --> I[Analog or Digital Output Signal]
    I --> J[DAQ System and Analysis FFT RMS]
```

### 4.2 Classification Tree of Vibration Sensors

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    VS[Vibration Sensors]
    VS --> CT[Contact Type]
    VS --> NCS[Non Contact Type]
    CT --> ACT[Acceleration Sensors]
    CT --> VCT[Velocity Sensors]
    CT --> DCT[Displacement Seismometer]
    ACT --> PIEZ[Piezoelectric]
    ACT --> PR[Piezoresistive]
    ACT --> CAP[Capacitive MEMS]
    VCT --> GEO[Electromagnetic Geophone]
    VCT --> MEM[MEMS Gyroscope]
    NCS --> EDD[Eddy Current Probe]
    NCS --> CAPN[Capacitive Probe]
    NCS --> LDV[Laser Doppler Vibrometer]
    NCS --> MIC[Acoustic Emission Sensor]
```

### 4.3 Piezoelectric Accelerometer Internal Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Base Stud to Machine] --> B[Sensor Casing]
    B --> C[Quartz or PZT Crystal Disc]
    C --> D[Seismic Mass m on Top]
    D --> E[Preload Bolt Spring Compression]
    C --> F[Output Electrodes]
    F --> G[Charge Amplifier External]
    G --> H[Voltage Output V proportional to Acceleration]
```

### 4.4 Frequency Response Regions Mapped to Sensor Choice

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R1[Frequency Ratio r omega over omega n]
    R1 --> LOW[r much less than 1]
    R1 --> MID[r approximately 1]
    R1 --> HIGH[r much greater than 1]
    LOW --> D1[DISPLACEMENT SENSOR Seismometer]
    MID --> D2[VELOCITY SENSOR Geophone zeta approx 0.7]
    HIGH --> D3[ACCELEROMETER Piezoelectric MEMS]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Memory Aid for Boards:** "**D**isplacement — **L**ow frequency, **V**elocity — **M**id, **A**cceleration — **H**igh" → remember **DLV-MA-H** mnemonic.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**State and explain the seismic mass principle of vibration measurement.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The seismic mass principle states that a known proof mass $m$ suspended inside a sensor housing by a spring of stiffness $k$ and damper of coefficient $c$ undergoes **relative motion** $x(t)$ with respect to the housing when subjected to base vibration $y(t)$. The governing equation is:

$$m\ddot{x} + c\dot{x} + kx = -m\ddot{y}$$

By selecting an appropriate operating region (low, mid, or high frequency ratio $r$), the relative displacement can be made proportional to base displacement, velocity, or acceleration, allowing the sensor to function as a **displacement sensor, velocity sensor, or accelerometer** respectively. **(3 marks)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Differentiate between piezoelectric and piezoresistive accelerometers.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Piezoelectric | Piezoresistive |
|---|---|---|
| **Operating Principle** | Generates charge via direct piezoelectric effect | Resistance changes due to strain |
| **Frequency Range** | High frequency (typically > 1 Hz to 50 kHz) | DC to a few kHz |
| **Output** | Charge (C) | Resistance change (Wheatstone bridge) |
| **Power Required** | None (self-generating) | External bridge excitation needed |
| **Sensitivity to Temp** | Lower | Higher (needs compensation) |
| **Typical Use** | High-freq. structural vibration, modal testing | Crash tests, low-freq. incl. static g |

**(3 marks)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> - Marks are **deducted if students skip derivation steps** — every transition must be justified (1–2 marks lost per skipped step).
> - Marks are deducted for **not stating units** of every physical quantity.
> - Marks are deducted for **confusing natural frequency ($f_n$)** with the operating (forcing) frequency $\omega$. Always define symbols before using them.
> - Diagrams in Q1/Q2 sub-parts are worth **2 marks minimum** — students skipping the labelled diagram lose 2 marks instantly.
> - Numerical answers must be boxed, e.g., $\boxed{8 \text{ mV/g}}$, or valuation is incomplete.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2023]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat schematic, explain the **principle of operation of a piezoelectric accelerometer**. Derive the governing equation for a SDOF seismic mass system and show that in the high-frequency limit, the displacement of the proof mass is proportional to the base acceleration. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A piezoelectric accelerometer has a seismic mass of $m = 10$ g, a natural frequency $f_n = 30$ kHz, and uses PZT crystal with $d_{33} = 300$ pC/N. It is connected to a charge amplifier with feedback capacitance $C_f = 2$ nF. The sensor is mounted on a machine vibrating at $f = 500$ Hz with amplitude $A = 0.5$ mm peak.
- **(i)** Compute the peak acceleration of the machine in m/s² and in $g$.
- **(ii)** Determine the sensitivity of the accelerometer in V/g.
- **(iii)** Calculate the peak output voltage from the charge amplifier. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) — 7 Marks:**

*Principle (3 marks):* A piezoelectric accelerometer consists of a piezoelectric crystal disc (quartz or PZT) sandwiched between a base stud and a seismic proof mass. When the base experiences acceleration, inertial force $F = m\ddot{y}$ is exerted on the crystal, producing a charge $q = d_{33} \cdot F$ by the direct piezoelectric effect. The charge is converted to voltage via a charge amplifier.

*Derivation (4 marks):*

Starting from Newton's law on the proof mass suspended inside the housing:

$$m\ddot{z} + c(\dot{z} - \dot{y}) + k(z - y) = 0$$

Let $x = z - y$. Then:

$$m(\ddot{x} + \ddot{y}) + c\dot{x} + kx = 0$$

Rearranging:

$$m\ddot{x} + c\dot{x} + kx = -m\ddot{y} \quad \text{...(master equation)}$$

For harmonic base motion $y = Y\sin(\omega t)$, the steady-state amplitude ratio is:

$$\dfrac{X}{Y} = \dfrac{r^2}{\sqrt{(1-r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2}}$$

In the high-frequency limit $r = \omega/\omega_n \gg 1$:

$$\dfrac{X}{Y} \approx 1 \quad \text{but} \quad X = \dfrac{m\ddot{y}}{k} = \dfrac{\ddot{y}}{\omega_n^2}$$

Hence relative displacement is **proportional to acceleration** with sensitivity $1/\omega_n^2$. ∎

*Diagram (in addition to the 7 marks, must be drawn for full marks):*

```
[ Schematic: Base stud → PZT disc → Mass m → Preload bolt ]
```

---

**(b) — 7 Marks:**

**(i) Peak acceleration (2 marks):**

Peak acceleration for $y = A\sin(\omega t)$ is $a_{peak} = \omega^2 A$.

Given: $A = 0.5 \text{ mm} = 0.5 \times 10^{-3}$ m, $f = 500$ Hz, $\omega = 2\pi f = 3141.59$ rad/s.

$$a_{peak} = (3141.59)^2 \cdot 0.5 \times 10^{-3} = 4.935 \times 10^{3} \text{ m/s}^2$$

In units of $g$:

$$a_{peak} = \dfrac{4935}{9.81} \approx \boxed{503\,g}$$

**(ii) Sensitivity (2 marks):**

$$S = \dfrac{q/a}{C_f} = \dfrac{d_{33} \cdot m}{C_f}$$

Plugging in values:

$$S = \dfrac{(300 \times 10^{-12} \text{ C/N}) \cdot (10 \times 10^{-3} \text{ kg})}{2 \times 10^{-9} \text{ F}}$$

$$S = \dfrac{3 \times 10^{-12}}{2 \times 10^{-9}} = 1.5 \times 10^{-3} \text{ V·m/s}^{-2}$$

Converting to V/g:

$$S = 1.5 \times 10^{-3} \times 9.81 = 0.01472 \text{ V/g} = \boxed{14.72 \text{ mV/g}}$$

**(iii) Peak output voltage (3 marks):**

$$V_{out, peak} = S \cdot a_{peak} = 14.72 \text{ mV/g} \times 503\,g$$

$$V_{out, peak} = \boxed{7.404 \text{ V peak}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiner note:** A common student error is to forget the factor of 2 between peak and RMS, or to mistakenly use $1g = 10$ m/s² (use exactly **9.81 m/s²** in KTU papers unless explicitly told otherwise). Marks deducted: typically 1 mark.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**

**(a)** Classify vibration sensors. With a neat block diagram, explain the working of a **Laser Doppler Vibrometer (LDV)** and derive its governing equation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **eddy current proximity probe** with a diagram. State two industrial applications and compare its performance with a piezoelectric accelerometer for measuring shaft vibration in a turbo-generator. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) — 7 Marks:**

*Classification (3 marks):* Vibration sensors are classified into three families based on the measured quantity:
1. **Acceleration sensors** (piezoelectric, piezoresistive, capacitive MEMS)
2. **Velocity sensors** (electromagnetic geophone, MEMS gyroscope)
3. **Displacement sensors** (eddy current probe, capacitive probe, LDV)

They are also classified by contact vs. non-contact, and active vs. passive.

*LDV Working Principle (2 marks):* A Laser Doppler Vibrometer projects a monochromatic laser beam (typically $\lambda = 632.8$ nm He–Ne or 1550 nm IR) onto a vibrating target. The back-scattered beam undergoes a **Doppler frequency shift** proportional to the instantaneous surface velocity. A Bragg cell or interferometric decoder extracts this shift and outputs a voltage proportional to velocity or displacement.

*Derivation of LDV Equation (2 marks):* When the target moves away with velocity $v$, the reflected beam receives a Doppler shift $\Delta f = 2v/\lambda$ (factor of 2 arises because the beam is first incident on the moving surface and then reflected). Therefore:

$$\boxed{v = \dfrac{\lambda \cdot f_D}{2}}$$

Displacement is obtained by integrating the velocity signal over time:

$$y(t) = \int v(t)\, dt$$

The LDV is a non-contact optical sensor with sub-nanometer resolution and bandwidths up to 20 MHz.

---

**(b) — 7 Marks:**

*Eddy Current Probe Description (3 marks):* An eddy current probe consists of a coil wound around a ferrite core, excited by a high-frequency oscillator (1–3 MHz). When a conductive target (e.g., steel shaft) approaches the probe tip, eddy currents induced in the target oppose the original magnetic flux, reducing the coil inductance. This change is detected by a bridge circuit and demodulated to yield a DC voltage proportional to the gap between probe and target.

The output voltage:

$$V_{out} = K \cdot x$$

where $K$ is the calibration constant (V/mm) and $x$ is the gap distance. Typical range: **0.5–5 mm**, resolution: **0.1 μm**.

*Two Industrial Applications (2 marks):*
1. **Shaft vibration monitoring** in turbo-generators and large rotating machinery — measures relative displacement between shaft and bearing housing.
2. **Blade tip clearance** in jet engines — measures tip-to-casing gap in real time during engine operation.

*Comparison with Piezoelectric Accelerometer (2 marks):*

| Feature | Eddy Current Probe | Piezoelectric Accelerometer |
|---|---|---|
| **Type** | Non-contact, displacement | Contact, acceleration |
| **Measured Quantity** | Absolute displacement of shaft | Vibration transmitted to housing |
| **Frequency Range** | DC to 10 kHz | 1 Hz to 50 kHz |
| **Target Material** | Conductive metals only | Any surface (via stud mount) |
| **Use Case** | Shaft-relative motion | Casing vibration |

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist (Save for Last-Minute Prep):**

- ✅ Master SDOF equation: $m\ddot{x} + c\dot{x} + kx = -m\ddot{y}$
- ✅ Natural frequency formula: $\omega_n = \sqrt{k/m}$ and $f_n = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{k/m}$
- ✅ Damping ratio: $\zeta = \dfrac{c}{2\sqrt{km}}$; critical damping $c_c = 2\sqrt{km}$
- ✅ Sensor type depends on $r = \omega/\omega_n$: **Displacement** for $r \ll 1$, **Velocity** for $r \approx 1$ with $\zeta \approx 0.7$, **Acceleration** for $r \gg 1$
- ✅ Piezoelectric charge: $q = d_{33} F$, with quartz $d_{33} \approx 2.3$ pC/N, PZT $d_{33} \approx 400$ pC/N
- ✅ Charge amplifier: $V_{out} = -q/C_f$
- ✅ Capacitive accelerometer: $\Delta C/C \approx \Delta d/d$
- ✅ Eddy current probe: $\Delta L \propto 1/x$, range 0.5–5 mm
- ✅ Laser Doppler Vibrometer: $f_D = 2v/\lambda$
- ✅ ISO 10816-3 vibration severity zones: **good < 4.5 mm/s**, **acceptable 4.5–7.1 mm/s**, **unacceptable > 11.2 mm/s** RMS
- ✅ Mnemonic: **D**isplacement-**L**ow, **V**elocity-**M**id, **A**cceleration-**H**igh frequency ratio
- ✅ Always draw a labelled schematic in 14-mark answers — it carries **2–3 marks**.
- ✅ Always box final numerical answers.
- ✅ Always state units (g, m/s², mm, Hz) — marks lost for missing units.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Inductive, Hall Effect, Hot Wire, Thermistor, Piezo Electric, Piezoresistive Based Sensors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMUT602 - MECHATRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Inductive, Hall Effect, Hot Wire, Thermistor, Piezo Electric, Piezoresistive Based Sensors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Introduction to Physical Sensors in Mechatronics

A **sensor** is a physical device that detects a physical, chemical, or biological stimulus (input) and produces a measurable electrical or optical signal (output) as a response. In a mechatronic system, sensors form the *nervous system* — they provide the feedback signal that closes the control loop. The six sensor families covered in this module (Inductive, Hall Effect, Hot Wire, Thermistor, Piezoelectric, and Piezoresistive) belong to the broader category of **transducers** — devices that convert energy from one domain to another.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU PCMUT602 Module 1):**
> The classification under study is based on the **principle of energy conversion**, not the measurand. A single measurand (e.g., displacement) can be measured by *multiple* principles (LVDT, Hall effect, strain gauge). Understanding the principle is what examiners test.

---

## 1.2 Inductive Sensors

### Formal Definition
An **inductive sensor** is a transducer that converts a mechanical quantity (displacement, position, velocity, force, pressure) into a change in inductance, mutual inductance, or induced EMF, exploiting **Faraday's Law of Electromagnetic Induction**.

### Conceptual Analogy
> [!NOTE]
> Think of an inductive sensor as a *two-player magnetic tug-of-war*. When a ferromagnetic target (the "iron bar") moves closer to a coil, it pulls the magnetic flux lines through the coil. The coil "feels" this change as a voltage spike. The further the bar moves, the louder the coil "screams" in voltage.

### Sub-Types Covered in KTU
| Sub-Type | Measurand | Working Principle |
|---|---|---|
| **LVDT** (Linear Variable Differential Transformer) | Linear displacement | Mutual inductance between 3 coils |
| **Variable Reluctance Sensor** | Position/Proximity | Change in magnetic circuit reluctance |
| **Eddy Current Sensor** | Displacement, Thickness | Induced eddy currents in target |

---

## 1.3 Hall Effect Sensor

### Formal Definition
A **Hall Effect sensor** is a solid-state device that produces an output voltage (the **Hall Voltage, $V_H$**) perpendicular to both the current flow and the applied magnetic field, due to the **Lorentz force** acting on moving charge carriers.

### Conceptual Analogy
> [!NOTE]
> Imagine water flowing through a river (current). Now a strong wind blows perpendicular to the river (magnetic field). The water gets pushed sideways against one bank, raising the level there. The **difference in water level between the two banks is your Hall voltage**. More current or stronger wind → bigger level difference.

### Key Constant
The **Hall Coefficient $R_H$** with units **m³/C (cubic meters per Coulomb)** characterizes the material.

---

## 1.4 Hot Wire Sensor (Thermal Anemometer)

### Formal Definition
A **hot wire sensor** is a thermal transducer that measures fluid (gas/liquid) flow velocity by sensing the **convective heat loss** from an electrically heated thin wire exposed to the flow.

### Conceptual Analogy
> [!NOTE]
> Picture a cup of hot tea on a windy day. The windier it gets, the faster the tea cools down. A hot wire sensor is like a *thermometer for wind speed* — the cooler the wire gets due to flow, the faster the flow must be. To maintain a constant temperature, the circuit must push more current through the wire — that extra current is the measured output.

### Governing Law
The relationship between heat loss and flow velocity is described by **King's Law of Heat Transfer**.

---

## 1.5 Thermistor

### Formal Definition
A **thermistor** (Thermal Resistor) is a passive solid-state temperature sensor whose **electrical resistance varies strongly and non-linearly with temperature**. The two principal types are **NTC** (Negative Temperature Coefficient) and **PTC** (Positive Temperature Coefficient).

### Conceptual Analogy
> [!NOTE]
> An NTC thermistor is like a *see-saw* between temperature and resistance: temperature goes UP, resistance goes DOWN. A PTC works the opposite way. This makes NTC the more common choice because as a thermistor self-heats (from current passing through it), it drops resistance → more current → more heat → a useful runaway for inrush current limiters, but a problem for precision measurement unless self-heating is suppressed.

### Classification Reminder
- **NTC**: $dR/dT < 0$ (most common)
- **PTC**: $dR/dT > 0$ (used as resettable fuses)

---

## 1.6 Piezoelectric Sensor

### Formal Definition
A **piezoelectric sensor** is an *active* transducer that converts dynamic mechanical stress (force, pressure, acceleration, vibration) into an electrical charge, exploiting the **direct piezoelectric effect** discovered by Jacques and Pierre Curie in **1880**.

### Conceptual Analogy
> [!NOTE]
> Imagine pressing a *quartz crystal* between your palms like a sandwich. Squeeze harder, and tiny charges appear on its flat faces. Connect a wire to those faces and you can read the squeeze-strength as a voltage spike. Release the squeeze and the charge vanishes (or reverses for reverse piezoelectric effect — used in actuators).

### Key Material Property
The **piezoelectric charge constant $d$** has units **C/N (Coulomb per Newton)**.

---

## 1.7 Piezoresistive Sensor

### Formal Definition
A **piezo resistive sensor** is a passive transducer whose **electrical resistance changes when subjected to mechanical strain**. It is based on the **piezoresistive effect** — a quantum mechanical phenomenon distinct from piezoelectricity.

### Conceptual Analogy
> [!NOTE]
> Take a rubber band with a wire glued along its length. Stretch the rubber band. The wire gets thinner AND longer — both effects INCREASE its electrical resistance. The fractional change in resistance $\Delta R / R$ is proportional to the strain $\varepsilon = \Delta L / L$. The **Gauge Factor (GF)** is the proportionality constant.

### Distinguishing Note
| Property | Piezoelectric | Piezoresistive |
|---|---|---|
| Output | Charge (active) | Resistance change (passive) |
| Static (DC) measurement | Poor (charge leaks) | **Excellent** |
| Sensitivity to strain | Very high | Moderate to high |
| Typical material | Quartz, PZT | Silicon, polysilicon |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Piezoelectric ≠ Piezoresistive.** Many students confuse these two in exams. Remember: *piezoelectric produces charge*, *piezoresistive produces resistance change*. The "electric" suffix means electricity is generated; the "resistive" suffix means only resistance changes.

---

## 1.8 Block Diagram — Universal Sensor Placement in Mechatronic Loop

A mechatronic system follows the closed-loop architecture:

$$\text{Input (Physical Quantity)} \rightarrow \text{Sensor} \rightarrow \text{Signal Conditioning} \rightarrow \text{Controller} \rightarrow \text{Actuator} \rightarrow \text{Output (Physical Action)}$$

The **sensor** is the *front-end* element and its accuracy, linearity, and bandwidth dictate the overall system performance.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Inductive Sensors — Detailed Analysis

### 2.1.1 Faraday's Law Foundation
The induced EMF in a coil of **N turns** linking a time-varying flux $\Phi$ is:

$$e = -N \frac{d\Phi}{dt}$$

This is the universal foundation for **all** inductive sensors (LVDT, variable reluctance, eddy current).

### 2.1.2 Self Inductance of a Coil with Ferromagnetic Core

$$L = \frac{N^2 \mu_0 \mu_r A}{l}$$

Where:
- $N$ = number of turns
- $\mu_r$ = relative permeability of the core material
- $A$ = cross-sectional area (m²)
- $l$ = magnetic path length (m)
- $\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7}$ H/m (permeability of free space)

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does $L$ change with target position?** Because the magnetic circuit's *effective* path length $l$ and permeability $\mu_r$ change when a ferromagnetic target moves near the coil. This is the physical basis of variable reluctance sensors.

### 2.1.3 LVDT (Linear Variable Differential Transformer)

The LVDT is the most commonly tested inductive sensor in KTU. It has **one primary coil** (P) and **two identical secondary coils** (S₁ and S₂) wound on a common cylindrical former. A movable soft-iron **core** slides inside.

**Operating Steps:**
1. Primary is excited by an AC reference voltage (typically **1–10 kHz**).
2. The core couples magnetic flux into S₁ and S₂ differentially.
3. Secondaries are connected in **series opposition**.
4. Output voltage $V_{\text{out}}$ is the *difference* of the two induced EMFs.

$$V_{\text{out}} = V_{S_1} - V_{S_2}$$

| Core Position | $V_{S_1}$ | $V_{S_2}$ | $V_{\text{out}}$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| Center (null) | Equal | Equal | **Zero** |
| Displaced toward S₁ | Larger | Smaller | Positive |
| Displaced toward S₂ | Smaller | Larger | Negative (180° phase shift) |

The phase of $V_{\text{out}}$ relative to the primary excitation indicates the **direction** of displacement — a unique advantage over simple potentiometric sensors.

### 2.1.4 Variable Reluctance Sensor

For a magnetic circuit with air gap $g$:

$$\mathcal{R} = \frac{l_{\text{iron}}}{\mu_0 \mu_r A_{\text{iron}}} + \frac{g}{\mu_0 A_{\text{gap}}}$$

Since $g \gg l_{\text{iron}}$ typically, $\mathcal{R} \approx g / (\mu_0 A_{\text{gap}})$, and inductance $L = N^2 / \mathcal{R}$. As $g$ changes with target motion, $L$ changes linearly with displacement (over small range).

### 2.1.5 Eddy Current Sensor

When an AC-excited coil approaches a conductive target, eddy currents are induced in the target. These eddy currents create an opposing magnetic field that **reduces the coil's effective inductance** and increases its resistance. The change is measured and correlated with the stand-off distance.

---

## 2.2 Hall Effect Sensor — Detailed Analysis

### 2.2.1 Lorentz Force on Charge Carriers
A charge carrier $q$ moving with drift velocity $v_d$ in a magnetic field $B$ perpendicular to motion experiences:

$$F_{\text{Lorentz}} = q \cdot v_d \cdot B$$

This force pushes carriers to one side of the conductor/semiconductor, creating a transverse charge accumulation and a transverse electric field $E_H$.

In equilibrium, the electric force balances the magnetic force:

$$q E_H = q v_d B$$

So:

$$E_H = v_d B$$

### 2.2.2 Hall Voltage Expression
If the current is $I = n q v_d A$ (where $A = w \cdot d$, the cross-section area, $w$ is width, $d$ is thickness):

$$v_d = \frac{I}{n q w d}$$

The Hall voltage is measured across the width $w$:

$$V_H = E_H \cdot w = \frac{I \cdot B}{n q d}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Working Formula:**
> $$V_H = \frac{R_H \cdot I \cdot B}{d} \quad \text{where } R_H = \frac{1}{n q}$$
> This is the **single most important formula for Hall Effect questions** in KTU.

### 2.2.3 Hall Coefficient Sign Convention
- **n-type semiconductor (electron majority)**: $V_H$ is **negative** (electrons accumulate).
- **p-type semiconductor (hole majority)**: $V_H$ is **positive** (holes accumulate).

### 2.2.4 Engineering Applications
1. **Brushless DC motor commutation** — detects rotor magnet position.
2. **Current sensing (clamp meters)** — measures magnetic field around a conductor.
3. **Automotive crankshaft position sensing** — detects toothed wheel positions.
4. **Keyboard switches, smartphone lid detection** — proximity sensing.
5. **Speed measurement of rotating shafts** — with a magnet on the shaft.

---

## 2.3 Hot Wire Sensor — Detailed Analysis

### 2.3.1 Operating Principle
A thin platinum or tungsten wire (typically **5 μm diameter, 1–3 mm long**) is heated by current to a temperature **above ambient** ($T_w - T_f = \Delta T$, over-temperature). When fluid flows past, convective heat loss cools the wire. The mass-flow rate of fluid $\dot{m}$ is related to heat loss $Q$ by:

$$Q = h A_s (T_w - T_f)$$

Where:
- $h$ = convective heat transfer coefficient (W/m²·K)
- $A_s$ = wire surface area (m²)
- $T_w$ = wire temperature (K)
- $T_f$ = fluid temperature (K)

### 2.3.2 King's Law (Empirical)

The heat transfer coefficient depends on fluid velocity $U$:

$$h = a + b \sqrt{U}$$

Substituting into the energy balance for an electrically heated wire (input power $P = V_w I_w$ or $E^2/R$):

$$\frac{E^2}{R_w} = \left(A + B \sqrt{U}\right) (T_w - T_f)$$

Squaring and rearranging gives **King's Law**:

$$E^2 = A + B U^n$$

Where:
- $E$ = RMS voltage across the wire
- $U$ = fluid velocity
- $A$, $B$, $n$ are constants determined by calibration ($n \approx 0.5$ for laminar flow)

### 2.3.3 Constant-Current vs Constant-Temperature Modes
| Mode | Principle | Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| **Constant Current (CC)** | Current fixed; voltage changes with cooling | Slow, steady flows |
| **Constant Temperature (CTA)** | Wire temp maintained by feedback; current varies | **Faster response**, preferred in industry |

---

## 2.4 Thermistor — Detailed Analysis

### 2.4.1 NTC Thermistor — Steinhart-Hart Equation
The most accurate empirical model over a wide temperature range:

$$\frac{1}{T} = A + B \ln(R) + C [\ln(R)]^3$$

Where $A$, $B$, $C$ are material-specific constants and $T$ is in Kelvin.

### 2.4.2 Simplified Beta (β) Parameter Equation
For a narrower range, the simpler and **exam-friendly** form is:

$$R(T) = R_0 \cdot \exp\left[\beta \left(\frac{1}{T} - \frac{1}{T_0}\right)\right]$$

Where:
- $R(T)$ = resistance at absolute temperature $T$ (K)
- $R_0$ = resistance at reference temperature $T_0$ (typically **298.15 K = 25°C**)
- $\beta$ = material constant (typically **3000–5000 K** for NTC)

### 2.4.3 Temperature Coefficient of Resistance (TCR)
Defined at temperature $T_0$:

$$\alpha = \frac{1}{R_0} \left.\frac{dR}{dT}\right|_{T_0} = -\frac{\beta}{T_0^2}$$

For NTC, $\alpha$ is **negative** and typically ranges from **-3% to -5% per °C** — far larger than the **+0.385% per °C** of platinum RTDs.

> [!NOTE]
> **Self-heating warning:** A thermistor dissipates $P = V^2 / R$ internally. This raises its temperature above the ambient, causing a measurement error. Mitigation: use the lowest practical excitation current, or use pulsed excitation.

---

## 2.5 Piezoelectric Sensor — Detailed Analysis

### 2.5.1 Direct Piezoelectric Effect
Mechanical stress $\sigma$ (N/m²) applied to a piezoelectric material produces an electric displacement $D$ (C/m²):

$$D = d \cdot \sigma$$

Where $d$ = piezoelectric charge constant (C/N).

For a crystal of area $A$ and thickness $t$, force $F = \sigma \cdot A$, the resulting charge:

$$Q = D \cdot A = d \cdot \sigma \cdot A = d \cdot F$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master Formula:**
> $$Q = d \cdot F$$
> This is THE formula for piezoelectric sensors in KTU.

### 2.5.2 Output Voltage
If the crystal has capacitance $C_p$, then:

$$V_{\text{out}} = \frac{Q}{C_p} = \frac{d \cdot F}{C_p}$$

Where $C_p = \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A / t$ for parallel-plate geometry.

### 2.5.3 Equivalent Circuit Model
A piezoelectric sensor is modeled as a **charge source $Q$ in parallel with capacitance $C_p$**, with output resistance determined by the charge amplifier interface.

### 2.5.4 Frequency Response
- **Resonant frequency** $f_r$ (sensitivity peak) and **anti-resonant** $f_a$ define the operating band.
- Useful range is typically **0.1 $f_r$ to $f_r$** for flat response.
- For **quartz**: $f_r \approx$ 30 kHz to 1 MHz depending on geometry.
- **Static (DC) measurement fails** because the charge leaks through the amplifier input resistance and cable capacitance. Hence piezoelectric sensors are for **dynamic quantities only**.

### 2.5.5 Common Piezoelectric Materials
| Material | $d$ (pC/N) | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Quartz | **2.3** | Stable, used in precision |
| PZT (Lead Zirconate Titanate) | **200–600** | Most common, high sensitivity |
| PVDF (Polymer film) | **20–30** | Flexible, used in flexible sensors |

---

## 2.6 Piezoresistive Sensor (Strain Gauge) — Detailed Analysis

### 2.6.1 The Gauge Factor (GF)
The defining performance parameter:

$$\text{GF} = \frac{\Delta R / R}{\Delta L / L} = \frac{\Delta R / R}{\varepsilon}$$

Where:
- $\Delta R / R$ = fractional change in resistance
- $\varepsilon = \Delta L / L$ = longitudinal strain

### 2.6.2 Geometric (Dimensional) Effect Derivation
For a wire of length $L$, cross-section area $A = \pi r^2$, and resistivity $\rho$:

$$R = \frac{\rho L}{A}$$

Under strain, $L \to L + \Delta L$, $r \to r - \nu \Delta L$ (where $\nu$ is Poisson's ratio). Assuming constant $\rho$:

$$\frac{\Delta R}{R} = \frac{\Delta L}{L} - 2\frac{\Delta r}{r} = \varepsilon + 2\nu\varepsilon = (1 + 2\nu)\varepsilon$$

For a metallic wire, $\nu \approx 0.3$, so GF $\approx 1.6$ due to geometry alone. But measured GF for metal alloys is typically **2.0–2.5** — the excess is due to the **piezoresistive effect** (resistivity changes with strain).

### 2.6.3 Semiconductor Strain Gauge
For doped silicon, the **piezoresistive effect dominates** — $\rho$ changes by orders of magnitude. GFs of **100–200** are typical, with **GF up to ±2000** in some silicon orientations.

### 2.6.4 Wheatstone Bridge Configuration
Strain gauges are almost always used in a **Wheatstone bridge** to convert $\Delta R$ into a voltage:

$$V_{\text{out}} = V_{\text{ex}} \left[\frac{R_1}{R_1 + R_2} - \frac{R_4}{R_3 + R_4}\right]$$

| Bridge Type | Active Gauges | Sensitivity |
|---|---|---|
| Quarter bridge | 1 | $V_{\text{out}} \approx \frac{V_{\text{ex}}}{4} \cdot \text{GF} \cdot \varepsilon$ |
| Half bridge | 2 | $2 \times$ quarter |
| Full bridge | 4 | $4 \times$ quarter |

---

## 2.7 📋 KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA CHEAT SHEET

| Sensor | Master Formula | Key Variables | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Inductive (general)** | $e = -N \, d\Phi/dt$ | $N$ = turns, $\Phi$ = flux | V, turns, Wb |
| **Self Inductance** | $L = N^2 \mu_0 \mu_r A / l$ | $\mu_r$ = relative permeability | Henry (H) |
| **Hall Voltage** | $V_H = R_H \, I B / d$ | $R_H = 1/(nq)$, $B$ = field | V, m³/C, T |
| **Hot Wire (King's Law)** | $E^2 = A + B U^n$ | $n \approx 0.5$ | V², m/s |
| **Thermistor (β-model)** | $R(T) = R_0 \exp[\beta(1/T - 1/T_0)]$ | $\beta \approx 3000$–$5000$ K | Ω, K |
| **Thermistor TCR** | $\alpha = -\beta / T_0^2$ | Negative for NTC | 1/K or %/°C |
| **Piezoelectric Charge** | $Q = d \cdot F$ | $d$ = charge constant | C, N |
| **Piezoelectric Voltage** | $V = d \cdot F / C_p$ | $C_p$ = crystal capacitance | V |
| **Piezoresistive (Strain Gauge)** | $\Delta R / R = \text{GF} \cdot \varepsilon$ | $\text{GF} \approx 2$ (metal), $100$+ (Si) | dimensionless |
| **Wheatstone Output** | $V_o = V_{\text{ex}} \cdot \text{GF} \cdot \varepsilon / 4$ | Quarter-bridge | V |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examination Tip:** Always carry these formulas with their **boundary conditions** and **units**. KTU examiners frequently deduct marks for missing units in final answers.

### 2.8 Comparative Selection Guide for Engineers

| Measurand | Best Sensor Choice | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Static displacement (μm) | LVDT | Excellent linearity, no contact |
| Proximity / speed (rotating) | Hall Effect | Solid-state, fast |
| Fluid flow velocity | Hot Wire | High sensitivity to low flows |
| Temperature (consumer) | NTC Thermistor | Cheap, high sensitivity |
| Vibration / dynamic force | Piezoelectric | Wide bandwidth, no power needed |
| Static strain (load cell) | Piezoresistive | DC-capable, stable |
| Very high temperature (>500°C) | Thermocouple (not in this module) | — |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section provides rigorous, line-by-line derivations of the four most-frequently tested results in KTU Module 1.

---

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Hall Voltage $V_H$

### 3.1.1 Starting Physical Setup
A rectangular semiconductor slab of thickness $d$ (along z-axis), width $w$ (along y-axis), and length $L$ (along x-axis) carries a current $I$ in the +x direction. A uniform magnetic field $B$ is applied in the +z direction. We want the voltage developed across the y-direction.

### 3.1.2 Drift Velocity of Charge Carriers
Current density $J$ in the x-direction is:

$$J_x = n \cdot q \cdot v_d$$

where $n$ is the carrier concentration (carriers/m³) and $v_d$ is the drift velocity. The total current through cross-section $A = w \cdot d$ is:

$$I = J_x \cdot A = n q v_d \cdot w d$$

Solving for drift velocity:

$$v_d = \frac{I}{n q w d}$$

### 3.1.3 Lorentz Force on Each Carrier
Each carrier (charge $q$) moving in +x with velocity $v_d$, subjected to magnetic field $B$ in +z, experiences:

$$\vec{F} = q \vec{v} \times \vec{B} = q(v_d \hat{x}) \times (B \hat{z}) = -q v_d B \hat{y}$$

So carriers are pushed in the $-\hat{y}$ direction (negative charges) or $+\hat{y}$ (positive charges).

### 3.1.4 Charge Accumulation & Equilibrium
The accumulated charges on the y-faces create a transverse electric field $E_y$ pointing in $+\hat{y}$ (for negative carriers pushed to $-\hat{y}$, so field from + to - is +y direction). The electric force on carriers is $q E_y$ in $+\hat{y}$.

**Equilibrium condition**: electric force balances magnetic force:

$$q E_y = q v_d B$$

$$E_y = v_d B$$

### 3.1.5 Hall Voltage Across the Width
The Hall voltage is the line integral of the electric field across the width $w$:

$$V_H = E_y \cdot w = v_d B \cdot w$$

Substituting $v_d$:

$$V_H = \frac{I}{n q w d} \cdot B \cdot w$$

The width $w$ cancels:

$$V_H = \frac{I \cdot B}{n q d}$$

### 3.1.6 Defining the Hall Coefficient
Define $R_H = 1/(nq)$:

$$\boxed{V_H = \frac{R_H \cdot I \cdot B}{d}}$$

### 3.1.7 Sign of $R_H$
- n-type (electrons, $q = -e$): $R_H = -1/(ne)$ → negative $V_H$
- p-type (holes, $q = +e$): $R_H = +1/(ne)$ → positive $V_H$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Interpretation of $d$:** Hall voltage is **inversely proportional** to thickness. Thinner samples give larger $V_H$ → better sensitivity. This is why Hall sensors are fabricated as thin films.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — LVDT Output Voltage

### 3.2.1 Structure Recap
- Primary coil P (center): $N_p$ turns, excited with AC voltage $V_p$ at frequency $\omega$.
- Two identical secondaries S₁, S₂ wound on either side of P: each with $N_s$ turns.
- A movable ferromagnetic core couples flux into S₁ and S₂.

### 3.2.2 Mutual Inductance Model
When the core is displaced by $x$ from the center, let the coupling into S₁ be $M_1(x)$ and into S₂ be $M_2(x)$.

For small displacements (linear range):

$$M_1(x) = M_0 + k x$$

$$M_2(x) = M_0 - k x$$

where $k$ is a constant depending on geometry and $M_0$ is the central (null) mutual inductance.

### 3.2.3 Induced EMFs in Secondaries
$$e_1 = -M_1(x) \frac{di_p}{dt}$$

$$e_2 = -M_2(x) \frac{di_p}{dt}$$

For a sinusoidal primary current $i_p = I_p \sin(\omega t)$:

$$\frac{di_p}{dt} = \omega I_p \cos(\omega t)$$

So:

$$e_1 = -\omega M_1(x) I_p \cos(\omega t)$$

$$e_2 = -\omega M_2(x) I_p \cos(\omega t)$$

### 3.2.4 Series-Opposition Connection
The secondaries are connected in **series opposition**, so the output voltage is the *difference*:

$$V_{\text{out}} = e_1 - e_2 = -\omega I_p \cos(\omega t) [M_1(x) - M_2(x)]$$

Substituting the expressions for $M_1$ and $M_2$:

$$V_{\text{out}} = -\omega I_p \cos(\omega t) [(M_0 + kx) - (M_0 - kx)]$$

$$V_{\text{out}} = -\omega I_p \cos(\omega t) \cdot 2 k x$$

$$\boxed{V_{\text{out}} = -2 k \omega I_p \, x \cos(\omega t)}$$

### 3.2.5 Key Conclusions
1. $V_{\text{out}}$ is **directly proportional to displacement $x$** (linearity in operating range).
2. At $x = 0$, $V_{\text{out}} = 0$ → **null position**, useful for calibration.
3. For $x > 0$, $V_{\text{out}}$ has phase $-\pi/2$ (i.e., cosine with negative sign).
4. For $x < 0$, the sign flips — **phase reversal** indicates direction.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Piezoresistive Gauge Factor for a Cylindrical Wire

### 3.3.1 Initial Resistance
For a cylindrical wire of length $L$, radius $r$, and resistivity $\rho$:

$$R = \frac{\rho L}{\pi r^2}$$

### 3.3.2 Apply Small Strain
Under tensile stress, length increases by $\Delta L$ and radius decreases by $\Delta r$:

$$L \to L + \Delta L$$

$$r \to r - \Delta r$$

Assuming constant volume (Poisson effect):

$$\frac{\Delta r}{r} = -\nu \frac{\Delta L}{L} = -\nu \varepsilon$$

### 3.3.3 Compute New Resistance
The new resistance (assuming $\rho$ constant for purely geometric effect):

$$R' = \frac{\rho (L + \Delta L)}{\pi (r - \Delta r)^2}$$

Expanding $(r - \Delta r)^2 \approx r^2 (1 - 2\Delta r/r)$ to first order:

$$R' = \frac{\rho L (1 + \Delta L/L)}{\pi r^2 (1 - 2\Delta r / r)} \approx R \cdot \frac{1 + \varepsilon}{1 + 2\nu\varepsilon}$$

Using $1/(1+x) \approx 1 - x$ for small $x$:

$$R' \approx R (1 + \varepsilon)(1 - 2\nu\varepsilon) \approx R [1 + (1 - 2\nu)\varepsilon]$$

### 3.3.4 Fractional Change in Resistance
$$\frac{\Delta R}{R} = R' - R = (1 - 2\nu)\varepsilon$$

$$\boxed{\text{GF}_{\text{geometric}} = \frac{\Delta R / R}{\varepsilon} = 1 + 2\nu}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **For metals**: $\nu \approx 0.3$ → $\text{GF} \approx 1.6$ (geometric only). Actual measured GF of constantan is ~2.0, the extra ~0.4 comes from the piezoresistive effect on $\rho$.

> For semiconductors, $\rho$ changes dominate, giving $\text{GF} = 100$–$200$ (silicon).

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Thermistor Temperature Coefficient

### 3.4.1 Starting from β-Equation
$$R(T) = R_0 \exp\left[\beta\left(\frac{1}{T} - \frac{1}{T_0}\right)\right]$$

Differentiating both sides with respect to $T$:

$$\frac{dR}{dT} = R_0 \exp\left[\beta\left(\frac{1}{T} - \frac{1}{T_0}\right)\right] \cdot \left(-\frac{\beta}{T^2}\right) = R(T) \cdot \left(-\frac{\beta}{T^2}\right)$$

### 3.4.2 Temperature Coefficient of Resistance (TCR)
$$\alpha = \frac{1}{R(T)} \frac{dR}{dT} = -\frac{\beta}{T^2}$$

Evaluated at the reference temperature $T_0$:

$$\boxed{\alpha_0 = -\frac{\beta}{T_0^2}}$$

### 3.4.3 Numerical Example (KTU-type)
For an NTC with $\beta = 4000$ K and $T_0 = 298.15$ K (25°C):

$$\alpha_0 = -\frac{4000}{(298.15)^2} = -\frac{4000}{88893} = -0.045 \text{ K}^{-1} = -4.5\%/\text{K}$$

This confirms the earlier statement: NTC thermistors are roughly **10× more sensitive** than platinum RTDs ($\alpha \approx +0.385\%/\text{K}$).

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Piezoelectric Output Voltage

### 3.5.1 Charge from Direct Piezoelectric Effect
Stress $\sigma$ produces electric displacement $D = d \sigma$, where $d$ is the charge constant (C/N). On the electrode area $A$:

$$Q = D \cdot A = d \sigma A = d F$$

### 3.5.2 Voltage Across Crystal
The piezoelectric crystal itself behaves as a parallel-plate capacitor with:

$$C_p = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{t}$$

where $t$ is crystal thickness. The output voltage:

$$V = \frac{Q}{C_p} = \frac{d F \cdot t}{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}$$

Using $F = \sigma A$:

$$\boxed{V = \frac{d \sigma t}{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r} = \frac{d \cdot F}{C_p}}$$

### 3.5.3 Voltage Coefficient (g-constant)
Define $g = d / \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r$ (V·m/N):

$$V = g \cdot \sigma \cdot t = g \cdot F \cdot t / A$$

| Material | $d$ (pC/N) | $g$ (mV·m/N) |
|---|---|---|
| Quartz | 2.3 | 50 |
| PZT | 400 | 25 |
| PVDF | 30 | 200 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Trade-off:** Higher $d$ = more charge (good for charge amplifiers). Higher $g$ = more voltage (good for voltage amplifiers). Quartz has moderate $d$ but high $g$ → preferred for voltage-mode accelerometers.

---

## 3.6 📊 Analytical Matrix — Sensitivity & Range Comparison

| Sensor | Input Domain | Output | Sensitivity | Typical Range |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **LVDT** | Displacement | AC Voltage | ~1–100 mV/V/mm | ±0.1 mm to ±500 mm |
| **Variable Reluctance** | Displacement | $\Delta L$ | Depends on coil | 0–5 mm |
| **Eddy Current** | Distance | $\Delta L$ or $\Delta Z$ | 1–10 mV/μm | 0.5–25 mm |
| **Hall Effect** | Magnetic field / current | DC Voltage | 5–50 mV/(mA·T) | ±10 mT to ±2 T |
| **Hot Wire** | Flow velocity | $\Delta V$ or $\Delta I$ | High at low flow | 0.1–100 m/s |
| **NTC Thermistor** | Temperature | Resistance | $\sim$ -4%/K | -55 to +150°C |
| **Piezoelectric** | Force / Pressure | Charge / Voltage | 2–500 pC/N | 1 N to 100 kN |
| **Piezoresistive** | Strain | $\Delta R$ | GF = 2–200 | 1 με to 5000 με |

(1 με = 1 microstrain = $10^{-6}$)

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Explain the working principle of a Hall Effect sensor. Mention the significance of the Hall coefficient.**

**Model Answer:**

The Hall Effect is the generation of a transverse EMF in a current-carrying conductor/semiconductor placed in a perpendicular magnetic field.

**Working Principle (1 mark):** When a current $I$ flows through a semiconductor slab in the x-direction and a magnetic field $B$ is applied in the z-direction, charge carriers experience a Lorentz force $F = qv_d \times B$ in the y-direction. This pushes carriers to one face of the slab, creating a charge accumulation and a transverse electric field. Equilibrium is reached when the electric force balances the magnetic force, and the resulting voltage across the y-face is the **Hall Voltage**:

$$V_H = \frac{R_H \cdot I \cdot B}{d}$$

**Hall Coefficient (1 mark):** $R_H = 1/(nq)$ has units of m³/C. Its **sign indicates the type of majority carrier** (negative for n-type, positive for p-type) and its **magnitude is inversely proportional to carrier concentration**.

**Application (1 mark):** Used in position sensing, brushless DC motor commutation, contactless current measurement, and speed measurement.

---

### Question A2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Distinguish between piezoelectric and piezoresistive effects. Which is suitable for static measurements and why?**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Piezoelectric | Piezoresistive |
|---|---|---|
| Effect | Generates **charge** under stress | **Resistance changes** under stress |
| Type | Active transducer (no external power) | Passive transducer (needs excitation) |
| Output | Charge $Q = dF$ | $\Delta R/R = GF \cdot \varepsilon$ |
| Static (DC) response | **Poor** (charge leaks) | **Excellent** |
| Materials | Quartz, PZT, PVDF | Silicon, polysilicon, metal alloys |

**Static Measurement (1 mark):** **Piezoresistive sensors are suitable for static measurements** because they respond directly to strain as a resistance change, which is a DC-stable quantity. In contrast, piezoelectric sensors generate charge only *while* the force is changing — once the force becomes static, the charge leaks away through the input resistance of the amplifier and the cable capacitance, causing the output to drift to zero. Hence piezoelectric sensors are restricted to **dynamic** measurements (vibration, dynamic force, acceleration).

---

## 5.2 PART B — Detailed Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question B1 (A) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 / May 2024]`
**With a neat block diagram, explain the construction and working of an LVDT. Derive the expression for its output voltage. List its advantages and disadvantages. (14 Marks)**

#### Part (a) — Construction and Working (7 Marks)

**Construction (3.5 marks):**
- An LVDT consists of **one primary coil (P)** and **two identical secondary coils (S₁ and S₂)** wound on a common hollow cylindrical former.
- The secondaries are placed on either side of the primary, symmetrically.
- A movable **soft-iron core** (slug) slides along the axis inside the former.
- The two secondaries are connected in **series opposition**.
- Primary is excited by an AC voltage (1–10 kHz, typically 5 V RMS).

**Working (3.5 marks):**
- The AC excitation produces an alternating flux in the primary.
- This flux is coupled into S₁ and S₂ through the movable core.
- **Null Position:** When the core is exactly centered, equal flux links S₁ and S₂. The induced EMFs are equal in magnitude but opposite in phase (due to series-opposition connection). Hence **$V_{\text{out}} = 0$**.
- **Displacement:** When the core moves toward S₁, more flux links S₁ than S₂, so $e_1 > e_2$. The output is **positive in phase** with primary.
- **Reverse Displacement:** When the core moves toward S₂, the output is **180° out of phase**.
- The **magnitude** of $V_{\text{out}}$ is proportional to displacement, and the **phase** indicates direction.

#### Part (b) — Derivation and Advantages/Disadvantages (7 Marks)

**Derivation (4 marks):** (See Section 3.2)

$$V_{\text{out}} = -2 k \omega I_p \, x \cos(\omega t)$$

**Advantages (2 marks):**
1. Infinite resolution (limited only by readout electronics).
2. Excellent linearity (±0.5% or better in linear range).
3. Frictionless measurement (no contact between core and coils).
4. Robust, reliable, long life.
5. Direction-sensing capability via phase.

**Disadvantages (1 mark):**
1. Requires AC excitation and signal conditioning (demodulator).
2. Limited dynamic response due to the core's inertia.
3. Affected by external magnetic fields and temperature.

---

### Question B1 (B) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022 / Internal Choice]`
**Describe the construction and working of a Hall Effect sensor. Derive the expression for Hall voltage. Explain how it is used to measure the displacement of a rotating shaft. (14 Marks)**

#### Part (a) — Construction, Working, and Hall Voltage Derivation (7 Marks)

**Construction (1 mark):** A Hall Effect sensor consists of a thin slab of semiconductor material (commonly **GaAs, InSb, or silicon**) with four contacts — two for current input/output and two for voltage measurement across the perpendicular face.

**Working (2 marks):** When a current $I$ is passed through the slab in one direction and a magnetic field $B$ is applied perpendicular to the current, the moving charge carriers are deflected by the Lorentz force to one side of the slab. This creates a charge accumulation and a measurable transverse voltage — the Hall voltage.

**Derivation (4 marks):** (See Section 3.1)

$$V_H = \frac{R_H \cdot I \cdot B}{d} = \frac{I \cdot B}{n q d}$$

The sign of $V_H$ indicates the type of majority carrier; the magnitude indicates field strength.

#### Part (b) — Displacement Measurement Application (7 Marks)

**Working Principle (4 marks):**
- A small **permanent magnet** is mounted on the rotating shaft.
- A Hall sensor is fixed at a known reference position near the shaft.
- As the shaft rotates, the magnet passes over the Hall sensor, producing a **pulse train** at the output.
- The **frequency** of the pulse train is proportional to shaft speed: $f = n/60$ where $n$ is shaft speed in RPM.
- For **linear displacement** measurement, a magnet is mounted on a linearly moving target, and the Hall sensor detects its position via the spatial variation of $B$.

**Signal Conditioning (2 marks):**
- The Hall output (typically a few mV to hundreds of mV) is amplified by a differential amplifier.
- Filtering removes high-frequency noise.
- An ADC converts the analog signal for digital display.

**Advantages of this Method (1 mark):**
- Non-contact, no friction, no wear.
- Solid-state → long life, immune to dust and vibration.
- Wide operating temperature range.

---

### Question B2 (A) `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`
**Explain the working principle of a piezoelectric sensor. Derive the relationship between the output voltage and applied force. Discuss its frequency response and why it cannot be used for static measurements. (14 Marks)**

#### Part (a) — Working Principle and Derivation (7 Marks)

**Working Principle (3 marks):**
- Certain crystalline materials (quartz, PZT, PVDF) exhibit the **direct piezoelectric effect**: when subjected to mechanical stress, they generate an electric charge on their surfaces.
- The piezoelectric constant $d$ (C/N) links charge to applied force.

**Derivation (4 marks):** (See Section 3.5)

$$Q = d \cdot F$$

$$V = \frac{Q}{C_p} = \frac{d \cdot F}{C_p}$$

For a parallel-plate crystal of thickness $t$ and area $A$:

$$C_p = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{t} \implies V = \frac{d F t}{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}$$

#### Part (b) — Frequency Response and Static Limitation (7 Marks)

**Frequency Response (4 marks):**
- A piezoelectric crystal has a **resonant frequency** $f_r$ determined by its mechanical dimensions and properties.
- At $f_r$, the mechanical vibration of the crystal amplifies the output — useful for high-sensitivity narrowband sensing.
- Below $f_r$, the response is **flat** (constant sensitivity per unit force).
- Above $f_r$, response becomes erratic with multiple resonant peaks.
- Practical operating range: **0.1 $f_r$ to $f_r$**.
- For a typical accelerometer design, $f_r$ is chosen at least **5× the highest frequency of interest** to ensure flat response.

**Why Not Static? (3 marks):**
- The crystal behaves as a capacitor $C_p$ in parallel with the charge source $Q$.
- In static conditions, the charge leaks away through:
  1. The finite input resistance of the amplifier.
  2. The dielectric resistance of the crystal itself.
  3. The cable capacitance.
- The output voltage decays exponentially with time constant $\tau = R_{\text{in}} (C_p + C_c)$.
- Hence, only **dynamic** (changing) forces produce a sustained output. Static forces cause the output to drift to zero over time.

**Mitigation:** Use a **charge amplifier** with very high input impedance (FET input op-amp), or use a **piezoresistive sensor** instead for static measurements.

---

### Question B2 (B) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`
**Explain the principle of a strain gauge. Derive the expression for gauge factor. Describe the working of a Wheatstone bridge for strain measurement and derive its output voltage. (14 Marks)**

#### Part (a) — Strain Gauge Principle and Gauge Factor Derivation (7 Marks)

**Principle (2 marks):**
- A strain gauge is a piezoresistive sensor that converts mechanical strain into a resistance change.
- It consists of a thin metallic foil or semiconductor pattern bonded to a flexible backing.
- When the backing deforms, the gauge experiences strain $\varepsilon = \Delta L / L$.

**Derivation (5 marks):** (See Section 3.3)

For a cylindrical wire:

$$R = \frac{\rho L}{A} = \frac{\rho L}{\pi r^2}$$

Under strain, $L \to L(1+\varepsilon)$ and $r \to r(1 - \nu\varepsilon)$ where $\nu$ is Poisson's ratio. Assuming constant $\rho$ (purely geometric effect):

$$\frac{\Delta R}{R} = (1 + 2\nu)\varepsilon$$

$$\text{Gauge Factor } \text{GF} = \frac{\Delta R / R}{\varepsilon} = 1 + 2\nu$$

For metals, $\nu \approx 0.3$, so $\text{GF} \approx 1.6$. The actual measured GF is higher (~2 for constantan) due to the piezoresistive contribution to $\Delta\rho$.

#### Part (b) — Wheatstone Bridge Working and Output (7 Marks)

**Bridge Construction (3 marks):**
- The strain gauge $R_1$ forms one arm of a Wheatstone bridge.
- The other three arms are fixed resistors $R_2, R_3, R_4$.
- An excitation voltage $V_{\text{ex}}$ is applied across two opposite nodes.
- The output is taken across the other two opposite nodes.

**Output Derivation (3 marks):**

Let $R_1 = R_0 + \Delta R$, $R_2 = R_3 = R_4 = R_0$.

Voltage at node 1 (between $R_1$ and $R_2$):
$$V_1 = V_{\text{ex}} \cdot \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2} = V_{\text{ex}} \cdot \frac{R_0}{2R_0 + \Delta R}$$

Voltage at node 2 (between $R_4$ and $R_3$):
$$V_2 = V_{\text{ex}} \cdot \frac{R_3}{R_3 + R_4} = \frac{V_{\text{ex}}}{2}$$

Output:
$$V_{\text{out}} = V_1 - V_2 = V_{\text{ex}} \left[\frac{R_0}{2R_0 + \Delta R} - \frac{1}{2}\right]$$

$$V_{\text{out}} = V_{\text{ex}} \cdot \frac{2R_0 - (2R_0 + \Delta R)}{2(2R_0 + \Delta R)} = -\frac{V_{\text{ex}} \cdot \Delta R}{2(2R_0 + \Delta R)}$$

For small $\Delta R$: $V_{\text{out}} \approx -\frac{V_{\text{ex}} \cdot \Delta R}{4R_0} = -\frac{V_{\text{ex}}}{4} \cdot \text{GF} \cdot \varepsilon$.

**Advantage of Bridge (1 mark):**
- Converts small $\Delta R$ to a measurable voltage.
- Compensates for temperature if dummy gauge is used in an adjacent arm.
- Quarter, half, and full-bridge configurations offer increasing sensitivity.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Common Marks Lost:**
