```
   │                  │  ╲        ╱  │
   │                  │   Spring    │
   │                  └─────┬──────┘
   │                        │
   │              ┌─────────┴──────────┐
   │              ▼                    ▼
   │     ┌──────────────┐     ┌──────────────┐
   └─────│  Flow Ctrl 1 │     │  Flow Ctrl 2 │
         │   ⌒▾⌒        │     │   ⌒▾⌒        │
         └──────┬───────┘     └──────┬───────┘
                │                    │
                ▼                    ▼
         ┌─────────────────────────────────┐
         │   DOUBLE ACTING CYLINDER       │
         │   A side ──→ [Piston] ←── B side│
         └─────────────────────────────────┘
```

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

*Draw the ISO 1219 standard symbols for the following components:*
*(a) Filter-Regulator-Lubricator (FRL) unit*
*(b) 4/3 DCV with spring-centered, pressure-isolated middle position, and solenoid actuation on both ends*
*(c) One-way flow control valve*

**Model Answer (3 marks — 1 mark each):**

**(a) FRL Unit:**

```
┌────┬────┬────┐
│ F  │ R  │ L  │   (Three connected square blocks)
└────┴────┴────┘
   ▲ Internal filter, regulator, lubricator
```

**(b) 4/3 DCV (solenoid-spring-centered, pressure isolated in mid-position):**

```
    ▭─────┐    P    T     ▭─────┐
          ├────[━━]──[━T━]──┤
    ▭─────┘    A    B     ╱╲
              ┌──[━P━]──┐
              └─────────┘
```

Each square block represents one spool position. Mid-position has **P blocked (T)**, **A and B connected to T (▲)**, **or P held** depending on the center condition.

**(c) One-way flow control valve:** Compound symbol of a variable throttle (⌒↓⌒) in **parallel** with a check valve (▼|).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - Jun 2023]**

*List any six ISO 1219 standard symbols used in hydraulic and pneumatic circuits, with their functional meaning.*

**Model Answer (3 marks — 0.5 mark each):**

| # | Symbol Description | Function |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Solid line (──) | Working line (main pressure line) |
| 2 | Dashed line (- - -) | Pilot/control line |
| 3 | Circle with curved arrow | Pump or motor |
| 4 | Square with arrows | Directional control valve |
| 5 | T inside a square | Port blocked in that position |
| 6 | Triangle (▲) at line end | Exhaust to atmosphere |
| 7 | Dot at line crossing | Lines connected (joined) |
| 8 | Two crescents (⌒⌒) | Throttle/flow restriction |

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

**Q3. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Develop a complete hydraulic circuit using a **4/3 DCV** to control a **double-acting cylinder** such that:
- Position 1 (left solenoid energized): Cylinder extends
- Center position (no coil energized): Cylinder is locked, ports blocked
- Position 2 (right solenoid energized): Cylinder retracts

Include a **pressure relief valve**, a **filter**, a **reservoir**, and a **one-way flow control valve** in the rod-end line for speed control during retraction. Label all components.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A double-acting hydraulic cylinder has a piston diameter of **100 mm** and rod diameter of **60 mm**. Pump pressure is **150 bar** and the pump delivers **60 L/min**. Calculate:
(i) The extending and retracting forces.
(ii) The extending and retracting velocities.
(iii) The time to complete a stroke of **500 mm** in both directions.

**Model Answer (a) — 7 marks:**

**Circuit Description:**

```
    ┌──────────[Reservoir]──────────┐
    │                              │
    │   ┌─[Pressure Relief Valve]──┘
    │   │   (set at 150 bar)
    │   ▼
  [Pump]
    │
    ▼
  [Filter]
    │
    ▼
  ┌────────────────────────────────┐
  │  4/3 DCV (solenoid-solenoid,  │
  │  spring-centered, BLOCKED MID) │
  │                                │
  │  Sol A:  [━▶━][━T━][━▶━] Sol B│
  │           P,A→B  P,A,B blocked P,B→A
  │           extended  locked  retracted
  └──────────┬─────────┬──────────┘
             │         │
             ▼         ▼
   ┌─[One-way FCV]─┐  │
   │  (Speed ctrl) │  │
   └──────┬────────┘  │
          ▼           ▼
   ┌──────────────────────────┐
   │ DOUBLE-ACTING CYLINDER   │
   │ [Piston D=100, d=60]     │
   │ Cap A ←──→ Rod B         │
   └──────────────────────────┘
              │
              ▼
         [to Reservoir]
```

**Component labeling (1 mark each, total 6):**
1. Reservoir (1)
2. Pump (1)
3. Pressure relief valve (1)
4. Filter (1)
5. 4/3 DCV (2)
6. Double-acting cylinder (1)
7. One-way flow control valve (1)

> **Mark Distribution Logic:** 6 marks for component identification + correct connections, 1 mark for proper ISO symbols and working line conventions.

---

**Model Answer (b) — 7 marks:**

**Given:**
- $D = 100 \text{ mm} = 0.1 \text{ m}$, $d = 60 \text{ mm} = 0.06 \text{ m}$
- $P = 150 \text{ bar} = 1.5 \times 10^7 \text{ Pa}$
- $Q = 60 \text{ L/min} = 1.0 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$
- $L = 500 \text{ mm} = 0.5 \text{ m}$

**Step 1: Calculate areas (1 mark)**

$$
A_1 = \frac{\pi}{4} (0.1)^2 = 7.854 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2
$$

$$
A_2 = \frac{\pi}{4} [(0.1)^2 - (0.06)^2] = \frac{\pi}{4} \times 0.0064 = 5.027 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2
$$

**Step 2: Forces (2 marks)**

$$
F_1 = P \times A_1 = 1.5 \times 10^7 \times 7.854 \times 10^{-3} = 117.81 \text{ kN}
$$

$$
F_2 = P \times A_2 = 1.5 \times 10^7 \times 5.027 \times 10^{-3} = 75.40 \text{ kN}
$$

**Step 3: Velocities (2 marks)**

$$
v_1 = \frac{Q}{A_1} = \frac{1.0 \times 10^{-3}}{7.854 \times 10^{-3}} = 0.1273 \text{ m/s}
$$

$$
v_2 = \frac{Q}{A_2} = \frac{1.0 \times 10^{-3}}{5.027 \times 10^{-3}} = 0.1989 \text{ m/s}
$$

**Step 4: Stroke times (2 marks)**

$$
t_1 = \frac{L}{v_1} = \frac{0.5}{0.1273} = 3.927 \text{ s}
$$

$$
t_2 = \frac{L}{v_2} = \frac{0.5}{0.1989} = 2.514 \text{ s}
$$

**Final Answer:**
- $F_1 = 117.81 \text{ kN}$ (extension force)
- $F_2 = 75.40 \text{ kN}$ (retraction force)
- $v_1 = 0.1273 \text{ m/s}$ (extension velocity)
- $v_2 = 0.1989 \text{ m/s}$ (retraction velocity)
- $t_1 = 3.93 \text{ s}$, $t_2 = 2.51 \text{ s}$

---

**Q4. [KTU University Exam - Jun 2024] (Alternative Choice for Q3)**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Develop the complete pneumatic circuit to control a **double-acting cylinder** using a **5/2 DCV** with the following sequence:
- Push-button 1 pressed: Cylinder extends (rod out)
- Push-button 2 pressed: Cylinder retracts (rod in)
- Both push-buttons released: Cylinder holds the last position
- Include: FRL unit, silencers on both exhaust ports, and a pressure gauge.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A pneumatic cylinder (D = 50 mm, d = 25 mm, stroke = 200 mm) operates at a gauge pressure of 6 bar. If the cylinder completes 20 cycles per minute, calculate the **free air consumption in L/min (FAD)**.

**Model Answer (a) — 7 marks:**

```
   [Atmosphere] → [Compressor] → [Receiver] → [FRL] → [5/2 DCV]
                                                       │
                  ┌────[PB1]──── Sol A                 │
                  │                                    ▼
                  │                          [Double-Acting Cyl]
                  │                                    │
                  └────[PB2]──── Sol B                 │
                                  ╲                ╱  │
                                   Spring return     │
                                                       ▼
                                                [Exhaust + Silencer]
                                                       │
                                                       ▼
                                                  [Atmosphere]
```

Components (6 × 1 mark + 1 mark for ISO symbols) = 7 marks:
- Compressor with reservoir (1)
- FRL unit (1)
- 5/2 DCV (2)
- Two push-buttons (1)
- Double-acting cylinder (1)
- Silencers (1)

**Model Answer (b) — 7 marks:**

**Given:**
- $D = 50 \text{ mm} = 0.05 \text{ m}$, $d = 25 \text{ mm} = 0.025 \text{ m}$, $L = 200 \text{ mm} = 0.2 \text{ m}$
- $P_{gauge} = 6 \text{ bar}$, $P_{atm} = 1.013 \text{ bar}$
- $P_{abs} = 6 + 1.013 = 7.013 \text{ bar}$
- Cycles per minute $n = 20$

**Step 1: Cap-end geometric volume per stroke (1 mark)**

$$
V_1 = \frac{\pi}{4} (0.05)^2 \times 0.2 = 3.927 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3
$$

**Step 2: Rod-end geometric volume per stroke (1 mark)**

$$
V_2 = \frac{\pi}{4} [(0.05)^2 - (0.025)^2] \times 0.2 = 2.945 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3
$$

**Step 3: Free air equivalent per cycle (2 marks)**

$$
V_{cycle} = (V_1 + V_2) \times \frac{P_{abs}}{P_{atm}} = (3.927 + 2.945) \times 10^{-4} \times \frac{7.013}{1.013}
$$

$$
V_{cycle} = 6.872 \times 10^{-4} \times 6.923 = 4.758 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3/\text{cycle}
$$

**Step 4: Free air per minute (2 marks)**

$$
\dot{V} = n \times V_{cycle} = 20 \times 4.758 \times 10^{-3} = 0.0952 \text{ m}^3/\text{min} = 95.2 \text{ L/min}
$$

**Step 5: Sizing compressor (1 mark)**

> Select a compressor with **FAD ≥ 100 L/min** to account for leakage and efficiency (margin of 10%).

**Final Answer:** Free air consumption ≈ **95.2 L/min**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Using non-ISO symbols** (e.g., drawing a circle for cylinder) = **2-mark deduction per occurrence**
> 2. **Confusing pneumatic (▲ exhaust) with hydraulic (return line to tank)** = **1–2 mark loss**
> 3. **Forgetting the pressure relief valve** in hydraulic circuits = **1 mark deduction**
> 4. **Mixing up 4/3 and 5/3 DCV center conditions** (P-blocked vs. T-blocked vs. P-holding) = **2-mark loss**
> 5. **Forgetting to convert bar to Pa** (or vice versa) in numericals = **1 mark deduction**
> 6. **Not using dashed lines for pilot signals** in pneumatic circuits = **1 mark loss**
> 7. **Sketching without labeling components** = **Up to 50% marks lost**

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Exam:**

- [ ] **Hydraulic** = Liquid (oil); **Pneumatic** = Gas (air)
- [ ] Standard = **ISO 1219-1:2012** (symbols) & **ISO 1219-2:2012** (circuits)
- [ ] **Square boxes** = valve positions; **Circles** = rotating machines
- [ ] **Solid line** = main; **Dashed line** = pilot/control
- [ ] **P** = pressure port; **T** = tank port; **A, B** = actuator ports
- [ ] **T inside square** = blocked; **▲ (triangle)** = exhaust
- [ ] **Crescents (⌒⌒)** = throttle; **Poppet on seat** = check valve
- [ ] **FRL** = first component after compressor/pump
- [ ] Pump **triangle pointing out** of line; compressor **triangle pointing in**
- [ ] Double-acting cylinder has **two chambers** (cap end A, rod end B)
- [ ] **4/3 DCV** = most tested in KTU (3 positions, 4 ports)
- [ ] **5/2 DCV** = most used in pneumatics
- [ ] **Formulas to memorize:** $F = PA$, $v = Q/A$, $t = LA/Q$, $Q = V_d \times n$
- [ ] Cap-end area $A_1 = \frac{\pi}{4} D^2$; Rod-end area $A_2 = \frac{\pi}{4} (D^2 - d^2)$
- [ ] Retraction is **faster but weaker**; extension is **slower but stronger**
- [ ] Air consumption: **must multiply by $P_{abs}/P_{atm}$** for free air
- [ ] Pressure relief valve is **mandatory** in every hydraulic circuit (safety)
- [ ] Silencers are required on **pneumatic exhaust ports** (noise + safety)
- [ ] **Practice drawing each symbol at least 5 times** before the exam
- [ ] Memorize spool position diagrams for 3/2, 4/2, 4/3, 5/2, 5/3 DCVs
- [ ] Always **label all components** in circuit diagrams (no marks without labels)

---

> **End of Module 2 Notes — Development of Simple Hydraulic and Pneumatic Circuits Using Standard Symbols**

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of modern automotive mechatronics, an **automotive sensor** is a transducer that converts a physical, chemical, or kinematic quantity associated with a vehicle into an electrical signal interpretable by the **Engine Control Unit (ECU)**, **Transmission Control Unit (TCU)**, or domain-specific controllers such as the **ABS/ESC module**, **Body Control Module (BCM)**, and **ADAS processors**. The KTU 2024 scheme categorises the in-vehicle sensor suite into the following functional clusters relevant to Module 2:

- **Powertrain sensors:** Throttle Position, Air Mass Flow, Crank/Cam Position, Manifold Pressure, Manifold Temperature, Engine Temperature, Lambda (O₂), Knock.
- **Chassis sensors:** Wheel Speed, Steering Position, Steering Torque, Brake Pressure, Tire Pressure.
- **Body and Safety sensors:** Crash (Acceleration), Fuel Level.

Each sensor interfaces with the ECU through standardized voltage (**0 – 5 V**), current (**4 – 20 mA**), frequency (digital pulses), or **CAN/LIN bus** protocols (typically **500 kbps** for CAN 2.0B in passenger cars).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):** The emphasis is on the *application*, *working principle*, and *output interface* of each sensor — not on manufacturer-specific part numbers. Focus on the *physical transduction mechanism* (resistive, inductive, piezoelectric, Hall, optical, thermistor, electrochemical).

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Nervous System" of a Vehicle

Imagine a human driving a car blindfolded. To drive safely, the brain must continuously receive signals from:
- **Eyes** → position of road, obstacles
- **Skin** → temperature, pressure
- **Ears** → engine knock, vibration
- **Muscles (proprioception)** → steering angle, torque feedback
- **Pain receptors** → crash impact

In exactly the same way, the **ECU is the brain**, and the **sensors are the nervous system**. Each sensor reports a specific physiological state of the car. The **actuators** (fuel injectors, spark plugs, brake modulators, steering motors) act as the muscles, executing decisions taken by the ECU.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Definition (KTU Terminology):**
> A *sensor* in mechatronics is a device that detects a change in a physical stimulus and converts it into a measurable electrical signal. When this signal is conditioned, processed, and fed back to a controller, it forms a **closed-loop mechatronic system** — the foundation of every modern Electronic Control Unit (ECU) function in an automobile.

## 1.3 Standard Reference Quantities

The following reference values are universally used in KTU 2024 automotive mechatronics problems and are worth memorising:

- **Stoichiometric Air-Fuel Ratio (Gasoline):** **14.7 : 1** (λ = 1.00)
- **Stoichiometric AFR (Diesel):** **14.5 : 1**
- **Lambda (λ):** **1.0** at stoichiometry
- **Standard NTC thermistor β-constant:** **3435 K** (typical for engine coolant sensors)
- **TPS voltage at idle (WOT closed):** **0.5 V** typical
- **TPS voltage at WOT:** **4.5 V** typical
- **CKP reluctor teeth:** **60-2 = 58 teeth** (one missing tooth for TDC reference)
- **Wheel Speed Sensor output:** Hall-effect square wave, **0 – 12 V**, frequency proportional to wheel RPM
- **Crash sensor threshold:** typically **3 g – 5 g** for airbag deployment pretensioners
- **Lambda sensor operating temperature:** **> 350 °C** (hence inbuilt heater)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

This section groups the sensors logically by transduction principle, which is the recommended study approach for KTU Module 2 because questions often test the underlying physics, not just memorised application names.

## 2.1 Sensor Grouping by Transduction Principle

### Group A — Resistive / Potentiometric Sensors
- **Throttle Position Sensor (TPS)**
- **Fuel Level Sensor**
- **(Older) Manifold Absolute Pressure sensors**

| Sensor | Physical Variable | Working Principle |
|---|---|---|
| **TPS** | Throttle plate angular position (0° – 90°) | Rotary potentiometer wiper sliding over resistive track; output voltage is a ratiometric function of throttle angle. |
| **Fuel Level** | Height of fuel in tank | Float attached to wiper arm sweeps a potentiometric track; resistance varies linearly with float height. |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pitfall:** The TPS is a *non-contact* sensor in most modern vehicles (Hall-effect or AMR). Examiners sometimes ask whether the TPS is a true potentiometer. Always state: *traditional = potentiometric, modern = non-contact Hall/AMR.*

### Group B — Magnetic / Inductive Sensors
- **Crank Position Sensor (CKP)**
- **Cam Position Sensor (CMP)**
- **Wheel Speed Sensor (WSS)**

These use either:
1. **Variable Reluctance (VR / magnetic pickup):** A permanent magnet with a coil surrounds a toothed reluctor wheel. As teeth pass, flux changes induce an **AC voltage** whose frequency is proportional to RPM. **No DC supply required.**
2. **Hall-effect:** A semiconductor slab in a magnetic field; rotation of an interrupter ring (or magnet ring) creates digital square-wave pulses. **Requires DC supply (typically 5 V or 12 V).**

### Group C — Hot-Film / Hot-Wire Thermal Mass Flow
- **Air Mass Flow Sensor (MAF)**

A heated sensing element (wire or film) is maintained at a constant temperature **ΔT** above the incoming air. Airflow cools the element, and the control circuit increases heating current to maintain ΔT. The added current (or voltage) is proportional to **mass air flow (kg/h)**.

### Group D — Piezoelectric / Piezoresistive
- **Knock Sensor** (piezoelectric, usually PZT ceramic)
- **Manifold Absolute Pressure (MAP)** (piezo-resistive Wheatstone bridge on silicon)
- **Brake Pressure Sensor** (strain-gauge or piezo-resistive)
- **Crash Sensor** (MEMS capacitive accelerometer or mechanical ball-in-tube)

> [!NOTE]
> **Distinguish clearly:** *Piezoelectric* sensors generate charge under mechanical stress (knock, vibration). *Piezoresistive* sensors change resistance under stress (MAP, brake pressure). Both are common 14-mark question stems.

### Group E — Thermistors (NTC)
- **Engine Coolant Temperature (ECT)**
- **Intake / Manifold Air Temperature (IAT / MAT)**

NTC (Negative Temperature Coefficient) thermistors have resistance that *decreases* exponentially with temperature. They are read by the ECU as part of a **voltage divider**.

### Group F — Electrochemical / Solid-State Gas Sensors
- **Lambda Sensor (O₂ Sensor)** — Zirconia (ZrO₂) or Titania (TiO₂)
- **Narrowband (Two-Step) and Wideband (Linear Lambda)**

### Group G — Strain-Gauge Based
- **Steering Torque Sensor**
- **Brake Pressure Sensor** (alternative form)

A **torsion bar** in the steering column twists slightly as the driver applies torque. Strain gauges bonded to the bar measure the shear strain, from which torque is calculated. The bar's twist is typically limited to **± 4° to ± 6°**.

### Group H — RF / Wireless Sensors
- **Tire Pressure Monitoring System (TPMS)** — Direct TPMS uses a MEMS pressure sensor + temperature sensor + RF transmitter (usually **315 MHz** North America / **433.92 MHz** Europe) inside each wheel. Battery life ≈ **5 – 10 years**.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas below are **mathematically verifiable** and represent the standard analytical framework expected at KTU 2024 B.Tech level. Memorise the units as well.

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\lambda = \dfrac{AFR_{actual}}{AFR_{stoich}}$ | Lambda definition (air-fuel equivalence ratio) | dimensionless |
| 2 | $m_{air} = \dfrac{V_d \cdot \eta_{vol} \cdot P_{man}}{R \cdot T_{man}}$ | Speed-Density air mass estimation | $\text{kg/rev}$ |
| 3 | $R(T) = R_0 \cdot e^{\beta \left( \dfrac{1}{T} - \dfrac{1}{T_0} \right)}$ | NTC thermistor resistance | $\Omega$ |
| 4 | $V_{out} = V_{ref} \cdot \dfrac{R_{therm}}{R_{therm} + R_{s}}$ | Voltage-divider output for thermistor circuit | V |
| 5 | $V_{TPS} = V_{ref} \cdot \dfrac{\theta}{\theta_{max}}$ | TPS ratiometric output (linear) | V |
| 6 | $f_{CKP} = \dfrac{N_{teeth} \cdot RPM}{60}$ | CKP signal frequency | Hz |
| 7 | $P_{wheel} = \dfrac{2 \pi \cdot N_{teeth} \cdot f \cdot r_{wheel}}{N_{teeth\_ring}}$ | Wheel speed from sensor frequency | Pa (pressure equivalent) |
| 8 | $q = d_{33} \cdot F$ | Piezoelectric charge (knock sensor) | C |
| 9 | $\dfrac{\Delta R}{R} = G \cdot \varepsilon$ | Piezoresistive gauge relation | dimensionless |
| 10 | $T = \dfrac{G \cdot J \cdot \theta}{L}$ | Torque from torsion bar twist angle | N·m |
| 11 | $Q = K \cdot \rho \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T$ | Hot-wire MAF heat loss per unit time | W |
| 12 | $P_{abs} = P_{gauge} + P_{atm}$ | Manifold absolute pressure | Pa |
| 13 | $V_{lambda} = f(\lambda)$ | Wideband lambda pump-cell voltage | V |

Where standard variables are:
- $V_d$ = engine displacement volume (m³)
- $\eta_{vol}$ = volumetric efficiency (0.7 – 0.95 typical)
- $R$ = specific gas constant for air = **287 J/(kg·K)**
- $\beta$ = thermistor beta constant (typically **3435 K**)
- $G$ = gauge factor (≈ **2.0** for constantan, ≈ **200** for silicon)
- $J$ = polar second moment of area of torsion bar (m⁴)
- $\theta$ = angle of twist (rad)
- $L$ = active length of torsion bar (m)

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

Every sensor studied here has a direct, mandated counterpart in a real ADAS / emissions / drivability function:

- **TPS + MAF + MAP + IAT + Lambda** → Closed-loop **air-fuel ratio control** for Euro 6 / BS-VI / Stage VI emission compliance.
- **CKP + CMP** → **Sequential fuel injection** and **variable valve timing (VVT)**. Without CMP, only bank-fire injection is possible (half the resolution).
- **Knock Sensor** → Prevents **engine detonation**, protecting pistons, allowing engines to run closer to MBT (Minimum advance for Best Torque), improving **thermal efficiency by 2 – 4%**.
- **Crash Sensor** → Triggers **airbag + pretensioner** deployment within **10 – 30 ms** of impact. The legal threshold in ECE R94 is **23 – 28 g** depending on crash pulse shape.
- **TPMS** → Mandatory in India under **AIS-145** since 2022 for new passenger vehicles. Indirect TPMS uses WSS data; direct TPMS uses RF pressure sensors.
- **Steering Torque + Position** → Enables **Electric Power Steering (EPS)** with **variable assist** curves and **Lane Keep Assist (LKA)** torque overlay.
- **Wheel Speed + Brake Pressure** → **ABS (Anti-lock Braking System)**, **EBD (Electronic Brakeforce Distribution)**, **ESC (Electronic Stability Control)**.

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section performs the heavy mathematical derivations that are typical of 14-mark KTU Part B questions. Three representative derivations are shown in full: the **Lambda (λ) equivalence ratio**, the **Hot-Wire MAF heat-transfer balance**, and the **NTC Thermistor voltage-divider calibration**.

---

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Lambda (λ) and the Air-Fuel Equivalence Ratio

**Step 1: Stoichiometric Combustion of Iso-Octane (C₈H₁₈)**

The complete combustion of gasoline (modelled as iso-octane) is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_8 H_{18} + a \cdot O_2 + 3.76 \cdot a \cdot N_2 \rightarrow 8 CO_2 + 9 H_2 O + 3.76 \cdot a \cdot N_2
\end{aligned}
$$

Balancing oxygen on both sides:
- Right-hand side oxygen atoms: $8 \times 2 + 9 \times 1 = 25$
- Therefore: $2a = 25 \Rightarrow a = 12.5$

So the stoichiometric reaction is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_8 H_{18} + 12.5 \, O_2 + 47 \, N_2 \rightarrow 8 CO_2 + 9 H_2 O + 47 \, N_2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Stoichiometric Air-Fuel Ratio by Mass**

Mass of air required per kmol of fuel:
- Mass of O₂ = $12.5 \times 32 = 400$ kg
- Mass of N₂ = $47 \times 28 = 1316$ kg
- **Total mass of air** = $400 + 1316 = 1716$ kg

Mass of 1 kmol of C₈H₁₈:
- $M_{fuel} = 8 \times 12 + 18 \times 1 = 114$ kg

Therefore:

$$
\begin{aligned}
AFR_{stoich} = \dfrac{1716}{114} = 15.05
\end{aligned}
$$

For real gasoline with average composition, this rounds to the **standard 14.7 : 1** used in engineering practice (the difference arises from the presence of aromatics, paraffins, and additives in commercial fuel).

**Step 3: Definition of Lambda**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\lambda = \dfrac{AFR_{actual}}{AFR_{stoich}} = \dfrac{m_{air}/m_{fuel}}{(m_{air}/m_{fuel})_{stoich}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Interpretation Table**

| Condition | λ value | Mixture | Sensor Reading |
|---|---|---|---|
| Stoichiometric | $\lambda = 1.00$ | Ideal combustion | Narrowband O₂ = 450 mV transition |
| Lean | $\lambda > 1.00$ | Excess air, lower flame temp | Narrowband O₂ → ~ 100 mV |
| Rich | $\lambda < 1.00$ | Excess fuel, smoke / CO | Narrowband O₂ → ~ 900 mV |

> [!NOTE]
> **Wideband (Linear Lambda) Sensor:** The narrowband (two-step) sensor only tells you *rich or lean*. The **wideband / linear lambda sensor** adds a *pump cell* that actively pumps O₂ in or out to maintain a reference voltage of **450 mV** across a *Nernst cell*. The pump current is linearly proportional to λ over a range of approximately **0.7 < λ < 1.3 (or wider: 0.5 – 4.0)**. The ECU converts this pump current to a 0 – 5 V signal for closed-loop control.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Hot-Wire MAF Sensor: Energy Balance

**Step 1: Setup**

A thin platinum wire of resistance $R_w$ is heated to a temperature $T_w$ above the incoming air temperature $T_a$. The temperature difference is held constant by a closed-loop control:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta T = T_w - T_a = \text{constant (typically 100 °C)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Heat Loss Mechanisms**

Heat is lost from the wire by:
1. **Convective heat loss** to the airflow (the dominant term)
2. **Conductive heat loss** to the supporting structure (constant offset)
3. **Radiative heat loss** (negligible at < 300 °C)

The convective heat loss is governed by King's Law (the empirical relation for hot-wire anemometry):

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{conv} = (A + B \sqrt{v}) \cdot \Delta T
\end{aligned}
$$

where $v$ is the air velocity and $A$, $B$ are constants for the sensor geometry.

**Step 3: Energy Balance at Steady State**

Electrical power supplied = Total heat lost:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dfrac{V^2}{R_w} = (A + B \sqrt{v}) \cdot \Delta T
\end{aligned}
$$

Solving for $V$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V = \sqrt{R_w \cdot (A + B \sqrt{v}) \cdot \Delta T}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Conversion to Mass Air Flow**

The velocity $v$ is related to mass flow $\dot{m}$ through the cross-sectional area of the intake tube $A_{tube}$ and the air density $\rho_a$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{m} = \rho_a \cdot v \cdot A_{tube}
\end{aligned}
$$

Substituting:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V = \sqrt{R_w \cdot \left( A + B \sqrt{\dfrac{\dot{m}}{\rho_a \cdot A_{tube}}} \right) \cdot \Delta T }
\end{aligned}
$$

The ECU inverts this relation (often via a 3-D lookup table in firmware) to obtain $\dot{m}$ from the measured $V$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why a hot wire instead of a vane?** A vane meter measures *volume* flow, requires a moving part, and has poor response to pulsations. A hot-wire meter measures *mass* directly, has no moving parts, and responds in **< 15 ms** — critical for transient throttle manoeuvres.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — NTC Thermistor in Engine Coolant Temperature (ECT) Circuit

**Step 1: Thermistor Resistance Equation**

For an NTC thermistor:

$$
\begin{aligned}
R(T) = R_0 \cdot e^{\beta \left( \dfrac{1}{T} - \dfrac{1}{T_0} \right)}
\end{aligned}
$$

where:
- $R_0$ = resistance at reference temperature $T_0$ (typically **10 kΩ at 25 °C = 298.15 K**)
- $T$ = absolute temperature (K)
- $\beta$ = material constant (typically **3435 K**)

**Step 2: Voltage Divider Interface**

The ECU applies a precision reference voltage $V_{ref} = 5.00$ V across a series combination of the thermistor and a fixed resistor $R_s$ (typically **2.2 kΩ**). The output voltage is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{out} = V_{ref} \cdot \dfrac{R(T)}{R(T) + R_s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Worked Numerical Example**

A KTU 2024 problem gives: $R_0 = 10$ kΩ, $T_0 = 298.15$ K, $\beta = 3435$ K, $R_s = 2.2$ kΩ, $V_{ref} = 5$ V. Find $V_{out}$ at $T = 363.15$ K (90 °C, normal operating coolant temperature).

Step 3a: Compute the exponent:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dfrac{1}{T} - \dfrac{1}{T_0} = \dfrac{1}{363.15} - \dfrac{1}{298.15} = 2.754 \times 10^{-3} - 3.354 \times 10^{-3} = -6.00 \times 10^{-4}
\end{aligned}
$$

Step 3b: Compute the exponent factor:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\beta \cdot \left( \dfrac{1}{T} - \dfrac{1}{T_0} \right) = 3435 \times (-6.00 \times 10^{-4}) = -2.061
\end{aligned}
$$

Step 3c: Compute $R(90\,^\circ C)$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
R(363.15) = 10{,}000 \cdot e^{-2.061} = 10{,}000 \times 0.1272 = 1272 \; \Omega
\end{aligned}
$$

Step 3d: Compute $V_{out}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{out} = 5 \cdot \dfrac{1272}{1272 + 2200} = 5 \cdot \dfrac{1272}{3472} = 5 \times 0.3663 = 1.83 \;\text{V}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Calibration Insight**

This is *exactly* how the ECU distinguishes a cold start (high resistance → $V_{out}$ close to 5 V) from a hot engine (low resistance → $V_{out}$ < 2 V). The firmware uses the inverse function to look up enrichment, idle speed, and cooling fan ON/OFF thresholds.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comprehensive Sensor Summary Table

The matrix below is a **high-density revision tool**. Each row gives the working principle, output, and primary ECU function. This is the *single most useful table* for the KTU Module 2 viva and 3-mark questions.

| # | Sensor | Physical Variable | Working Principle | Typical Output | Primary ECU Function |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **TPS** | Throttle angle | Potentiometric / Hall | 0.5 – 4.5 V | Fuel injection duration, gear shift |
| 2 | **MAF** | Mass air flow | Hot-wire / hot-film | 0 – 5 V (≈ 0 – 800 kg/h) | Air-fuel ratio, injection timing |
| 3 | **CKP** | Crank angle + RPM | Variable reluctance / Hall | AC sine / digital pulse | Ignition timing, misfire detection |
| 4 | **CMP** | Cam position | Hall effect | 0 or 5 V square | Sequential injection, VVT |
| 5 | **WSS** | Wheel RPM | Hall / magnetic | 0 – 12 V square | ABS, ESC, speedometer |
| 6 | **Steering Position** | Steering angle | Optical encoder / Hall | PWM or CAN | EPS, LKA, ADAS |
| 7 | **TPMS** | Tire pressure | MEMS piezo + RF | 433.92 MHz RF | Tyre pressure warning |
| 8 | **Brake Pressure** | Hydraulic pressure | Strain gauge / piezo-resistive | 0 – 5 V | ABS, EBD, brake assist |
| 9 | **Steering Torque** | Steering torque | Strain gauge on torsion bar | 0 – 5 V (differential) | EPS assist curve |
| 10 | **Fuel Level** | Fuel height | Float + potentiometer | Variable resistance | Fuel gauge, range estimation |
| 11 | **Crash** | Deceleration | MEMS / mechanical | Threshold trigger | Airbag, pretensioner |
| 12 | **Lambda (O₂)** | O₂ partial pressure | ZrO₂ Nernst cell | 0 – 1 V (narrow) / 0 – 5 V (wide) | Closed-loop AFR control |
| 13 | **Knock** | Vibration 5 – 15 kHz | Piezoelectric | 0 – 5 V AC | Ignition retard on knock |
| 14 | **ECT** | Coolant temp | NTC thermistor | 0 – 5 V divider | Cold start enrichment, fan |
| 15 | **IAT / MAT** | Intake air temp | NTC thermistor | 0 – 5 V divider | Air density correction |
| 16 | **MAP** | Manifold pressure | Piezoresistive / capacitive | 0 – 5 V (0 – 200 kPa) | Speed-density fuel calc |

**Interpretation of the diagram:**

- The **powertrain cluster** (left) routes to the **ECU**, which actuates fuel injectors and spark plugs.
- The **chassis cluster** (centre) routes to the **ABS/ESC module** and **ADAS processor** for braking and steering intervention.
- The **body/safety cluster** (right) has direct hardwired links to actuators like airbags (for **< 10 ms response time**; CAN bus latency is too high for crash deployment).
- Sensor **redundancy** is a key design principle: for example, MAP + MAF can cross-validate air-flow estimates, and steering torque + position together implement closed-loop EPS control.

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

The following questions are modelled on the KTU 2024 End-Semester Examination (ESE) pattern: **Part A (3 marks each)** and **Part B (14 marks with internal choice, split as 7 + 7)**.

---

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Pattern]**
**Explain the working principle of a Throttle Position Sensor (TPS). Mention its output range and one major application in the engine management system.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The Throttle Position Sensor is a rotary position transducer mounted on the throttle body shaft. As the driver presses the accelerator pedal, the throttle plate rotates, and a wiper (in potentiometric type) or a Hall-effect element (in non-contact type) produces an output voltage proportional to the throttle plate angle.

- **Output range:** typically **0.5 V at idle (WOT closed)** to **4.5 V at full throttle**.
- **Signal type:** ratiometric analog (linear with angle), updated at ≈ **100 Hz**.
- **Major application:** the ECU uses the TPS signal to determine the driver's torque demand, which directly schedules the **fuel injection pulse width** and **ignition advance**. A sudden voltage change rate (dV/dt) is also used to detect *tip-in / tip-out* transients for transient fuel enrichment.

*(Examiner's note: Mentioning both potentiometric and non-contact types earns a full mark.)*

---

### Question 2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Pattern]**
**Distinguish between Narrowband (Two-Step) and Wideband (Linear) Lambda Sensors. Why is the wideband sensor preferred for modern BS-VI / Euro 6 engines?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Narrowband (Two-Step) | Wideband (Linear) |
|---|---|---|
| Output | Switches between ≈ 100 mV (lean) and ≈ 900 mV (rich) at λ = 1 | Continuous 0 – 5 V output, linear over λ ≈ 0.7 – 4.0 |
| Components | Single Nernst cell | Nernst cell + pump cell + diffusion gap |
| Accuracy | Indicates only rich/lean | Exact AFR value |
| Heater | Required | Required (faster light-off) |

**Why preferred for BS-VI / Euro 6:** The emission regulations demand AFR control to within **± 1%** of λ = 1.0 over the entire driving cycle, including cold start, idle, and transient acceleration. A narrowband sensor is a *bang-bang controller* and oscillates around stoichiometry, while a wideband sensor enables **true PID closed-loop control** of the air-fuel mixture, dramatically reducing NOx, CO, and HC emissions.

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> The structure below is a *true KTU ESE Module 2 internal choice.* Both Question A and Question B carry equal marks and should be attempted as alternatives. Each is split as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks** with detailed model solutions.

---

### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the working of a **Crank Position Sensor (CKP)** using a variable reluctance (VR) principle. Derive the expression for the output voltage and state how the ECU determines engine RPM and TDC reference from the signal. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the construction and working of a **piezoelectric Knock Sensor**. Explain how the ECU uses the knock sensor signal for **closed-loop ignition timing control**. Why is the frequency band **5 – 15 kHz** significant? **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question A(a) — CKP (7 marks)

**Construction (2 marks):** The VR-type CKP consists of:
- A **reluctor wheel** (toothed wheel) mounted on the crankshaft, typically with **60 − 2 = 58 teeth** (two missing teeth mark Top-Dead-Centre reference).
- A **pickup unit** containing a **permanent magnet**, soft-iron pole piece, and a **sense coil**.

**Working (2 marks):** As the reluctor wheel rotates, the air gap between the tooth tip and the pole piece varies cyclically. This varies the magnetic reluctance of the magnetic circuit, and the resulting change in flux links the coil and induces an EMF by Faraday's Law.

**Derivation (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
e(t) = -N \dfrac{d\Phi}{dt}
\end{aligned}
$$

For a sinusoidal flux variation $\Phi(t) = \Phi_{max} \sin(\omega t)$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
e(t) = -N \cdot \Phi_{max} \cdot \omega \cdot \cos(\omega t) = \omega \cdot N \cdot \Phi_{max} \cdot \sin\left(\omega t - \dfrac{\pi}{2}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

where the angular frequency $\omega$ is related to engine speed $N_e$ (rev/s) and the number of teeth $T$ as $\omega = 2 \pi \cdot N_e \cdot T$.

**ECU interpretation (1 mark):**
- The frequency of the AC output is measured by counting zero-crossings → gives **engine RPM**.
- The two missing teeth produce a longer pulse interval → identified by the ECU as the **TDC reference** for cylinder #1.
- A companion **CMP sensor** (one pulse per cam revolution) disambiguates *which* cylinder is at TDC (compression stroke vs. exhaust stroke).

---

#### Model Solution to Question A(b) — Knock Sensor (7 marks)

**Construction (2 marks):** A knock sensor is a **piezoelectric accelerometer** consisting of a PZT (lead zirconate titanate) ceramic disc, a seismic mass, and a metallic housing bolted to the engine block (typically between cylinders 2 and 3, or directly above the combustion chamber on the block side).

**Working (2 marks):** Engine knock produces high-frequency vibration (acoustic shock waves from auto-ignition of end-gas). The seismic mass loads the piezo disc, generating a charge proportional to acceleration:

$$
\begin{aligned}
q = d_{33} \cdot F = d_{33} \cdot m \cdot a
\end{aligned}
$$

This charge is converted to a voltage by a **charge amplifier** inside the ECU.

**Frequency band 5 – 15 kHz (1.5 marks):** The fundamental frequency of engine knock lies in **5 – 15 kHz** (it scales with cylinder bore; smaller bore → higher frequency). The ECU uses a **band-pass filter** centred in this band to *separate knock vibration from normal engine noise* (valve closing, piston slap, etc., are typically below 5 kHz).

**Closed-loop ignition timing (1.5 marks):**
- The ECU runs ignition timing at the **MBT (Minimum advance for Best Torque)** map value.
- If knock is detected (signal amplitude > threshold), the ECU **retards** timing by 1 – 3° in steps.
- If no knock is detected for several cycles, timing is **re-advanced** slowly.
- This is a classic *boundary-seeking* control loop, maximising efficiency while protecting the engine.

---

### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** Explain the working of a **Hot-Wire Air Mass Flow Sensor (MAF)**. Derive the energy balance equation relating voltage, air velocity, and temperature difference. List two advantages of a hot-wire MAF over a vane-type meter. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a neat diagram, explain the working of a **Narrowband (Two-Step) Lambda Sensor** based on a ZrO₂ Nernst cell. Show how the sensor's output voltage depends on the air-fuel ratio. Why is an inbuilt heater provided, and what is the typical light-off time? **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question B(a) — Hot-Wire MAF (7 marks)

**Construction (1.5 marks):** A Hot-Wire MAF sensor contains a thin platinum wire (≈ 70 µm diameter) suspended in the intake airstream, a temperature-compensating cold-wire resistor, and an electronic control circuit mounted on the sensor housing.

**Working (1.5 marks):** The hot wire is maintained at a constant temperature difference (≈ 100 °C) above the incoming air. As air flows past, it cools the wire by forced convection. The control circuit increases heating current to compensate, and the resulting current/voltage is the output signal.

**Derivation (3 marks):**

King's Law for convective heat loss from the wire:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{conv} = (A + B \sqrt{v}) \cdot \Delta T
\end{aligned}
$$

At steady state, electrical heating equals convective cooling:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dfrac{V^2}{R_w} = (A + B \sqrt{v}) \cdot \Delta T
\end{aligned}
$$

Solving for $V$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V = \sqrt{R_w \cdot (A + B \sqrt{v}) \cdot \Delta T}
\end{aligned}
$$

Since mass flow $\dot{m} = \rho_a \cdot v \cdot A_{tube}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V = \sqrt{R_w \cdot \left( A + B \sqrt{\dfrac{\dot{m}}{\rho_a \cdot A_{tube}}} \right) \cdot \Delta T }
\end{aligned}
$$

The ECU stores the inverse mapping $\dot{m}(V, T_a)$ as a calibration table.

**Advantages over vane meter (1 mark):**
1. Measures *mass* flow directly (no separate temperature/pressure correction needed).
2. No moving parts → faster response (< 15 ms), better at transient throttle.
3. Lower pressure drop across the meter.

---

#### Model Solution to Question B(b) — Narrowband Lambda Sensor (7 marks)

**Construction (2 marks):** A narrowband lambda sensor is a **thimble-shaped ZrO₂ (zirconia) ceramic** element, coated inside and outside with porous platinum electrodes. The exhaust gas flows on the outer (test) side; ambient air flows on the inner (reference) side. The element is enclosed in a protective steel housing with slots for gas access, and contains an **inbuilt resistive heater**.

**Working (2.5 marks):** At operating temperature (> 350 °C), the ZrO₂ becomes an **O²⁻ conductor**. The Nernst equation gives the EMF across the cell:

$$
\begin{aligned}
E = \dfrac{RT}{4F} \cdot \ln \left( \dfrac{P_{O_2,\,ref}}{P_{O_2,\,exh}} \right)
\end{aligned}
$$

where $F$ is Faraday's constant (**96 485 C/mol**). Because the reference side has $P_{O_2} \approx 0.21$ atm (air), the output is ≈ 450 mV when $P_{O_2,\,exh}$ corresponds to stoichiometric exhaust.

**Output characteristic (1.5 marks):**
- **Rich (λ < 1):** very low O₂ in exhaust → high cell EMF, output ≈ **800 – 900 mV**.
- **Stoichiometric (λ = 1):** sudden transition → **≈ 450 mV**.
- **Lean (λ > 1):** excess O₂ → low EMF, output ≈ **0 – 100 mV**.

The output is a steep sigmoid curve, hence "two-step" or "switch-type."

**Inbuilt heater (1 mark):** Exhaust gas temperature at cold start may be < 100 °C, well below the 350 °C threshold needed for O²⁻ conduction. The internal heater rapidly brings the sensor to operating temperature in **< 15 – 30 seconds** ("light-off time"), enabling closed-loop fuel control as early as possible after start — critical for **BS-VI cold-start emission compliance**.

---

## 5.1 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common places students lose marks in Module 2:**
> 1. **Confusing CKP and CMP:** The CKP fires *every* tooth (high frequency) and gives RPM; the CMP fires *once per cam revolution* and identifies the *phase* of cylinder #1. Writing "CKP tells the ECU which cylinder is firing" loses 2 marks — the CMP does that.
> 2. **Confusing Narrowband and Wideband Lambda:** Many students write "wideband has higher voltage" — wrong. *Wideband* uses an additional **pump cell** and outputs a *linear* 0 – 5 V signal proportional to λ. The output is **not** the Nernst voltage itself; it is a control voltage generated by the ECU to maintain the pump cell current.
> 3. **Forgetting units on the speed-density equation:** Examiners specifically dock 1 mark if $P_{man}$ is left in bar (use Pa!) or $T_{man}$ in °C (use K!).
> 4. **Calling the Knock Sensor a "vibration sensor" without naming the piezoelectric principle:** Always write *piezoelectric* explicitly. Generic terms cost marks.
> 5. **Mistaking TPMS as "indirect only":** India (AIS-145) accepts both direct and indirect, but **direct TPMS is the dominant market technology** since 2014. Examiners expect "MEMS pressure sensor + RF transmitter" as the answer.

---

## 5.2 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [x] **TPS** = rotary position sensor, 0.5 – 4.5 V, used for fuel injection pulse width.
- [x] **MAF** = hot-wire, mass-flow measurement, response < 15 ms.
- [x] **CKP** = VR or Hall, 60-2 teeth, gives RPM and TDC reference.
- [x] **CMP** = Hall, 1 pulse/cam rev, identifies cylinder phase for sequential injection.
- [x] **Wheel Speed** = Hall, feeds ABS/ESC; WSS failure → ABS warning lamp.
- [x] **Steering Position** = optical encoder, used by EPS and ADAS (LKA).
- [x] **TPMS** = MEMS + RF (direct) or WSS-based (indirect); mandatory under AIS-145.
- [x] **Brake Pressure** = strain gauge, used in ABS/EBD/ESC algorithms.
- [x] **Steering Torque** = strain gauge on torsion bar, used for variable-assist EPS.
- [x] **Fuel Level** = float + potentiometer, drives dashboard gauge.
- [x] **Crash Sensor** = accelerometer, must respond in < 30 ms for airbag.
- [x] **Lambda (Narrowband)** = ZrO₂, two-step output (~100 mV / ~900 mV), λ = 1 ± small.
- [x] **Lambda (Wideband)** = ZrO₂ + pump cell, linear 0 – 5 V over wide λ range.
- [x] **Knock** = piezoelectric, 5 – 15 kHz band, retard timing on detection.
- [x] **ECT / IAT** = NTC thermistor, voltage divider, exponential R-T relation with β ≈ 3435 K.
- [x] **MAP** = piezo-resistive, 0 – 200 kPa, used in speed-density fuel equation.
- [x] **Stoichiometric AFR for gasoline = 14.7** — *memorise this number.*
- [x] **Lambda definition** $\lambda = AFR_{actual} / AFR_{stoich}$ — *always write the equation; never just the value.*
- [x] **Piezoelectric vs Piezoresistive** — distinguish explicitly in any answer.
- [x] **Speed-density formula** $m_{air} = V_d \eta_{vol} P_{man} / (R T_{man})$ — *use SI units: Pa, K, m³.*
- [x] **King's Law** for hot-wire MAF: $Q = (A + B\sqrt{v}) \Delta T$ — *memorise.*
- [x] **NTC thermistor equation** with β = 3435 K — *memorise the form, not just the name.*
- [x] **Crash sensor threshold** ≈ 3 – 5 g; airbag fires within 10 – 30 ms.
- [x] **TPMS frequency** 433.92 MHz (Europe/India) vs. 315 MHz (North America).
- [x] **CKP missing teeth = 60-2** for the TDC reference pulse.
- [x] **In a CAN bus system**, sensor signals are digitised at the sensor and broadcast as messages (e.g., EngineSpeed_0x0CF00400 for CKP).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fabrication: Deposition, Lithography, Micromachining Methods for MEMS
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMUT602 - MECHATRONICS
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Fabrication: Deposition, Lithography, Micromachining Methods for MEMS

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 MEMS — The Formal Definition

**Micro-Electro-Mechanical Systems (MEMS)** are integrated micro-scale devices that combine mechanical elements, sensors, actuators, and electronics on a common silicon substrate through microfabrication technology. As per the KTU 2024 syllabus (PCMUT602, Module 3), MEMS fabrication is the foundational manufacturing technology that enables the realization of mechanical structures, sensors, and actuators at the micrometer scale (typically **1 µm to 1000 µm** feature size).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PCMUT602 – Module 3):**
> MEMS fabrication encompasses three fundamental process families:
> 1. **Deposition** — adding thin films of material onto a substrate.
> 2. **Lithography** — transferring geometric patterns onto the substrate.
> 3. **Micromachining** — selectively removing or modifying material to form 3D structures.
>
> These three pillars operate sequentially to manufacture mechanical and electronic microsystems.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Sandwich Bakery" Model

Imagine MEMS fabrication as baking an extremely precise layered cake in a microscopic kitchen:

| MEMS Step | Bakery Analogy | Purpose |
|-----------|---------------|---------|
| **Deposition** | Spreading chocolate/cream layers on the cake | Adds new material layers |
| **Lithography** | Using a stencil to sprinkle sugar in patterns | Defines where material stays/goes |
| **Micromachining** | Cutting the cake into 3D shapes | Removes unwanted material to form structure |

**Intuitive takeaway:** Just as a baker carefully layers, stencils, and carves a multi-tier cake, a MEMS engineer **adds**, **patterns**, and **removes** materials on a silicon wafer — typically a circular disc of diameter **75 mm to 300 mm** and thickness **525 µm**.

## 1.3 Why MEMS Fabrication Matters in Mechatronics

MEMS is the **bridge** between the mechanical and electronic worlds. For a Mechatronics engineer, mastery of these fabrication methods is non-negotiable because:

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Significance of MEMS Fabrication:**
> - It enables integration of **sensors** (accelerometers, gyroscopes, pressure sensors) directly with **control electronics** on one chip.
> - It enables fabrication of **actuators** (micro-mirrors, micro-valves, micro-pumps).
> - It miniaturizes macroscale systems by a factor of **10³ to 10⁶**, reducing **mass, cost, and power consumption** simultaneously.
> - It allows **batch fabrication** — thousands of identical devices from one wafer, reducing per-unit cost drastically.

## 1.4 Process Flow — Big Picture

The complete MEMS process flow is a sequence of **deposition → lithography → etching → release**, often repeated **5 to 20+ times** to build up a finished device. This sequence is generically called a **process module**.

The **characteristic length scale** of MEMS devices ranges from **1 µm to 1 mm**, the operating voltages are typically **1 V to 100 V**, and the response times are usually in the range of **microseconds to milliseconds**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 DEPOSITION METHODS

Deposition is the process of growing or depositing thin films of material onto a substrate. The two principal families are **Physical Vapor Deposition (PVD)** and **Chemical Vapor Deposition (CVD)**.

### 2.1.1 Physical Vapor Deposition (PVD)

PVD relies on physical processes (no chemical reaction) to transfer atoms from a source (target) to the substrate.

**(a) Thermal Evaporation**
- The source material is heated in a vacuum chamber until it **evaporates** and condenses on the colder substrate.
- Vacuum required: typically **10⁻⁵ to 10⁻⁷ Torr** (**1 Torr ≈ 133.322 Pa**).
- Typical deposition rate: **1 to 100 nm/min**.
- **Limitation:** Poor step coverage; cannot deposit alloys uniformly.

**(b) Sputtering**
- A high-energy inert gas ion (typically **Argon⁺**, Ar⁺) is accelerated by an electric field to **bombard a target**.
- Momentum transfer ejects target atoms, which travel ballistically and deposit on the substrate.
- Sputtering yield **S** = number of target atoms ejected per incident ion (typically **S = 0.5 to 5** for most metals at **1 keV**).
- Types: **DC Sputtering**, **RF Sputtering** (for insulators), **Magnetron Sputtering** (higher rates).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reactive Sputtering:** When a reactive gas (e.g., O₂ or N₂) is introduced alongside Ar, the sputtered atoms react on the substrate to form compounds (e.g., SiO₂, TiN). This is called **reactive sputtering** and is widely used for MEMS structural layers.

### 2.1.2 Chemical Vapor Deposition (CVD)

CVD relies on **chemical reactions** of gaseous precursors on a heated substrate to form a solid thin film.

**Generic CVD Reaction:**

$$
\text{Gas precursor (e.g., SiH}_4\text{)} \xrightarrow{\text{heat (600–900°C)}} \text{Solid film (e.g., Si)} + \text{By-products (e.g., H}_2\text{)}
$$

**Variants of CVD:**

| Variant | Pressure | Temperature | Use in MEMS |
|---------|----------|-------------|-------------|
| **APCVD** | Atmospheric | High | Low-cost films |
| **LPCVD** | Low (0.1–10 Torr) | High (600–800°C) | Conformal coatings, structural Si |
| **PECVD** | Low | Low (200–400°C) | Films on temperature-sensitive substrates |
| **HDPCVD** | Low | Moderate | High-aspect-ratio gap-fill |

> [!NOTE]
> **LPCVD vs PECVD — A Key Comparison for KTU:**
> - **LPCVD** = Low Pressure, High Temperature, **better film quality**, conformality, used for **polysilicon structural layers**.
> - **PECVD** = Plasma Enhanced, Low Temperature, **faster deposition**, used for **sacrificial layer SiO₂** (because it can be deposited without damaging underlying aluminum interconnects).

### 2.1.3 Electrodeposition (Electroplating)

- Used to deposit **thick metal films** (1 µm to 500 µm) — far thicker than PVD/CVD can achieve.
- A conductive seed layer is first deposited (by sputtering), then the wafer is immersed in an electrolyte bath.
- An applied current drives metal ions (e.g., **Cu²⁺, Ni²⁺, Au⁺**) to deposit onto the seed layer.
- **Used in LIGA process** (see Section 2.3).

### 2.1.4 Epitaxy

- Growth of a crystalline layer on a crystalline substrate where the new layer **inherits the crystal orientation**.
- **MBE (Molecular Beam Epitaxy):** Ultra-high vacuum, atomic-layer precision.
- **LPE (Liquid Phase Epitaxy):** From a melt.
- **VPE (Vapor Phase Epitaxy):** CVD-based epitaxial growth.

---

## 2.2 LITHOGRAPHY

Lithography is the process of **transferring a geometric pattern from a mask to a photoresist-coated substrate**. It is the **pattern-defining step** in MEMS fabrication.

### 2.2.1 Photolithography Steps (The 8-Step Recipe)

The standard photolithographic process is the most frequently asked topic in KTU Module 3.

| Step | Action | Engineering Purpose |
|------|--------|---------------------|
| **1. Substrate Cleaning** | RCA clean, piranha clean, or solvent clean | Remove organic/inorganic contaminants |
| **2. Dehydration Bake** | 150–200°C for 30 min | Remove moisture for resist adhesion |
| **3. Priming (HMDS)** | Hexamethyldisilazane vapor coating | Promote photoresist adhesion |
| **4. Spin Coating** | Photoresist spun at **1000–6000 rpm** | Uniform thin film (~0.5–2 µm) |
| **5. Soft Bake** | 90–110°C for 60 sec on hot plate | Evaporate solvent, densify resist |
| **6. Exposure** | UV light through mask (i-line = **365 nm**, h-line = **405 nm**) | Photochemical reaction in exposed regions |
| **7. Development** | Immersion in TMAH or KOH solution | Dissolve exposed (positive) or unexposed (negative) regions |
| **8. Hard Bake** | 120–180°C for 30 min | Improve etch resistance and adhesion |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Positive vs Negative Photoresist (Very High Yield for KTU):**
> - **Positive resist (e.g., AZ-series, S1813):** Exposed regions become **soluble** → mask opening = final pattern.
> - **Negative resist (e.g., SU-8, NR7):** Exposed regions become **insoluble (cross-linked)** → mask opening = inverse pattern.
> - **SU-8** is a thick negative resist (1 µm to 2 mm) widely used for **high-aspect-ratio MEMS structures** and is often asked in KTU Module 3.

### 2.2.2 Resolution Limit of Photolithography

The minimum resolvable feature size is governed by the **Rayleigh criterion**:

$$
R = k_1 \cdot \frac{\lambda}{NA}
$$

where:
- $R$ = minimum resolution (line width),
- $\lambda$ = wavelength of exposure light,
- $NA$ = numerical aperture of the lens system,
- $k_1$ = process factor (typically **0.4 to 0.7**).

> **Depth of Focus (DOF):**
> $$ DOF = k_2 \cdot \frac{\lambda}{NA^2} $$
> where $k_2 \approx 1$.

| Light Source | Wavelength $\lambda$ | Achievable Resolution |
|--------------|----------------------|------------------------|
| **g-line (Hg)** | **436 nm** | ~0.5 µm |
| **h-line (Hg)** | **405 nm** | ~0.4 µm |
| **i-line (Hg)** | **365 nm** | ~0.35 µm |
| **DUV (KrF excimer)** | **248 nm** | ~0.13 µm |
| **EUV** | **13.5 nm** | ~10 nm |

### 2.2.3 Advanced Lithography for MEMS

- **Electron Beam Lithography (EBL):** Uses focused electron beam to directly write patterns. Resolution ~**10 nm**, but **slow** (serial process).
- **X-ray Lithography:** Uses synchrotron radiation ($\lambda \approx 0.5$ to $5$ nm). Excellent for high-aspect-ratio LIGA.
- **Focused Ion Beam (FIB):** Uses Ga⁺ ions; can both **image** and **mill** (no mask required).
- **Nanoimprint Lithography (NIL):** Mechanical stamping of a hard mold into a resist — sub-10 nm resolution, low cost.

---

## 2.3 MICROMACHINING METHODS

Micromachining is the process of **selectively removing material** from the substrate or from deposited layers to form 3D mechanical structures. Two principal categories: **Bulk** and **Surface** micromachining.

### 2.3.1 Bulk Micromachining

The **substrate itself** is etched away to form the mechanical structure.

**(a) Wet Bulk Etching (Chemical Etching)**

- Uses liquid etchants: most commonly **KOH (Potassium Hydroxide)** or **TMAH (Tetramethylammonium Hydroxide)** or **EDP (Ethylene Diamine Pyrocatechol)**.
- Highly **anisotropic** on silicon — etches along specific crystallographic planes.
- Silicon etch rate depends on crystal orientation:

| Crystal Plane | Etch Rate in KOH | Relative Rate |
|---------------|------------------|---------------|
| {100} | ~1.0 µm/min | **Fast** |
| {110} | ~1.8 µm/min | **Fastest** |
| {111} | ~0.005 µm/min | **Extremely slow** (acts as etch stop) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Anisotropy Ratio (AR):** AR = (etch rate of {100}) / (etch rate of {111}) in KOH ≈ **100:1 to 400:1**. This means the {111} plane effectively stops etching and produces characteristic **V-grooves and pyramidal pits** bounded by {111} walls.

**Undercutting** occurs because {111} planes stop vertical etching only when the sidewall angle reaches **54.74°** for a (100) wafer.

**(b) Dry Bulk Etching**

- **DRIE (Deep Reactive Ion Etching) — the Bosch Process:**
  - Alternates between **etch step (SF₆)** and **passivation step (C₄F₈)**.
  - Produces nearly **vertical sidewalls**, **high aspect ratios** (up to **50:1**), and deep etches (>500 µm).
  - Also called the **"scalloping" process** because the alternation produces characteristic scalloped sidewalls at the nanometer scale.

### 2.3.2 Surface Micromachining

- The mechanical structure is built up from **thin films deposited on top of the substrate**, not by etching the substrate itself.
- Uses a **sacrificial layer** that is later removed to "release" the mechanical structure.

**Process Flow of Surface Micromachining:**

| Step | Action |
|------|--------|
| 1 | Deposit **sacrificial layer** (e.g., SiO₂ or PSG — Phosphosilicate Glass) |
| 2 | Deposit **structural layer** (e.g., polysilicon) |
| 3 | Pattern structural layer by lithography + etch |
| 4 | **Release** the structure by selectively etching the sacrificial layer (e.g., **HF — Hydrofluoric Acid** for SiO₂) |
| 5 | Critical-point drying to prevent **stiction** (sticking) |

> [!WARNING]
> **Stiction Problem:** After wet release, capillary forces from the rinsing liquid can pull microstructures into contact with the substrate where they stick permanently — a major failure mode in MEMS. Solutions include **critical-point drying**, **freeze-drying**, and **self-assembled monolayer (SAM) coatings**.

### 2.3.3 LIGA Process

**LIGA** = German acronym: **Li**thographie, **G**alvanoformung, **A**bformung (Lithography + Electroplating + Molding).

| Stage | Action |
|-------|--------|
| **L** — Lithography | X-ray exposure of thick PMMA resist through a mask |
| **I** — Galvanoformung (Electroplating) | Electroplate metal (e.g., Ni, Au) into the PMMA mold cavities |
| **G** — Abformung (Molding) | Use the metal mold to mass-produce polymer or metal parts by injection molding |

- LIGA enables **extremely tall structures** (hundreds of micrometers to millimeters) with **near-vertical sidewalls** and **sub-micron lateral precision**.
- Disadvantage: requires expensive synchrotron X-ray source.

---

## 2.4 KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **THE COMPLETE FORMULA SHEET — KTU PCMUT602 MODULE 3**

| # | Formula / Concept | Equation | Engineering Use |
|---|-------------------|----------|-----------------|
| 1 | **Deposition rate** | $R_d = \dfrac{M \cdot N_A}{\rho \cdot N_A \cdot t}$ (atoms/time) | Process throughput |
| 2 | **Sputtering yield** | $S = \dfrac{\text{Target atoms ejected}}{\text{Incident ions}}$ | PVD efficiency |
| 3 | **CVD growth rate (mass-transport limited)** | $r \propto \dfrac{D \cdot C_s}{\delta}$ | Film growth control |
| 4 | **Resist thickness** | $t_r = \dfrac{K \cdot C^\beta \cdot \omega^{-\alpha}}{}$ | Spin-coating design |
| 5 | **Rayleigh resolution** | $R = k_1 \dfrac{\lambda}{NA}$ | Lithography resolution |
| 6 | **Depth of focus** | $DOF = k_2 \dfrac{\lambda}{NA^2}$ | Lithography tolerance |
| 7 | **Etch rate** | $ER = \dfrac{\Delta z}{\Delta t}$ | Etch throughput |
| 8 | **Selectivity** | $S_e = \dfrac{ER_{\text{film}}}{ER_{\text{substrate}}}$ | Mask design |
| 9 | **Anisotropy factor** | $AF = 1 - \dfrac{ER_{\text{lateral}}}{ER_{\text{vertical}}}$ | Sidewall control |
| 10 | **Etch profile angle** | $\theta = \arctan\left(\dfrac{ER_v}{ER_l}\right)$ | Wall slope |
| 11 | **Etch depth (time)** | $d = ER \cdot t$ | Process control |
| 12 | **Aspect ratio (DRIE)** | $AR = \dfrac{\text{Depth}}{\text{Width}}$ | Structure design |
| 13 | **V-groove depth** | $d_{\text{groove}} = \dfrac{w}{2}\cot(54.74°) = 0.707\,w$ | KOH wet etch |
| 14 | **Stiction force** | $F_s = \dfrac{12\pi \eta R^2 v}{h}$ (lubrication theory) | Anti-stiction design |
| 15 | **Spring constant (cantilever)** | $k = \dfrac{E w t^3}{4 L^3}$ | MEMS resonator design |

> **Conversion reminders:**
> - **1 Å = 0.1 nm = 10⁻¹⁰ m**
> - **1 µm = 10⁻⁶ m**
> - **1 Torr = 133.322 Pa**
> - **Standard atmospheric pressure = 760 Torr = 101.325 kPa**

---

## 2.5 Process Selection Matrix — When to Use What?

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world engineering decision matrix for KTU viva/practical:**

| Goal | Recommended Method |
|------|---------------------|
| Sub-µm metal line | **Electron-beam lithography + evaporation** |
| Thick structural metal (50 µm) | **LIGA** |
| Deep silicon cavity (500 µm depth, vertical walls) | **DRIE (Bosch process)** |
| Angled silicon features (V-grooves, pyramids) | **KOH wet etching** |
| Free-standing polysilicon beam | **Surface micromachining + HF release** |
| Piezoelectric MEMS (PZT, AlN) | **Sputtering or sol-gel + photolithography** |
| Optical MEMS (micro-mirrors) | **Surface micromachining on SOI** |
| Polymer microfluidics | **SU-8 photolithography** or **soft lithography (PDMS)** |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Rayleigh Resolution Limit (Detailed)

We need to derive the minimum resolvable feature size in optical lithography.

**Setup:** Two point sources imaged through a circular lens of numerical aperture $NA$. By the Rayleigh criterion, two points are **just resolved** when the central maximum of one Airy pattern coincides with the first minimum of the other.

**Step 1:** The radius of the first dark ring of the Airy pattern formed by a circular aperture of radius $a$ is given by the diffraction condition:

$$
\sin\theta \approx \theta = \frac{1.22\,\lambda}{2a} = \frac{0.61\,\lambda}{a}
$$

**Step 2:** The numerical aperture of a lens is defined as:

$$
NA = n \sin\theta_{\text{max}} \approx \dfrac{a}{f}
$$

where $n$ is the refractive index of the medium (≈ 1 for air) and $f$ is the focal length. Substituting:

$$
\sin\theta_{\text{max}} \approx \dfrac{0.61\,\lambda}{f \cdot NA} \cdot f = \dfrac{0.61\,\lambda}{NA}
$$

**Step 3:** Setting $R$ equal to the half-pitch (line + space) and solving:

$$
R = k_1 \cdot \frac{\lambda}{NA}
$$

where $k_1 = 0.61$ for ideal Rayleigh imaging, but in practice the process factor $k_1$ lies between **0.4 and 0.7** due to resist processing, lens aberrations, and illumination effects.

**Step 4:** Depth of focus derivation: For a defocus of $\pm \dfrac{\lambda}{2\,NA^2}$, the wavefront error remains below the Rayleigh tolerance. Thus:

$$
DOF = k_2 \cdot \frac{\lambda}{NA^2}
$$

with $k_2 \approx 1$ for production processes.

**Step 5 — Numerical Illustration (KTU exam favorite):**
For i-line lithography with $\lambda = 365$ nm, $NA = 0.65$, $k_1 = 0.5$:

$$
R = 0.5 \cdot \dfrac{365 \text{ nm}}{0.65} \approx 281 \text{ nm}
$$

The corresponding depth of focus:

$$
DOF = 1 \cdot \dfrac{365 \text{ nm}}{(0.65)^2} \approx 864 \text{ nm}
$$

**Conclusion:** Higher NA gives finer resolution but a shallower depth of focus — a fundamental trade-off in lithography tool design.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — V-Groove Geometry in KOH Etching of (100) Silicon

**Setup:** A rectangular opening of width $w$ is aligned along the $\langle 110 \rangle$ direction on a (100) silicon wafer and etched in KOH.

**Step 1:** The {111} planes form an angle of $\theta = 54.74°$ with the (100) surface.

**Step 2:** When the {111} planes from opposite sidewalls meet at the bottom, etching self-terminates and a V-shaped groove is formed.

**Step 3 — Depth Calculation:**
The groove depth $d$ is related to the opening width $w$ by:

$$
\tan(54.74°) = \frac{w/2}{d} \implies d = \frac{w/2}{\tan(54.74°)}
$$

**Step 4:** Numerically, $\tan(54.74°) \approx 1.414 = \sqrt{2}$, hence:

$$
d = \dfrac{w}{2\sqrt{2}} = 0.3536\, w
$$

**Step 5 — Numerical Example:**
If $w = 100$ µm, then $d = 35.36$ µm.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** The etched depth is strictly **fixed** by the mask opening width — independent of etch time (because {111} acts as an etch stop). This makes KOH wet etching highly reproducible for V-groove fabrication in MEMS.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Selectivity and Anisotropy Definitions

**Step 1:** Define the etch rate of film $A$ (the layer we want to etch) and substrate $B$ (the layer we want to preserve):

$$
ER_A = \frac{\Delta z_A}{\Delta t}, \qquad ER_B = \frac{\Delta z_B}{\Delta t}
$$

**Step 2:** Selectivity is the ratio:

$$
S_e = \frac{ER_A}{ER_B}
$$

**Step 3:** Anisotropy factor — for vertical etch rate $ER_v$ and lateral etch rate $ER_l$:

$$
AF = 1 - \frac{ER_l}{ER_v}
$$

**Step 4 — Limiting cases:**
- $AF = 1$ → perfectly anisotropic (vertical only) → **DRIE ideal case**.
- $AF = 0$ → perfectly isotropic (equal in all directions) → **pure chemical wet etch**.

**Step 5 — Profile Angle:**

$$
\theta = \arctan\!\left(\dfrac{ER_v}{ER_l}\right) = \arctan\!\left(\dfrac{1}{1 - AF}\right)
$$

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Cantilever Spring Constant for a Free-Standing MEMS Beam

A common MEMS structural element is a cantilever released by surface micromachining. Its mechanical behavior is governed by:

**Step 1:** For an end-loaded cantilever of length $L$, width $w$, thickness $t$, and Young's modulus $E$, the deflection under end force $F$ is:

$$
\delta = \dfrac{4 F L^3}{E w t^3}
$$

**Step 2:** Spring constant (slope of $F$ vs $\delta$):

$$
k = \dfrac{F}{\delta} = \dfrac{E w t^3}{4 L^3}
$$

**Step 3 — Numerical Example (Polysilicon cantilever):**
- $E = 160$ GPa, $w = 2$ µm, $t = 2$ µm, $L = 100$ µm.

$$
k = \dfrac{(160 \times 10^9)(2 \times 10^{-6})(2 \times 10^{-6})^3}{4 (100 \times 10^{-6})^3}
$$

$$
k = \dfrac{(1.6 \times 10^{11})(2 \times 10^{-6})(8 \times 10^{-18})}{4 \times 10^{-12}}
$$

$$
k = \dfrac{2.56 \times 10^{-12}}{4 \times 10^{-12}} = 0.64 \text{ N/m}
$$

**Step 4 — Resonance Frequency:**

$$
f_0 = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{k}{m_{\text{eff}}}}
$$

where $m_{\text{eff}} = 0.24 \rho L w t$ for a cantilever (with $\rho = 2330$ kg/m³ for polysilicon):

$$
m_{\text{eff}} = 0.24 \times 2330 \times 100 \times 10^{-6} \times 2 \times 10^{-6} \times 2 \times 10^{-6} \approx 2.24 \times 10^{-13} \text{ kg}
$$

$$
f_0 = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{0.64}{2.24 \times 10^{-13}}} \approx 268 \text{ kHz}
$$

**Engineering insight:** The spring constant scales as $t^3/L^3$ — thickness control in deposition (e.g., LPCVD polysilicon) is the single most important variable for MEMS resonator design.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Comparing Micromachining Routes

| Parameter | Wet Bulk (KOH) | Dry Bulk (DRIE) | Surface Micromachining | LIGA |
|-----------|----------------|------------------|------------------------|------|
| **Etch profile** | V-groove, 54.74° walls | Vertical, high AR | Defined by sacrificial undercut | Vertical, very high AR |
| **Max depth** | ~500 µm | ~800 µm | ~10 µm typical | >1000 µm |
| **Aspect ratio** | Low (1:1 to 5:1) | High (up to 50:1) | Moderate (10:1 typical) | Very high (100:1) |
| **Selectivity** | High to SiO₂ mask | Moderate | Tunable via chemistry | High |
| **Mask compatibility** | SiO₂, SiN | Photoresist, SiO₂ | Photoresist, hard mask | PMMA |
| **Surface roughness** | Smooth (nm level) | Scalloped (Bosch) | Smooth | Smooth sidewalls |
| **Cost** | Low | Moderate | Moderate | Very high |
| **Common device** | Pressure sensor membrane | Through-wafer vias | Accelerometer proof mass | Micro-gears, nozzles |

---

## 3.6 Summary of Critical Numerical Relationships

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Five 'Must-Know' Order-of-Magnitude Facts for KTU Exam:**
> 1. Si {111} etch rate in KOH at 80°C ≈ **1 µm/min**; {100} ≈ 200× faster.
> 2. Typical LPCVD polysilicon deposition rate ≈ **10–100 nm/min** at 620°C.
> 3. Typical DRIE etch rate (Bosch) ≈ **1–3 µm/min** for silicon.
> 4. SU-8 thickness range achievable in a single coat ≈ **1 µm to 2 mm**.
> 5. Minimum practical photolithography resolution in a university cleanroom ≈ **1–2 µm** (contact printing).

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1 — Complete MEMS Process Flow (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Silicon Wafer] --> B[Step 1: RCA Cleaning]
    B --> C[Step 2: Thermal Oxidation SiO2 Growth]
    C --> D[Step 3: Photoresist Spin Coating]
    D --> E[Step 4: Soft Bake]
    E --> F[Step 5: UV Exposure Through Mask]
    F --> G[Step 6: Development in TMAH]
    G --> H[Step 7: Hard Bake]
    H --> I[Step 8: Etch Process Selection]
    I --> I1[Wet Etch KOH]
    I --> I2[Dry Etch RIE DRIE]
    I --> I3[Surface Micromachining HF Release]
    I1 --> J[Step 9: Strip Resist]
    I2 --> J
    I3 --> J
    J --> K[Step 10: Inspect and Measure]
    K --> L{Multiple Layers Needed?}
    L -- Yes --> M[Loop back to Deposition]
    L -- No --> N[Step 11: Dicing and Packaging]
    N --> O[Final MEMS Device]
```

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Silicon Substrate] --> A2[Step 1 Deposit Sacrificial Layer SiO2]
    A2 --> A3[Step 2 Deposit Structural Layer Polysilicon]
    A3 --> A4[Step 3 Lithography and Etch Polysilicon]
    A4 --> A5[Step 4 Isotropic Etch Sacrificial Layer using HF]
    A5 --> A6[Step 5 Critical Point Drying]
    A6 --> A7[Free Standing MEMS Structure Released]
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram 4 — Bulk Micromachining Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[Silicon Wafer Choice] --> P1[100 Wafer]
    P --> P2[110 Wafer]
    P1 --> Q[Target Geometry]
    Q --> Q1[V Groove Pyramid Membrane]
    Q --> Q2[Vertical High AR Sidewall]
    Q1 --> R1[KOH TMAH Wet Etch Anisotropic]
    Q2 --> R2[DRIE Bosch Process Dry Etch]
    P2 --> S[Microfluidic Channels Vertical Sidewalls]
    S --> S1[KOH Wet Etch or DRIE]
```

## 4.5 ASCII Cross-Section Schematic — Surface Micromachined Cantilever

```
         BEFORE RELEASE                    AFTER HF RELEASE
   =========================         =========================
   |    Polysilicon (2 µm)  |        |    Polysilicon (2 µm)  |
   |=========================|        |=========================|
   |   SiO2 Sacrificial (1µm)|   -->  |                         |  (sacrificial
   |=========================|        |=========================|   etched away)
   |    Silicon Substrate   |        |    Silicon Substrate    |
   =========================         =========================
                                  ↑
                                  |
                                  Free-standing cantilever
                                  (gap = original SiO2 thickness)
```

## 4.6 ASCII Schematic — Bosch DRIE Process Cycle (Time-Multiplexed)

```
   Cycle Time ──────────────────────────────►
   
   Etch Step (SF6)       : ████████  (5–10 sec)   →  isotropically removes ~1 µm Si
   Passivation (C4F8)    : ████████  (3–5 sec)    →  deposits Teflon-like polymer on sidewalls
   Etch Step (SF6)       : ████████                →  ions remove polymer from bottom only
   Passivation (C4F8)    : ████████                →  sidewalls re-protected
        .  .  .
        (repeats 50–500 times to reach desired depth)
   
   Result → vertical sidewalls + characteristic "scallops" of ~50–200 nm
```
