# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern (ESE):** Part A questions test direct recall of definitions, classifications, and short reasoning. Each is worth **3 marks**, answer length = **80–120 words**.

---

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – June 2024 (Model Paper)]

**Differentiate between Physical Vapor Deposition (PVD) and Chemical Vapor Deposition (CVD) with two suitable examples each. [3 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | PVD | CVD |
|--------|-----|-----|
| **Mechanism** | Physical — atoms ejected from source by evaporation or ion bombardment | Chemical — gas precursors react on heated substrate |
| **Temperature** | Lower (room temp to ~400°C) | Higher (typically 600–900°C) |
| **Pressure** | High vacuum (10⁻⁵ to 10⁻⁷ Torr) | Atmospheric to low pressure |
| **Step coverage** | Poor (line-of-sight) | Excellent (conformal) |
| **Examples** | Thermal evaporation, Sputtering, e-beam evaporation | LPCVD polysilicon, PECVD SiO₂, APCVD |

PVD uses **physical transfer of atoms** (e.g., evaporation of Al at ~1200°C), whereas CVD uses **chemical reactions** (e.g., SiH₄ → Si + 2H₂ at ~700°C).

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 (Model Paper)]

**Explain the working of a positive and a negative photoresist with one example each. [3 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

In **positive photoresist** (e.g., AZ-5214E, S1813), the regions exposed to UV light undergo **chain scission** (breaking of polymer chains) and become **more soluble** in the developer (e.g., TMAH). Hence the **final pattern matches the mask openings** — light-exposed areas are washed away.

In **negative photoresist** (e.g., SU-8, NR7-2070), the exposed regions undergo **photo-initiated cross-linking**, becoming **insoluble** in the developer. Therefore the **final pattern is the inverse of the mask openings** — exposed material remains on the wafer.

SU-8 is a thick negative resist (up to 2 mm) widely used for high-aspect-ratio MEMS structures such as micro-fluidic channels and bio-MEMS components.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern (ESE Module 3):** Part B questions carry **14 marks** with **internal choice**. Each question has two sub-parts of **7 marks each**. Expect either descriptive diagrams or numerical computations.

---

### QUESTION A — [KTU University Exam – Sample QP, Module 3]

**(a)** With a neat cross-sectional diagram, explain the **eight steps of the photolithography process**. State two examples of positive and negative photoresist. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** Define **selectivity** and **anisotropy factor** in etching. A silicon wafer is etched with $ER_v = 1.2$ µm/min (vertical) and $ER_l = 0.06$ µm/min (lateral). Compute the (i) anisotropy factor, (ii) profile angle, (iii) etch depth in 8 minutes. **[7 Marks]**

---

#### Model Solution to Q-A(a):

**Step 1 — Substrate Cleaning:** RCA clean or piranha clean removes organic, ionic, and metallic contaminants; ensures adhesion of subsequent films.

**Step 2 — Dehydration Bake:** 150–200°C for 30 min removes adsorbed moisture.

**Step 3 — Priming:** HMDS (hexamethyldisilazane) vapor is applied to promote resist adhesion on the Si/SiO₂ surface.

**Step 4 — Spin Coating:** Liquid photoresist is dispensed and the wafer is spun at 1000–6000 rpm for 30 sec, producing a uniform film of thickness 0.5–2 µm.

**Step 5 — Soft Bake:** 90–110°C for 60 sec on a hot plate to drive off solvent and densify the resist.

**Step 6 — Exposure:** Wafer is aligned with the photomask and exposed to UV light through a stepper or mask aligner (wavelengths 365 nm i-line to 436 nm g-line).

**Step 7 — Development:** The wafer is immersed in a developer (TMAH for positive, xylene/acetone for negative) to dissolve exposed (positive) or unexposed (negative) regions.

**Step 8 — Hard Bake:** 120–180°C for 30 min — improves etch resistance and removes residual solvent.

**Cross-Sectional Diagram:**

```
  Mask                     Photoresist after Development
  ┌──────┐                 ┌──────────┐
  │░░░░░░│   UV light  →   │ Si       │
  │░░░░░░│ ────────────►  │ Substrate│
  └──────┘                 └──────────┘
   (opaque                  (resist pattern
    region)                  transferred)
```

**Examples:**
- **Positive:** AZ-series, S1813, SPR-955
- **Negative:** SU-8, NR7-2070, AZ-nLOF

---

#### Model Solution to Q-A(b):

**Definitions:**

**Selectivity $S_e$** of an etch process is the ratio of etch rate of the target film to that of the underlying mask/substrate:

$$
S_e = \frac{ER_{\text{film}}}{ER_{\text{substrate}}}
$$

High selectivity means the etch stops sharply at the underlying layer, protecting it.

**Anisotropy Factor $AF$** quantifies directionality of an etch process:

$$
AF = 1 - \frac{ER_l}{ER_v}
$$

where $ER_v$ is the vertical etch rate (downward) and $ER_l$ is the lateral etch rate (sideways). $AF = 1$ means perfectly anisotropic (vertical only); $AF = 0$ means perfectly isotropic (equal in all directions).

**Given:** $ER_v = 1.2$ µm/min, $ER_l = 0.06$ µm/min.

**(i) Anisotropy factor:**

$$
AF = 1 - \dfrac{0.06}{1.2} = 1 - 0.05 = 0.95
$$

This corresponds to **highly anisotropic** etching (almost vertical walls), typical of DRIE.

**(ii) Profile angle:**

$$
\theta = \arctan\!\left(\dfrac{ER_v}{ER_l}\right) = \arctan\!\left(\dfrac{1.2}{0.06}\right) = \arctan(20) \approx 87.14°
$$

Sidewalls are nearly vertical — only 2.86° off-vertical.

**(iii) Etch depth in 8 minutes:**

$$
d = ER_v \cdot t = 1.2 \,\tfrac{\mu\text{m}}{\text{min}} \times 8 \,\text{min} = 9.6 \,\mu\text{m}
$$

---

### QUESTION B (Internal Choice) — [KTU University Exam – Sample QP, Module 3]

**(a)** Explain the **LIGA process** with its three stages. List two advantages and two disadvantages. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** In KOH wet etching of a (100) silicon wafer, a square mask opening of side $w = 80$ µm is aligned along the $\langle 110 \rangle$ direction. Compute (i) the depth of the etched pit, (ii) the etch time if the {100} etch rate is 1.2 µm/min, and (iii) the width of the undercut if the etch is allowed to continue for 5 extra minutes beyond V-groove completion (lateral etch rate = 0.012 µm/min). **[7 Marks]**

---

#### Model Solution to Q-B(a):

**LIGA** (German: **Li**thographie, **G**alvanoformung, **A**bformung) is a microfabrication process used to create **tall, high-aspect-ratio microstructures** of metals, polymers, or ceramics.

**Stage 1 — Lithography (L):**
A thick layer of polymethyl methacrylate (PMMA) resist (height 100 µm to 1000 µm) is exposed to **synchrotron X-rays** ($\lambda \approx 0.2$ to $5$ nm) through a patterned mask. The exposed regions undergo chain scission and become soluble in a developer (e.g., GG developer). This produces a deep, high-aspect-ratio PMMA mold.

**Stage 2 — Electroplating (Galvanoformung, I):**
The developed PMMA mold is placed in an electroplating bath. A conductive seed layer is present at the bottom. **Metal ions** (e.g., Ni²⁺, Au⁺, Cu²⁺) are reduced and deposited into the mold cavities, building up a solid metal replica.

**Stage 3 — Molding (Abformung, G):**
The PMMA is dissolved, leaving a free-standing metal mold insert. This metal mold is then used to mass-produce polymer parts by **injection molding** or hot embossing, enabling low-cost replication.

**Cross-Sectional Schematic of LIGA:**

```
   X-ray ────►    ┌──────────────────┐
                   │     PMMA         │ ← Thick resist (mm scale)
                   │  ┌──────────┐    │
                   │  │  Metal   │    │ ← Electroplated
                   │  │  (Ni)    │    │
                   │  └──────────┘    │
                   │  Seed layer      │
                   └──────────────────┘
                       Substrate
```

**Advantages:**
1. Extremely **high aspect ratios** (up to 100:1) and structural heights >1 mm.
2. Excellent **sidewall verticality** and surface finish (sub-µm roughness).

**Disadvantages:**
1. Requires **expensive synchrotron X-ray source** — not accessible to most labs.
2. **Multi-step, slow, and costly** for low-volume production.

---

#### Model Solution to Q-B(b):

**Given:** (100) silicon wafer, square mask opening of side $w = 80$ µm aligned along $\langle 110 \rangle$, {100} ER = 1.2 µm/min, lateral ER = 0.012 µm/min, angle between (100) and (111) = 54.74°.

**(i) Etch depth of V-groove pit:**

$$
d = \dfrac{w}{2\tan(54.74°)} = \dfrac{80 \,\mu\text{m}}{2 \times 1.414} = \dfrac{80}{2.828} \approx 28.28 \,\mu\text{m}
$$

**(ii) Etch time to complete V-groove:**

$$
t_{\text{V-groove}} = \dfrac{d}{ER_{\{100\}}} = \dfrac{28.28 \,\mu\text{m}}{1.2 \,\mu\text{m/min}} \approx 23.57 \,\text{min}
$$

**(iii) Undercut width after 5 extra minutes:**

The V-groove is complete in 23.57 min. If etching continues for 5 more min, the {111} planes act as etch stop — vertical etching essentially halts, but **lateral etching of the {111} planes themselves** continues (slowly).

$$
\text{Lateral undercut} = ER_l \cdot \Delta t = 0.012 \,\tfrac{\mu\text{m}}{\text{min}} \times 5 \,\text{min} = 0.06 \,\mu\text{m} = 60 \,\text{nm}
$$

> **Engineering interpretation:** The undercut is negligible compared to the original pit width — confirming that {111} planes are highly effective etch stops and KOH etching is reproducible for V-groove fabrication.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU VALUATION PITFALLS — Where students typically lose marks on this topic:**
> 1. **Confusing positive vs negative resist behavior** — remember: in positive resist, **exposed** regions are dissolved; in negative resist, **exposed** regions remain. Mix this up and lose 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Forgetting to specify units** in the formula sheet. Always write $\mu$m/min, not just "µm". Examiners deduct 0.5 marks per missing unit.
> 3. **Skipping the diagram** in photolithography questions. A labeled cross-section showing mask, UV light, photoresist, and substrate is **mandatory** — at least 2 marks are reserved for the diagram.
> 4. **Mixing up anisotropy and selectivity.** Anisotropy = directionality (vertical vs lateral); Selectivity = material selectivity (target vs mask). These are independent properties.
> 5. **Not mentioning stiction** in surface micromachining answers — this is a **favorite viva question** and worth 1 mark if included naturally.
> 6. **In numerical problems, computing V-groove depth with the wrong formula** — use $d = w/(2\sqrt{2})$, NOT $d = w\sqrt{2}$. The pit is **shallower** than the mask width.
> 7. **Omitting the 54.74° angle** in KOH-related answers — it is the **defining** geometric constant for anisotropic Si etching in KOH and must always be cited.
> 8. **In LIGA answers, not writing the German expansion** — examiners reward students who write "Lithographie, Galvanoformung, Abformung" rather than just "LIGA".

---

## 5.4 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-DENSITY RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST — KTU PCMUT602 MODULE 3**

**📌 DEPOSITION**
- PVD = physical, includes **evaporation** and **sputtering**; uses vacuum.
- Sputtering uses Ar⁺ bombardment; reactive sputtering adds O₂/N₂ for oxides/nitrides.
- CVD = chemical reaction of gas precursors; **LPCVD** for high-temp polysilicon, **PECVD** for low-temp SiO₂.
- Electroplating gives thick (µm–mm) metal films; key step in LIGA.

**📌 LITHOGRAPHY**
- 8 steps: **clean → prime → spin → soft bake → expose → develop → hard bake → inspect**.
- Positive resist: exposed = soluble (AZ-series). Negative resist: exposed = insoluble (SU-8).
- Rayleigh resolution $R = k_1 \lambda / NA$; DOF $= k_2 \lambda / NA^2$.
- EBL = 10 nm resolution, slow. X-ray = LIGA-grade. NIL = sub-10 nm at low cost.

**📌 MICROMACHINING**
- **Bulk:** etches the substrate itself — KOH wet etch is anisotropic, {111} is etch-stop.
- **DRIE / Bosch:** alternating SF₆ etch + C₄F₈ passivation → vertical walls, AR up to 50:1.
- **Surface micromachining:** uses a **sacrificial layer** (SiO₂/PSG) released by HF; risk of **stiction**.
- **LIGA:** X-ray lithography + electroplating + injection molding → very tall, AR > 100:1 structures.

**📌 NUMERICAL CONSTANTS TO MEMORIZE**
- {111}/{100} etch rate ratio in KOH ≈ **1:200** (anisotropy).
- (100) wafer V-groove wall angle = **54.74°**.
- V-groove depth $d = w/(2\sqrt{2}) = 0.354\, w$.
- Standard atmospheric pressure = **760 Torr = 101.325 kPa**.
- Standard wafer diameter = **75 / 100 / 150 / 200 / 300 mm**.
- Standard Si wafer thickness = **525 µm (4-inch) to 775 µm (12-inch)**.

**📌 KEY RELATIONSHIPS**
- Anisotropy factor: $AF = 1 - ER_l/ER_v$.
- Selectivity: $S_e = ER_{\text{film}}/ER_{\text{substrate}}$.
- Profile angle: $\theta = \arctan(ER_v / ER_l)$.
- Spring constant of cantilever: $k = E w t^3 / (4 L^3)$ — thickness is dominant.

**📌 ENGINEERING JUDGMENT**
- Choose **wet etch** for V-grooves, membranes, low-cost bulk features.
- Choose **DRIE** for vertical through-wafer features and high-AR MEMS.
- Choose **surface micromachining** for integrated MEMS with on-chip electronics.
- Choose **LIGA** only when extreme AR or metal microstructures are required.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Deep Reactive Ion Etching (DRIE) and LIGA Processes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMUT602 - MECHATRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Deep Reactive Ion Etching (DRIE) and LIGA Processes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Deep Reactive Ion Etching (DRIE)

**Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard):**
Deep Reactive Ion Etching (DRIE) is an advanced, highly **anisotropic** dry-etching technique used in microfabrication to produce **high-aspect-ratio** microstructures in silicon and other substrates. It is essentially a specialized variant of Reactive Ion Etching (RIE) in which the chemical and physical etch mechanisms are deliberately decoupled (or operated under cryogenic conditions) to achieve etch depths of several hundreds of micrometres with near-vertical sidewalls.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** DRIE is the *workhorse technology* behind MEMS devices, **Through-Silicon Vias (TSV)** for 3-D chip stacking, micro-fluidic channels, and comb-drive structures. KTU examiners frequently test the distinction between the **Bosch process** and **Cryogenic DRIE**.

### 1.1.1 Conceptual Analogy — "The Sculptor with a Mask and a Shield"

Imagine a stone sculptor (the plasma) trying to carve a deep, vertical groove into a marble block (the silicon wafer). If the sculptor only uses a chisel (pure RIE), the chisel hits the bottom but also chips the sidewalls, leaving a V-shaped trench — this is **isotropic / low-aspect-ratio etching**.

In the **Bosch process**, the sculptor alternates between two tools every few seconds:
1. A **chisel** (SF₆ plasma) that cuts straight down.
2. A **spray-can of Teflon** (C₄F₈ polymer) that coats the *entire* freshly cut surface with a thin protective layer.

When the cycle repeats, ions from the next SF₆ pulse bombard the trench bottom vertically (because the plasma sheath directs ions normal to the surface), removing the Teflon only at the bottom. The sidewalls remain shielded. The result is an almost perfectly **rectangular, deep trench** — this is the essence of DRIE.

In the **Cryogenic process**, the entire silicon wafer is chilled to about **-110 °C** so that the etch products freeze on the sidewalls, naturally forming a SiO₂F passivation layer during a continuous SF₆ + O₂ etch — a single, uninterrupted process with smoother sidewalls.

---

## 1.2 LIGA Process

**Formal Definition:**
**LIGA** is a German acronym for **LI**thographie (deep X-ray lithography), **GA**lvanoformung (electroforming/electroplating), and **A**bformung (molding). It is a micro-fabrication technology that uses **synchrotron X-ray radiation** to expose a thick **PMMA** (polymethyl methacrylate) resist, followed by electroplating of a metal (typically **nickel**) into the developed resist cavities, and finally polymer injection molding to mass-produce metallic or plastic micro-parts.

> [!NOTE]
> **LIGA Process Signature Feature:** LIGA uniquely delivers **micron-level lateral precision** combined with **structural heights of several hundred micrometres to millimetres** — i.e., aspect ratios of **> 100 : 1**, which is essentially impossible with conventional UV lithography or even DRIE.

### 1.2.1 Conceptual Analogy — "The X-Ray Cookie Cutter"

Think of pouring a thick slab of soft butter (PMMA resist) on a metal plate (a conductive seed layer). Now press a very powerful, sharp shadow — say from a high-intensity X-ray beam filtered through a stencil mask — onto the butter. The exposed butter polymerises/hardens differently (or in negative-tone resists, becomes soluble). A chemical bath washes out the unexposed regions, leaving a deep, perfectly vertical **mould cavity**. Pour liquid metal (nickel) into this cavity, let it solidify, and peel off the butter — you have a precise metallic micro-structure. Finally, use this metal master as a die to stamp out thousands of plastic replicas via injection moulding. That is LIGA in one sentence.

---

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why these two processes are grouped together in Module 3:**
> Both are *high-aspect-ratio microfabrication* (HARM) technologies — they are the two principal pillars for manufacturing 3-D MEMS and micro-mechanical parts. DRIE is **silicon-centric and subtractive**; LIGA is **polymer/metal-centric and additive/replicative**. KTU expects students to clearly differentiate them and to associate each with its correct application domain.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The DRIE Bosch Process — Cyclic Operating Principle

The **Bosch process** (patented by Robert Bosch GmbH in the 1990s) is a *time-multiplexed*, multi-step cyclic etch in a single plasma chamber.

### 2.1.1 Step-by-Step Cyclic Logic

**Step 1 — Passivation (Duration ≈ 5 – 10 s)**
- Gas: **C₄F₈** (octafluorocyclobutane).
- A low-energy plasma polymerises C₄F₈ into a **fluorocarbon film** (~ 10 – 50 nm) that deposits conformally on **all** exposed surfaces (bottom and sidewalls).
- This film is chemically similar to Teflon (PTFE) and is **resistant to chemical attack by fluorine radicals**.

**Step 2 — Etch (Duration ≈ 5 – 12 s)**
- Gas: **SF₆** (sulphur hexafluoride).
- The plasma generates **F• radicals** (isotropic chemical etchant) and **SF₃⁺ ions** (vertical physical etchant).
- The high **DC bias** (~ 50 – 200 V) accelerates ions *normal* to the wafer. They **sputter the fluorocarbon off the bottom** but cannot reach the sidewalls (they travel vertically).
- Once the bottom is clean, fluorine radicals react with the exposed silicon:
$$\text{Si} + 4\text{F} \rightarrow \text{SiF}_4 \,(\uparrow \text{ volatile})$$
- The sidewalls remain protected by the fluorocarbon.

**Step 3 — Repeat**
- Each cycle removes a "scallop" of silicon (typically 0.5 – 2 µm of vertical etch per cycle). After 100 – 200 cycles, etch depths of 200 – 500 µm are routinely achieved.

### 2.1.2 The "Scallop" Side-Effect
Because the passivation is periodically stripped at the bottom, the sidewall of a Bosch-etched trench has a characteristic **wave-like ripple** of amplitude ~ 50 – 200 nm called the **scallop**. Reducing cycle time reduces scallop amplitude but slows the net etch rate.

### 2.1.3 Cryogenic DRIE — Continuous Process

| Parameter | Bosch DRIE | Cryogenic DRIE |
|---|---|---|
| Temperature | 0 to 20 °C | **-110 °C to -130 °C** |
| Gases | SF₆ (etch) + C₄F₈ (passivation) | SF₆ + O₂ (continuous) |
| Passivation mechanism | C₄F₈ polymer (cyclic) | SiOₓFᵧ from O + Si at low T (continuous) |
| Sidewall roughness | Scalloped (~100 nm) | Smooth (< 20 nm) |
| Selectivity to SiO₂ | ~ 100 : 1 | ~ 30 : 1 |
| Max aspect ratio | ~ 50 : 1 | ~ 20 : 1 |
| Throughput | High (cyclic) | Lower (slow etch rate) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why oxygen at low temperature works:** At -110 °C, the reaction between Si and O radicals to form SiO₂ is favoured kinetically on the *sidewalls* (where ion bombardment is absent) but suppressed at the *bottom* (where ion bombardment breaks the oxide). Hence passivation is *self-aligned* to vertical surfaces — an elegant single-step process.

### 2.1.4 Aspect-Ratio Dependent Etching (ARDE / RIE Lag)

The etch rate is **not constant**; it drops as the trench gets deeper relative to its width. The semi-empirical model used in industry is:

$$E_{R}(AR) = \frac{E_{R,0}}{1 + k \cdot AR}$$

Where:
- $E_R$ = local etch rate at aspect ratio $AR$
- $E_{R,0}$ = open-area etch rate (top of the trench)
- $AR = D/W$ = etch depth / trench width
- $k$ = process-dependent ARDE constant (typically $0.02 - 0.1$)

This phenomenon is called **RIE Lag** or **Aspect Ratio Dependent Etching** and is critical for designing through-silicon vias of varying widths on the same wafer.

---

## 2.2 LIGA Process — Detailed Step-by-Step Logic

### 2.2.1 The Three (or Four) Sub-Steps of LIGA

**Stage 1 — Deep X-Ray Lithography**
- A thick layer of **PMMA** (100 – 1000 µm) is bonded to a conductive substrate (e.g., Cr/Au/Cu seed layer).
- The resist is exposed to **synchrotron X-rays** (typical photon energy 0.2 – 2 keV, wavelength $\lambda \approx 0.2 - 1$ nm).
- A high-precision **X-ray mask** (made of a thin SiC / Si membrane with Au absorber patterns) defines the exposed regions.
- In *negative-tone* operation: exposed PMMA becomes **cross-linked** and insoluble; unexposed PMMA is dissolved in a developer (GG developer: 2-(2-butoxyethoxy)ethanol + morpholine + ethanolamine).
- In *positive-tone* operation: exposed PMMA chain-scissions and dissolves; unexposed PMMA remains.

**Stage 2 — Electroforming (Galvanoformung)**
- The developed PMMA mould (with cavities down to the conductive seed) is immersed in an **electroplating bath**.
- **Nickel** is the most common electroplated metal (sometimes Ni-Fe, Cu, or Au for special applications).
- Ni²⁺ ions in a sulphamate bath are reduced and deposited selectively in the mould cavities:
$$\text{Ni}^{2+} + 2e^{-} \rightarrow \text{Ni} \,(\text{solid})$$
- Plating continues until the cavities are **completely filled** and a solid metal "master" is built up.

**Stage 3 — Mould Stripping**
- The PMMA is dissolved (e.g., in hot NMP or acetone), leaving a free-standing **metallic micro-structure** (often called the "LIGA part" or "primary structure").

**Stage 4 — Replica Moulding (Abformung) [Optional]**
- The metal master is used as an **injection-mould insert** to mass-produce polymer (e.g., POM, PMMA, COC) replicas by **hot embossing** or **injection moulding**.
- This is the step that makes LIGA **economically viable**: one expensive synchrotron exposure produces millions of cheap polymer parts.

### 2.2.2 Why Synchrotron X-Rays?
- Conventional UV lithography is limited to depths of ~ 1 – 2 µm because of resist absorption and diffraction.
- X-rays have **very short wavelength** ($\lambda \ll 1$ µm) → diffraction negligible → **sidewalls vertical to within 0.1 – 1 µm** over hundreds of microns.
- Synchrotrons deliver **high collimated flux** → short exposure times even for 1-mm-thick resists.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table is the single most important revision sheet for any DRIE / LIGA numerical in the KTU exam.**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Symbols & Units | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Aspect Ratio (AR) | $AR = D / W$ | $D$ = etch depth (µm), $W$ = feature width (µm) | Vertical-vs-lateral geometry metric |
| 2 | Etch Rate | $E_R = D / t$ | $D$ in µm/min, $t$ = time (min) | How fast material is removed |
| 3 | Selectivity | $S = E_{R,\text{material}} / E_{R,\text{mask}}$ | Dimensionless | Ability to preserve mask layer |
| 4 | Anisotropy | $A = 1 - (E_{R,\text{lateral}} / E_{R,\text{vertical}})$ | Dimensionless, $0 \le A \le 1$ | $A = 1$ → perfectly vertical |
| 5 | ARDE Model | $E_R(AR) = E_{R,0} / (1 + k \cdot AR)$ | $k$ empirical | Etch rate vs. aspect ratio |
| 6 | X-ray Attenuation (Beer-Lambert) | $I(x) = I_0 \cdot e^{-\mu \rho x}$ | $\mu$ = mass absorption coefficient (cm²/g), $\rho$ = density (g/cm³), $x$ = depth (cm) | Intensity surviving at depth $x$ |
| 7 | X-ray Penetration Depth (1/e) | $\delta = 1 / (\mu \rho)$ | cm | Characteristic exposure depth |
| 8 | Electroplating Mass (Faraday's Law) | $m = \dfrac{M \cdot I \cdot t}{n \cdot F}$ | $M$ = molar mass (g/mol), $I$ = current (A), $t$ = time (s), $n$ = electrons transferred, $F = 96485$ C/mol | Mass of Ni deposited |
| 9 | Electroplating Thickness | $d = \dfrac{M \cdot J \cdot t}{n \cdot F \cdot \rho_m}$ | $J$ = current density (A/m²), $\rho_m$ = metal density (kg/m³) | Height of plated structure |
| 10 | Plating Time Required | $t_{\text{plate}} = \dfrac{d \cdot n \cdot F \cdot \rho_m}{M \cdot J}$ | s | Time to fill the LIGA mould |
| 11 | Bosch Cycle Net Etch Depth | $D_{\text{cycle}} = N \cdot (E_{R,\text{etch}} \cdot t_{\text{etch}} - E_{R,\text{dep}} \cdot t_{\text{dep}})$ | $N$ = number of cycles | Net Si removed per cycle |
| 12 | Synchrotron Dose Required | $D_{\text{exp}} = E \cdot t_{\text{exp}}$ | J/cm³ | Energy absorbed per unit volume |

> **Pitfall Avoidance Note:** Note the use of `\vert` and `\mid` in formulae like $\vert I \vert$ — KTU scripts often lose marks due to table-rendering errors when a student writes `|I|` inside a markdown table. Always use LaTeX vertical bars.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Aspect-Ratio Dependent Etch Rate in DRIE

We derive the semi-empirical ARDE relation starting from a mass-transport argument.

### Assumptions
1. Etchant species (F radicals) must diffuse from the bulk plasma down to the trench bottom.
2. The diffusion path is roughly cylindrical, length $D$ (depth), radius $W/2$.
3. The consumption of F radicals at the bottom obeys first-order kinetics with rate constant $k_s$.

### Derivation

Let $J_{\text{top}}$ be the flux of F radicals arriving at the trench opening. By Fick's first law, the flux reaching the bottom is:

$$J_{\text{bottom}} = \frac{D_{\text{diff}} \cdot (C_{\text{top}} - C_{\text{bottom}})}{D}$$

where $D_{\text{diff}}$ is the diffusivity of F radicals in the gas mixture. The etch rate at the bottom is proportional to the bottom flux:

$$E_R = \kappa \cdot J_{\text{bottom}}$$

with $\kappa$ = reactive sticking coefficient. Rearranging:

$$E_R = \frac{\kappa D_{\text{diff}} C_{\text{top}}}{D + (\kappa D_{\text{diff}} / k_s)}$$

Define $D_{\text{open}} = \kappa k_s D_{\text{diff}} C_{\text{top}} / (k_s D)$ for a wide-open area. Substituting and dividing numerator/denominator by $k_s D$:

$$E_R = \frac{E_{R,0}}{1 + (k_s D / D_{\text{diff}})}$$

Since aspect ratio $AR = D/W$ and for a slit/trench the Knudsen-like constant is $k = k_s W / D_{\text{diff}}$, we finally obtain:

$$
\boxed{\,E_R(AR) = \frac{E_{R,0}}{1 + k \cdot AR}\,}
$$

### Physical Interpretation
- $AR = 0$ (top, unetched) → $E_R = E_{R,0}$ (highest rate).
- As $AR \to \infty$ (very deep, narrow) → $E_R \to 0$ (etchant cannot reach bottom).
- **Knudsen transport** of neutrals and **ion-shadowing** in the sheath are the dominant physical mechanisms.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — X-Ray Penetration Depth in PMMA for LIGA

The synchrotron X-ray intensity decays exponentially inside the PMMA resist (Beer-Lambert law).

### Starting Equation
By definition of linear absorption coefficient $\alpha = \mu \rho$:

$$I(x) = I_0 \, e^{-\alpha x}$$

The **1/e penetration depth** $\delta$ is the depth at which $I = I_0 / e$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{I_0}{e} &= I_0 \, e^{-\alpha \delta} \\[4pt]
\Rightarrow \quad -\alpha \delta &= -1 \\[4pt]
\Rightarrow \quad \delta &= \frac{1}{\alpha} = \frac{1}{\mu \rho}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Numerical Example (PMMA at 1 keV)
For PMMA, $\rho = 1.18$ g/cm³ and at 1 keV photon energy, $\mu \approx 1600$ cm²/g (carbon-dominated). Hence:

$$
\delta = \frac{1}{1600 \times 1.18} \; \text{cm} \approx 5.3 \times 10^{-4} \text{ cm} = 5.3 \; \mu\text{m}
$$

> **Interpretation:** At 1 keV, the X-ray is fully absorbed in tens of microns. For 500-µm-thick PMMA, the photon energy must be raised to **2 – 5 keV** so that $\delta$ exceeds the resist thickness, ensuring **uniform exposure from top to bottom**. This is why LIGA uses *hard* X-rays.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Electroplating Time to Fill a LIGA Cavity

Faraday's first law of electrolysis relates charge passed to mass deposited:

$$m = \frac{M \, Q}{n \, F} = \frac{M \, I \, t}{n \, F}$$

For a uniform deposit of height $d$ covering an area $A$:

$$m = \rho_m \, A \, d$$

Equating the two expressions and solving for $t$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\rho_m \, A \, d &= \frac{M \, I \, t}{n \, F} \\[4pt]
\Rightarrow \quad t_{\text{plate}} &= \frac{\rho_m \, A \, d \, n \, F}{M \, I} = \frac{\rho_m \, d \, n \, F}{M \, J}
\end{aligned}
$$

where $J = I / A$ is the **current density** (A/m²).

### Numerical Worked Example
To fill a $200 \, \mu\text{m} \times 200 \, \mu\text{m}$ square cavity that is $300 \, \mu\text{m}$ deep with nickel:

- $\rho_m (\text{Ni}) = 8900$ kg/m³
- $M(\text{Ni}) = 0.0587$ kg/mol
- $n = 2$ (Ni²⁺)
- $F = 96485$ C/mol
- $J = 5 \, \text{A/dm}^2 = 500 \, \text{A/m}^2$ (typical for Ni sulphamate)
- $d = 300 \, \mu\text{m} = 3 \times 10^{-4}$ m

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_{\text{plate}} &= \frac{8900 \times 3 \times 10^{-4} \times 2 \times 96485}{0.0587 \times 500} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{8.9 \times 10^3 \times 3 \times 10^{-4} \times 1.93 \times 10^5}}{0.0587 \times 5 \times 10^2} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{5.15 \times 10^5}{29.35} \approx 17550 \; \text{s} \approx 4.9 \; \text{hours}
\end{aligned}
$$

So a typical LIGA Ni plating run takes **~ 5 hours per wafer** — illustrating why replication (Stage 4) is essential for economic mass production.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — DRIE vs LIGA

| Parameter | DRIE (Bosch) | LIGA | Engineering Implication |
|---|---|---|---|
| Substrate | Silicon, glass, SiC | PMMA + any metal | DRIE is Si-centric; LIGA is material-agnostic |
| Max AR | ~ 50 : 1 | **> 100 : 1** | LIGA wins for ultra-tall structures |
| Sidewall verticality | 90° ± 1° | 90° ± 0.01° | LIGA gives near-perfect 90° |
| Sidewall roughness | 50 – 200 nm (scallop) | < 50 nm (smooth) | LIGA preferred for optical surfaces |
| Min feature size | ~ 0.5 µm | ~ 0.2 µm | Comparable |
| Throughput | High (batch wafer) | **Low** (synchrotron) | DRIE for volume; LIGA for masters |
| Cost per wafer | Moderate | Very high | LIGA used for *moulds* that replicate cheap parts |
| Material freedom | Mostly Si | Any metal + polymer | LIGA is far more flexible |
| Equipment | ICP/RIE plasma tool | Synchrotron + plating line | Synchrotron access is the bottleneck |
| Aspect-ratio control | ARDE effect severe | Negligible | LIGA gives uniform depth |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Bosch DRIE Cyclic Process

The following Mermaid diagram maps the **closed-loop feedback architecture** of the Bosch DRIE process — emphasising the alternating chemistry between passivation (C₄F₈) and etch (SF₆) gases, and how plasma parameters form a control loop that determines scallop amplitude, etch rate, and selectivity.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Wafer Load into ICP/RIE Chamber] --> B[Set Baseline Pressure and Temperature]
    B --> C{Tool Waits for Cycle Trigger}
    C -->|Cycle n starts| D[Passivation Phase: C4F8 Gas Inlet ON]
    D --> D1[Low Bias Plasma Polymerises C4F8 to ~30 nm Fluorocarbon Layer]
    D1 --> D2[Gas Switch: C4F8 OFF, SF6 ON]
    D2 --> E[Etch Phase: SF6 Plasma Ignited]
    E --> E1[High DC Bias Accelerates SF3+ Ions Normal to Wafer]
    E1 --> E2[Ions Sputter Fluorocarbon Only from Trench Bottom]
    E2 --> E3[F Radicals React with Si to Form SiF4 Volatile]
    E3 --> E4[Sidewall Fluorocarbon Remains Intact]
    E4 --> F[End of Cycle n: Etch Depth Increment dN]
    F --> G{Target Depth Reached?}
    G -->|No| H[Increment n: n = n + 1]
    H --> C
    G -->|Yes| I[Process Complete: Unload Wafer]
    I --> J[Final Inspection: SEM Cross-Section, Profilometry]

    style A fill:#fde2e2,stroke:#cc0000
    style I fill:#d2f8d2,stroke:#006600
    style J fill:#cfe8ff,stroke:#003366
    style D1 fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#cc9900
    style E3 fill:#ffe0b3,stroke:#cc6600
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The **outer loop** `C → D → D1 → D2 → E → E1 → E2 → E3 → E4 → F` is *one* Bosch cycle.
- The **inner cycle counter** `H → C` repeats the process until the desired depth is reached.
- The `style` directives highlight (red) wafer entry, (green) process exit, (yellow) passivation chemistry, and (orange) the Si-etch reaction.

---

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — LIGA Process Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Substrate Prep: Deposit Cr-Au-Cu Seed Layer on Si Wafer] --> P2[Spin/Bond Thick PMMA Resist 100-1000 um]
    P2 --> P3[Soft Bake to Remove Solvent]
    P3 --> P4[Synchrotron X-Ray Exposure through X-Ray Mask]
    P4 --> P5[Development in GG Developer: Dissolves Unexposed PMMA]
    P5 --> P6[Mould Cavities Formed Down to Seed Layer]
    P6 --> P7[Electroplating: Ni2+ Reduced to Ni in Sulphamate Bath]
    P7 --> P8[Continue Plating Until Cavities Fully Filled]
    P8 --> P9[PMMA Strip: Dissolve in Hot NMP or Acetone]
    P9 --> P10[Free-Standing Ni Micro-Structure: LIGA Primary Part]
    P10 --> P11{Reproduction Route?}
    P11 -->|Yes, mass production| P12[Mount Ni Master in Injection Moulding Tool]
    P12 --> P13[Hot Emboss / Injection Mould Polymer Pellets]
    P13 --> P14[Demould: Replica Plastic Micro-Part]
    P11 -->|No, single part| P15[Direct Use: Micro-Gear, Sensor, Optical Element]

    style P1 fill:#e6f0ff,stroke:#003399
    style P4 fill:#ffe6e6,stroke:#990000
    style P7 fill:#e6ffe6,stroke:#006600
    style P10 fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#996600
    style P14 fill:#f0e6ff,stroke:#330099
    style P15 fill:#ffe6f0,stroke:#990066
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The **left half** (`P1 → P10`) is the *primary* LIGA process — produces a single metal master.
- The **right half** (`P11 → P14`) is the *replication* stage — converts one master into millions of cheap polymer parts.
- The diamond `P11` is the **decision node** that determines economic route.

---

## 4.3 Parameter Coupling Diagram — Why DRIE and LIGA Behave Differently

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1[Aspect Ratio AR = D / W] --> Q2{Process Type}
    Q2 -->|DRIE Bosch| Q3[ARDE Effect: ER decreases as AR increases]
    Q2 -->|LIGA| Q4[Synchrotron X-Ray: delta large, AR independent]
    Q3 --> Q5[Etch Depth Limited by Knudsen Diffusion]
    Q4 --> Q6[Etch Depth Limited only by X-Ray Absorption]
    Q5 --> Q7[Max AR ~ 50:1]
    Q6 --> Q8[Max AR > 100:1]
    Q7 --> Q9[TSV, MEMS, Microfluidics]
    Q8 --> Q10[Micro-Gears, Optics, Replicated Parts]

    style Q3 fill:#ffd6d6,stroke:#990000
    style Q4 fill:#d6f5d6,stroke:#006600
    style Q7 fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#cc0000
    style Q8 fill:#ccffcc,stroke:#006600
```

This shows the **fundamental physics divergence**: DRIE is mass-transport limited (so AR matters), while LIGA is photon-absorption limited (so AR barely matters, making it ideal for ultra-tall structures).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Mark Distribution Reminder (2024 Scheme):** As per the KTU B.Tech ESE pattern, Module 3 carries ~ 20 % weightage. Expect *one full 14-mark question* (with sub-parts) OR *two 7-mark sub-parts combined* from this module. Definition questions (3 marks) from DRIE/LIGA are very common in **Part A**.

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Similar Pattern]**
**Define DRIE. With a neat block diagram, explain the Bosch process of DRIE.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
1. **Definition (1 mark):** DRIE is a highly anisotropic dry-etching process used to etch deep, narrow features (aspect ratio > 20:1) into silicon substrates by alternating between an etching plasma and a passivation plasma inside a single chamber.
2. **Block diagram / flow (1 mark):**
   - **Step 1 — Passivation:** C₄F₈ plasma deposits a ~30 nm fluorocarbon (Teflon-like) polymer on all surfaces.
   - **Step 2 — Etch:** SF₆ plasma produces F• radicals and energetic SF₃⁺ ions; ions sputter the polymer only at the trench bottom (vertical ion trajectory), exposing silicon. F• then volatilises Si as SiF₄.
3. **Outcome (1 mark):** Repeating this cycle 100 – 200 times produces trenches 200 – 500 µm deep with vertical sidewalls (90° ± 1°). This is the *workhorse* process for TSV and MEMS.

---

### Question A2
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Similar Pattern]**
**What is LIGA? List and briefly explain its main process steps.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
1. **Definition (1 mark):** LIGA is a German acronym for **Li**thographie (X-ray), **Ga**lvanofor**m**ung (electroforming), and **A**bformung (moulding) — a microfabrication technology for ultra-high aspect ratio ( > 100 : 1) metallic and polymer micro-structures.
2. **Process steps (2 marks):**
   - **X-ray lithography:** Synchrotron X-rays expose thick PMMA resist through an X-ray mask; unexposed regions are dissolved in developer.
   - **Electroforming:** The developed mould is filled with metal (typically Ni) via electroplating until a solid metal master is formed.
   - **Mould stripping & replication:** PMMA is dissolved; the Ni master is used as an insert for injection moulding to mass-produce polymer replicas.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> **ESE Pattern:** You will be given a choice between **Question A** and **Question B**. Both carry **14 marks**, broken into sub-parts (typically 7 + 7). Each sub-part tests one Course Outcome at Bloom's L3 (Apply) or L4 (Analyse) level.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — DRIE-Focused

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Explain in detail the Deep Reactive Ion Etching (DRIE) process with a clear description of the Bosch process. Discuss its key process parameters and applications.**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Introduction to DRIE (1 mark):**
DRIE is an advanced extension of Reactive Ion Etching (RIE) that achieves etch depths of several hundred micrometres with high aspect ratios (> 30:1) and near-vertical sidewalls. The two main variants are the **Bosch (time-multiplexed)** process and the **Cryogenic (continuous)** process.

**(ii) Bosch Process — Step-by-Step (3 marks):**

| Step | Gas | Plasma Condition | Purpose | Time |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Passivation | C₄F₈ | Low bias (~ 5 – 20 V), high pressure | Conformal deposition of ~ 10 – 50 nm fluorocarbon polymer on all surfaces | 5 – 10 s |
| Etch | SF₆ | High bias (~ 50 – 200 V), low pressure | Generate F• radicals + SF₃⁺ ions; sputter polymer from bottom only; react Si + F → SiF₄↑ | 5 – 12 s |

The cycle is repeated 50 – 200 times. Net etch per cycle ≈ 0.5 – 2 µm.

**(iii) Key Process Parameters (2 marks):**
- **Plasma source:** Inductively Coupled Plasma (ICP) — provides independent control of ion density and ion energy.
- **DC bias voltage:** Controls ion energy → determines bottom sputter rate.
- **Gas flow ratio (SF₆ / C₄F₈):** Controls the etch-to-passivation balance.
- **Chamber pressure:** 10 – 50 mTorr typical.
- **Cycle times ($t_{\text{etch}}, t_{\text{dep}}$):** Trade-off between scallop amplitude and etch rate.
- **Aspect Ratio Dependent Etching (ARDE):** $E_R = E_{R,0} / (1 + k \cdot AR)$ — etch rate falls as the trench deepens.

**(iv) Applications (1 mark):**
- **Through-Silicon Vias (TSV)** for 3-D integrated circuits and CMOS image sensors.
- **MEMS devices** — accelerometers, gyroscopes, microphones.
- **Microfluidic channels** for lab-on-a-chip.
- **RF MEMS** — high-Q inductors, capacitive switches.

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Compare and contrast the Bosch process and the Cryogenic process of DRIE. Under what circumstances would you prefer the Cryogenic process over the Bosch process?**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Tabular Comparison (4 marks):**

| Parameter | Bosch DRIE | Cryogenic DRIE |
|---|---|---|
| Process type | Time-multiplexed (cyclic) | Continuous |
| Substrate temperature | 0 °C to + 20 °C | -110 °C to -130 °C |
| Gases | SF₆ + C₄F₈ | SF₆ + O₂ |
| Passivation mechanism | Fluorocarbon polymer (C₄F₈) | SiOₓFᵧ formed by O + Si at low T |
| Sidewall roughness | Scalloped, ~ 50 – 200 nm | Smooth, < 20 nm |
| Selectivity to SiO₂ mask | ~ 100 : 1 | ~ 30 : 1 |
| Aspect ratio capability | Up to 50 : 1 | Up to 20 : 1 |
| Etch rate | High (2 – 10 µm/min) | Lower (1 – 3 µm/min) |
| Process complexity | Multi-step, complex gas control | Single-step, requires cryo-cooling |

**(ii) When to Prefer Cryogenic (2 marks):**
- When **sidewall smoothness is critical** — e.g., optical MEMS, micro-fluidic channels that must not disturb laminar flow, or sharp biomedical needles.
- When **scallop-induced stress concentration** is unacceptable — e.g., high-reliability mechanical springs.
- When a **single-step process is preferred** for process simplicity, even at the cost of selectivity.

**(iii) When to Prefer Bosch (1 mark):**
- When **high aspect ratio** (> 20:1), **high etch rate**, and **high mask selectivity** are required — e.g., TSVs for 3-D ICs, deep trench capacitors.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — LIGA-Focused

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Explain the LIGA process in detail. Discuss the role of synchrotron radiation, the materials used, and the typical process parameters. List at least four major applications.**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Process Definition (1 mark):**
LIGA = **Li**thographie + **Ga**lvanofor**m**ung + **A**bformung (X-ray lithography + electroforming + moulding). It is a *high-aspect-ratio micro-fabrication* technology producing metallic and polymer micro-structures with aspect ratios exceeding 100:1 and sidewall verticality better than 0.1°.

**(ii) Step-by-Step Process (3 marks):**
1. **Substrate preparation:** A conductive seed layer (Cr 30 nm / Au 200 nm / Cu 1 µm) is sputtered/evaporated on a silicon or stainless-steel substrate. Thick PMMA (100 – 1000 µm) is bonded/solution-cast on top and soft-baked.
2. **X-ray exposure:** The resist is exposed to synchrotron X-rays (photon energy 0.5 – 5 keV, wavelength 0.2 – 2 nm) through an X-ray mask (SiC membrane with Au absorber). In *negative-tone* PMMA, exposed regions cross-link and become insoluble; unexposed regions remain soluble.
3. **Development:** The wafer is immersed in a GG developer (2-(2-butoxyethoxy)ethanol + morpholine + 2-aminoethanol); the unexposed PMMA is dissolved, leaving deep vertical mould cavities down to the seed layer.
4. **Electroforming (Galvanoformung):** The wafer is immersed in a Ni-sulphamate plating bath ($T \approx 50$ °C, pH 3.5 – 4.2, $J \approx 1 - 10$ A/dm²). Ni²⁺ is reduced at the seed layer and grows upward, filling the cavities. Plating continues until the cavities are completely filled and a solid over-plate is formed.
5. **PMMA stripping:** The PMMA is dissolved in hot N-methyl-2-pyrrolidone (NMP) at ~ 80 °C, leaving a free-standing Ni micro-structure.
6. **Replication (Abformung) [Optional]:** The Ni master is mounted as an insert in an injection-moulding machine. Polymer pellets (POM, PMMA, COC) are melted and forced into the mould cavity, solidifying into thousands of identical polymer replicas.

**(iii) Role of Synchrotron Radiation (1 mark):**
- **Short wavelength** (sub-nm) → negligible diffraction → sidewalls vertical over mm depths.
- **High collimation** → parallel exposure of deep resist.
- **High flux** → short exposure times (minutes) even for mm-thick resists.
- **Tunable photon energy** → can match penetration depth $\delta = 1 / (\mu \rho)$ to resist thickness.

**(iv) Materials and Parameters (1 mark):**
- Resist: PMMA (positive or negative tone).
- Mask: SiC or Si membrane (2 µm) + Au absorber (10 – 20 µm).
- Plating metal: Ni (most common), also Ni-Fe (alloy), Cu, Au.
- Resist thickness: 100 – 1000 µm.
- Typical AR: 50 – 100:1.
- Sidewall roughness: < 50 nm.

**(v) Applications (1 mark):**
1. **Micro-gears and micro-turbines** (watch industry, micro-pumps).
2. **Micro-optical elements** (Fresnel lenses, fibre-alignment structures).
3. **Micro-sensors** (accelerometers, pressure sensors).
4. **Micro-fluidic devices** and **biomedical implants** (hearing aids, dental components).
5. **X-ray masks and zone plates** themselves.

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Estimate the time required to electroplate a nickel LIGA structure 300 µm deep over an area of 1 cm × 1 cm, using a current density of 5 A/dm². Given: atomic mass of Ni = 58.7 g/mol, density of Ni = 8.9 g/cm³, n = 2, F = 96 485 C/mol.**

**Model Solution (Full Step-by-Step):**

**Given Data:**
- Depth to be plated: $d = 300 \, \mu\text{m} = 3 \times 10^{-4}$ m = 0.03 cm
- Area: $A = 1 \text{ cm} \times 1 \text{ cm} = 1 \text{ cm}^2$
- Current density: $J = 5 \, \text{A/dm}^2 = 5 \times 10^{-2} \text{ A/cm}^2 = 0.05 \, \text{ A/cm}^2$
- $M(\text{Ni}) = 58.7$ g/mol
- $\rho_m(\text{Ni}) = 8.9$ g/cm³
- $n = 2$ (Ni²⁺ → Ni)
- $F = 96\,485$ C/mol

**Step 1 — Compute Volume of Ni to be Deposited (1 mark):**
$$
V = A \times d = 1 \, \text{cm}^2 \times 0.03 \, \text{cm} = 0.03 \, \text{cm}^3
$$

**Step 2 — Compute Mass of Ni Required (1 mark):**
$$
m = \rho_m \times V = 8.9 \times 0.03 = 0.267 \, \text{g}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply Faraday's First Law (2 marks):**
$$
m = \frac{M \cdot I \cdot t}{n \cdot F} \quad \Rightarrow \quad t = \frac{m \cdot n \cdot F}{M \cdot I}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the Current $I$ (1 mark):**
$$
I = J \times A = 0.05 \, \text{A/cm}^2 \times 1 \, \text{cm}^2 = 0.05 \, \text{A}
$$

**Step 5 — Substitute and Solve (2 marks):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
t &= \frac{0.267 \times 2 \times 96485}{58.7 \times 0.05} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{51523.0}{2.935} \\[4pt]
&\approx 17\,556 \; \text{s} \approx 4.88 \; \text{hours} \approx 4 \, \text{h} \, 53 \, \text{min}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answer (in box):**
$$
\boxed{\,t_{\text{plate}} \approx 17\,556 \; \text{s} \approx 4.88 \; \text{hours}\,}
$$

**Conclusion / Engineering Comment:** This long plating time is precisely why LIGA is used for *making one mould*, and the subsequent hot-emboss / injection-moulding step is essential for cost-effective mass production.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> 1. **Unit trap in J:** $J = 5 \, \text{A/dm}^2$ must be converted to **A/m²** (or A/cm²) before being used. Forgetting the $10^4$ cm²/m² conversion is the most common error → wrong answer by factor 100.
> 2. **Sign and n-value:** Ni is reduced from Ni²⁺, so $n = 2$ — using $n = 1$ gives a wrong answer by factor 2.
> 3. **Density vs. resistivity confusion:** In plating problems, use **density** (g/cm³), not electrical resistivity.
> 4. **DRIE vs LIGA confusion in essay questions:** A frequent mistake is to mention the *Bosch process* in the LIGA answer or vice versa. KTU examiners deduct **2 full marks** for such cross-blunder.
> 5. **Forgetting the replication step:** If asked to "explain LIGA with applications", a 14-mark answer that omits *Abformung* (moulding) loses 3 marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Memorise this before every KTU exam.**

### DRIE — Quick Facts
- DRIE = **D**eep **R**eactive **I**on **E**tching — achieves aspect ratios > 30:1 in silicon.
- Two variants: **Bosch (cyclic, C₄F₈ + SF₆)** and **Cryogenic (continuous, SF₆ + O₂ at -110 °C)**.
- Bosch produces **scalloped sidewalls** (50 – 200 nm roughness); Cryogenic gives **smooth sidewalls** (< 20 nm).
- Bosch is the **industry standard for TSV**; Cryogenic is preferred for **optical / biomedical MEMS**.
- Key formula: $E_R(AR) = E_{R,0} / (1 + k \cdot AR)$ — this is the **ARDE / RIE Lag** effect.
- Selectivity of Bosch to SiO₂ mask: **~ 100 : 1**; Cryogenic: **~ 30 : 1**.
- Typical etch rate: **2 – 10 µm/min** (Bosch), **1 – 3 µm/min** (Cryogenic).

### LIGA — Quick Facts
- LIGA = **Li**thographie + **Ga**lvanofor**m**ung + **A**bformung.
- Lithography source: **Synchrotron X-rays** (0.5 – 5 keV).
- Resist: **PMMA** (positive or negative tone, thickness 100 – 1000 µm).
- Plating metal: **Nickel** (most common); bath = **Ni-sulphamate**.
- X-ray penetration depth: $\delta = 1 / (\mu \rho)$.
- Faraday's law: $m = M I t / (n F)$.
- Aspect ratio: **> 100 : 1** (highest among all micro-fab technologies).
- Key economic insight: **Synchrotron exposure is expensive → use Ni master as mould insert for mass production** of polymer replicas.
- Sidewall verticality: **< 0.1°** — the best in class.
- Major applications: **micro-gears, micro-optics, micro-sensors, biomedical implants**.

### Common Confusions to Avoid
- DRIE is **subtractive** (etches Si away); LIGA is **additive** (builds metal into a mould).
- DRIE works on **silicon**; LIGA works on **PMMA + any metal**.
- DRIE is **plasma-based**; LIGA is **lithography + electrochemistry + replication**.
- Bosch uses **two gas chemistries** alternately; Cryogenic uses **one chemistry continuously**.
- The **"A" in LIGA is for Abformung** = moulding — *do not skip this step in essays*.

### Numericals to Practise
1. Compute electroplating time for a given Ni structure — use Faraday's law with proper unit conversion of $J$.
2. Compute X-ray penetration depth $\delta$ for PMMA at a given photon energy — use $\delta = 1 / (\mu \rho)$.
3. Compute aspect ratio and predict ARDE etch rate drop.
4. Convert between µm/min and nm/s for DRIE etch rates.
5. Compute scallop amplitude from cycle time and passivation film thickness.

> **Last-Word Mantra for KTU Exam:**
> *"DRIE carves silicon vertically with alternating chemistry; LIGA builds metal vertically with X-rays and electrochemistry — both conquer the third dimension, but along opposite routes."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Principle, Fabrication and Working of MEMS Based Pressure Sensor, Accelerometer and Gyroscope
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMUT602 - MECHATRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Principle, Fabrication and Working of MEMS Based Pressure Sensor, Accelerometer and Gyroscope

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Introduction to MEMS (Micro-Electro-Mechanical Systems)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition:** MEMS (Micro-Electro-Mechanical Systems) are integrated systems that combine mechanical elements, sensors, actuators, and electronics on a common silicon substrate through microfabrication technology. They typically range in size from **1 µm to 1 mm** and merge computational electronics with mechanical functionality at the microscale.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 Scheme):** MEMS bridges the gap between the physical world (mechanical) and the digital world (electronics). For Mechatronics students, MEMS devices are the *sensory frontier* of any modern automated system — from smartphones to automotive ABS.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Imagine a **suspension bridge** shrunk down a million times. The bridge deck (a tiny cantilever beam) bends slightly when wind (pressure/acceleration/rotation) pushes against it. At the bottom of the bridge, a small electrical gauge (a piezoresistor) detects the bend and converts it into a measurable signal. That is essentially how a MEMS sensor works — mechanical motion → electrical signal.

---

## 1.2 MEMS-Based Pressure Sensor

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition:** A MEMS pressure sensor is a microfabricated device that converts an applied **pressure** into a measurable electrical signal (typically voltage) by detecting the deflection of a thin silicon diaphragm using **piezoresistive**, **capacitive**, or **piezoelectric** transduction principles.

### Working Principle (Intuition)
Picture a **thin drum membrane** stretched over a hollow cavity. When you blow on the membrane from one side, it bulges inward by a microscopic amount. Tiny strain gauges embedded in the membrane stretch or compress — this resistance change is converted to voltage.

- **Diaphragm:** Thin silicon membrane (typical thickness: **5–50 µm**)
- **Cavity:** Etched reference chamber (often sealed at vacuum for absolute pressure measurement)
- **Transduction:** Piezoresistive / Capacitive

### Transduction Methods at a Glance

| Method | Physical Quantity Changed | Output |
|---|---|---|
| **Piezoresistive** | Resistance ($\Delta R/R$) | Voltage (Wheatstone bridge) |
| **Capacitive** | Gap distance ($\Delta C/C$) | Frequency / Voltage |
| **Piezoelectric** | Charge generation ($q = dF$) | Voltage pulse |

---

## 1.3 MEMS-Based Accelerometer

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition:** A MEMS accelerometer is a micro-scale inertial sensor that measures **proper acceleration** (the rate of change of velocity relative to free-fall) along one or more axes using the displacement of a **proof mass** suspended by compliant flexures relative to a fixed frame.

### Working Principle (Intuition)
Think of a small **lead ball resting on a soft spring inside a box**. When you push the box forward, the ball lags behind due to inertia, compressing the spring. The amount of compression tells you exactly how hard you pushed (= acceleration). MEMS accelerometers replicate this with a silicon proof mass hanging from micron-scale flexure springs.

- **Proof Mass:** Typically made of polysilicon or single-crystal silicon (mass: **~1–10 µg**)
- **Flexures (Springs):** Torsional/folded beam suspensions providing restoring force
- **Damping:** Squeezed-film gas damping (often air or vacuum)
- **Sensitivity:** Often in the range of **0.1 to 10 V/g** (where g = 9.81 m/s²)

---

## 1.4 MEMS-Based Gyroscope (Angular Rate Sensor)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition:** A MEMS gyroscope (angular rate sensor) is a vibrating-structure device that measures **angular velocity (°/s or rad/s)** by exploiting the **Coriolis Effect** — the apparent force experienced by a mass moving in a rotating reference frame.

### Working Principle (Intuition)
Imagine **spinning a playground roundabout while walking outward**. The faster the roundabout spins, the more your body wants to be flung sideways — that sideways push is the *Coriolis force*. A MEMS gyroscope vibrates a tiny mass back and forth, then applies Coriolis logic: the harder the chip rotates, the larger the perpendicular vibration amplitude, which is read out electrically.

- **Drive Mode:** Primary mass vibrates at resonance (~ **20–30 kHz**)
- **Sense Mode:** Coriolis-induced secondary vibration is detected
- **Bandwidth:** Typically **>50 Hz** for consumer, **>1 kHz** for tactical grade
- **Output:** Voltage proportional to angular rate

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction:** An accelerometer measures **linear acceleration** (translation), while a gyroscope measures **rotational rate** (spin). They are complementary — together they form an **Inertial Measurement Unit (IMU)**.

---

## 1.5 Common Fabrication Materials & Substrates

> [!NOTE]
> **Substrate:** Single-crystal silicon wafer (**<100> or <111> orientation**)
> **Structural Layer:** Polysilicon / silicon nitride / silicon dioxide
> **Doping Species:** Boron (p-type), Phosphorus / Arsenic (n-type)
> **Etchants:** KOH, TMAH (anisotropic wet); DRIE (Bosch process — anisotropic dry)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Generic Architecture of a MEMS Sensor

Every MEMS sensor consists of **four functional blocks**:

1. **Mechanical Structure** — Proof mass, diaphragm, flexure, tethers
2. **Transduction Mechanism** — Piezoresistor, capacitor, piezoelectric crystal
3. **Signal Conditioning Circuit** — Amplifier, bridge, ADC
4. **Packaging** — Hermetic seal, pressure port, bond wires

---

## 2.2 Pressure Sensor — Theory of Operation

### 2.2.1 Piezoresistive Pressure Sensor

A thin circular silicon diaphragm of radius $a$ and thickness $h$ is clamped at its edges. Pressure $P$ applied from below causes out-of-plane deflection $w(r)$.

**Governing Differential Equation (small deflection theory):**

$$\nabla^{4} w(r) = \frac{P}{D}$$

where $D$ is the flexural rigidity:

$$D = \frac{E \, h^{3}}{12 \left(1 - \nu^{2}\right)}$$

**Maximum deflection (at center, $r=0$):**

$$w_{\max} = \frac{P \, a^{4}}{64 \, D}$$

**Maximum stress (at edge, $r=a$):**

$$\sigma_{\max} = \frac{3 P \, a^{2}}{4 h^{2}}$$

The piezoresistors are strategically placed near the diaphragm edges (where stress is maximum) and connected in a **Wheatstone bridge** to maximize sensitivity.

### 2.2.2 Capacitive Pressure Sensor

A pressure-induced change in diaphragm position changes the gap $d$ of a parallel-plate capacitor:

$$C = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \, \varepsilon_r \, A}{d}$$

$$\frac{\Delta C}{C_0} = \frac{\Delta d}{d_0 + \Delta d} \approx \frac{\Delta d}{d_0} \quad \text{(for } \Delta d \ll d_0\text{)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Capacitive sensors are more sensitive and consume less power than piezoresistive ones, but require complex readout ASICs.**

---

## 2.3 Accelerometer — Theory of Operation

### 2.3.1 Governing Equation (1-DOF Mass-Spring-Damper)

The proof mass $m$ is suspended by a spring of stiffness $k$ with viscous damping coefficient $b$.

$$m \, \ddot{x} + b \, \dot{x} + k \, x = m \, a_{\text{ext}}$$

**Natural frequency:**

$$\omega_n = \sqrt{\frac{k}{m}}$$

**Damping ratio:**

$$\zeta = \frac{b}{2 \sqrt{k m}}$$

**Static Sensitivity (DC response):**

$$S = \frac{\Delta x}{a_{\text{ext}}} = \frac{m}{k} = \frac{1}{\omega_n^{2}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Design Trade-off:** Higher sensitivity ($\omega_n$ small) conflicts with wider bandwidth ($\omega_n$ large). Typical consumer MEMS accelerometers operate at $\omega_n \approx 1.5$–$5$ kHz.

### 2.3.2 Capacitive Readout (Differential Capacitor)

The proof mass forms a differential capacitor with two fixed electrodes at gaps $d_1$ and $d_2$:

$$C_1 = \frac{\varepsilon_0 A}{d_0 + \Delta x}, \quad C_2 = \frac{\varepsilon_0 A}{d_0 - \Delta x}$$

$$\Delta C = C_1 - C_2 = \frac{2 \, \varepsilon_0 A \, \Delta x}{d_0^{2} - (\Delta x)^{2}}$$

$$\frac{\Delta C}{C_1 + C_2} = \frac{\Delta x}{d_0} \quad \text{(linear approximation)}$$

---

## 2.4 Gyroscope — Theory of Operation (Coriolis Effect)

### 2.4.1 Coriolis Force Derivation

A mass moving with velocity $\vec{v}$ in a frame rotating with angular velocity $\vec{\Omega}$ experiences a Coriolis acceleration:

$$\vec{a}_{\text{Cor}} = 2 \, \vec{\Omega} \times \vec{v}$$

Coriolis force on the moving mass:

$$\vec{F}_{\text{Cor}} = -2 \, m \, \vec{\Omega} \times \vec{v}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Practical Implementation:** The drive-mode velocity is sinusoidal: $v_x(t) = V_0 \cos(\omega_d t)$. This produces a Coriolis force in the *y-direction*: $F_y = -2 m \Omega V_0 \cos(\omega_d t)$, which drives the sense-mode mass at the same frequency as the drive.

### 2.4.2 Sense Mode Equation of Motion

The sense mode is itself a second-order mass-spring-damper system:

$$m_s \, \ddot{y} + b_s \, \dot{y} + k_s \, y = F_{\text{Cor}} = 2 \, m \, \Omega \, V_0 \cos(\omega_d t)$$

**Steady-state amplitude of sense-mode displacement:**

$$Y = \frac{2 \, m \, \Omega \, V_0}{\sqrt{(k_s - m_s \omega_d^{2})^{2} + (b_s \omega_d)^{2}}}$$

**At resonance** ($\omega_d = \omega_{n,s}$), amplification factor $Q_s = \frac{1}{2\zeta_s}$ boosts the signal by 20–100×.

**Scale Factor:**

$$SF = \frac{V_{\text{out}}}{\Omega} \propto \frac{2 \, m \, V_0 \, Q_s}{k_s \, C_{\text{conv}}}$$

where $C_{\text{conv}}$ is the displacement-to-voltage conversion gain (V/m).

### 2.4.3 Mechanical Coupling (Mode-Matching)

$$\eta = \frac{\Delta \omega}{\omega_0} = \frac{\vert \omega_d - \omega_s \vert}{\omega_0}$$

To maximize Coriolis coupling, ideally $\omega_d = \omega_s$ (perfect mode-matching, $\eta = 0$). In practice, $\eta < 1\%$ for high-performance gyroscopes.

---

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Tip:** Memorize the boxed constants and core equations. KTU examiners frequently ask for derivations of $\omega_n$, $w_{\max}$, or $\vec{F}_{\text{Cor}}$.

| # | Concept | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Diaphragm max deflection | $w_{\max} = \dfrac{P a^{4}}{64 D}$ | $P$ = pressure, $a$ = radius, $D$ = flexural rigidity | m |
| 2 | Flexural rigidity | $D = \dfrac{E h^{3}}{12(1-\nu^{2})}$ | $E$ = Young's modulus, $h$ = thickness, $\nu$ = Poisson's ratio | N·m |
| 3 | Diaphragm max stress | $\sigma_{\max} = \dfrac{3 P a^{2}}{4 h^{2}}$ | — | Pa |
| 4 | Piezoresistor sensitivity | $\dfrac{\Delta R}{R} = \pi_L \, \sigma$ | $\pi_L$ = longitudinal piezoresistive coefficient | — |
| 5 | Parallel-plate capacitance | $C = \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d}$ | $A$ = area, $d$ = gap | F |
| 6 | Capacitor sensitivity | $\dfrac{\Delta C}{C} \approx \dfrac{\Delta d}{d_0}$ | small-signal approximation | — |
| 7 | Accelerometer natural frequency | $\omega_n = \sqrt{\dfrac{k}{m}}$ | $k$ = spring stiffness, $m$ = proof mass | rad/s |
| 8 | Accelerometer static sensitivity | $S = \dfrac{1}{\omega_n^{2}} = \dfrac{m}{k}$ | — | s²/rad |
| 9 | Differential capacitance | $\Delta C = \dfrac{2 \varepsilon_0 A \, \Delta x}{d_0^{2} - (\Delta x)^{2}}$ | $\Delta x$ = proof mass displacement | F |
| 10 | Coriolis force | $\vec{F}_{\text{Cor}} = -2 m \, \vec{\Omega} \times \vec{v}$ | $\vec{\Omega}$ = angular vel., $\vec{v}$ = drive velocity | N |
| 11 | Coriolis acceleration magnitude | $a_{\text{Cor}} = 2 \, \Omega \, v \sin \theta$ | $\theta$ = angle between $\vec{\Omega}$ and $\vec{v}$ | m/s² |
| 12 | Sense mode resonance amplitude | $Y = \dfrac{2 m \Omega V_0}{m_s \sqrt{(2\zeta_s \omega_n)^{2} + (\omega_d - \omega_n)^{2}}}$ | $V_0$ = drive velocity amplitude | m |
| 13 | Quality factor | $Q = \dfrac{1}{2\zeta} = \dfrac{\sqrt{k m}}{b}$ | — | dimensionless |
| 14 | Brownian noise (accel.) | $a_n = \sqrt{\dfrac{4 k_B T \omega_n}{m Q}}$ | $k_B$ = Boltzmann const., $T$ = temperature | m/s²/√Hz |
| 15 | Gauge factor (piezoresistor) | $G = \dfrac{\Delta R/R}{\varepsilon}$ | $\varepsilon$ = strain | dimensionless |

> [!NOTE]
> **Useful Physical Constants (for KTU numericals):**
> - Silicon Young's Modulus: $E_{Si} = \mathbf{1.69 \times 10^{11} \ Pa}$
> - Silicon Poisson's Ratio: $\nu_{Si} = \mathbf{0.28}$
> - Density of Silicon: $\rho_{Si} = \mathbf{2330 \ kg/m^{3}}$
> - Permittivity of free space: $\varepsilon_0 = \mathbf{8.854 \times 10^{-12} \ F/m}$
> - Boltzmann constant: $k_B = \mathbf{1.38 \times 10^{-23} \ J/K}$
> - Standard gravity: $g = \mathbf{9.81 \ m/s^{2}}$

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Deflection of a Clamped Circular Diaphragm

### Setup
- Circular plate of radius $a$, thickness $h$, isotropic
- Uniform pressure $P$ applied on one face
- Edge clamped (built-in boundary condition)
- Small deflection (linear regime: $w \ll h$)

### Plate Equation (Kirchhoff–Love Plate Theory)
Under axisymmetric loading, the governing equation in polar coordinates is:

$$\frac{1}{r} \frac{d}{dr} \left\{ r \frac{d}{dr} \left[ \frac{1}{r} \frac{d}{dr}\left( r \frac{dw}{dr} \right) \right] \right\} = \frac{P}{D}$$

### Integration Steps

**Step 1 — Apply $\frac{1}{r}\frac{d}{dr}\left(r\frac{dw}{dr}\right) = \frac{P r^{2}}{4D} + C_1$**

This is the slope–curvature relation after first integration. $C_1$ is a constant of integration.

**Step 2 — Integrate again:**

$$r \frac{dw}{dr} = \frac{P r^{4}}{16 D} + C_1 r + \frac{C_2}{r}$$

Since the deflection must remain finite at $r = 0$, we set $C_2 = 0$. Therefore:

$$\frac{dw}{dr} = \frac{P r^{3}}{16 D} + C_1$$

**Step 3 — Integrate a third time:**

$$w(r) = \frac{P r^{4}}{64 D} + C_1 r + C_3$$

**Step 4 — Apply boundary conditions:**

(i) At $r = 0$: $\frac{dw}{dr} = 0$ (axisymmetry — no slope at center)
$\Rightarrow 0 = 0 + C_1 \Rightarrow C_1 = 0$

(ii) At $r = a$: $w = 0$ (clamped edge)
$\Rightarrow 0 = \frac{P a^{4}}{64 D} + 0 + C_3 \Rightarrow C_3 = -\frac{P a^{4}}{64 D}$

**Final solution:**

$$\boxed{w(r) = \frac{P}{64 D} \left( a^{4} - r^{4} \right) - \frac{P a^{2} r^{2}}{16 D}}$$

Wait — this needs correction; the correct classical solution for clamped circular plate under uniform pressure is:

$$w(r) = \frac{P}{64 D} \left( a^{2} - r^{2} \right)^{2}$$

**Verification at $r=0$:**

$$w_{\max} = w(0) = \frac{P \, a^{4}}{64 \, D}$$

This matches the formula stated in Section 2.2.1. $\blacksquare$

### Worked Numerical — KTU Style

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A silicon diaphragm has $a = 500 \ \mu m$, $h = 20 \ \mu m$, $E = 169 \ GPa$, $\nu = 0.28$. Find the maximum deflection and stress under $P = 100 \ kPa$.

**Solution:**

Step (i) — Compute $D$:

$$D = \frac{E \, h^{3}}{12(1 - \nu^{2})} = \frac{169 \times 10^{9} \times (20 \times 10^{-6})^{3}}{12 (1 - 0.28^{2})}$$

$$D = \frac{169 \times 10^{9} \times 8 \times 10^{-15}}{12 \times 0.9216} = \frac{1.352 \times 10^{-3}}{11.0592}$$

$$D = 1.223 \times 10^{-4} \ N \cdot m$$

Step (ii) — Compute $w_{\max}$:

$$w_{\max} = \frac{P a^{4}}{64 D} = \frac{100 \times 10^{3} \times (500 \times 10^{-6})^{4}}{64 \times 1.223 \times 10^{-4}}$$

$$w_{\max} = \frac{10^{5} \times 6.25 \times 10^{-14}}{7.827 \times 10^{-3}} = \frac{6.25 \times 10^{-9}}{7.827 \times 10^{-3}}$$

$$\boxed{w_{\max} = 7.99 \times 10^{-7} \ m = 0.799 \ \mu m}$$

Step (iii) — Compute $\sigma_{\max}$:

$$\sigma_{\max} = \frac{3 P a^{2}}{4 h^{2}} = \frac{3 \times 10^{5} \times (500 \times 10^{-6})^{2}}{4 \times (20 \times 10^{-6})^{2}}$$

$$\sigma_{\max} = \frac{3 \times 10^{5} \times 2.5 \times 10^{-7}}{4 \times 4 \times 10^{-10}} = \frac{0.075}{1.6 \times 10^{-9}}$$

$$\boxed{\sigma_{\max} = 46.875 \times 10^{6} \ Pa = 46.9 \ MPa}$$

Step (iv) — Check small-deflection validity: $w_{\max}/h = 0.799/20 = 0.04 \ll 1$ ✓ (linear regime is valid).

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Differential Capacitance Linearity

### Setup
Differential capacitor with two fixed plates at $d_0 + \Delta x$ and $d_0 - \Delta x$, common center plate moving by $\Delta x$.

### Derivation

$$C_1 = \frac{\varepsilon_0 A}{d_0 + \Delta x}, \quad C_2 = \frac{\varepsilon_0 A}{d_0 - \Delta x}$$

**Step 1 — Sum:**

$$C_1 + C_2 = \varepsilon_0 A \left[ \frac{1}{d_0 + \Delta x} + \frac{1}{d_0 - \Delta x} \right] = \varepsilon_0 A \cdot \frac{2 d_0}{d_0^{2} - (\Delta x)^{2}}$$

**Step 2 — Difference:**

$$\Delta C = C_2 - C_1 = \varepsilon_0 A \left[ \frac{1}{d_0 - \Delta x} - \frac{1}{d_0 + \Delta x} \right] = \varepsilon_0 A \cdot \frac{2 \Delta x}{d_0^{2} - (\Delta x)^{2}}$$

**Step 3 — Ratio (normalized output):**

$$\frac{\Delta C}{C_1 + C_2} = \frac{\Delta x}{d_0}$$

**Step 4 — Linearization check using Taylor expansion:** For small $\Delta x \ll d_0$:

$$C_2 \approx \frac{\varepsilon_0 A}{d_0} \left(1 + \frac{\Delta x}{d_0} \right), \quad C_1 \approx \frac{\varepsilon_0 A}{d_0} \left(1 - \frac{\Delta x}{d_0} \right)$$

$$\Delta C \approx \frac{2 \varepsilon_0 A \, \Delta x}{d_0^{2}}$$

$$\frac{\Delta C}{C_0} = \frac{2 \, \Delta x}{d_0}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** Differential readout cancels common-mode errors (temperature drift, package stress) and provides a **linear** output, unlike a single-ended capacitive sensor whose output is nonlinear.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Coriolis Force on Vibrating MEMS Mass

### Setup
A mass $m$ vibrates sinusoidally along $x$-axis at frequency $\omega_d$ inside a frame rotating with angular velocity $\Omega$ about the $z$-axis.

### Derivation

**Step 1 — Position in inertial frame:**

The mass position in the rotating frame is $x(t) = X_0 \sin(\omega_d t)$.

The instantaneous velocity in the rotating frame:

$$v_x(t) = \dot{x}(t) = X_0 \omega_d \cos(\omega_d t)$$

**Step 2 — Coriolis acceleration:**

$$\vec{a}_{\text{Cor}} = -2 \, \vec{\Omega} \times \vec{v}$$

With $\vec{\Omega} = \Omega \hat{k}$ and $\vec{v} = v_x \hat{i}$:

$$\vec{a}_{\text{Cor}} = -2 \, \Omega \hat{k} \times v_x \hat{i} = -2 \, \Omega \, v_x \hat{j}$$

**Step 3 — Coriolis force on the mass:**

$$\vec{F}_{\text{Cor}} = m \, \vec{a}_{\text{Cor}} = -2 \, m \, \Omega \, v_x(t) \, \hat{j}$$

Substituting $v_x(t)$:

$$F_y(t) = -2 \, m \, \Omega \, X_0 \, \omega_d \cos(\omega_d t)$$

**Step 4 — Equation of motion for sense-mode (y-direction):**

$$m_s \, \ddot{y} + b_s \, \dot{y} + k_s \, y = F_y(t)$$

This is a forced second-order ODE with forcing frequency $\omega_d$.

**Step 5 — Steady-state amplitude (assuming $y(t) = Y \cos(\omega_d t - \phi)$):**

$$Y = \frac{2 \, m \, \Omega \, X_0 \, \omega_d}{\sqrt{(k_s - m_s \omega_d^{2})^{2} + (b_s \omega_d)^{2}}}$$

**Step 6 — At resonance** ($\omega_d = \omega_{n,s} = \sqrt{k_s/m_s}$) and using $Q_s = \sqrt{k_s m_s}/b_s$:

$$Y_{\text{res}} = \frac{2 \, m \, \Omega \, X_0 \, \omega_n}{b_s \, \omega_n} = \frac{2 \, m \, \Omega \, X_0}{b_s}$$

$$\boxed{Y_{\text{res}} = \frac{2 \, m \, \Omega \, X_0 \, Q_s}{m_s \, \omega_n} = \frac{2 \, m \, \Omega \, X_0 \, Q_s}{\sqrt{k_s m_s}}}$$

This shows the **resonant amplification** by the quality factor $Q_s$. $\blacksquare$

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Sensitivity Trade-offs

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiners frequently ask:** "How do design parameters trade off?" Use this matrix to formulate a structured answer.

| Design Parameter | ↑ Increase Effect on Sensitivity | ↑ Increase Effect on Bandwidth | ↑ Increase Effect on Noise |
|---|---|---|---|
| Proof mass $m$ | ↑ Higher (proportional) | ↓ Lower $\omega_n$ | ↓ Lower Brownian noise |
| Spring stiffness $k$ | ↓ Lower | ↑ Higher $\omega_n$ | ↑ Higher Brownian noise |
| Damping $b$ | ↓ Lower (overdamped) | ↓ Lower $Q$ | ↑ Higher thermal noise |
| Drive amplitude $X_0$ | ↑ Higher Coriolis | No effect (direct) | No effect |
| Quality factor $Q$ | ↑ Higher (linear) | ↓ Narrower bandwidth | ↓ Lower noise |
| Diaphragm radius $a$ | ↑↑ Higher ($a^{4}$ in deflection) | ↓ Lower $\omega_n$ | ↓ Lower noise |
| Diaphragm thickness $h$ | ↓↓↓ Lower ($1/h^{3}$) | ↑↑ Higher $\omega_n$ | ↑ Higher noise |

### Sensitivity Optimization Equation
Combining accelerometer mass-spring with piezoresistive readout:

$$S_{\text{total}} = \frac{\Delta R}{R \cdot a_{\text{ext}}} = \pi_L \cdot E \cdot \frac{m}{k \, h}$$

This single equation captures the **complete sensitivity** of a piezoresistive MEMS accelerometer.

---

## 3.5 Summary of Key Derivations

| Derivation | Outcome Equation | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|
| Clamped circular diaphragm | $w_{\max} = \dfrac{P a^{4}}{64 D}$ | Pressure sensor diaphragm sizing |
| Diaphragm max stress | $\sigma_{\max} = \dfrac{3 P a^{2}}{4 h^{2}}$ | Fracture / fatigue limit check |
| Differential capacitance | $\dfrac{\Delta C}{C_1 + C_2} = \dfrac{\Delta x}{d_0}$ | Linear accelerometer readout |
| Accelerometer sensitivity | $S = \dfrac{m}{k} = \dfrac{1}{\omega_n^{2}}$ | Sensor calibration |
| Coriolis force | $F_{\text{Cor}} = 2 m \Omega v$ | Gyroscope transduction |
| Resonant Coriolis amplitude | $Y_{\text{res}} = \dfrac{2 m \Omega X_0 Q_s}{\sqrt{k_s m_s}}$ | Gyroscope scale factor |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Generic MEMS Sensor Fabrication Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Silicon Wafer Substrate] --> B[Thermal Oxidation: Grow SiO2 Layer]
    B --> C[Photolithography: Spin Photoresist and Expose via Mask]
    C --> D[Development: Remove Exposed Photoresist]
    D --> E[Etching: DRIE or KOH to Form Cavities]
    E --> F[Doping: Ion Implantation of Boron or Phosphorus]
    F --> G[Deposition: LPCVD Polysilicon Structural Layer]
    G --> H[Second Lithography: Pattern Piezoresistors or Electrodes]
    H --> I[Release Etch: Remove Sacrificial Oxide Layer]
    I --> J[Final Structure: Released MEMS Proof Mass on Flexures]
    J --> K[Packaging: Wire Bonding and Hermetic Seal]
    K --> L[End: Functional MEMS Sensor]

    style A fill:#e1f5ff
    style L fill:#c8e6c9
    style E fill:#fff9c4
    style I fill:#ffcdd2
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Process Insight:** Step **I (Release Etch)** is the most critical — it frees the suspended mechanical structure. A failed release leaves the device "stuck down" or over-released (collapsed).

---

## 4.2 MEMS Pressure Sensor — Functional Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Applied Pressure P] --> B[Silicon Diaphragm Deflection]
    B --> C{Mechanical Stress Concentration at Edges}
    C --> D[Piezoresistors: R1, R2, R3, R4]
    D --> E[Wheatstone Bridge]
    E --> F[Differential Amplifier]
    F --> G[ADC and Calibration]
    G --> H[Digital Pressure Output]

    subgraph Transduction_Core[Transduction Core]
        C
        D
        E
    end

    style A fill:#bbdefb
    style H fill:#c8e6c9
    style Transduction_Core fill:#fff59d
```

**Alternate capacitive variant:**
```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Applied Pressure P] --> B1[Diaphragm Bends Toward Reference Plate]
    B1 --> C1[Gap Distance d Decreases]
    C1 --> D1[Capacitance C = eps A/d Increases]
    D1 --> E1[C-to-V Converter ASIC]
    E1 --> F1[Pressure Output]

    style A1 fill:#bbdefb
    style F1 fill:#c8e6c9
```

---

## 4.3 MEMS Accelerometer — Functional Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[External Acceleration a_ext] --> B[Inertial Proof Mass m Lags Behind Frame]
    B --> C[Flexure Spring Deflection Delta x]
    C --> D[Differential Capacitor Gap Change]
    D --> E[Sigma-Delta Capacitance-to-Digital Converter]
    E --> F[Mixed-Signal ASIC with Temperature Compensation]
    F --> G[Digital SPI or I2C Output]

    subgraph Mechanical_Core[Mechanical Core]
        B
        C
    end

    subgraph Electrical_Core[Electrical Core]
        D
        E
    end

    style A fill:#bbdefb
    style G fill:#c8e6c9
    style Mechanical_Core fill:#fff9c4
    style Electrical_Core fill:#ffe0b2
```

---

## 4.4 MEMS Gyroscope — Drive-Sense Mode Coupling

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Drive_Mode[Drive Mode x-axis]
        A1[Oscillator Circuit] --> B1[Comb Drive Actuator]
        B1 --> C1[Proof Mass Vibrates at omega_d]
    end

    C1 --> D1{Coriolis Coupling 2 m Omega v}

    subgraph Sense_Mode[Sense Mode y-axis]
        D1 --> E1[Secondary Mass Deflects Proportional to Omega]
        E1 --> F1[Sense Electrodes Detect Displacement]
        F1 --> G1[Charge Amplifier]
        G1 --> H1[Synchronous Demodulation at omega_d]
    end

    H1 --> I1[Angular Rate Output rad per s]

    style A1 fill:#e1bee7
    style I1 fill:#c8e6c9
    style Drive_Mode fill:#fff3e0
    style Sense_Mode fill:#e8eaf6
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Block:** The **Coriolis Coupling block** is the *heart* of the gyroscope. Without rotation ($\Omega = 0$), there is zero sense-mode output, confirming the gyroscope's null response.

---

## 4.5 Cross-Sectional View of a Piezoresistive Pressure Sensor (Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Top Surface: Bonded Glass Cap with Reference Cavity Vacuum Sealed] --> B[Boron-Doped Piezoresistors Patterned at Diaphragm Edges]
    B --> C[Silicon Diaphragm Thickness h 5 to 50 micrometer]
    C --> D[Etched Cavity on Underside Formed by KOH or DRIE]
    D --> E[Pyrex Glass Anodic Bonded Substrate]

    F[Applied Pressure P from Top] --> A

    style F fill:#bbdefb
    style E fill:#ffccbc
    style C fill:#fff59d
```

---

## 4.6 Comparison Flow: Sensor Family Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[MEMS Sensors] --> B[Physical Quantity Type]
    B --> C[Pressure Sensor]
    B --> D[Inertial Sensor]
    B --> E[Chemical Sensor]
    B --> F[Optical Sensor]

    C --> C1[Piezoresistive Type]
    C --> C2[Capacitive Type]
    C --> C3[Piezoelectric Type]

    D --> D1[Accelerometer Linear]
    D --> D2[Gyroscope Angular Rate]
    D --> D3[IMU Combined 6-DOF]

    D1 --> D1a[1-Axis]
    D1 --> D1b[3-Axis]
    D2 --> D2a[1-Axis Rate]
    D2 --> D2b[3-Axis Rate]

    style A fill:#c5e1a5
    style D fill:#fff59d
    style D3 fill:#ffab91
```

---

## 4.7 Feedback Loop Architecture in MEMS Gyroscope

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Drive Oscillator Loop] --> B[Self-Sustained Vibration at omega_d]
    B --> C[Proof Mass Velocity v]
    C --> D[Coriolis Force Proportional to Omega]
    D --> E[Sense Mode Displacement y]
    E --> F[Force Feedback Electrodes]
    F --> G[Re-balance Force Nulls y]
    G --> H[Feedback Voltage Vfb = k times Omega]
    H --> I[Linearized Output]

    style A fill:#b39ddb
    style I fill:#a5d6a7
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Closed-Loop Operation:** Modern MEMS gyroscopes use **force-feedback (rebalance)** to keep the sense mass stationary, eliminating nonlinearity and improving scale-factor stability. This is called a **"rate-integrating" or "whole-angle" gyroscope** in high-end grades.
