# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Define MEMS. List any four characteristic features of MEMS technology.**

**Model Answer:**
MEMS (Micro-Electro-Mechanical Systems) are integrated devices that combine mechanical elements, sensors, actuators, and electronic circuits fabricated on a common silicon substrate using microfabrication techniques.

**Four characteristic features:**
1. **Small size** — Features range from 1 µm to 1 mm.
2. **Batch fabrication** — Hundreds of devices fabricated simultaneously on a single wafer using photolithography → low unit cost.
3. **Integration** — Mechanical + electrical functions co-exist on the same chip.
4. **Low power consumption** — Typically in the µW range, suitable for portable and battery-operated systems.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Distinguish between a MEMS accelerometer and a MEMS gyroscope based on the physical quantity measured and the underlying principle.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | MEMS Accelerometer | MEMS Gyroscope |
|---|---|---|
| Quantity measured | Linear acceleration (m/s² or g) | Angular rate (°/s or rad/s) |
| Underlying principle | Inertial displacement of proof mass against spring/damper | Coriolis effect on a vibrating mass |
| Governing equation | $m\ddot{x} + b\dot{x} + kx = m a$ | $F_{\text{Cor}} = 2m\Omega v$ |
| Output | Proportional to acceleration | Proportional to rotation rate |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **As per KTU 2024 ESE regulations:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks** with internal choice. Sub-parts are typically **7 marks each**.

---

### Question A (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Modified]

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat diagram, explain the **principle, construction, and working of a piezoresistive MEMS pressure sensor**. Derive the expression for the maximum deflection of a clamped circular diaphragm under uniform pressure.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A silicon diaphragm has radius $a = 0.5 \ mm$, thickness $h = 25 \ \mu m$, Young's modulus $E = 169 \ GPa$, Poisson's ratio $\nu = 0.28$. If applied pressure is $P = 200 \ kPa$, compute: (i) the maximum deflection at the center, (ii) the maximum stress at the edge, and (iii) the resulting change in resistance of a piezoresistor placed at the edge with $\pi_L = 400 \times 10^{-11} \ Pa^{-1}$ and gauge length producing strain $\varepsilon = 5 \times 10^{-4}$.

#### Model Solution

**(a) [7 Marks] — Theory**

**Construction:**
A piezoresistive MEMS pressure sensor consists of a thin circular silicon diaphragm (~5–50 µm thick) formed by anisotropic etching of a silicon wafer. Four piezoresistors are ion-implanted near the diaphragm edges (at the points of maximum stress concentration) and connected in a Wheatstone bridge configuration. The wafer is anodically bonded to a Pyrex glass substrate containing a sealed reference cavity.

**Working:**
- External pressure deflects the diaphragm downward.
- Maximum tensile/compressive stresses occur at the clamped edges (at $r = a$).
- Piezoresistors change resistance according to $\Delta R/R = \pi_L \sigma + \pi_T \sigma_{\perp}$.
- The Wheatstone bridge converts this resistance imbalance into a differential voltage output $V_{\text{out}} = \frac{V_{\text{in}}}{4} \left( \frac{\Delta R_1}{R_1} - \frac{\Delta R_2}{R_2} + \frac{\Delta R_3}{R_3} - \frac{\Delta R_4}{R_4} \right)$.

**Derivation of $w_{\max}$:**
(See Section 3.1 for full derivation.)

$$\boxed{w_{\max} = \frac{P a^{4}}{64 D}, \quad \text{where } D = \frac{E h^{3}}{12(1-\nu^{2})}}$$

**(b) [7 Marks] — Numerical Solution**

**Given:**
$a = 0.5 \times 10^{-3} \ m$, $h = 25 \times 10^{-6} \ m$, $E = 169 \times 10^{9} \ Pa$, $\nu = 0.28$, $P = 200 \times 10^{3} \ Pa$, $\pi_L = 400 \times 10^{-11} \ Pa^{-1}$, $\varepsilon = 5 \times 10^{-4}$.

**Step (i) — Maximum deflection:**

$$D = \frac{E h^{3}}{12(1-\nu^{2})} = \frac{169 \times 10^{9} \times (25 \times 10^{-6})^{3}}{12 \times (1 - 0.0784)}$$

$$D = \frac{169 \times 10^{9} \times 1.5625 \times 10^{-14}}{12 \times 0.9216} = \frac{2.6406 \times 10^{-3}}{11.0592}$$

$$D = 2.388 \times 10^{-4} \ N \cdot m$$

$$w_{\max} = \frac{P a^{4}}{64 D} = \frac{200 \times 10^{3} \times (0.5 \times 10^{-3})^{4}}{64 \times 2.388 \times 10^{-4}}$$

$$w_{\max} = \frac{2 \times 10^{5} \times 6.25 \times 10^{-14}}{1.528 \times 10^{-2}} = \frac{1.25 \times 10^{-8}}{1.528 \times 10^{-2}}$$

$$\boxed{w_{\max} = 8.18 \times 10^{-7} \ m = 0.818 \ \mu m}$$

**Step (ii) — Maximum stress:**

$$\sigma_{\max} = \frac{3 P a^{2}}{4 h^{2}} = \frac{3 \times 2 \times 10^{5} \times (0.5 \times 10^{-3})^{2}}{4 \times (25 \times 10^{-6})^{2}}$$

$$\sigma_{\max} = \frac{6 \times 10^{5} \times 2.5 \times 10^{-7}}{4 \times 6.25 \times 10^{-10}} = \frac{0.15}{2.5 \times 10^{-9}}$$

$$\boxed{\sigma_{\max} = 6 \times 10^{7} \ Pa = 60 \ MPa}$$

**Step (iii) — Change in resistance:**

$$\frac{\Delta R}{R} = \pi_L \, \sigma = 400 \times 10^{-11} \times 6 \times 10^{7} = 2.4 \times 10^{-2}$$

$$\boxed{\frac{\Delta R}{R} = 0.024 = 2.4\%}$$

(Alternatively, gauge factor method: $G = \Delta R/(R\varepsilon)$; if given $G$, then $\Delta R/R = G \cdot \varepsilon$.)

---

### Question B (Choice 2) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **principle, construction, and working of a MEMS accelerometer**. Derive the governing equation for the proof mass displacement and show that the static sensitivity is $S = m/k$.

**(b) [7 Marks]** With a block diagram, explain the working of a **MEMS gyroscope using the Coriolis effect**. Derive the expression for the Coriolis force experienced by a vibrating proof mass.

#### Model Solution

**(a) [7 Marks] — Theory**

**Construction:**
A MEMS accelerometer consists of a silicon proof mass suspended by compliant flexure springs anchored to a fixed frame. Combs or plates attached to the proof mass form differential capacitors with stationary electrodes on the substrate. The structure is fabricated using surface micromachining or deep reactive ion etching (DRIE).

**Working:**
- When the chip experiences acceleration $a_{\text{ext}}$, the proof mass tends to remain inertial due to Newton's second law.
- The frame moves with the chip, but the proof mass lags by displacement $\Delta x = (m/k) a_{\text{ext}}$.
- This displacement is detected via:
  - Differential capacitance change ($\Delta C \propto \Delta x$), OR
  - Piezoresistive strain in flexures, OR
  - Tunneling current variation.

**Governing Equation Derivation:**

Force balance on proof mass:

$$m \ddot{x} + b \dot{x} + k x = F_{\text{ext}} = m a_{\text{ext}}(t)$$

Static case ($\ddot{x} = \dot{x} = 0$):

$$k x = m a_{\text{ext}} \Rightarrow \boxed{x = \frac{m a_{\text{ext}}}{k}}$$

Therefore, **static sensitivity:**

$$\boxed{S = \frac{x}{a_{\text{ext}}} = \frac{m}{k}}$$

Substituting $\omega_n = \sqrt{k/m}$:

$$S = \frac{m}{k} = \frac{1}{\omega_n^{2}}$$

**(b) [7 Marks] — Gyroscope Theory**

**Block Diagram** (refer to Section 4.4 for Mermaid diagram).

**Construction:**
A MEMS gyroscope contains two perpendicular modes of vibration:
- **Drive mode** (x-axis): Proof mass vibrates sinusoidally via electrostatic comb drives.
- **Sense mode** (y-axis): Coriolis-induced motion is detected capacitively.

**Working Principle:**
When the device rotates with angular velocity $\Omega$ about an axis perpendicular to both drive and sense directions, the vibrating mass experiences a Coriolis force proportional to $\Omega$, which deflects the sense-mode mass.

**Coriolis Force Derivation:**

For a mass $m$ with velocity $\vec{v}$ in a frame rotating at $\vec{\Omega}$:

$$\vec{F}_{\text{Cor}} = -2m \, \vec{\Omega} \times \vec{v}$$

Let $\vec{\Omega} = \Omega \hat{k}$, $\vec{v} = v_x \hat{i}$:

$$\vec{F}_{\text{Cor}} = -2m \, \Omega \, v_x (\hat{k} \times \hat{i}) = -2m \, \Omega \, v_x \, \hat{j}$$

If drive velocity is $v_x(t) = V_0 \cos(\omega_d t)$:

$$\boxed{F_{\text{Cor}}(t) = 2m \, \Omega \, V_0 \cos(\omega_d t)}$$

This force drives the sense-mode mass, whose displacement amplitude at resonance is amplified by the quality factor $Q_s$:

$$Y_{\text{res}} = \frac{2m \, \Omega \, V_0 \, Q_s}{\sqrt{k_s m_s}}$$

Capacitive readout converts $Y$ into voltage → angular rate output.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls — Where KTU Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Unit Mistakes (–1 mark):** Writing $w_{\max}$ in cm instead of m, or $\omega_n$ in Hz instead of rad/s. Always state SI units.
>
> 2. **Confusing "natural frequency" with "operating frequency":** The proof mass resonates at $\omega_n$, but MEMS accelerometers are designed to operate **below** $\omega_n$ (in the flat-response region) for accurate measurement. Writing "operates at resonance" loses a mark.
>
> 3. **Missing the Cross Product in Coriolis Force:** Many students write $F_{\text{Cor}} = 2m\Omega v$ without the cross product form. Examiners *require* $\vec{F}_{\text{Cor}} = -2m \, \vec{\Omega} \times \vec{v}$ for full marks (vector nature emphasized).
>
> 4. **Forgetting Damping in Steady-State:** In gyroscope derivation, omitting $b_s$ in the denominator of $Y$ results in an incorrect "infinite" response at resonance. Always include the full denominator.
>
> 5. **Pressure Sensor Diaphragm Assumptions:** Failing to mention **"small deflection"** (linear plate theory) assumption leads to –1 mark deduction.
>
> 6. **Confusing "accelerometer" with "gyroscope" physical quantity:** Mixing these up in the answer script is a fatal error costing up to 3 marks.
>
> 7. **No Block Diagram in (a) part:** For 7-mark theory questions, KTU examiners *mandate* a labeled diagram. Without it, expect a 2-mark deduction.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Read before entering the exam hall!**

### 🔹 MEMS Pressure Sensor
- [x] Diaphragm shape: **circular** (most common), square, or rectangular.
- [x] Material: **single-crystal silicon** (anisotropic etching).
- [x] Transduction types: **Piezoresistive, Capacitive, Piezoelectric**.
- [x] Key equation: $w_{\max} = \dfrac{P a^{4}}{64 D}$, $\sigma_{\max} = \dfrac{3 P a^{2}}{4 h^{2}}$.
- [x] Piezoresistors placed at **diaphragm edges** (max stress).
- [x] Bridge: **Wheatstone bridge** for differential output.
- [x] Capacitive sensors are **more sensitive & low-power**, but need complex ASIC.

### 🔹 MEMS Accelerometer
- [x] Measures **linear acceleration** ($g$ or m/s²).
- [x] Components: **proof mass + flexure spring + damper + readout**.
- [x] Equation: $m\ddot{x} + b\dot{x} + kx = m a_{\text{ext}}$.
- [x] Natural frequency: $\omega_n = \sqrt{k/m}$.
- [x] Static sensitivity: $S = m/k = 1/\omega_n^{2}$.
- [x] Operating range: **below $\omega_n$** for flat response.
- [x] Typical bandwidth: **DC to 1–5 kHz**.

### 🔹 MEMS Gyroscope
- [x] Measures **angular rate** (°/s or rad/s).
- [x] Principle: **Coriolis Effect** on vibrating mass.
- [x] Drive mode: **vibration** along one axis.
- [x] Sense mode: **perpendicular deflection** due to rotation.
- [x] Coriolis force: $\vec{F}_{\text{Cor}} = -2m\vec{\Omega} \times \vec{v}$.
- [x] Quality factor $Q$ **amplifies sense-mode signal**.
- [x] Mode-matching: $\omega_d \approx \omega_s$ for high sensitivity.

### 🔹 Fabrication
- [x] Substrate: **Silicon wafer**.
- [x] Steps: Oxidation → Lithography → Etching → Doping → Deposition → Release.
- [x] Etching: **DRIE (dry, anisotropic)** preferred over **KOH (wet, anisotropic)**.
- [x] Release etch: **critical step** — removes sacrificial layer.
- [x] Packaging: **Anodic bonding** (Si-Pyrex), **hermetic seal**.

### 🔹 Design Trade-offs
- [x] ↑ mass → ↑ sensitivity, ↓ bandwidth, ↓ noise.
- [x] ↑ stiffness → ↓ sensitivity, ↑ bandwidth, ↑ noise.
- [x] ↑ diaphragm radius → ↑↑ sensitivity (4th power), ↓ bandwidth.
- [x] ↑ Q-factor → ↑ sensitivity, ↓ bandwidth.

### 🔹 Must-Memorize Constants
- [x] $E_{Si} = 1.69 \times 10^{11} \ Pa$
- [x] $\nu_{Si} = 0.28$
- [x] $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12} \ F/m$
- [x] $k_B = 1.38 \times 10^{-23} \ J/K$
- [x] $g = 9.81 \ m/s^{2}$

### 🔹 Examiner-Preferred Keywords (use these in answers!)
> **Use terms like:** "small-deflection regime," "pseudoresonance," "mode-matched," "squeezed-film damping," "Coriolis coupling," "Wheatstone bridge configuration," "force-feedback rebalance," "differential capacitive readout," "anisotropic etching," "LPCVD polysilicon."

---

> **End of Module 3 Notes — MEMS Sensors**

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus)

A **Programmable Logic Controller (PLC)** is a ruggedized, solid-state, digital computer specifically designed for the real-time control of industrial automation processes. It uses a programmable memory for the internal storage of user-oriented instructions that implement specific functions such as logic, sequencing, timing, counting, and arithmetic operations. These instructions are used to control, through digital or analog input/output (I/O) modules, a wide variety of machines and processes.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition (Memorize this):**
> A PLC is a *"solid-state industrial control device that receives inputs from the controlled process, executes stored logic programs, and produces outputs to manipulate the process — all in real-time and in a rugged environment-tolerant package."*

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Smart Electrical Panel Box" Analogy**
>
> Think of a traditional **electrical relay panel** found in old factories. It consists of hundreds of wires, electromagnetic relays, timers, and counters hard-wired together to control a machine. If you wanted to change the sequence of operations, you had to **re-wire the entire panel** — a tedious, error-prone, and expensive job.
>
> Now imagine replacing all those wires and relays with a **small industrial computer** that can be re-programmed in minutes by typing new instructions. That is exactly what a PLC is:
> - The **inputs** = signals from sensors (buttons, limit switches, temperature probes).
> - The **CPU** = the "brain" that runs your logic.
> - The **outputs** = signals to actuators (motor starters, solenoid valves, lamps).
> - The **ladder program** = the "virtual wires" connecting inputs to outputs.

### 1.3 Salient Features of a PLC (KTU High-Yield Points)

- **Solid-state construction** — no moving parts in the logic unit.
- **Ruggedized design** — operates in **0 °C to 60 °C**, withstands electrical noise, vibration, and humidity.
- **Modular and expandable** — I/O modules can be added on a rack.
- **Easy programming** — typically via **Ladder Logic (LD)**, Function Block Diagram (FBD), or Structured Text (ST).
- **Real-time response** — deterministic scan cycle, typically **10–50 ms**.
- **High reliability** — **Mean Time Between Failures (MTBF)** often exceeds **100,000 hours**.
- **Built-in diagnostics** — fault indicators, status LEDs, and online monitoring.

> [!TIP]
> **Why PLC and not a PC?** A desktop PC cannot survive the heat, dust, electrical transients, and 24×7 continuous operation of a factory floor. PLCs are built **specifically** for this hostile environment.

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Basic Block Diagram of a PLC — Functional Modules

A PLC system is composed of **five major functional blocks** interconnected through a common **backplane bus** or **communication system**.

#### 2.1.1 Central Processing Unit (CPU)
- The "brain" of the PLC containing the **microprocessor / microcontroller**.
- Comprises:
  - **Arithmetic Logic Unit (ALU)** — performs Boolean and arithmetic operations.
  - **Control Unit (CU)** — fetches, decodes, and executes instructions.
  - **Registers** — accumulator, status register, program counter.
- Runs the **scan cycle** continuously (input scan → program scan → output scan).

#### 2.1.2 Memory Unit
Divided into distinct functional regions:

| Memory Region | Function | Typical Size |
|---|---|---|
| **System ROM / Firmware** | Stores the operating system, scan scheduler, and interpreter (non-volatile) | 64 KB – 512 KB |
| **RAM (User Program)** | Holds the user ladder/FB/ST program (volatile, battery-backed) | 16 KB – 4 MB |
| **Data Memory (I/O Image Table)** | Stores status of inputs (Input Image) and outputs (Output Image) | A few KB |
| **Retentive Memory** | Preserves counters, timers, and flags on power loss | Battery-backed CMOS |

#### 2.1.3 Input Module (Interface)
- Accepts signals from field devices — push buttons, limit switches, proximity sensors, photoelectric sensors, thermocouples, RTDs, transmitters (4–20 mA), etc.
- Performs **signal conditioning, isolation, and addressing**.
- Converts real-world signals to logic-level voltages (e.g., 0 V / 5 V DC).

#### 2.1.4 Output Module (Interface)
- Drives field actuators — solenoids, contactors, motor starters, indicator lamps, proportional valves, variable frequency drives.
- Provides **isolation and amplification** between the low-power CPU and the high-power load.
- Typical switching elements: **relays, transistors (BJT/MOSFET), triacs, or SSRs**.

#### 2.1.5 Power Supply Unit (PSU)
- Converts mains AC (230 V, 50 Hz) to regulated DC voltages (typically **+5 V, +24 V, ±15 V**).
- Provides **isolated, regulated power** to the CPU, memory, and I/O modules.
- Often includes **battery backup** for RAM retention.

#### 2.1.6 Programming Device (External)
- Personal computer, handheld programmer, or HMI panel.
- Communicates via **RS-232, RS-485, USB, Ethernet, or MPI/PPI protocols**.

### 2.2 Input/Output (I/O) Processing — The Core Topic

#### 2.2.1 Input Section — Detailed Architecture

The input section does **not directly connect** the field device to the CPU. Instead, the signal flows through the following stages:

1. **Field Terminal Block** — screw terminals where wires from sensors are connected.
2. **Input Filter (RC / Debounce)** — removes electrical noise and contact bounce (typical time constant: **5–20 ms**).
3. **Signal Conditioning** — level shifting, voltage/current conversion.
4. **Electrical Isolation** — **Optical Isolation (Optocoupler)** is the industry standard. The input side and CPU side are separated by a **light beam** inside an opto-isolator, providing galvanic isolation of typically **1500–2500 V RMS**.
5. **Status Indicator (LED)** — shows real-time ON/OFF state.
6. **Input Register (Input Image Table)** — a word in RAM that stores the binary status of all inputs.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Optical Isolation Principle:**
> When the input is ON, an **internal LED** inside the optocoupler emits light → falls on a **phototransistor** → transistor conducts → CPU sees a logic '1'. There is **no electrical (galvanic) connection** between field and CPU. This protects the CPU from surges, spikes, and ground loops.

#### 2.2.2 Output Section — Detailed Architecture

The output section is a mirror image of the input, but in reverse:

1. **Output Register (Output Image Table)** — CPU writes the result of the user program here.
2. **Isolation** — optocoupler or pulse transformer separates CPU from load.
3. **Driver / Switching Element** — amplifies the signal to drive the load. Three common families:
   - **Relay Output** — electromechanical, handles AC and DC, slow (~10 ms), limited life (~100,000 operations).
   - **Transistor Output** — DC only, fast, long life, requires flyback diode for inductive loads.
   - **Triac / SSR Output** — AC only, zero-crossing switching, optically isolated.
4. **Snubber / Flyback Protection** — RC snubbers for triacs, freewheeling diodes for transistors.
5. **Status Indicator (LED)** — shows whether the output is energized.
6. **Field Terminal Block** — connected to actuator wiring.

#### 2.2.3 Types of I/O Modules

| Module Type | Signal Type | Examples | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Digital DC Input** | 0 / 24 V DC | PNP / NPN sensors, limit switches | Conveyor control |
| **Digital AC Input** | 0 / 110 / 230 V AC | Selector switches, AC relays | Power distribution |
| **Digital DC Output** | 24 V DC, 0.5–2 A | Solenoid valves, LED indicators | Low-voltage DC loads |
| **Digital AC Output** | 110 / 230 V AC | Motor contactors, lamps | Industrial AC loads |
| **Relay Output (Dry Contact)** | Voltage-free NO/NC | Universal — any voltage up to **250 V AC / 30 V DC** | Mixed-voltage panels |
| **Analog Input** | 4–20 mA, 0–10 V, RTD, TC | Temperature, pressure, flow transmitters | Process control |
| **Analog Output** | 4–20 mA, 0–10 V | Control valves, VFD speed reference | Variable-speed drives |

#### 2.2.4 Sourcing vs. Sinking (KTU Frequently Asked)

- **Sinking Input (NPN sensor):** current flows *from* the PLC input *into* the sensor. PLC input provides a path to ground.
- **Sourcing Input (PNP sensor):** current flows *from* the sensor *into* the PLC input. Sensor supplies positive voltage.
- **Rule of Thumb:** European/Japanese convention = **PNP (sourcing)**; American convention = **NPN (sinking)**. Modern PLCs accept either using **IEC 61131-2 Type 1/2/3** input standards.

### 2.3 I/O Addressing Convention

Each I/O point has a unique address. Different manufacturers use different schemes:

| Manufacturer | Input Example | Output Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Allen-Bradley (SLC-500)** | `I:1/5` | `O:2/7` |
| **Siemens (S7-200/300)** | `I0.0` | `Q0.1` |
| **Mitsubishi (FX series)** | `X001` | `Y002` |
| **Schneider (Modicon)** | `%I0.1` | `%Q0.2` |

### 2.4 The PLC Scan Cycle (KTU High-Yield Concept)

The PLC does **not** continuously monitor inputs. Instead, it executes a **cyclic, deterministic scan**:

| Step | Action | Time |
|---|---|---|
| 1. **Self-test / Diagnostics** | Check RAM, ROM, watchdog, I/O bus | 1–2 ms |
| 2. **Input Scan** | Read all input terminals → store in **Input Image Table (IIT)** | ~1 ms per module |
| 3. **Program Scan** | Execute user program (ladder logic) **using IIT values** (not live inputs) | Variable, depends on program length |
| 4. **Output Scan** | Transfer **Output Image Table (OIT)** to physical output terminals | ~1 ms per module |
| 5. **Housekeeping / Communications** | Handle HMI, networks, interrupts | Few ms |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Total Scan Time = T_diag + T_in + T_prog + T_out + T_comm**
> A typical mid-range PLC has a scan time of **10–50 ms**. For high-speed applications, dedicated **high-speed counter (HSC)** modules or **interrupt-driven tasks** bypass the scan cycle.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Relation | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Total Scan Time** | $T_{scan} = T_{diag} + T_{in} + T_{prog} + T_{out} + T_{comm}$ | Sum of all scan phases |
| 2 | **Per-instruction Execution Time** | $T_{prog} = N \cdot t_{inst}$ | $N$ = number of rungs/instructions; $t_{inst}$ ≈ 0.1–10 µs per instruction |
| 3 | **Maximum I/O Response Time** | $T_{resp} = 2 \cdot T_{scan} - T_{in}$ | Worst-case delay from input change to output change |
| 4 | **Sampling Rate (Analog Input)** | $f_s = \dfrac{1}{T_{scan}}$ | Nyquist criterion: $f_s \geq 2 f_{signal,max}$ |
| 5 | **Nyquist Sampling Frequency** | $f_N = 2 \cdot f_{m}$ | $f_m$ = highest frequency in input signal |
| 6 | **Boolean Logic (Output Equation)** | $Y = (X_1 \cdot X_2) + (X_3 \cdot \overline{X_4})$ | Generic ladder-logic Boolean form |
| 7 | **MTBF (Reliability)** | $MTBF = \dfrac{1}{\lambda}$ | $\lambda$ = failure rate (failures/hour) |
| 8 | **Number of Inputs Addressable** | $N_{addr} = 2^{n}$ | $n$ = address bus width (typically 8, 16, or 32 bits) |
| 9 | **Optical Isolation Voltage** | $V_{iso} \geq 1500 \, V_{RMS}$ | Industry-standard galvanic isolation rating |
| 10 | **Input Filter Time Constant** | $\tau = R \cdot C$ | Typical 5–20 ms debounce filter |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL:** Always write pipe symbols in math as `\vert` or `\mid` — never as the raw character `|` — to prevent breaking markdown table rendering.

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

PLCs are the invisible backbone of nearly every modern industry:
- **Automotive assembly lines** — robotic welding, painting, and trim-fitting.
- **Food & beverage** — batching, filling, capping, CIP (clean-in-place).
- **Power plants** — boiler control, turbine sequencing, load shedding.
- **Water treatment** — pump alternation, chemical dosing, level control.
- **Traffic signalling** — phase sequencing, pedestrian interlocks.
- **Elevators** — floor selection, door control, safety interlocks.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> This section provides **exhaustive step-by-step derivations** for the most important quantitative aspects of PLC I/O processing. Each algebraic and logical transition is expanded with explanatory text.

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Total PLC Scan Time

**Given:**
- Diagnostics time: $T_{diag} = 2 \, \text{ms}$
- 4 input modules, each with $T_{in,mod} = 0.8 \, \text{ms}$
- User program: $N = 1500$ instructions, each with $t_{inst} = 0.5 \, \mu s$
- 3 output modules, each with $T_{out,mod} = 0.7 \, \text{ms}$
- Communication time: $T_{comm} = 3 \, \text{ms}$

**Find:** Total scan time $T_{scan}$.

**Step 1 — Total input scan time:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{in} &= (\text{Number of input modules}) \times T_{in,mod} \\
       &= 4 \times 0.8 \, \text{ms} \\
       &= 3.2 \, \text{ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Logic:* The PLC polls each input module sequentially. Total input scan time = count × per-module time.

**Step 2 — Total program execution time:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{prog} &= N \times t_{inst} \\
         &= 1500 \times 0.5 \, \mu s \\
         &= 750 \, \mu s \\
         &= 0.75 \, \text{ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Logic:* The CPU executes each instruction in series. 1500 instructions × 0.5 µs each = 750 µs total, converted to 0.75 ms.

**Step 3 — Total output scan time:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{out} &= (\text{Number of output modules}) \times T_{out,mod} \\
        &= 3 \times 0.7 \, \text{ms} \\
        &= 2.1 \, \text{ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Logic:* Output image table is transferred module-by-module to the physical terminals.

**Step 4 — Sum all phases:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{scan} &= T_{diag} + T_{in} + T_{prog} + T_{out} + T_{comm} \\
         &= 2 + 3.2 + 0.75 + 2.1 + 3 \\
         &= 11.05 \, \text{ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** $\boxed{T_{scan} = 11.05 \, \text{ms}}$

This is well within the typical 10–50 ms range for industrial PLCs.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Worst-Case I/O Response Time

**Given:** Scan time $T_{scan} = 11.05 \, \text{ms}$ and input scan phase $T_{in} = 3.2 \, \text{ms}$.

**Step 1 — Define the worst-case scenario:**

An input transitions *just after* the input scan has completed. The PLC will:
- **Miss it** in the current scan's input phase.
- **Detect it** at the *next* scan's input phase.

**Step 2 — Express worst-case response:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{resp,max} &= (T_{scan} - T_{in}) + T_{scan} \\
             &= 2 \cdot T_{scan} - T_{in}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Logic:* First, wait out the remainder of the current scan $(T_{scan} - T_{in})$, then complete a full additional scan $T_{scan}$.

**Step 3 — Substitute numerical values:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{resp,max} &= 2(11.05) - 3.2 \\
             &= 22.10 - 3.2 \\
             &= 18.90 \, \text{ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** $\boxed{T_{resp,max} = 18.90 \, \text{ms}}$

For a high-speed packaging line with parts arriving every 25 ms, this is acceptable. For a 1 kHz encoder pulse train, a **high-speed counter module** is mandatory.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Sampling Frequency and Nyquist Limit

**Given:** A temperature transmitter in a furnace has a signal varying with a maximum frequency component of $f_m = 0.5 \, \text{Hz}$ (slow process).

**Step 1 — Compute Nyquist frequency:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_N &= 2 \cdot f_m \\
    &= 2 \times 0.5 \\
    &= 1.0 \, \text{Hz}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Logic:* Shannon's sampling theorem requires sampling at *at least* twice the highest signal frequency for lossless reconstruction.

**Step 2 — Determine minimum scan rate:**

$$
f_s \geq f_N \quad \Rightarrow \quad f_s \geq 1.0 \, \text{Hz}
$$

**Step 3 — Express as a maximum allowed scan time:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{scan,max} &= \frac{1}{f_s} \\
             &= \frac{1}{1.0} \\
             &= 1.0 \, \text{s}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Logic:* The analog-to-digital converter (ADC) must be polled at least once per second.

**Result:** $\boxed{f_s \geq 1 \, \text{Hz}, \quad T_{scan,max} \leq 1 \, \text{s}}$

Since the PLC's main scan runs at 10–50 ms, the analog input is oversampled — which is good for noise rejection.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Input/Output Module Selection

| Application | Sensor / Actuator | Required Module | Why |
|---|---|---|---|
| Conveyor start/stop | Pushbutton (24 V DC) | **Digital DC Input (24 V)** | Discrete ON/OFF signal |
| Motor starter coil | Contactor (230 V AC) | **Digital AC Output / Relay** | AC load, high current |
| Bottle count | Proximity sensor (24 V DC, PNP) | **Digital DC Input** | Discrete signal |
| Tank level control | Ultrasonic sensor (4–20 mA) | **Analog Input (4–20 mA)** | Continuous variable |
| Heater control | SSR / Triac | **Digital AC Output (TRIAC)** | AC zero-cross switching |
| Variable speed pump | VFD (4–20 mA reference) | **Analog Output (4–20 mA)** | Continuous setpoint |
| Bottle filling solenoid | 24 V DC solenoid | **Digital DC Output (Transistor)** | DC inductive load |

---

### 3.5 Boolean Logic Derivation — Traffic Light Ladder Logic

**Problem:** A pedestrian crossing must turn the vehicle green OFF only when the *cross button* is pressed *AND* the *timer* is 30 s *AND* *no emergency vehicle* is detected.

**Step 1 — Define inputs and outputs:**

| Symbol | Meaning |
|---|---|
| $X_1$ | Pedestrian cross button |
| $X_2$ | Timer (T30s) reaches preset |
| $X_3$ | Emergency vehicle sensor |
| $Y_1$ | Vehicle Green Lamp |
| $Y_2$ | Pedestrian Walk Lamp |

**Step 2 — Write Boolean output equation:**

$$
Y_2 = X_1 \cdot X_2 \cdot \overline{X_3}
$$

**Step 3 — Derive complementary output:**

The vehicle green should be OFF whenever the pedestrian walk is ON, so:

$$
Y_1 = \overline{Y_2} = \overline{X_1 \cdot X_2 \cdot \overline{X_3}}
$$

**Step 4 — Apply De Morgan's theorem for implementation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Y_1 &= \overline{X_1} + \overline{X_2} + X_3
\end{aligned}
$$

*Logic:* Vehicle green is ON if: button is NOT pressed, OR timer has NOT elapsed, OR an emergency vehicle IS present. Ladder logic implements this with one NC (normally closed) contact for each of $X_1$, $X_2$, and one NO (normally open) contact for $X_3$, all in parallel — energizing coil $Y_1$.

---

### 3.6 Reliability Calculation — MTBF

**Given:** A PLC has a documented failure rate $\lambda = 1.2 \times 10^{-6}$ failures/hour.

$$
\begin{aligned}
MTBF &= \frac{1}{\lambda} \\
     &= \frac{1}{1.2 \times 10^{-6}} \\
     &= 8.33 \times 10^{5} \, \text{hours} \\
     &= \approx 95 \, \text{years}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Engineering interpretation:* This is the *theoretical* operating life between inherent failures. In practice, environmental derating, capacitor aging, and solder fatigue reduce this. Industrial PLCs are typically rated for a **service life of 10–15 years** before preventive replacement.

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Overall PLC Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    PSU[Power Supply Unit 230V AC to 5V 24V DC]
    CPU[Central Processing Unit ALU and Control Unit]
    ROM[Firmware ROM Operating System]
    RAM[User Program RAM Battery Backed]
    IIT[Input Image Table in RAM]
    OIT[Output Image Table in RAM]
    INMOD[Input Module Signal Conditioning and Isolation]
    OUTMOD[Output Module Driver and Isolation]
    FIELD_IN[Field Sensors Pushbutton Limit Switch Sensor]
    FIELD_OUT[Field Actuators Contactor Solenoid Lamp]
    PROG[Programming Device PC or HMI]
    BUS[Backplane Bus Address Data and Control Lines]

    PSU --> CPU
    PSU --> INMOD
    PSU --> OUTMOD
    ROM --> CPU
    RAM --> CPU
    IIT --> CPU
    OIT --> CPU
    CPU --> IIT
    CPU --> OIT
    FIELD_IN --> INMOD
    INMOD --> IIT
    OIT --> OUTMOD
    OUTMOD --> FIELD_OUT
    PROG --> BUS
    CPU --> BUS
    INMOD --> BUS
    OUTMOD --> BUS
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Flowchart — The PLC Scan Cycle

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start of Scan Cycle] --> B[Self Test and Diagnostics]
    B --> C{All Tests Pass?}
    C -- No --> Z[Error LED and Stop CPU]
    C -- Yes --> D[INPUT SCAN: Read all field inputs]
    D --> E[Store values in Input Image Table IIT]
    E --> F[PROGRAM SCAN: Execute ladder logic using IIT]
    F --> G[Update Output Image Table OIT based on logic results]
    G --> H[OUTPUT SCAN: Transfer OIT to output terminals]
    H --> I[Actuators receive new control signals]
    I --> J[Housekeeping: Handle HMI, Network, Interrupts]
    J --> A
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Functional Flow — Input Module Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    FS[Field Sensor 24V or 4-20mA] --> TB[Terminal Block]
    TB --> FIL[Input Filter RC Debounce 5 to 20ms]
    FIL --> SC[Signal Conditioning Level Shifting]
    SC --> ISO[Optical Isolator 1500V Galvanic Isolation]
    ISO --> LED[Status Indicator LED]
    ISO --> REG[Input Register in IIT]
    REG --> CPU[CPU Reads during Input Scan Phase]
```

### 4.4 Mermaid Functional Flow — Output Module Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    CPU[CPU Writes OIT after Program Scan] --> REG[Output Register OIT]
    REG --> ISO2[Optical Isolator Galvanic Separation]
    ISO2 --> DRV[Driver Stage Relay or Transistor or Triac]
    DRV --> PROT[Snubber or Flyback Diode Protection]
    PROT --> LED2[Status Indicator LED]
    LED2 --> TB2[Terminal Block]
    TB2 --> ACT[Field Actuator 24V DC Solenoid or 230V AC Contactor]
```

### 4.5 Mermaid State Diagram — I/O Signal Processing States

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Idle
    Idle --> ReadingInput: Input Scan Phase
    ReadingInput --> ProgramExecution: IIT Updated
    ProgramExecution --> OutputUpdate: Logic Evaluated
    OutputUpdate --> ActuatorActive: OIT Transferred
    ActuatorActive --> Idle: Cycle Restart
    ReadingInput --> Fault: Sensor Open or Short Circuit
    OutputUpdate --> Fault: Actuator Overload
    Fault --> [*]
```

### 4.6 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Closed-Loop PLC System

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    OP[Operator HMI Panel] --> CMD[Command Input Start Stop Setpoint]
    CMD --> PLC[PLC with Ladder Logic]
    PROC[Process: Tank Motor Conveyor] --> FB[Feedback Sensor Temperature Level Speed]
    FB --> SIG[Signal Conditioner 4-20mA 0-10V]
    SIG --> PLC
    PLC --> ACT[Actuator Heater Pump Valve]
    ACT --> PROC
    PLC --> OP
```
