```text
       └───| |─── (seal-in from M_CYCLE_ACTIVE)
                broken by ESTOP or end-of-cycle for single-shot mode.

RUNG 2: Inlet valve energised when tank is not full
   M_CYCLE_ACTIVE     LOW_LEVEL     HIGH_LEVEL
───| |──────────────| / |──────────[TON T_FILL, PT=T#60s]──┬──( ) INLET Q0.0──
                                                            │
   MIXER_RUNNING ──|/|─── (blocks fill while mixer runs)    │
                                                            │
   OUTLET_RUNNING ──|/|─── (blocks fill while draining)     │
                                                            │
   T_FILL.Q ──|/|─── (block fill if alarm has tripped)      │

RUNG 3: When HIGH_LEVEL reached, stop inlet and start mixer
   INLET      HIGH_LEVEL     ESTOP
───| |──────| |──────| / |────────(S) M_MIXER_ACTIVE─────────
                AND (R) INLET Q0.0

RUNG 4: Mixer runs for 30 s
   M_MIXER_ACTIVE     ESTOP
───| |──────| / |──────[TON T_MIX, PT=T#30s]───────( ) MIXER Q0.1──
                                       │
                                       └── T_MIX.Q triggers outlet phase.

RUNG 5: Outlet valve opens when mixer finishes
   T_MIX.Q         ESTOP
───| |──────| / |──────(S) M_OUTLET_ACTIVE──────────────────

RUNG 6: Outlet runs for 15 s then ends cycle
   M_OUTLET_ACTIVE      ESTOP
───| |──────| / |──────[TON T_OUTLET, PT=T#15s]─────
   T_OUTLET.Q ──(R) M_OUTLET_ACTIVE──(R) M_MIXER_ACTIVE──
   AUTO_MODE ──| |── (R) M_CYCLE_ACTIVE (only if NOT auto)
```

**Marks Distribution (a):**
- Correct I/O mapping table — 1 mark
- Inlet valve conditional logic — 2 marks
- Mixer timer 30 s — 2 marks
- Outlet timer 15 s + cycle completion — 1 mark
- AUTO_MODE branch — 1 mark

**(b) 60-Second Fill Alarm — Rungs:**

```text
RUNG 7: Watchdog timer for fill phase
   INLET_Q0.0
───| |─────────────[TON T_FILL_ALARM, PT=T#60s]──────

RUNG 8: Latch buzzer when watchdog expires (Set-Dominant for safety)
   T_FILL_ALARM.Q
───| |────────────────────(S) BUZZER Q0.3─────────────────
                           │
                           AND inhibit filling (see Rung 2).

RUNG 9: Operator acknowledgement
   BUZZER_ACK      ESTOP
───| |──────| / |────────(R) BUZZER Q0.3─────────────────
───| |──────| / |────────[RES T_FILL_ALARM]──────────────
```

**Boolean Equation:**

$$
Q_{0.3} = \big( T_{FILL\_ALARM} \cdot Q \big) + Q_{0.3}
$$

$$
Q_{0.3} \cdot \overline{BUZZER\_ACK} \cdot \overline{ESTOP} \implies Q_{0.3} = 0
$$

**Marks Distribution (b):**
- Watchdog timer T_FILL_ALARM = 60 s — 2 marks
- Buzzer Set-Dominant latch — 2 marks
- Inhibition of filling when alarm active — 1 mark
- Acknowledgement and reset logic — 2 marks

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Triggers in Ladder Program Questions (based on KTU 2018–2024 valuation trends):**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to debounce inputs** — KTU expects either a **TON with PT = 50 ms** or an **edge detector** on every mechanical push-button. -1 mark per missed debounce.
> 2. **Using NO instead of NC for ESTOP** — Always assume **fail-safe**: ESTOP and overload contacts MUST be NC in the schematic, appearing as `|/|` in the ladder. Wrong = -2 marks.
> 3. **Missing electrical interlock in reversing circuits** — Forward and reverse contactors must have **each other's NC contacts** in series. Missing interlock = full 0 on the safety sub-part.
> 4. **No reset path for counters and latches** — Every `CTU` must have an explicit `[RES]` or `(R)` coil. Examiners dock 1 mark per dangling counter.
> 5. **Seal-in written but not labelled** — Just drawing parallel branches without naming the auxiliary contact (e.g., `M0.0 aux NO`) loses 1 mark for "lack of clarity".
> 6. **Mixing scan-time dependent logic with millisecond timers** — A timer below 2× the scan time (~20 ms) will be unreliable. Mention this if asked.

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — "Ladder Programs for Specific Purposes"**

- 🔹 A ladder program has **two vertical power rails** and **horizontal rungs** — each rung is one Boolean statement.
- 🔹 **NO contact `| |`** closes when input is TRUE; **NC contact `|/|`** opens when input is TRUE.
- 🔹 **Seal-in (latching)** is implemented by paralleling a NO aux contact of the output coil with the START button.
- 🔹 **STOP / ESTOP / Overload** must always be **NC contacts** to ensure fail-safe behaviour.
- 🔹 **TON** delays the rising edge; **TOF** delays the falling edge. Both have `IN`, `PT`, `ET`, `Q`.
- 🔹 **CTU** counts up on rising edges; **RES** or `(R)` must explicitly clear the counter.
- 🔹 **Set coil `(S)`** latches; **Reset coil `(R)`** unlatches. Use **Reset-Dominant** for safety circuits.
- 🔹 **Edge detectors** (`|P|`, `|N|`) produce a one-scan pulse; essential for counting push-button presses reliably.
- 🔹 For **reversing motors**, include BOTH **electrical** (NC contacts) AND **mechanical** (NC contacts) interlocks.
- 🔹 For **state-based sequences** (traffic lights), use **memory bits** as state flags — one bit SET at a time.
- 🔹 **Watchdog timers** (alarm timers) protect against sensor or actuator failures by latching a fault if a phase exceeds a sane duration.
- 🔹 Every rung must be **independently readable**; group related rungs and add **comments** (KTU rewards clear presentation).
- 🔹 **Boolean equations** `Y = A·B + C` map to ladder as: **series** `A` and `B`, then **parallel** with `C`, driving coil `Y`.
- 🔹 Typical scan time = **10 ms** — design timers with PT ≥ 2 × scan time to guarantee one full execution.
- 🔹 KTU **2024 Scheme** marks distribution for Module 3 ladder programs is usually **7 marks per sub-question** with **rung diagram (3 marks) + Boolean explanation (2 marks) + truth table / state table (2 marks)**.
</mm:think><!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Microprocessor Architecture
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMUT602 - MECHATRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Microprocessor Architecture

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Microprocessor** is a single-chip, large-scale integrated (LSI) circuit that contains the **Central Processing Unit (CPU)** of a computer on a single semiconductor substrate. It integrates the **Arithmetic Logic Unit (ALU)**, the **Control Unit (CU)**, the **register array**, and the **internal bus interface** onto one monolithic silicon die using VLSI (Very Large Scale Integration) technology.

In the context of Mechatronics (KTU PCMUT602), the microprocessor acts as the **central decision-making and computational core** of an embedded or electromechanical system. It executes programmed instructions sequentially (or with pipelining) to interpret sensor data, drive actuators, and coordinate communication between subsystems.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):**
> The syllabus specifically expects students to understand the **internal architecture** (not just the external pin diagram) of a representative 8-bit microprocessor — typically the **Intel 8085** — along with its **register organization, bus structure, memory addressing, and instruction execution cycle**.

## 1.2 Real-World Analogy — The "Factory Manager"

Imagine a modern automated bottling plant:

| Plant Component | Microprocessor Equivalent | Function |
|---|---|---|
| Factory Manager | **Control Unit (CU)** | Issues orders, sequences tasks |
| Quality Inspector & Calculator | **Arithmetic Logic Unit (ALU)** | Performs math and logic checks |
| Manager's Notebook | **Registers (A, B, C, etc.)** | Temporarily holds data being worked on |
| Conveyor Belt System | **Data Bus** | Carries bottles (data) to/from stations |
| Address Labels on Boxes | **Address Bus** | Specifies *which* memory location |
| Stop / Start / Reset Buttons | **Control Bus** | Synchronizes overall operation |
| Warehouse | **Memory (RAM/ROM)** | Stores raw materials (program + data) |

Just as a factory manager reads a checklist, asks workers to bring specific boxes (addressing), inspects them (ALU operations), and writes results back into the logbook (registers), the microprocessor fetches instructions, decodes them, performs operations, and stores results.

## 1.3 The 8085 — Our Reference Architecture

The **Intel 8085** is an **8-bit microprocessor** that became the de-facto teaching reference for KTU and most Indian engineering curricula.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key 8085 Specifications (Must Memorize for KTU):**
> - **Data width:** **8 bits**
> - **Address width:** **16 bits** → Addressable memory = $2^{16}$ = **65,536 bytes = 64 KB**
> - **Pin count:** 40 pins (DIP package)
> - **Supply voltage:** Single **+5 V**
> - **Clock:** Internal oscillator; external crystal **6.144 MHz** (internally divided by 2 → **3.072 MHz** internal clock)
> - **Instruction set:** **246 instructions** with **74 opcodes**
> - **Technology:** NMOS

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Internal Sub-Systems

The 8085 microprocessor is partitioned into three principal functional units:

### A. The Arithmetic Logic Unit (ALU)
- Performs **arithmetic operations**: addition, subtraction, increment, decrement.
- Performs **logical operations**: AND, OR, XOR, NOT, compare.
- Performs **rotate** and **complement** operations.
- The result is always stored in the **Accumulator (Register A)**.
- The ALU updates the **Flag Register** (status register) after every operation.

### B. The Register Array
The 8085 contains **seven 8-bit general-purpose registers** that can also be paired as **four 16-bit registers** for memory operations.

> [!NOTE]
> **Register Pair Grouping Rule:** Registers are *paired in the order B-C, D-E, H-L*. The pair is named by the higher-order register. So `BC`, `DE`, and `HL` are valid 16-bit pairs.

#### General-Purpose 8-Bit Registers

| Register | Size | Primary Use in KTU Context |
|---|---|---|
| **A** (Accumulator) | 8-bit | Default operand + result storage for ALL ALU operations |
| **B, C, D, E, H, L** | 8-bit (×6) | Temporary data storage; can be paired to hold 16-bit memory addresses (H-L most commonly) |

#### Special-Purpose Registers (Critical for KTU)

| Register | Size | Function | KTU Exam Relevance |
|---|---|---|---|
| **PC** (Program Counter) | 16-bit | Holds address of the **next** instruction to be fetched. Auto-increments after each fetch. | **Very High** |
| **SP** (Stack Pointer) | 16-bit | Points to the **top of the stack** in RAM. Decremented on PUSH, incremented on POP. | **Very High** |
| **Flag Register** (F) | 8-bit (5 active flags) | Stores status bits from the last ALU operation. | **Very High** |

#### The Flag Register Layout

The 8085 Flag register is 8 bits wide, but only **5 bits are actively used**. The remaining 3 are reserved (always read as `1` for S80/85, or as `0` in 8085A variants — verify with your textbook).

| Bit | D7 | D6 | D5 | D4 | D3 | D2 | D1 | D0 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Flag | **S** | **Z** | | **AC** |   | **P** |   | **CY** |
| Name | Sign | Zero | (Reserved) | Auxiliary Carry | (Reserved) | Parity | (Reserved) | Carry |

| Flag | Set When (`=1`) | Cleared When (`=0`) |
|---|---|---|
| **S** (Sign) | Result has MSB = 1 (negative in signed interpretation) | Result has MSB = 0 |
| **Z** (Zero) | Result is exactly 0 | Result is non-zero |
| **AC** (Aux Carry) | Carry generated from **bit 3 → bit 4** (used in BCD arithmetic) | No such carry |
| **P** (Parity) | Result has an **even** number of 1s | Odd number of 1s |
| **CY** (Carry) | Carry generated from **MSB (bit 7)** | No MSB carry |

### C. The Control Unit (CU) and Timing
- Decodes the fetched instruction (opcode).
- Generates the precise sequence of **control signals** (RD̄, WR̄, IO/M̄, S1, S0) at the correct timing intervals.
- Synchronizes with the system clock using a **Timing and Control block**.

## 2.2 The Bus System

The 8085 communicates with the outside world via three buses:

| Bus | Width | Direction | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Address Bus** | **16-bit** (A0–A15) | Unidirectional (CPU → Memory/I/O) | Carries the memory address of the location being accessed |
| **Data Bus** | **8-bit** (D0–D7, multiplexed with A0–A7) | Bidirectional | Carries the actual data (instruction opcode or operand) |
| **Control Bus** | Mixed | Mixed | Carries timing and command signals: RD̄, WR̄, ALE, IO/M̄, S0, S1, READY, RESET, etc. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Bus Multiplexing:** Pins **AD0–AD7** of the 8085 carry **both** the lower 8 bits of the address AND the 8-bit data, but at *different times*. The signal **ALE (Address Latch Enable)** tells external latches (like the 74LS373) to capture the address in the first clock cycle, after which the same pins become the data bus.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following formulas are **guaranteed KTU exam material**. Memorize them.

| # | Formula / Expression | Physical Meaning | Unit / Valid Range |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $M_{max} = 2^N$ | Maximum memory capacity for an N-bit address bus | Bytes |
| 2 | $N = \log_2(M)$ | Required address lines for M memory locations | Pure number (round UP) |
| 3 | $T_{inst} = k \cdot T_{clk}$ | Instruction execution time (k = T-states per instruction, $T_{clk}$ = clock period) | Seconds |
| 4 | $f_{int} = \dfrac{f_{xtal}}{2}$ | 8085 internal clock frequency = half of crystal frequency | Hz |
| 5 | $T_{clk} = \dfrac{1}{f_{int}}$ | One T-state (basic clock period inside CPU) | Seconds |
| 6 | $E = \sum_{i=1}^{n} k_i \cdot T_{clk}$ | Total execution time for *n* instructions in sequence | Seconds |
| 7 | $A_{eff} = A_{15}A_{14}\ldots A_{1}A_{0}$ | 16-bit effective address formed on the address bus | Hex address |
| 8 | $P_{stack\;loss} = 2 \cdot (\text{number of CALL/PUSH operations})$ | Stack pointer decrement per push (16-bit → 2 bytes) | Bytes |

> **Engineering Utility:** In Mechatronics, the microprocessor is interfaced with **sensors** (via ADC), **actuators** (via drivers), and **communication buses** (UART, SPI, I²C). Knowing the bus width and clock timing lets the engineer predict whether the processor can sample a sensor fast enough for real-time control of a motor or robotic arm.

## 2.4 Why "Architecture" Matters in Mechatronics

The choice between a **Von Neumann** and **Harvard** architecture (discussed in Section 4) determines whether a controller is suitable for **deterministic real-time control**. Most modern microcontrollers used in mechatronic systems (PIC, ARM Cortex-M, AVR) use **modified Harvard** to allow simultaneous instruction and data fetches — critical for fast servo loops.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Maximum Memory Capacity of the 8085

The 8085 has a **16-bit address bus**, meaning it can emit $2^{16}$ distinct binary addresses.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Number of unique addresses} &= 2^{N} \\
&= 2^{16} \\
&= 65{,}536 \text{ locations}
\end{aligned}
$$

Since each location holds **1 byte (8 bits)** of data, total memory capacity is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_{max} &= 2^{16} \times 1 \text{ byte} \\
&= 65{,}536 \text{ bytes} \\
&= \dfrac{65{,}536}{1024} \text{ KB} \\
&= 64 \text{ KB}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Conversion Logic:** $1 \text{ KB} = 2^{10} = 1024 \text{ bytes}$. Dividing by 1024 (not 1000) is the standard in digital systems engineering.

**Result:** $M_{max} = \mathbf{64 \text{ KB}}$

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Instruction Execution Time

In the 8085, each clock cycle of the **internal** clock corresponds to one **T-state**. An instruction requires a fixed number of T-states ($k$).

Given: External crystal frequency $f_{xtal} = 6.144 \text{ MHz}$.

**Step 1 — Find internal clock frequency:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_{int} &= \dfrac{f_{xtal}}{2} \\
&= \dfrac{6.144 \text{ MHz}}{2} \\
&= 3.072 \text{ MHz}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Find one T-state duration:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{clk} &= \dfrac{1}{f_{int}} \\
&= \dfrac{1}{3.072 \times 10^{6}} \text{ s} \\
&= 0.3255 \text{ \mu s} \\
&\approx 0.326 \text{ \mu s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply to a sample instruction.**

Take the instruction **MVI A, 32H** (Move immediate 32H to Accumulator), which requires **7 T-states** in the 8085.

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{inst} &= k \cdot T_{clk} \\
&= 7 \times 0.326 \text{ \mu s} \\
&= 2.282 \text{ \mu s}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **KTU Exam Tip:** The number of T-states is **opcode-specific**. Common values you should know: NOP = 4, MVI = 7, MOV r1,r2 = 4, ADD r = 4, JMP = 10, CALL = 18, RET = 10.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Stack Pointer Behaviour on PUSH and POP

The stack in the 8085 grows **downwards** (toward lower addresses). When a 16-bit register pair is pushed:

**PUSH B (pushes BC onto the stack):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
(SP) &\leftarrow (SP) - 1 \\
M(SP) &\leftarrow (C) \quad \text{(low byte written first)} \\
(SP) &\leftarrow (SP) - 1 \\
M(SP) &\leftarrow (B) \quad \text{(high byte written second)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**POP D (pops top of stack into DE):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
(E) &\leftarrow M(SP) \quad \text{(low byte read first)} \\
(SP) &\leftarrow (SP) + 1 \\
(D) &\leftarrow M(SP) \quad \text{(high byte read second)} \\
(SP) &\leftarrow (SP) + 1
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Logic Explanation:** Because 8085 is **little-endian**, the **low byte** is always stored at the **lower** address. PUSH decrements SP *twice* because two bytes (16 bits) are being stored.

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Required Address Lines for a Given Memory

**Problem:** A mechatronic controller needs to interface **8 KB of external SRAM**. How many address lines must be dedicated to the SRAM chip?

$$
\begin{aligned}
M &= 8 \text{ KB} = 8 \times 1024 = 8192 \text{ bytes} \\
N &= \log_2(M) \\
&= \log_2(8192) \\
&= \log_2(2^{13}) \\
&= 13
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** **13 address lines** (e.g., A0–A12) are required. The remaining 3 lines (A13–A15) can be used for **chip selection** decoding.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Flag Register After Sample Operations

Starting flag state: All flags = 0. Accumulator initially = `F5H` (`1111 0101`).

| Operation | Result (Hex) | Result (Binary) | S | Z | AC | P | CY |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| `ADD A` (F5H + F5H) | `EAH` | `1110 1010` | **1** | 0 | 0 | 0 | **1** |
| `ANA A` (F5H AND F5H) | `F5H` | `1111 0101` | **1** | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 |
| `XRA A` (F5H XOR F5H) | `00H` | `0000 0000` | 0 | **1** | 0 | **1** | 0 |
| `CMA` (complement of F5H) | `0AH` | `0000 1010` | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 |

> **Logic for `ADD A`:** F5H + F5H = 1EAH. The lower 8 bits are EAH = `1110 1010`. Bit 7 = 1 → S=1. Bit 3→4: bit 3 = 1, bit 4 = 0, but addition was 1+1 at bit 4 → carry out to bit 5, hence AC = 0. Carry from bit 7 (1+1+0) = 1 → CY=1. Even number of 1s in EAH (five 1s — odd) → P=0.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Intel 8085

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    X1["External Crystal 6.144 MHz"]
    CLK["Clock and Timing Generator"]
    CU["Control Unit - Decodes opcode and generates control signals"]
    IR["Instruction Register - Holds current opcode"]
    DR["Instruction Decoder"]

    subgraph ALU_BLOCK ["Arithmetic Logic Unit"]
        ALU["8-bit ALU - Add Subtract AND OR XOR Compare Rotate"]
        ACC["Accumulator A - 8 bit default operand and result"]
        TMP["Temporary Register - 8 bit hidden to programmer"]
    end

    subgraph REGS ["Register Array - 8 bit x 7 visible registers"]
        B["B register"]
        C["C register"]
        D["D register"]
        E["E register"]
        H["H register"]
        L["L register"]
    end

    FLG["Flag Register - 5 active flags S Z AC P CY"]
    PC["Program Counter PC - 16 bit points to next instruction"]
    SP["Stack Pointer SP - 16 bit points to top of stack"]

    subgraph BUS_EXT ["External Interface - Multiplexed"]
        AD["AD0 to AD7 - Address and Data multiplexed"]
        A8_15["A8 to A15 - Upper address bus"]
        CTRLBUS["Control Bus - RD WR ALE IO/M S0 S1 READY RESET"]
    end

    INC_DEC["Increment or Decrement Logic for PC and SP"]

    X1 --> CLK
    CLK --> CU
    CLK --> ALU
    CLK --> INC_DEC

    CU --> IR
    IR --> DR
    DR --> CTRLBUS

    CU --> ALU
    ALU <--> ACC
    ALU <--> TMP
    ACC <--> FLG

    ACC <--> REGS
    REGS --> ALU
    PC --> AD
    PC --> A8_15
    SP --> AD
    SP --> A8_15
    INC_DEC --> PC
    INC_DEC --> SP
    ACC --> AD
    AD --> CTRLBUS
    A8_15 --> CTRLBUS
```

## 4.2 Von Neumann vs Harvard Architecture — Comparison Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Q{"How are instruction and data buses organized?"}
    Q -->|"Single shared memory and bus"| VN["Von Neumann Architecture"]
    Q -->|"Separate instruction and data memories and buses"| HV["Harvard Architecture"]

    VN --> VN1["Pros - Simpler hardware, cheaper, flexible memory allocation"]
    VN --> VN2["Cons - Bottleneck - instruction fetch and data access cannot occur simultaneously - called the Von Neumann bottleneck"]
    VN --> VN3["Used in - Desktop CPUs, most general purpose processors, 8086 in Von Neumann mode"]

    HV --> HV1["Pros - Simultaneous fetch and data access - faster deterministic timing"]
    HV --> HV2["Cons - More pins, more complex, dual memory paths"]
    HV --> HV3["Used in - DSP chips, microcontrollers PIC AVR, ARM Cortex M uses Modified Harvard"]

    style VN fill:#fef3c7
    style HV fill:#dbeafe
```

## 4.3 Fetch-Decode-Execute Cycle State Machine

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Reset
    Reset --> Fetch1: PC loaded with start address
    Fetch1: T1 - Address placed on AD0-AD7 and A8-A15, ALE high
    Fetch1 --> Fetch2
    Fetch2: T2 - ALE low, RD low, memory places opcode on data bus
    Fetch2 --> Fetch3
    Fetch3: T3 - Opcode latched into Instruction Register, RD high
    Fetch3 --> Decode
    Decode: Decode opcode, determine instruction length and operands
    Decode --> OperandFetch: instruction needs operand
    Decode --> Execute: no operand needed
    OperandFetch: Fetch operand byte or bytes from memory
    OperandFetch --> Execute
    Execute: ALU performs operation, registers updated, flags updated
    Execute --> StoreResult: result needs writing to memory
    StoreResult: Write result back to memory or register
    StoreResult --> IncrementPC: PC points to next instruction
    IncrementPC --> Fetch1
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the State Diagram:** Each box labelled T1, T2, T3 represents one **T-state**. A typical instruction like MVI requires: **Fetch (3 T-states) → Operand Fetch (3 T-states) → Execute (1 T-state) = 7 T-states**, matching the value used in Section 3.2.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, May 2024]`

**Explain the function of the Program Counter (PC) and the Stack Pointer (SP) in the 8085 microprocessor. How does the SP behave during a PUSH and a POP operation?**

**Model Answer:**

The **Program Counter (PC)** is a **16-bit register** that always holds the **memory address of the next instruction** to be fetched. After each instruction byte is fetched, the PC is automatically incremented by one. It is therefore the "instruction pointer" of the 8085 — it tells the CPU *where* in program memory to look next.

The **Stack Pointer (SP)** is also a **16-bit register**, but it points to the **topmost occupied location of the stack** in RAM. The 8085 stack grows **downward** (toward lower addresses).

- During a **PUSH** of a register pair, SP is **decremented twice** (once per byte) and the high and low bytes of the pair are written into successive memory locations starting at the new SP.
- During a **POP**, the two bytes at SP are read (low byte first, then high byte) and loaded into the destination register pair, after which SP is **incremented twice**.

Together, PC manages **sequential program flow**, while SP manages **temporary data storage and subroutine returns**.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Nov 2024]`

**List any five flags of the 8085 microprocessor and state the condition under which each flag is set.**

**Model Answer:**

| # | Flag | Full Name | Condition for Set (`= 1`) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **S** | Sign Flag | MSB of the result is 1 (result is negative in signed interpretation) |
| 2 | **Z** | Zero Flag | The result of an arithmetic or logical operation is exactly 0 |
| 3 | **AC** | Auxiliary Carry Flag | A carry is generated from **bit 3 to bit 4** during an addition (used in BCD arithmetic) |
| 4 | **P** | Parity Flag | The result contains an **even** number of 1-bits |
| 5 | **CY** | Carry Flag | A carry is generated **out of bit 7** (MSB) during an addition, or a borrow occurs during subtraction |

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Draw and explain the **internal block diagram of the Intel 8085 microprocessor**. Label all major units, buses, and registers. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With the help of a timing diagram, explain the **Fetch operation** of the 8085. Explain why ALE is needed and why the address/data bus is multiplexed. **(7 marks)**

### MODEL SOLUTION for Question A

#### Part (a) — Internal Block Diagram of 8085

The 8085 consists of three major units:

**1. Arithmetic Logic Unit (ALU):**
- Performs 8-bit arithmetic (+, −, INC, DEC), logical (AND, OR, XOR, NOT, Compare), and rotate operations.
- Operands are taken from the Accumulator (A) and a temporary register (hidden to the programmer).
- Result is stored back in A.
- Updates the Flag Register.

**2. Register Array:**

| Register | Width | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| A (Accumulator) | 8-bit | Primary operand and result register |
| B, C, D, E, H, L | 8-bit | Six general-purpose registers; BC, DE, HL usable as 16-bit pairs |
| PC | 16-bit | Holds next instruction address |
| SP | 16-bit | Points to top of stack |
| Flag (F) | 8-bit (5 active) | Stores ALU status bits |

**3. Control Unit and Timing:**
- Includes the Instruction Register, Decoder, and Timing & Control block.
- Generates external control signals: RD̄, WR̄, ALE, IO/M̄, S0, S1, READY, RESET.

**Bus System:**
- **Address Bus:** 16 lines (A0–A15), of which A0–A7 are multiplexed with the data bus on pins AD0–AD7.
- **Data Bus:** 8 lines shared with lower address.
- **Control Bus:** RD̄, WR̄, ALE, IO/M̄, clock, reset, etc.

> See the **Mermaid block diagram in Section 4.1** of these notes for the full graphical depiction expected in the answer script.

---

#### Part (b) — Fetch Operation Timing Diagram

The **Fetch operation** retrieves the opcode of the next instruction from memory. It occupies the **first three T-states (T1, T2, T3)** of every instruction cycle.

| T-State | CPU Action | Signal States |
|---|---|---|
| **T1** | PC contents placed on A8–A15; lower address on AD0–AD7. **ALE = 1** so external latch captures the address. | `ALE = 1`, `IO/M̄ = 0` (memory), `S1 = 1`, `S0 = 1` |
| **T2** | `ALE = 0`; address removed from AD bus; `RD̄ = 0` (read active); memory places instruction opcode on AD0–AD7. | `RD̄ = 0`, data bus floats → input mode |
| **T3** | Opcode latched into the **Instruction Register**; `RD̄ = 1`. | `RD̄ = 1`, opcode now inside CPU |
| **T4 onward** | Instruction decoded; further T-states used for operand fetch or execution as needed. | Controlled by decoder |

**Why is the bus multiplexed?**
The 8085 has only **40 pins** (DIP package). By time-multiplexing the 8 lower address lines with the 8 data lines, Intel saved 8 physical pins. The same physical pins carry the address in T1, then carry data from T2 onward.

**Why is ALE needed?**
Because the address is only on the multiplexed bus during T1, an **external latch** (such as the 74LS373) must capture and hold the address for the rest of the cycle. **ALE = 1** during T1 acts as a *latch enable strobe* telling the external latch: "Sample the address now and hold it."

```
       T1         T2         T3         T4
   ___|________|________|________|________|___
PC →  A8-A15  ─────► held by external latch
AD   A0-A7    data    data     data
ALE  ___|‾‾‾‾‾|______________________________
RD   ‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾|___________|‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾
                ↑ read pulse
IO/M̄ ‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾ (low = memory)
```

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. Many students **forget to state the value of S1 and S0** during the fetch. For an opcode fetch, **S1 = 1 and S0 = 1**. Writing just `IO/M̄ = 0` loses 1 mark.
> 2. Do **not** say "the address remains on the bus throughout." It is **latched externally** — this distinction is what ALE is about.
> 3. The fetch is **always 4 T-states** for the opcode byte (T1, T2, T3, T4 in standard reference; some textbooks count it as 3 or 4 depending on whether T4 is shared with decode). Be consistent with the textbook your module staff prescribes.

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Compare **Von Neumann** and **Harvard** architectures. Which one is used in the 8085 and why? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A mechatronic control system uses an 8085 running at a 4 MHz crystal frequency. Calculate the **(i)** internal clock frequency, **(ii)** duration of one T-state, and **(iii)** time required to execute a `MVI A, data` instruction (7 T-states). **(7 marks)**

### MODEL SOLUTION for Question B

#### Part (a) — Von Neumann vs Harvard Architecture

| Parameter | Von Neumann | Harvard |
|---|---|---|
| **Memory** | Single shared memory for instructions and data | Separate memories for instructions and data |
| **Bus** | Single shared bus for both | Separate instruction and data buses |
| **Speed** | Slower — instruction fetch and data access cannot occur at the same time (the **Von Neumann bottleneck**) | Faster — both can occur simultaneously |
| **Hardware** | Simpler, fewer pins, lower cost | Complex, more pins, higher cost |
| **Flexibility** | Program and data memory can be sized dynamically | Fixed partition between instruction and data memory |
| **Typical Use** | General-purpose CPUs, desktop processors | DSPs, microcontrollers, signal-processing |

**Architecture of 8085:**
The 8085 uses the **Von Neumann** architecture because it has a **single memory address space** of 64 KB and **one set of buses** that are time-multiplexed to carry both instructions and data. There is no physically separate instruction memory — both program code and data reside in the same memory map and are accessed through the same address bus.

#### Part (b) — Timing Calculations

**Given:** Crystal frequency $f_{xtal} = 4 \text{ MHz}$, $k = 7$ T-states for `MVI A, data`.

**(i) Internal clock frequency:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_{int} &= \dfrac{f_{xtal}}{2} \\
&= \dfrac{4 \text{ MHz}}{2} \\
&= 2 \text{ MHz}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Duration of one T-state:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{clk} &= \dfrac{1}{f_{int}} \\
&= \dfrac{1}{2 \times 10^{6}} \text{ s} \\
&= 0.5 \text{ \mu s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Execution time for `MVI A, data` (7 T-states):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{inst} &= k \times T_{clk} \\
&= 7 \times 0.5 \text{ \mu s} \\
&= 3.5 \text{ \mu s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answers:**
- $f_{int} = \mathbf{2 \text{ MHz}}$
- $T_{clk} = \mathbf{0.5 \text{ \mu s}}$
- $T_{MVI} = \mathbf{3.5 \text{ \mu s}}$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Part B (b):**
> 1. **Do not use the crystal frequency directly as the CPU clock.** Always divide by 2 because the 8085's internal oscillator divides the crystal by 2 before generating T-states. Using $T_{clk} = 1/4\text{MHz}$ will lose 2 marks.
> 2. **Show the unit cancellation explicitly.** Writing just "3.5" without "μs" loses 0.5 mark.
> 3. **KTU sometimes expects microseconds AND clock cycles.** Write both: `3.5 μs = 7 T-states`.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Before entering the exam hall, confirm you can tick every box below:**

- [ ] **8085 = 8-bit data, 16-bit address, 40-pin DIP, +5 V, ~246 instructions.**
- [ ] **Addressable memory = $2^{16}$ = 64 KB.**
- [ ] **Register pair rule:** B-C, D-E, H-L only. Cannot pair A with B.
- [ ] **PC holds address of NEXT instruction**, not current one.
- [ ] **SP is decremented on PUSH, incremented on POP** — and the stack grows *downwards*.
- [ ] **Flag register has 5 active bits:** S, Z, AC, P, CY — in that order (D7, D6, D4, D2, D0).
- [ ] **Auxiliary Carry is from bit 3 → bit 4**; main Carry is from bit 7 → out.
- [ ] **Bus multiplexing:** AD0–AD7 carries lower address in T1, then data from T2.
- [ ] **ALE = high in T1** tells external latch to capture the address.
- [ ] **Fetch cycle occupies the first 3 T-states** of every instruction (T1, T2, T3).
- [ ] **Internal clock = crystal / 2.** $T_{clk} = 1/f_{int}$.
- [ ] **Instruction time = (T-states) × (T-state duration).**
- [ ] **Architecture:** 8085 uses **Von Neumann**; modern mechatronic microcontrollers (PIC, ARM) use **Modified Harvard**.
- [ ] **Memorize T-state counts** for NOP (4), MOV r,r (4), MVI (7), ADD (4), JMP (10), CALL (18), RET (10).
- [ ] **Sign Flag = copy of MSB of result**, not really about "positive vs negative" but about bit-7 state.
- [ ] **Parity = even** when set (counter-intuitive but standard).

**Quick-formula flashcards:**
- $M_{max} = 2^N$ bytes
- $N = \lceil \log_2 M \rceil$
- $f_{int} = f_{xtal}/2$
- $T_{inst} = k/f_{int}$
- Stack grows down: SP decreases on PUSH, increases on POP.

**Last-minute tip:** In KTU answer scripts, always pair a **diagram** with a **labelled table** for architecture questions. Examiners reward visual clarity — a clean block diagram + a 3-column register table typically secures full marks for 7-mark sub-parts.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Open and Closed Loop Control Strategies
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMUT602 - MECHATRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Open and Closed Loop Control Strategies

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Open Loop Control System — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition**
> An **Open Loop Control System** is a non-feedback control configuration in which the control action is entirely independent of the output. The system applies a predetermined input signal, and the resulting output is neither measured nor compared with the reference (desired) value. There is **no feedback path** from output to input.

**Key Characteristics (K1 — Recall Level):**
- No feedback element.
- Output has **no influence** on the input.
- Accuracy depends entirely on the **calibration of components**.
- Susceptible to **disturbances and parameter variations**.
- Simpler, cheaper, and faster response (no feedback delay).

### 1.2 Closed Loop Control System — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition**
> A **Closed Loop Control System** (also called a **Feedback Control System**) is a control configuration in which the output is continuously measured, fed back through a feedback element, and compared with the reference input. The resulting **error signal** drives the controller to minimize the deviation between desired and actual output.

**Key Characteristics:**
- Contains a **feedback path** (sensor → comparator).
- Uses an **error detector** to compute `e(t) = r(t) − c(t)`.
- Self-correcting and disturbance-rejecting.
- More complex, costlier, but **far more accurate** and reliable.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Kerala Weather" Way 🏖️

| System | Real-World Analogy | Logic |
|---|---|---|
| **Open Loop** | An old **ceiling fan with a regulator knob**. You set the knob to position 3, and it stays there. If the room gets hotter, the fan does **nothing** to compensate. | Fixed input, no awareness of output. |
| **Closed Loop** | A **modern Inverter AC with a thermostat**. You set 24 °C, the AC measures room temperature via a sensor, compares it, and **automatically adjusts compressor speed** to maintain 24 °C. | Constant sensing, comparison, and correction. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Mechatronics Insight:** Closed loop is the **heart of mechatronics** because it integrates **sensors (feedback), controllers (logic), and actuators (correction)** — the three pillars of any mechatronic system.

### 1.4 Standard KTU Engineering Examples

**Open Loop Examples:**
- Automatic washing machine (timed cycles only).
- Electric toaster (fixed timer).
- Traffic light controller.
- Bread toaster, hand dryer, simple fan regulator.

**Closed Loop Examples:**
- Cruise control in cars.
- Temperature-controlled oven/AC.
- Servo motor position control.
- Autopilot in aircraft.
- Voltage stabilizer (servo type).
- Human body temperature regulation (biological feedback).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Anatomy of an Open Loop System

The system is a **unidirectional signal flow chain**:

$$\text{Input } r(t) \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Controller } G_c \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Plant / Process } G_p \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Output } c(t)$$

Mathematically:

$$c(t) = G(s) \cdot r(t)$$

Where `G(s)` is the **forward path transfer function**.

### 2.2 Anatomy of a Closed Loop System

The system has a **loop (return path)**:

- **Reference input:** `r(t)`
- **Error signal:** `e(t) = r(t) − b(t)` (where `b(t)` is feedback)
- **Controller:** `G_c(s)` processes error
- **Plant:** `G_p(s)` produces output
- **Feedback element:** `H(s)` senses output
- **Output:** `c(t)`

The signal flow in a **unity negative feedback** (H(s) = 1) system is:

$$e(s) = R(s) - C(s) \cdot H(s)$$

$$C(s) = G(s) \cdot E(s)$$

Combining these:

$$C(s) = G(s) \cdot [R(s) - C(s) \cdot H(s)]$$

$$C(s) \cdot [1 + G(s)H(s)] = G(s) \cdot R(s)$$

$$\boxed{\frac{C(s)}{R(s)} = \frac{G(s)}{1 + G(s)H(s)}}$$

This is the **Closed Loop Transfer Function (CLTF)** — the most frequently asked KTU formula.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master These Equations for KTU ESE — Every Single One Has Been Asked Repeatedly**

| **Concept** | **Formula** | **Description** |
|---|---|---|
| Open Loop TF | $G(s) = \dfrac{C(s)}{R(s)}$ | Output-to-input ratio in OL system |
| Error Signal | $e(t) = r(t) - c(t)$ | Difference (unity feedback) |
| Error (General) | $E(s) = R(s) - B(s)$ | General feedback form |
| Closed Loop TF | $\dfrac{C(s)}{R(s)} = \dfrac{G(s)}{1 + G(s)H(s)}$ | **Negative feedback CLTF** |
| Unity Feedback CLTF | $\dfrac{G(s)}{1 + G(s)}$ | When $H(s) = 1$ |
| Positive Feedback CLTF | $\dfrac{G(s)}{1 - G(s)H(s)}$ | Unstable — rarely used |
| Forward Path TF | $G(s) = G_c(s) \cdot G_p(s)$ | Cascade of controller and plant |
| Loop TF (Open Loop of Closed System) | $L(s) = G(s)H(s)$ | Product around the loop |
| Error Ratio | $\dfrac{E(s)}{R(s)} = \dfrac{1}{1 + G(s)H(s)}$ | Tracking accuracy |
| Sensitivity | $S = \dfrac{1}{1 + G(s)H(s)}$ | Robustness to plant variation |

### 2.4 Comparison Table — KTU Board Favourite

> [!NOTE]
> This table is a **guaranteed question** either as a direct Part A or a sub-part of a Part B question. Memorize verbatim.

| **Parameter** | **Open Loop** | **Closed Loop** |
|---|---|---|
| Feedback | Absent | Present (essential) |
| Accuracy | Low — depends on calibration | High — self-correcting |
| Stability | Always stable (no oscillations possible) | Stability analysis required (Routh-Hurwitz) |
| Sensitivity to disturbances | High — no compensation | Low — disturbance rejected |
| Cost & complexity | Low | High |
| Bandwidth | Wide (no feedback limiting) | Limited by feedback |
| Response speed | Fast (no loop delay) | Slower (loop delay) |
| Error correction | Impossible | Automatic |
| Example | Toaster, traffic light | AC, cruise control, servo |
| Reliability | Less (drift over time) | More (self-correcting) |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Closed Loop Transfer Function (Standard KTU Problem)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Note:** This derivation is worth **7 marks** if asked as a full Part B sub-question. Show **every step** clearly with arrow-based reasoning.

**Given:** A negative feedback system with:
- Forward path transfer function: `G(s)`
- Feedback path transfer function: `H(s)`
- Reference input: `R(s)`
- Output: `C(s)`
- Feedback signal: `B(s) = H(s) \cdot C(s)`
- Error: `E(s) = R(s) − B(s)`

**Step 1 — Write the error equation** (Output of summing junction):

$$E(s) = R(s) - B(s)$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the feedback definition** `B(s) = H(s) \cdot C(s)`:

$$E(s) = R(s) - H(s) \cdot C(s)$$

**Step 3 — Write the output equation** (Controller + Plant):

$$C(s) = G(s) \cdot E(s)$$

**Step 4 — Substitute `E(s)` from Step 2 into Step 3**:

$$C(s) = G(s) \cdot [R(s) - H(s) \cdot C(s)]$$

**Step 5 — Expand the bracket**:

$$C(s) = G(s) \cdot R(s) - G(s) \cdot H(s) \cdot C(s)$$

**Step 6 — Collect `C(s)` terms on the left-hand side**:

$$C(s) + G(s) \cdot H(s) \cdot C(s) = G(s) \cdot R(s)$$

**Step 7 — Factor out `C(s)`**:

$$C(s) \cdot [1 + G(s) \cdot H(s)] = G(s) \cdot R(s)$$

**Step 8 — Isolate the ratio `C(s)/R(s)`**:

$$\frac{C(s)}{R(s)} = \frac{G(s)}{1 + G(s) \cdot H(s)}$$

$$\boxed{\text{CLTF} = \frac{G(s)}{1 + G(s)H(s)}}$$

### 3.2 Special Case Derivation — Unity Negative Feedback

When `H(s) = 1` (direct feedback, no sensor scaling), substitute into the CLTF:

$$\frac{C(s)}{R(s)}\bigg\vert_{H=1} = \frac{G(s)}{1 + G(s) \cdot 1}$$

$$\boxed{\frac{C(s)}{R(s)} = \frac{G(s)}{1 + G(s)}}$$

**Physical Meaning:** For a unity feedback system, the denominator `1 + G(s)` is called the **characteristic equation**, and its roots determine the **stability** of the closed loop system.

### 3.3 Numerical KTU-Style Solved Example

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem (Typical 7-mark KTU Part B):** For a unity negative feedback system with forward path transfer function
> $G(s) = \dfrac{10}{s(s+2)}$,
> determine the closed loop transfer function and the steady-state error for a unit step input.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Identify parameters.** Unity feedback implies `H(s) = 1`. Forward TF:

$$G(s) = \frac{10}{s(s+2)} = \frac{10}{s^2 + 2s}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the CLTF formula for unity feedback**:

$$\frac{C(s)}{R(s)} = \frac{G(s)}{1 + G(s)}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute `G(s)`**:

$$\frac{C(s)}{R(s)} = \frac{\frac{10}{s(s+2)}}{1 + \frac{10}{s(s+2)}}$$

**Step 4 — Multiply numerator and denominator by `s(s+2)`**:

$$\frac{C(s)}{R(s)} = \frac{10}{s(s+2) + 10}$$

**Step 5 — Expand the denominator**:

$$\frac{C(s)}{R(s)} = \frac{10}{s^2 + 2s + 10}$$

**Step 6 — Steady-state error using Final Value Theorem** (for unit step, `R(s) = 1/s`):

$$e_{ss} = \lim_{s \to 0} s \cdot E(s) = \lim_{s \to 0} s \cdot \frac{R(s)}{1 + G(s)}$$

**Step 7 — Apply the limit**:

$$e_{ss} = \lim_{s \to 0} s \cdot \frac{1/s}{1 + \frac{10}{s(s+2)}} = \lim_{s \to 0} \frac{1}{1 + \frac{10}{s(s+2)}}$$

**Step 8 — Evaluate as `s → 0`**: The term $\dfrac{10}{s(s+2)} \to \infty$, so the denominator → ∞.

$$e_{ss} = \frac{1}{\infty} = 0$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** A **Type-1 system** (one integrator in forward path) yields **zero steady-state error for step input**. This is why controllers add integrators to track constant references.

### 3.4 Effect of Disturbance (Open vs Closed)

Consider a plant `G(s)` with a disturbance `D(s)` entering at the output:

**Open Loop:**

$$C(s) = G(s) \cdot R(s) + D(s)$$

The disturbance **passes straight to output** — no way to reject it.

**Closed Loop (Unity Feedback):**

$$C(s) = \frac{G(s)}{1 + G(s)} \cdot R(s) + \frac{1}{1 + G(s)} \cdot D(s)$$

The disturbance is **attenuated by the factor $\dfrac{1}{1 + G(s)}$**. As `G(s)` increases (high loop gain), the disturbance effect **vanishes** — a key reason feedback is used.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Open Loop Control System — Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA["REFERENCE INPUT R(s)"] --> nodeB["CONTROLLER Gc(s)"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["PLANT OR PROCESS Gp(s)"]
    nodeC --> nodeD["OUTPUT C(s)"]
    nodeE["DISTURBANCE D(s)"] -.-> nodeD
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** The dashed arrow from `DISTURBANCE` to `OUTPUT` shows that disturbances **affect the output directly** with **no correction mechanism** — the defining weakness of open loop systems.

### 4.2 Closed Loop Control System — Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeR["REFERENCE R(s)"] --> nodeSUM(("SUMMING JUNCTION + and -"))
    nodeSUM --> nodeE["ERROR E(s) = R - B"]
    nodeE --> nodeC["CONTROLLER Gc(s)"]
    nodeC --> nodeP["PLANT Gp(s)"]
    nodeP --> nodeOUT["OUTPUT C(s)"]
    nodeOUT --> nodeH["FEEDBACK SENSOR H(s)"]
    nodeH --> nodeB["FEEDBACK SIGNAL B(s)"]
    nodeB --> nodeSUM
    nodeD["DISTURBANCE D(s)"] -.-> nodeOUT
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** The **solid return arrow** from `OUTPUT` back to the `SUMMING JUNCTION` is the **feedback path** — the lifeline of any closed loop system. The summing junction performs `R(s) − B(s)` to compute the error.

### 4.3 Functional Architecture — Open vs Closed Loop Strategy

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    start["CONTROL STRATEGY DECISION"] --> q1{"Is accuracy and disturbance rejection critical?"}
    q1 -- NO --> ol["OPEN LOOP SYSTEM"]
    q1 -- YES --> cl["CLOSED LOOP SYSTEM"]
    ol --> ol1["Use when: simple, fast, low cost"]
    ol1 --> ol2["Examples: toaster, fan, traffic light"]
    cl --> cl1["Use when: accuracy, robustness, tracking required"]
    cl1 --> cl2["Examples: AC, cruise control, servo motor"]
    style start fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style ol fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style cl fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
```

### 4.4 Signal Flow Graph Representation (Mason's Gain Formula Ready)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    rnode["R(s)"] -->|G1| xnode["X(s)"]
    xnode -->|G2| ynode["C(s)"]
    ynode -->|H| xnode
```

**Forward Path Gain:** $P_1 = G_1 \cdot G_2$

**Loop Gain:** $L_1 = -G_1 \cdot G_2 \cdot H$

**Closed Loop TF (Mason):**

$$T(s) = \frac{P_1}{1 - L_1} = \frac{G_1 G_2}{1 + G_1 G_2 H}$$
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Differentiate between open loop and closed loop control systems. List any four differences.**

**Model Answer:**

> An **open loop control system** is one where the control action is independent of the output — there is no feedback. The output has no influence on the input. Example: a simple electric toaster with a fixed timer.

> A **closed loop control system** uses feedback — the output is measured, compared with the reference, and the resulting error drives the controller to correct deviations. Example: a servo motor position control.

**Four Differences:**

| **Sl. No.** | **Open Loop** | **Closed Loop** |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | No feedback element | Feedback element present |
| 2 | Low accuracy | High accuracy |
| 3 | Cannot reject disturbances | Disturbance rejection capability |
| 4 | Always stable | Stability analysis required |

*(3 marks — 1 for definition of each + table differences)*

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Define the term "transfer function" of a control system. State its significance.**

**Model Answer:**

> The **transfer function** of a linear time-invariant (LTI) system is defined as the ratio of the Laplace transform of the output to the Laplace transform of the input, under the assumption of **zero initial conditions**.

$$G(s) = \frac{C(s)}{R(s)}\bigg\vert_{\text{zero IC}}$$

**Significance:**
- It provides a complete mathematical model of the system.
- It enables **stability analysis** (poles of CLTF).
- It allows **frequency response and time response** studies.
- It is essential for **controller design** (PID, lead-lag).
- It forms the basis of **block diagram reduction**.

*(3 marks — 1 for formula + 1 for definition + 1 for significance)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Derive the closed loop transfer function of a negative feedback control system having forward path transfer function `G(s)` and feedback path transfer function `H(s)`. State the special case for unity feedback. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a unity negative feedback system, $G(s) = \dfrac{K}{s(s+4)}$, determine:
   (i) The closed loop transfer function.
   (ii) The steady-state error for a unit step input.
   (iii) The steady-state error for a unit ramp input. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question A

**Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> **Step-by-step derivation as already given in Section 3.1 above — present it cleanly in the exam.**

**Step 1:** Define the error signal at the summing junction:

$$E(s) = R(s) - B(s)$$

**Step 2:** Feedback signal is `B(s) = H(s) · C(s)`. Substituting:

$$E(s) = R(s) - H(s) \cdot C(s)$$

**Step 3:** Output of plant:

$$C(s) = G(s) \cdot E(s)$$

**Step 4:** Substitute Step 2 into Step 3:

$$C(s) = G(s) \cdot [R(s) - H(s) \cdot C(s)]$$

**Step 5:** Expand and collect `C(s)` terms:

$$C(s) \cdot [1 + G(s) H(s)] = G(s) \cdot R(s)$$

**Step 6:** Final CLTF:

$$\frac{C(s)}{R(s)} = \frac{G(s)}{1 + G(s)H(s)}$$

**Step 7 — Special case (Unity feedback):** When `H(s) = 1`:

$$\frac{C(s)}{R(s)} = \frac{G(s)}{1 + G(s)}$$

*(1 mark per critical step; 7 marks total)*

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Problem (7 marks):**

Given: $G(s) = \dfrac{K}{s(s+4)}$, unity feedback, so `H(s) = 1`.

**(i) Closed Loop Transfer Function (2 marks):**

$$\frac{C(s)}{R(s)} = \frac{G(s)}{1 + G(s)} = \frac{\frac{K}{s(s+4)}}{1 + \frac{K}{s(s+4)}}$$

Multiplying numerator and denominator by `s(s+4)`:

$$\frac{C(s)}{R(s)} = \frac{K}{s^2 + 4s + K}$$

**(ii) Steady-state error for unit step input (2.5 marks):**

For unit step, $R(s) = \dfrac{1}{s}$. Error in Laplace domain:

$$E(s) = \frac{R(s)}{1 + G(s)} = \frac{1/s}{1 + \frac{K}{s(s+4)}}$$

Apply Final Value Theorem:

$$e_{ss} = \lim_{s \to 0} s \cdot E(s) = \lim_{s \to 0} \frac{1}{1 + \frac{K}{s(s+4)}}$$

As $s \to 0$, $\dfrac{K}{s(s+4)} \to \infty$, so:

$$e_{ss} = \frac{1}{\infty} = 0$$

**(iii) Steady-state error for unit ramp input (2.5 marks):**

For unit ramp, $R(s) = \dfrac{1}{s^2}$. Velocity error constant:

$$K_v = \lim_{s \to 0} s \cdot G(s) = \lim_{s \to 0} s \cdot \frac{K}{s(s+4)} = \frac{K}{4}$$

Steady-state error for ramp:

$$e_{ss} = \frac{1}{K_v} = \frac{4}{K}$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the working of a closed loop control system. Compare its performance with an open loop system on the basis of: (i) accuracy, (ii) stability, (iii) sensitivity to disturbances, (iv) cost. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A temperature control system has a forward path transfer function $G(s) = \dfrac{2}{s+1}$ and a feedback element $H(s) = 0.5$. Determine:
   (i) The closed loop transfer function.
   (ii) The error transfer function $\dfrac{E(s)}{R(s)}$.
   (iii) The steady-state output for unit step input. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question B

**Part (a) — Block Diagram + Comparison (7 marks):**

**Block Diagram Description (3 marks):**

> A closed loop control system consists of a **reference input** `R(s)` entering a **summing junction** where it is compared with the **feedback signal** `B(s)`. The difference is the **error signal** `E(s)`, which drives the **controller** `Gc(s)`. The controller output feeds the **plant** `Gp(s)`, producing the **output** `C(s)`. The output is sensed by a **feedback element** `H(s)`, generating `B(s)`, which is returned to the summing junction — completing the loop.

**Comparison Table (4 marks — 1 mark per parameter):**

| **Parameter** | **Open Loop** | **Closed Loop** |
|---|---|---|
| Accuracy | Low (calibration dependent) | High (self-correcting) |
| Stability | Always stable | Requires Routh-Hurwitz analysis |
| Disturbance Sensitivity | High — no compensation | Low — feedback rejects disturbance |
| Cost | Low (fewer components) | High (sensors, controller, comparator) |

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Problem (7 marks):**

Given: $G(s) = \dfrac{2}{s+1}$, $H(s) = 0.5$.

**(i) Closed Loop Transfer Function (2.5 marks):**

$$\frac{C(s)}{R(s)} = \frac{G(s)}{1 + G(s)H(s)} = \frac{\frac{2}{s+1}}{1 + \frac{2}{s+1} \cdot 0.5}$$

$$\frac{C(s)}{R(s)} = \frac{\frac{2}{s+1}}{1 + \frac{1}{s+1}} = \frac{\frac{2}{s+1}}{\frac{s+1+1}{s+1}} = \frac{2}{s+2}$$

**(ii) Error Transfer Function (2.5 marks):**

$$\frac{E(s)}{R(s)} = \frac{1}{1 + G(s)H(s)} = \frac{1}{1 + \frac{1}{s+1}} = \frac{s+1}{s+2}$$

**(iii) Steady-state output for unit step (2 marks):**

For unit step, $R(s) = \dfrac{1}{s}$. Output:

$$C(s) = \frac{2}{s+2} \cdot \frac{1}{s} = \frac{2}{s(s+2)}$$

Apply Final Value Theorem:

$$c_{ss} = \lim_{s \to 0} s \cdot C(s) = \lim_{s \to 0} \frac{2}{s+2} = \frac{2}{2} = 1$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Meaning:** The steady-state output equals the reference (1) — meaning **zero steady-state error** for this first-order system with step input. The system perfectly tracks the reference.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks**
>
> - **Missing the +/− signs** at the summing junction → −2 marks (forgetting it's *negative* feedback).
> - **Forgetting to substitute `H(s) = 1`** for unity feedback problems → −1.5 marks.
> - **Not applying Final Value Theorem correctly** (forgetting to multiply by `s` before taking limit) → −2 marks.
> - **Failing to state stability conditions** when asked about closed loop → −1 mark.
> - **Not drawing the feedback path** in block diagram questions → −2 marks (always draw a clear return arrow).
> - **Confusing open loop and closed loop transfer functions** in numerical problems → −3 marks (write both formulas clearly on the answer sheet before substituting).

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember 🚀

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Read This Night Before Exam**

- [x] **Open loop = no feedback**; **Closed loop = negative feedback** (positive feedback is unstable, rarely used).
- [x] **CLTF formula** (master this): $\dfrac{C(s)}{R(s)} = \dfrac{G(s)}{1 + G(s)H(s)}$
- [x] **Unity feedback** means `H(s) = 1` — formula simplifies to $\dfrac{G(s)}{1 + G(s)}$.
- [x] **Characteristic equation**: $1 + G(s)H(s) = 0$ — its roots determine stability.
- [x] **Final Value Theorem**: $x_{ss} = \lim_{s \to 0} s \cdot X(s)$ — valid only if all poles of `sX(s)` are in LHP.
- [x] **Steady-state error for step input** in a Type-1 system = 0.
- [x] **Steady-state error for ramp input** = $\dfrac{1}{K_v}$ where $K_v = \lim_{s \to 0} s \cdot G(s)$.
- [x] **Disturbance rejection**: Open loop is helpless; closed loop attenuates disturbance by $\dfrac{1}{1+G(s)H(s)}$.
- [x] **Open loop examples** (KTU favourites): Toaster, traffic light, hand dryer, fan regulator.
- [x] **Closed loop examples** (KTU favourites): AC, cruise control, voltage stabilizer, autopilot, servo motor, human body temperature.
- [x] **Block diagram must show**: Summing junction with +/− signs, forward path G(s), feedback path H(s), return arrow.
- [x] **Comparison table** — memorize the 8-point version in Section 2.4 verbatim.
- [x] **Sensitivity formula**: $S = \dfrac{1}{1+G(s)H(s)}$ — small sensitivity = robust system.
- [x] **Open loop is always stable** but not accurate; **Closed loop is accurate but may be unstable** — the engineering trade-off.
- [x] **Mason's Gain Formula** can be used as an alternative derivation method for CLTF.
- [x] **Remember the + sign in denominator** for negative feedback — the most common sign error in KTU papers.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: PID Control
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMUT602 - MECHATRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** PID Control

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **PID Controller** (Proportional-Integral-Derivative Controller) is a closed-loop feedback control mechanism widely used in industrial control systems. It continuously calculates an **error value** $e(t)$ as the difference between a desired setpoint (SP) and a measured process variable (PV), and applies a correction based on three distinct terms: **proportional**, **integral**, and **derivative**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** A PID controller is the most widely used industrial controller that combines the benefits of proportional, integral, and derivative actions to produce a control signal $u(t)$ that drives the process variable to the desired setpoint with minimum steady-state error, acceptable transient response, and adequate stability margins.

The continuous-time PID control law is expressed as:

$$u(t) = K_p \, e(t) + K_i \int_{0}^{t} e(\tau) \, d\tau + K_d \frac{de(t)}{dt}$$

where:
- $K_p$ = **Proportional Gain**
- $K_i$ = **Integral Gain**
- $K_d$ = **Derivative Gain**
- $e(t) = SP - PV$ = **Error Signal**

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Form Convention used in KTU:** The "parallel form" (also called the "non-interacting form") is shown above. An equivalent form often used is $u(t) = K_p \left[ e(t) + \frac{1}{T_i} \int e(t) \, dt + T_d \frac{de(t)}{dt} \right]$ where $T_i$ and $T_d$ are time constants.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are **driving a car** and your goal is to maintain a speed of **60 km/h** on a highway:

- **Proportional (P) Action:** Think of this as **how hard you press the accelerator pedal based on how far you are from 60 km/h**. If you are at 40 km/h, you press harder than if you are at 55 km/h. The bigger the gap (error), the bigger the accelerator input — proportional response.

- **Integral (I) Action:** Think of this as **your memory of past mistakes**. If you have been driving below 60 km/h for a long time (e.g., on a slight uphill), the integral term keeps accumulating and pushes the accelerator a little harder to eliminate that persistent lag. It eliminates the "leftover error" that the P-controller alone cannot fix.

- **Derivative (D) Action:** Think of this as **anticipating the future based on the rate of change**. If you suddenly see the speed dropping rapidly (e.g., approaching a steep uphill), the D-term boosts the accelerator input *before* the speed actually drops too much. It acts like a "look-ahead" or "damping" mechanism.

> [!TIP]
> **Plain-English Summary:** P reacts to the **present**, I reacts to the **past**, and D reacts to the **future** (rate of change). Together, they form a robust controller that is the **workhorse of over 90% of industrial control loops** worldwide.

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Standard Tuning Constants:** $K_p$ (dimensionless or with units depending on the process), $K_i$ (sec$^{-1}$), $K_d$ (sec)
- **Performance Metrics:** Rise Time ($t_r$), Peak Overshoot ($M_p$), Settling Time ($t_s$), Steady-State Error ($e_{ss}$)
- **Industry Standard:** ISA (International Society of Automation) 5.1 — symbol for PID control loop
- **Common Tuning Benchmarks:** Ziegler-Nichols (1942), Cohen-Coon, Tyreus-Luyben

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Fact:** Approximately **95% of all industrial controllers** in process control are PID-based, despite decades of research on advanced control techniques — making this a guaranteed topic in the university exam.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Structural Breakdown of the PID Controller

The PID controller is the **cascaded sum of three independent control actions**, each addressing a specific aspect of system performance:

#### 2.1.1 Proportional (P) Action
- **Output:** $u_P(t) = K_p \, e(t)$
- **Purpose:** Provides an immediate control action proportional to the current error.
- **Effect on System:**
  - ↑ $K_p$ → Faster response, **reduced rise time**
  - ↑ $K_p$ → **Increased overshoot** and possible oscillation
  - Cannot eliminate steady-state error by itself (offset problem)
- **Real-world Example:** A home heating system where the thermostat turns the heater ON/OFF based on temperature difference.

#### 2.1.2 Integral (I) Action
- **Output:** $u_I(t) = K_i \int_{0}^{t} e(\tau) \, d\tau$
- **Purpose:** Accumulates past errors to drive the steady-state error to zero.
- **Effect on System:**
  - **Eliminates steady-state error** completely (in step inputs)
  - ↑ $K_i$ → Faster elimination of offset
  - ↑ $K_i$ → Increases overshoot and settling time
  - Can cause **integral windup** if not properly bounded
- **Real-world Example:** Cruise control in a car that compensates for long, gradual inclines.

#### 2.1.3 Derivative (D) Action
- **Output:** $u_D(t) = K_d \frac{de(t)}{dt}$
- **Purpose:** Predicts future error based on current rate of change; provides damping.
- **Effect on System:**
  - **Reduces overshoot** and oscillation
  - **Improves stability** and transient response
  - Highly sensitive to **measurement noise** (high-frequency signals)
  - Cannot act alone (needs P or PI to function)
- **Real-world Example:** Shock absorbers in a car — they respond to the *rate* of suspension compression.

### 2.2 Transfer Function Representation

Taking the Laplace Transform of the PID equation (assuming zero initial conditions):

$$U(s) = \left( K_p + \frac{K_i}{s} + K_d \, s \right) E(s)$$

Therefore, the **PID Controller Transfer Function** is:

$$C(s) = \frac{U(s)}{E(s)} = K_p + \frac{K_i}{s} + K_d \, s$$

Multiplying numerator and denominator by $s$:

$$C(s) = \frac{K_d \, s^2 + K_p \, s + K_i}{s}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Observation:** The PID controller introduces a **double zero** and a **pole at the origin** ($s = 0$). The integrator pole at the origin is what allows the controller to achieve zero steady-state error for step inputs.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Formula / Concept** | **Mathematical Expression** | **Variables / Units** | **Physical Significance** |
|---|---|---|---|
| PID Control Law (Time Domain) | $u(t) = K_p e(t) + K_i \int e(t)\,dt + K_d \frac{de(t)}{dt}$ | $u(t)$ = control signal; $K_p$ = proportional gain; $K_i$ = integral gain (s$^{-1}$); $K_d$ = derivative gain (s) | Generates the actuator command |
| Error Signal | $e(t) = r(t) - y(t)$ | $r(t)$ = reference/setpoint; $y(t)$ = measured output | Difference between desired and actual |
| PID Transfer Function | $C(s) = K_p + \frac{K_i}{s} + K_d s = \frac{K_d s^2 + K_p s + K_i}{s}$ | $s$ = Laplace operator | Frequency-domain controller model |
| Open-Loop Transfer Function | $G_{OL}(s) = C(s) \cdot G(s) \cdot H(s)$ | $G(s)$ = plant; $H(s)$ = feedback sensor | Used for stability analysis |
| Closed-Loop Transfer Function | $G_{CL}(s) = \frac{C(s) G(s)}{1 + C(s) G(s) H(s)}$ | Unity feedback when $H(s)=1$ | Overall system response |
| Steady-State Error (Step Input) | $e_{ss} = \lim_{s \to 0} s \cdot E(s)$ | For PID, $e_{ss} = 0$ | I-action guarantees zero offset |
| Z-N Ultimate Gain | $K_u$ = gain at sustained oscillation | Critical proportional gain | Tuning parameter |
| Z-N Ultimate Period | $T_u$ = period of oscillation | Seconds | Tuning parameter |
| Z-N Tuning (First Method) | $K_p = 0.6 K_u,\; T_i = 0.5 T_u,\; T_d = 0.125 T_u$ | $T_i, T_d$ in seconds | Classic PID tuning |
| Z-N Tuning (Second Method) | $K_p = 0.33 K_u,\; T_i = 0.5 T_u,\; T_d = 0.33 T_u$ | Some overshoot tuning | Reaction curve method |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

PID controllers are the **backbone of industrial automation** and are deployed in:

- **Process Industries:** Temperature control in chemical reactors, flow control in pipelines, pressure regulation in boilers
- **Motion Control:** Robotic arm positioning, CNC machine tool servo control, drone flight stabilization
- **Automotive:** Electronic Stability Control (ESC), Anti-lock Braking Systems (ABS), adaptive cruise control
- **Consumer Electronics:** Drone gimbal stabilization, camera autofocus systems, 3D printer temperature control
- **Aerospace:** Autopilot systems, rocket thrust vectoring, satellite attitude control

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Insight:** In practice, many engineers use **PI controllers** (90% of applications) rather than full PID. The D-term is often omitted because of noise sensitivity and is replaced with a **filtered derivative** or a **lead compensator** in critical applications.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: From Time Domain to Laplace Domain

**Starting Equation:** The continuous-time PID control law in the time domain is:

$$u(t) = K_p \, e(t) + K_i \int_{0}^{t} e(\tau) \, d\tau + K_d \frac{de(t)}{dt}$$

**Step 1:** Apply the Laplace Transform operator $\mathcal{L}\{\cdot\}$ to both sides of the equation. The Laplace transform is linear, so we can apply it term-by-term:

$$\mathcal{L}\{u(t)\} = \mathcal{L}\left\{ K_p \, e(t) \right\} + \mathcal{L}\left\{ K_i \int_{0}^{t} e(\tau) \, d\tau \right\} + \mathcal{L}\left\{ K_d \frac{de(t)}{dt} \right\}$$

**Step 2:** Use standard Laplace transform pairs:
- $\mathcal{L}\{e(t)\} = E(s)$
- $\mathcal{L}\left\{\int_0^t e(\tau) d\tau\right\} = \frac{E(s)}{s}$ (Integration in time $\Leftrightarrow$ Division by $s$ in frequency)
- $\mathcal{L}\left\{\frac{de(t)}{dt}\right\} = sE(s) - e(0)$ (Assuming zero initial conditions, $e(0) = 0$)

Substituting these identities:

$$U(s) = K_p \, E(s) + K_i \cdot \frac{E(s)}{s} + K_d \cdot s E(s)$$

**Step 3:** Factor out $E(s)$ from the right-hand side:

$$U(s) = \left( K_p + \frac{K_i}{s} + K_d \, s \right) E(s)$$

**Step 4:** Solve for the controller transfer function $C(s)$:

$$C(s) = \frac{U(s)}{E(s)} = K_p + \frac{K_i}{s} + K_d \, s$$

**Step 5:** To put this in standard polynomial form, multiply numerator and denominator by $s$:

$$C(s) = \frac{s(K_p) + (K_i) + s^2(K_d)}{s} = \frac{K_d \, s^2 + K_p \, s + K_i}{s}$$

This is the **standard parallel (non-interacting) PID transfer function** used in KTU examinations.

### 3.2 Derivation: Closed-Loop Transfer Function with PID

**Setup:** Consider a unity-feedback control system with plant $G(s)$ and PID controller $C(s)$.

**Step 1:** The forward path transfer function is the product of controller and plant:

$$G_{forward}(s) = C(s) \cdot G(s) = \left( \frac{K_d s^2 + K_p s + K_i}{s} \right) \cdot G(s)$$

**Step 2:** For a unity negative-feedback system, the closed-loop transfer function is:

$$T(s) = \frac{G_{forward}(s)}{1 + G_{forward}(s) \cdot H(s)}$$

With $H(s) = 1$ (unity feedback):

$$T(s) = \frac{C(s) \, G(s)}{1 + C(s) \, G(s)}$$

**Step 3:** Substitute the PID expression:

$$T(s) = \frac{\left(\frac{K_d s^2 + K_p s + K_i}{s}\right) G(s)}{1 + \left(\frac{K_d s^2 + K_p s + K_i}{s}\right) G(s)}$$

**Step 4:** Multiply numerator and denominator by $s$ to clear the inner fraction:

$$T(s) = \frac{(K_d s^2 + K_p s + K_i) G(s)}{s + (K_d s^2 + K_p s + K_i) G(s)}$$

This is the **general closed-loop PID expression** used for stability and transient analysis.

### 3.3 Step-by-Step Worked Example (KTU Board Exam Pattern)

**Problem:** A unity-feedback system has plant $G(s) = \frac{1}{s(s+5)}$. A PID controller is applied with gains $K_p = 30$, $K_i = 50$, $K_d = 5$. **Determine:**
1. The closed-loop transfer function $T(s)$
2. The steady-state error for a unit step input
3. The characteristic equation

---

#### **Part (a): Closed-Loop Transfer Function**

**Step 1:** Write the PID transfer function:

$$C(s) = \frac{K_d s^2 + K_p s + K_i}{s} = \frac{5s^2 + 30s + 50}{s}$$

**Step 2:** Compute the forward path:

$$C(s) G(s) = \frac{5s^2 + 30s + 50}{s} \cdot \frac{1}{s(s+5)} = \frac{5s^2 + 30s + 50}{s^2(s+5)}$$

**Step 3:** Apply unity-feedback closed-loop formula:

$$T(s) = \frac{C(s)G(s)}{1 + C(s)G(s)}$$

$$T(s) = \frac{\frac{5s^2 + 30s + 50}{s^2(s+5)}}{1 + \frac{5s^2 + 30s + 50}{s^2(s+5)}}$$

**Step 4:** Multiply numerator and denominator by $s^2(s+5)$:

$$T(s) = \frac{5s^2 + 30s + 50}{s^2(s+5) + (5s^2 + 30s + 50)}$$

**Step 5:** Expand the denominator:

$$s^2(s+5) = s^3 + 5s^2$$

$$T(s) = \frac{5s^2 + 30s + 50}{s^3 + 5s^2 + 5s^2 + 30s + 50}$$

$$T(s) = \frac{5s^2 + 30s + 50}{s^3 + 10s^2 + 30s + 50}$$

**Final Answer (a):** $T(s) = \frac{5s^2 + 30s + 50}{s^3 + 10s^2 + 30s + 50}$

---

#### **Part (b): Steady-State Error for Unit Step Input**

**Step 1:** Identify the system type. The open-loop transfer function is:

$$G_{OL}(s) = \frac{(5s^2 + 30s + 50)}{s^2(s+5)}$$

This is a **Type-2 system** (two poles at the origin from the $s^2$ factor).

**Step 2:** Recall the static error constants:
- Type-0 system → finite position error constant $K_p$
- Type-1 system → zero $e_{ss}$ for step, finite for ramp
- Type-2 system → zero $e_{ss}$ for step AND ramp

**Step 3:** For a unit step input $R(s) = \frac{1}{s}$ and Type-2 system:

$$e_{ss} = \lim_{s \to 0} s \cdot \frac{1}{1 + G_{OL}(s)} \cdot R(s) = \lim_{s \to 0} \frac{s}{1 + G_{OL}(s)} \cdot \frac{1}{s}$$

Wait — let's compute carefully:

$$E(s) = \frac{R(s)}{1 + G_{OL}(s)} = \frac{1/s}{1 + G_{OL}(s)}$$

$$e_{ss} = \lim_{s \to 0} s \cdot E(s) = \lim_{s \to 0} \frac{1}{1 + G_{OL}(s)}$$

**Step 4:** Evaluate the limit. As $s \to 0$:

$$G_{OL}(s) \to \frac{50}{0^+ \cdot 5} = \infty$$

Therefore:

$$e_{ss} = \lim_{s \to 0} \frac{1}{1 + \infty} = 0$$

**Final Answer (b):** $e_{ss} = 0$ — the PID controller eliminates steady-state error for step inputs.

---

#### **Part (c): Characteristic Equation**

The characteristic equation is the denominator of $T(s)$ set equal to zero:

$$s^3 + 10s^2 + 30s + 50 = 0$$

This cubic equation determines the **closed-loop poles** and hence the **stability** of the system. By the Routh-Hurwitz criterion, a 3rd-order system $s^3 + a_2 s^2 + a_1 s + a_0 = 0$ is stable if:
- All coefficients are positive ✓ (here: 1, 10, 30, 50 all positive)
- $a_2 \cdot a_1 > a_0$ → $10 \cdot 30 = 300 > 50$ ✓

**Conclusion:** The system is **stable**.

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Effect of Each PID Term

| **Performance Metric** | **Increasing $K_p$** | **Increasing $K_i$** | **Increasing $K_d$** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Rise Time | **Decreases** ↑ speed | Slight decrease | Negligible / minor |
| Overshoot | **Increases** | **Increases significantly** | **Decreases** (damping) |
| Settling Time | Minimal change | **Increases** (oscillation) | **Decreases** |
| Steady-State Error | **Reduces** (but not zero) | **Eliminates** (zero) | No effect |
| Stability Margin | **Degrades** at high $K_p$ | **Degrades** at high $K_i$ | **Improves** |
| Noise Sensitivity | No direct effect | No direct effect | **Amplifies noise** |
| Disturbance Rejection | Improves | **Improves significantly** | Improves |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Design Trade-off:** Tuning a PID is fundamentally a **multi-objective optimization**. There is no single "correct" set of gains; the engineer must balance speed, accuracy, and stability based on application requirements.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram: PID Controller in a Closed-Loop System

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the **functional architecture** of a PID-controlled closed-loop system, including the three parallel control paths and the feedback loop.

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeR[Reference Setpoint r of t] --> nodeSum{Summing Junction}
    nodeY[Output Sensor H of s] --> nodeSum
    nodeSum --> nodeE[Error Signal e of t]
    nodeE --> nodeP[Proportional Block Kp]
    nodeE --> nodeI[Integral Block Ki over s]
    nodeE --> nodeD[Derivative Block Kd times s]
    nodeP --> nodeSum2{Summing Junction}
    nodeI --> nodeSum2
    nodeD --> nodeSum2
    nodeSum2 --> nodeU[Control Signal u of t]
    nodeU --> nodePlant[Plant Process G of s]
    nodePlant --> nodeOut[Process Variable y of t]
    nodeOut --> nodeY
    style nodeR fill:#ffe4b5,stroke:#333
    style nodeE fill:#ffb6c1,stroke:#333
    style nodeP fill:#b0e0e6,stroke:#333
    style nodeI fill:#98fb98,stroke:#333
    style nodeD fill:#dda0dd,stroke:#333
    style nodeU fill:#ffd700,stroke:#333
    style nodeOut fill:#ffa07a,stroke:#333
```

**Diagram Interpretation:**
- The **error signal** $e(t)$ branches into three parallel paths (P, I, D)
- Each term computes its contribution independently
- The contributions are summed at the second junction to form $u(t)$
- The plant $G(s)$ processes $u(t)$ to produce the output $y(t)$
- The feedback sensor $H(s)$ measures $y(t)$ and feeds it back to compare with $r(t)$

### 4.2 Functional Architecture Flow: PID Signal Processing

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeA[Start: New Sample at Time t] --> nodeB[Read Setpoint r t]
    nodeA --> nodeC[Read Process Variable y t]
    nodeB --> nodeD[Compute Error e t = r t minus y t]
    nodeC --> nodeD
    nodeD --> nodeE1[Compute P Term: up = Kp times e t]
    nodeD --> nodeE2[Compute I Term: ui increment = Ki times e t times dt]
    nodeD --> nodeE3[Compute D Term: ud = Kd times e t minus e prev over dt]
    nodeE2 --> nodeE4[Update Integral Accumulator]
    nodeE3 --> nodeE5[Store Current Error as Previous Error]
    nodeE1 --> nodeF[Sum Total Control u t = up + ui + ud]
    nodeE4 --> nodeF
    nodeE5 --> nodeF
    nodeF --> nodeG[Apply Saturation and Anti-Windup Limits]
    nodeG --> nodeH[Send u t to Actuator]
    nodeH --> nodeI[Wait for Next Sampling Period]
    nodeI --> nodeA
    style nodeA fill:#ffeb3b,stroke:#333
    style nodeD fill:#ff9800,stroke:#333
    style nodeF fill:#4caf50,stroke:#333,color:#fff
    style nodeG fill:#f44336,stroke:#333,color:#fff
    style nodeH fill:#2196f3,stroke:#333,color:#fff
```

**Architecture Interpretation:**
- Shows the **algorithmic flow** of how a PID algorithm is executed in discrete-time (microcontroller/DCS implementation)
- Highlights the **anti-windup** block — critical for preventing integral term from growing unbounded
- Demonstrates the **sampling loop** structure used in real digital implementations

### 4.3 Conceptual Block Diagram: Tuning Method Comparison

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeStart[PID Tuning Problem] --> nodeQ1{Plant Model Available?}
    nodeQ1 --> nodeYes[Yes: Known Transfer Function]
    nodeQ1 --> nodeNo[No: Black-Box System]
    nodeYes --> nodeMethod1[Analytical Methods]
    nodeMethod1 --> nodeM1A[Pole Placement]
    nodeMethod1 --> nodeM1B[Root Locus Design]
    nodeMethod1 --> nodeM1C[ISE ITAE Optimization]
    nodeNo --> nodeMethod2[Experimental Methods]
    nodeMethod2 --> nodeM2A[Ziegler Nichols First Method]
    nodeMethod2 --> nodeM2B[Ziegler Nichols Second Method]
    nodeMethod2 --> nodeM2C[Cohen Coon Tuning]
    nodeM1A --> nodeEnd[Obtain Kp Ki Kd]
    nodeM1B --> nodeEnd
    nodeM1C --> nodeEnd
    nodeM2A --> nodeEnd
    nodeM2B --> nodeEnd
    nodeM2C --> nodeEnd
    style nodeStart fill:#ffeb3b,stroke:#333
    style nodeEnd fill:#4caf50,stroke:#333,color:#fff
    style nodeMethod1 fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#333
    style nodeMethod2 fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#333
```

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Practice:** The **Ziegler-Nichols Second Method** (also called the "Ultimate Cycling Method") is the most commonly tested tuning method in KTU board exams. Students should memorize the formula table for both P, PI, and PID controller tuning.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

#### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: Define a PID controller. State any two advantages of using a PID controller in industrial applications.**

**Model Answer:**

A PID (Proportional-Integral-Derivative) controller is a feedback control mechanism that computes the control action based on three terms: proportional to the present error, integral of past errors, and derivative of the current error.

The control law is:

$$u(t) = K_p e(t) + K_i \int_0^t e(\tau) d\tau + K_d \frac{de(t)}{dt}$$

**Two Advantages:**
1. **Eliminates steady-state error** completely due to the integral action, while maintaining good transient response through proportional and derivative actions.
2. **Robust and versatile** — can be tuned to control a wide variety of processes (temperature, flow, pressure, position, speed) without requiring an accurate mathematical model of the plant.

---

#### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: Explain the effect of the derivative term $K_d$ on the transient response of a closed-loop control system.**

**Model Answer:**

The derivative term $K_d \frac{de(t)}{dt}$ acts on the **rate of change** of the error and serves as a **predictive/damping element** in the control loop. Its effects on transient response are:

1. **Reduces overshoot:** The derivative term provides an opposing control action when the error is changing rapidly, preventing the system from overshooting the setpoint.
2. **Improves stability and damping:** It introduces phase lead in the frequency response, increasing phase margin and damping oscillations.
3. **Faster settling time:** By suppressing oscillations, the system reaches steady state more quickly.
4. **Limitations:** The derivative term is highly sensitive to high-frequency measurement noise and cannot act alone (requires P or PI). It is often implemented as a filtered derivative:

$$u_D(s) = \frac{K_d \, s}{\tau_f s + 1} E(s)$$

where $\tau_f$ is a small filter time constant (typically $T_d/10$).

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice Pattern)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: (a)** Derive the transfer function of a PID controller from its time-domain equation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a unity-feedback control system with plant $G(s) = \frac{2}{s(s+3)(s+6)}$ and a PID controller $C(s) = K_p + \frac{K_i}{s} + K_d s$ with $K_p = 25$, $K_i = 20$, $K_d = 3$, determine: **(i)** the closed-loop transfer function, **(ii)** the characteristic equation, and **(iii)** the steady-state error for a unit step input. **(7 marks)**

---

##### **Model Solution for Part (a):**

**Step 1:** Write the time-domain PID equation:

$$u(t) = K_p e(t) + K_i \int_0^t e(\tau) d\tau + K_d \frac{de(t)}{dt}$$

**Step 2:** Apply Laplace transform assuming zero initial conditions. Using standard transform pairs ($\mathcal{L}\{e(t)\} = E(s)$, integration $\to 1/s$, derivative $\to s$):

$$U(s) = K_p E(s) + \frac{K_i}{s} E(s) + K_d \, s \, E(s)$$

**Step 3:** Factor out $E(s)$:

$$U(s) = \left( K_p + \frac{K_i}{s} + K_d s \right) E(s)$$

**Step 4:** Define the controller transfer function:

$$C(s) = \frac{U(s)}{E(s)} = K_p + \frac{K_i}{s} + K_d s$$

**Step 5:** Combine into a single fraction (multiply by $s/s$):

$$\boxed{C(s) = \frac{K_d s^2 + K_p s + K_i}{s}}$$

This represents a PID controller with one pole at origin and two zeros that can be placed via tuning.

---

##### **Model Solution for Part (b):**

**Step (i) — Closed-Loop Transfer Function:**

Compute the controller transfer function:

$$C(s) = \frac{3s^2 + 25s + 20}{s}$$

Compute the forward path:

$$C(s) G(s) = \frac{(3s^2 + 25s + 20)}{s} \cdot \frac{2}{s(s+3)(s+6)} = \frac{2(3s^2 + 25s + 20)}{s^2(s+3)(s+6)}$$

Apply unity-feedback closed-loop formula:

$$T(s) = \frac{C(s) G(s)}{1 + C(s) G(s)}$$
