Multiply numerator and denominator by $s^2(s+3)(s+6)$:

$$T(s) = \frac{2(3s^2 + 25s + 20)}{s^2(s+3)(s+6) + 2(3s^2 + 25s + 20)}$$

Expand the denominator:

$$s^2(s+3)(s+6) = s^2(s^2 + 9s + 18) = s^4 + 9s^3 + 18s^2$$

$$2(3s^2 + 25s + 20) = 6s^2 + 50s + 40$$

Sum: $s^4 + 9s^3 + 18s^2 + 6s^2 + 50s + 40 = s^4 + 9s^3 + 24s^2 + 50s + 40$

$$\boxed{T(s) = \frac{6s^2 + 50s + 40}{s^4 + 9s^3 + 24s^2 + 50s + 40}}$$

**Step (ii) — Characteristic Equation:**

Set the denominator to zero:

$$\boxed{s^4 + 9s^3 + 24s^2 + 50s + 40 = 0}$$

**Step (iii) — Steady-State Error:**

The open-loop transfer function:

$$G_{OL}(s) = \frac{2(3s^2 + 25s + 20)}{s^2(s+3)(s+6)}$$

This is a **Type-2 system** (two poles at origin from $s^2$). For a unit step input on a Type-2 system:

$$e_{ss} = \lim_{s \to 0} \frac{1}{1 + G_{OL}(s)}$$

As $s \to 0$: $G_{OL}(s) \to \frac{2 \cdot 20}{0 \cdot 3 \cdot 6} = \infty$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{e_{ss} = 0}$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: (a)** Explain the **Ziegler-Nichols Ultimate Cycle Method** for tuning a PID controller. State the recommended values of $K_p$, $T_i$, and $T_d$ for P, PI, and PID controllers. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A second-order process has the open-loop step response: the output reaches 63.2% of the final value at $t = 2$ seconds, and the slope of the response at $t = 0$ is $0.5$ units/sec. Using the **Ziegler-Nichols Reaction Curve Method**, determine the PID controller settings. **(7 marks)**

---

##### **Model Solution for Part (a):**

**Ziegler-Nichols Ultimate Cycle (Second) Method — Procedure:**

**Step 1:** Set the integral and derivative gains to zero: $K_i = 0$, $K_d = 0$.

**Step 2:** Apply only proportional control with a small $K_p$ and gradually increase $K_p$ until the system exhibits **sustained oscillations** (constant amplitude) at the output. The gain at which this occurs is the **ultimate gain** $K_u$.

**Step 3:** Measure the **period of oscillation** $T_u$ from the time-domain response.

**Step 4:** Use the following tuning formulas based on $K_u$ and $T_u$:

| **Controller Type** | **$K_p$** | **$T_i$** | **$T_d$** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **P** | $0.5 \, K_u$ | — | — |
| **PI** | $0.45 \, K_u$ | $0.8 \, T_u$ | — |
| **PID** | $0.6 \, K_u$ | $0.5 \, T_u$ | $0.125 \, T_u$ |

**Step 5:** Convert to actual gains using:
- $K_i = K_p / T_i$
- $K_d = K_p \cdot T_d$

**Limitations:** This method is aggressive — it produces ~25% overshoot. Use Cohen-Coon or Tyreus-Luyben for less aggressive tuning.

---

##### **Model Solution for Part (b):**

**Ziegler-Nichols Reaction Curve (First) Method:**

**Step 1:** Apply an open-loop step input to the process and record the response curve (S-shaped curve for first-order-plus-dead-time systems).

**Step 2:** Identify two parameters from the curve:
- **L (Dead Time):** Apparent delay before the output starts to respond
- **T (Time Constant):** Time for the output to reach 63.2% of its final value
- **K (Process Gain):** Ratio of final output change to input change

**Step 3:** From the given data:
- $T = 2$ seconds (time to reach 63.2%)
- Initial slope $= \frac{K}{T} = 0.5$ units/sec (since for a FOPDT system, $y(t) \approx \frac{K \cdot \Delta u}{T} \cdot t$ for small $t$)
- Therefore: $K/T = 0.5$

**Step 4:** Apply Z-N Reaction Curve tuning formulas for PID:

| **Parameter** | **Formula** |
|---|---|
| $K_p$ | $1.2 \cdot \frac{T}{L}$ |
| $T_i$ | $2L$ |
| $T_d$ | $0.5L$ |

**Step 5:** Without the value of $L$, we assume a typical dead time. From the problem, since only $T = 2$ s is given, we use $L = T/4 = 0.5$ s as a reasonable approximation (commonly used when the curve passes through the inflection point early).

**Step 6:** Compute the PID settings:

$$K_p = 1.2 \cdot \frac{T}{L} = 1.2 \cdot \frac{2}{0.5} = 4.8$$

$$T_i = 2L = 2 \cdot 0.5 = 1.0 \text{ sec}$$

$$T_d = 0.5L = 0.5 \cdot 0.5 = 0.25 \text{ sec}$$

**Step 7:** Convert to parallel-form gains:

$$K_i = \frac{K_p}{T_i} = \frac{4.8}{1.0} = 4.8 \text{ s}^{-1}$$

$$K_d = K_p \cdot T_d = 4.8 \cdot 0.25 = 1.2 \text{ sec}$$

$$\boxed{K_p = 4.8, \quad K_i = 4.8, \quad K_d = 1.2}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing parallel and standard form:** KTU accepts both, but students must clearly state which form is being used. Mixing $T_i$, $T_d$ with $K_i$, $K_d$ in the same equation loses **2 marks**.
> 2. **Forgetting to verify stability:** After computing PID gains, always apply Routh-Hurwitz to the characteristic equation. A system can have *all positive coefficients* but still be unstable if Routh conditions fail.
> 3. **Ignoring units:** $K_i$ has units of s$^{-1}$ and $K_d$ has units of seconds. Examiners explicitly check units in the answer — wrong units = **−1 mark**.
> 4. **Steady-state error miscalculation:** Many students forget to identify the **system type** before applying the final value theorem. Always check the number of pure integrators (poles at origin) in $G_{OL}(s)$.
> 5. **Skipping Z-N tuning formulas:** The Z-N table (P, PI, PID rows) must be memorized exactly. Off-by-one decimal coefficients (e.g., writing 0.55 instead of 0.5 for $K_p/K_u$ ratio) results in full marks deduction.
> 6. **Not stating assumptions:** Always state "zero initial conditions" before applying Laplace transform; failure to do so costs **1 mark**.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Must Memorize Before Exam:**

- [ ] **PID Control Law:** $u(t) = K_p e(t) + K_i \int e(t) dt + K_d \frac{de(t)}{dt}$
- [ ] **PID Transfer Function:** $C(s) = \frac{K_d s^2 + K_p s + K_i}{s}$ (parallel/non-interacting form)
- [ ] **Standard Form:** $C(s) = K_p\left[1 + \frac{1}{T_i s} + T_d s\right]$ (relate via $K_i = K_p/T_i$, $K_d = K_p T_d$)
- [ ] **P-action:** Reduces rise time, decreases (but does not eliminate) steady-state error, can cause overshoot
- [ ] **I-action:** Eliminates steady-state error, increases overshoot, can cause integral windup
- [ ] **D-action:** Reduces overshoot, improves damping, sensitive to noise, requires filtered derivative in practice
- [ ] **Z-N Ultimate Cycle Tuning:** $K_p = 0.6 K_u$, $T_i = 0.5 T_u$, $T_d = 0.125 T_u$
- [ ] **Z-N Reaction Curve Tuning:** $K_p = 1.2 T/L$, $T_i = 2L$, $T_d = 0.5L$
- [ ] **Type-N systems:** Type-0 → step error; Type-1 → ramp error; Type-2 → parabolic error; PID always gives zero step error
- [ ] **Closed-loop formula:** $T(s) = \frac{C(s)G(s)}{1 + C(s)G(s)H(s)}$ with $H(s) = 1$ for unity feedback
- [ ] **Characteristic equation:** Set denominator of $T(s)$ to zero
- [ ] **Stability check:** Use Routh-Hurwitz — all coefficients positive AND auxiliary conditions satisfied
- [ ] **Anti-windup:** Required for I-action to prevent integral saturation; use clamping or back-calculation
- [ ] **Real-world tuning:** Start with $K_i = K_d = 0$, increase $K_p$ until acceptable, then add $K_i$ for offset removal, then add $K_d$ for damping

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Tip:** KTU board exams almost always include either (1) a PID transfer function derivation, (2) a closed-loop response calculation with given gains, or (3) Z-N tuning application. Practice all three types from past year papers!

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Look Up Tables
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMUT602 - MECHATRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Look Up Tables

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Look Up Table (LUT)** is a pre-computed data structure, typically an array stored in the read-only memory (ROM, Flash, or EEPROM) of a microcontroller, that replaces a computationally expensive mathematical operation with a fast memory-access and interpolation routine. In mechatronic systems, LUTs are primarily used for the real-time evaluation of complex non-linear functions such as trigonometric (sine, cosine, tangent), exponential, logarithmic, and piecewise-defined functions used in motor control, PWM waveform synthesis, and sensor linearization.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** A Look Up Table is classified as a **software-based functional approximation technique** that is mathematically equivalent to a piecewise polynomial approximation of a target function. It is one of the most frequently tested topics in Module 4 of Mechatronics because it directly bridges the gap between continuous mathematics and discrete embedded-system implementation.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Real-World View)

Imagine you are a student writing an exam and you are asked: *"What is $17 \times 23$?"*

- **Method 1 (No LUT):** You perform long multiplication step-by-step in your head. This takes **time and effort**, but uses **no external memory**.
- **Method 2 (LUT Approach):** You have memorized a multiplication table in your mind. You simply **"look up"** the answer from your memory. This is **instant**, but you had to invest **memory (brain space)** to store the table.

A LUT works exactly on this principle. Instead of asking the microcontroller's Central Processing Unit (CPU) to compute $\sin(47.5^\circ)$ using a slow Taylor series expansion (often requiring **20–40 clock cycles**), we pre-store the sine values for a finite set of angles in a table. When the angle arrives, the CPU instantly **fetches** the value from memory, reducing computation to typically **1–3 clock cycles**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Trade-off:** LUTs exchange **memory space** (a finite, non-renewable resource in a microcontroller) for **computational speed** (a critical resource in real-time control loops running at 1 kHz – 20 kHz).

## 1.3 Key Terminology for KTU Board Examinations

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Table Entry (N)** | The total number of pre-stored samples in the LUT |
| **Step Size (Δx)** | The resolution of the input variable between consecutive table entries |
| **Index (k)** | The integer position used to address a specific memory location in the LUT |
| **Quantization Error (e_q)** | The maximum error introduced by approximating a continuous function with discrete samples |
| **Interpolation** | The mathematical technique used to estimate values between two stored table entries |
| **Tabular Interval** | The closed interval $[x_{min}, x_{max}]$ over which the LUT is valid |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic — How a LUT Works (Step-by-Step)

The execution of any LUT-based function evaluation follows a deterministic 4-stage pipeline:

1. **Stage 1 — Input Scaling (Normalization):** The raw input variable `x` (which may be in degrees, radians, or sensor units) is mapped into a discrete integer index space.
2. **Stage 2 — Index Computation:** The scaled input is converted to an integer index `k` that will be used to address the memory array.
3. **Stage 3 — Memory Fetch:** The value $y_k$ is retrieved from the pre-computed table at the address `k`. This is a single instruction in assembly language (e.g., `MOVF INDEX, W` in PIC microcontrollers).
4. **Stage 4 — Optional Interpolation:** If the input lies between two tabulated points, linear or higher-order interpolation is performed using adjacent entries $y_k$ and $y_{k+1}$.

## 2.2 The "Why" and "How" Behind LUTs

- **Why use them?** In mechatronic applications like **Field Oriented Control (FOC)** of Permanent Magnet Synchronous Motors (PMSM) or **Brushless DC (BLDC)** drives, the control loop must compute $\sin(\theta)$ and $\cos(\theta)$ at every PWM switching cycle. A standard ARM Cortex-M4 processor takes ~30 cycles for a Taylor series, but only ~3 cycles for a LUT fetch. This 10× speed-up is the difference between a 30 kHz and a 3 kHz control loop.
- **How are they generated?** Either (a) computed offline in MATLAB/Python and burned into ROM, or (b) computed once at system startup if the function is parameter-dependent.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

The following table contains **every** equation you will need to solve KTU Board examination problems on Look Up Tables. Master these — they are the high-yield formulas.

| # | Formula Name | Mathematical Expression | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Step Size | $\Delta x = \dfrac{x_{max} - x_{min}}{N - 1}$ | Spacing between adjacent input samples |
| 2 | Index Calculation | $k = \text{round}\!\left(\dfrac{x - x_{min}}{\Delta x}\right)$ | Integer address into the LUT |
| 3 | Memory Footprint | $M = N \times B$ bytes | Total memory required; $B$ = bytes per sample |
| 4 | Linear Interpolation | $y = y_0 + \dfrac{(y_1 - y_0)(x - x_0)}{(x_1 - x_0)}$ | Estimate $y$ for $x$ between $x_0$ and $x_1$ |
| 5 | Maximum Quantization Error | $e_q \le \dfrac{\Delta x}{2} \cdot \max\!\vert f'(x) \vert$ | Upper bound on the approximation error |
| 6 | RMS Quantization Error | $e_{rms} = \dfrac{\Delta x}{\sqrt{12}} \cdot \max\!\vert f'(x) \vert$ | Statistical error for uniform sampling |
| 7 | Trigonometric Reduction (Quadrant) | $k = k \bmod N$ | Reduces any angle to the principal domain |
| 8 | Symmetry Compression Factor | $N_{eff} = \dfrac{N}{4}$ | Storage savings using $\sin/4$-wave symmetry |
| 9 | Output Scaling | $y_{out} = y_{LUT} \times G_{scale} + y_{offset}$ | Restores LUT-internal integer values to physical units |
| 10 | Interpolation Slope | $m = \dfrac{y_{k+1} - y_k}{x_{k+1} - x_k}$ | Slope used in linear interpolation |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Examiner Note:** In the `Max f'(x)` expressions above, the symbol `\vert` is used to denote absolute value. **Do not** use the vertical bar `|` symbol when writing these formulas in your answer sheet, as it can be confused with the numeral 1 or with conditional probability notation. The use of `\vert` is also the KTU 2024 standard.

## 2.4 Real-World Utility in Mechatronics

- **Motor Control:** $\sin/\cos$ tables used in Space Vector PWM (SVPWM) for 3-phase inverter drives.
- **Sensor Linearization:** Thermocouple and thermistor response curves stored as LUTs to convert ADC counts directly to temperature in °C.
- **PWM Waveform Generation:** Pre-stored duty-cycle tables used to synthesize arbitrary analog waveforms (sine, triangular) via DDS (Direct Digital Synthesis).
- **Robotics:** Inverse Kinematics solutions for robotic arms often use 2D LUTs indexed by $(q_1, q_2)$ joint angles.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Step Size and Index Relationship

**Given:** A function $f(x)$ must be approximated by a LUT with $N$ entries, spanning the input range $[x_{min}, x_{max}]$.

**Step 1 — Define the tabulated points.** The LUT contains $N$ samples at points $x_0, x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_{N-1}$, equally spaced across the interval. Therefore, the $i$-th tabulated point is:

$$x_i = x_{min} + i \cdot \Delta x, \quad i = 0, 1, 2, \ldots, N-1$$

**Step 2 — Express the step size.** Substituting $i = 0$ gives $x_0 = x_{min}$, and substituting $i = N-1$ gives $x_{N-1} = x_{min} + (N-1)\Delta x$. Setting $x_{N-1} = x_{max}$ and solving for $\Delta x$:

$$\Delta x = \frac{x_{max} - x_{min}}{N - 1}$$

**Step 3 — Derive the index formula.** For an arbitrary input $x$, we need the index $k$ such that $x_k \le x \le x_{k+1}$. From the equation $x = x_{min} + k \cdot \Delta x$:

$$k = \frac{x - x_{min}}{\Delta x}$$

**Step 4 — Apply integer rounding.** Since $k$ must be an integer for memory addressing, the practical formula is:

$$k = \text{round}\left(\frac{x - x_{min}}{\Delta x}\right)$$

**Conversion logic:** We divided the continuous range by the step size to find "how many steps fit between $x_{min}$ and $x$". Rounding ensures the result is an integer addressable memory location.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Linear Interpolation Between Two LUT Entries

**Given:** The input $x$ lies between two tabulated points $x_k$ and $x_{k+1}$, with corresponding table values $y_k = f(x_k)$ and $y_{k+1} = f(x_{k+1})$.

**Step 1 — Assume a linear model.** Within the small interval $[x_k, x_{k+1}]$, the function $f(x)$ is approximated by a straight line passing through $(x_k, y_k)$ and $(x_{k+1}, y_{k+1})$.

**Step 2 — Write the two-point straight line equation.** The general form of a line through two points is:

$$\frac{y - y_k}{x - x_k} = \frac{y_{k+1} - y_k}{x_{k+1} - x_k}$$

**Step 3 — Cross-multiply to isolate $y$:**

$$y - y_k = (y_{k+1} - y_k) \cdot \frac{x - x_k}{x_{k+1} - x_k}$$

**Step 4 — Rearrange for the final interpolation formula:**

$$y = y_k + \frac{(y_{k+1} - y_k)(x - x_k)}{x_{k+1} - x_k}$$

**Conversion logic:** This is a weighted average of the two table values, weighted by the fractional distance of $x$ from $x_k$ relative to the total step size.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Maximum Quantization Error Bound

**Step 1 — Apply the Mean Value Theorem.** For any continuous function $f(x)$ in the interval $[x_k, x_{k+1}]$, there exists a point $\xi$ such that:

$$f(x) - y(x) = \frac{1}{2} f''(\xi) (x - x_k)(x - x_{k+1})$$

**Step 2 — Bound the product term.** The term $(x - x_k)(x - x_{k+1})$ reaches its maximum absolute value at the midpoint $x = (x_k + x_{k+1})/2$, where it equals $-\Delta x^2 / 4$.

**Step 3 — Substitute and take the absolute value:**

$$\vert f(x) - y(x) \vert \le \frac{\Delta x^2}{8} \cdot \max \vert f''(x) \vert$$

**Step 4 — State the final result:** This is the upper bound on interpolation error and is the formula examiners expect in **14-mark derivations**.

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

**Problem:** Design a Look Up Table for $\sin(\theta)$ over $\theta \in [0^\circ, 90^\circ]$ with $N = 5$ entries. Find the value of $\sin(53^\circ)$ using linear interpolation.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Calculate the step size:**

$$\Delta \theta = \frac{90^\circ - 0^\circ}{5 - 1} = 22.5^\circ$$

**Step 2 — Construct the LUT:**

| Index $k$ | Angle $\theta_k$ | $\sin(\theta_k)$ |
|---|---|---|
| 0 | $0.0^\circ$ | 0.0000 |
| 1 | $22.5^\circ$ | 0.3827 |
| 2 | $45.0^\circ$ | 0.7071 |
| 3 | $67.5^\circ$ | 0.9239 |
| 4 | $90.0^\circ$ | 1.0000 |

**Step 3 — Find the index for $\theta = 53^\circ$:**

$$k = \text{round}\left(\frac{53 - 0}{22.5}\right) = \text{round}(2.356) = 2$$

**Step 4 — Identify bracketing entries:** $53^\circ$ lies between $\theta_2 = 45^\circ$ and $\theta_3 = 67.5^\circ$, so $y_2 = 0.7071$ and $y_3 = 0.9239$.

**Step 5 — Apply linear interpolation:**

$$y = 0.7071 + \frac{(0.9239 - 0.7071)(53 - 45)}{67.5 - 45}$$

**Step 6 — Compute step-by-step:**

$$y = 0.7071 + \frac{0.2168 \times 8}{22.5} = 0.7071 + \frac{1.7344}{22.5}$$

**Step 7 — Final calculation:**

$$y = 0.7071 + 0.0771 = 0.7842$$

**Step 8 — Compare with the true value:** The actual $\sin(53^\circ) = 0.7986$. The interpolation error is:

$$e = \vert 0.7986 - 0.7842 \vert = 0.0144$$

This corresponds to a relative error of **1.80%**, which is acceptable for most low-cost BLDC motor control applications.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 LUT Operational Pipeline (Mermaid Flowchart)

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the complete 4-stage pipeline of LUT-based function evaluation, including the branching path for interpolation.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Input Variable x] --> B[Stage 1: Input Scaling and Normalization]
    B --> C[Stage 2: Index Computation k]
    C --> D{Stage 3: Is x exactly at a tabulated point?}
    D -- Yes --> E[Fetch yk from LUT at address k]
    D -- No --> F[Stage 4a: Identify bracketing points xk and xk plus 1]
    F --> G[Stage 4b: Linear Interpolation Calculation]
    G --> H[Final Output Value y]
    E --> H
    H --> I[Send to Downstream Control Block]
```

## 4.2 LUT Memory Architecture and Addressing (Mermaid Block Diagram)

The following Mermaid diagram depicts the internal memory layout and the address-generation unit of a microcontroller-based LUT system.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Input x from Sensor or Register] --> B[Scaling Unit]
    B --> C[Subtractor: x minus xmin]
    C --> D[Divider: result divided by Delta x]
    D --> E[Rounding Unit to Integer]
    E --> F[Memory Address Bus]
    F --> G[LUT Memory Array]
    G --> H[Output yk]
    H --> I[Interpolation Engine if Required]
    I --> J[Final Output to Actuator or PWM Block]
```

## 4.3 LUT vs Direct Computation: Decision Logic (Mermaid)

This diagram summarizes the design decision a mechatronics engineer must make when implementing a real-time mathematical function.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Function Required in Real Time Loop] --> B{Is the function non-linear or complex?}
    B -- No --> C[Use Direct Algebraic Evaluation]
    B -- Yes --> D{Is memory size limited in the microcontroller?}
    D -- Yes --> E[Use Taylor Series with few terms]
    D -- No --> F{Is the application BLDC, PMSM, or SVPWM motor control?}
    F -- Yes --> G[Use Look Up Table with Linear Interpolation]
    F -- No --> H[Use Polynomial Approximation via Curve Fitting]
    G --> I[Final Implementation in C or Assembly]
    E --> I
    H --> I
    C --> I
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define a Look Up Table (LUT) as used in mechatronic systems. List any two advantages and one disadvantage.**

**Model Answer:**

A Look Up Table is a pre-computed data array stored in the non-volatile memory of a microcontroller that is indexed by an input variable to retrieve the corresponding function value, thereby avoiding complex real-time computations.

**Advantages:**
1. **Speed:** Function evaluation reduces from ~30 clock cycles (Taylor series) to ~3 clock cycles (single memory fetch), enabling faster real-time control loops.
2. **Determinism:** The execution time is constant and predictable, which is critical for hard real-time systems.

**Disadvantage:**
1. Consumes valuable ROM/Flash memory, which is a limited resource in low-cost microcontrollers.

*(3 marks — 1 for definition, 1 for advantages, 1 for disadvantage)*

---

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**State the formula for calculating the step size of a LUT with N entries spanning an input range from $x_{min}$ to $x_{max}$. What is the maximum quantization error if the function has a maximum derivative of 0.05 and the step size is 0.02?**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Step size formula:**

$$\Delta x = \frac{x_{max} - x_{min}}{N - 1}$$

**Step 2 — Quantization error formula:**

$$e_q \le \frac{\Delta x}{2} \cdot \max \vert f'(x) \vert$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the given values:**

$$e_q \le \frac{0.02}{2} \times 0.05 = 0.01 \times 0.05 = 0.0005$$

**Final Answer:** $e_q \le 0.0005$ (or $5 \times 10^{-4}$).

*(3 marks — 1 for step size formula, 1 for error formula, 1 for numerical substitution)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1): `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`

**(a) Derive the linear interpolation formula used to estimate a function value between two adjacent entries of a Look Up Table. Clearly state the assumptions made. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Assumption 1:** The function $f(x)$ is continuous and smooth (differentiable) over the interval $[x_k, x_{k+1}]$.

**Assumption 2:** Within the small sub-interval between two adjacent table entries, the function is well-approximated by a straight line.

**Step 1 — Define the bracketing points:** Let $x_k$ and $x_{k+1}$ be two adjacent tabulated inputs with corresponding outputs $y_k = f(x_k)$ and $y_{k+1} = f(x_{k+1})$.

**Step 2 — Write the equation of a straight line through the two points:**

$$\frac{y - y_k}{x - x_k} = \frac{y_{k+1} - y_k}{x_{k+1} - x_k}$$

**Step 3 — Cross-multiply:**

$$y - y_k = \frac{(y_{k+1} - y_k)(x - x_k)}{x_{k+1} - x_k}$$

**Step 4 — Final interpolated output:**

$$y = y_k + (y_{k+1} - y_k) \cdot \frac{x - x_k}{x_{k+1} - x_k}$$

**Step 5 — Boundary verification:** If $x = x_k$, then the second term vanishes and $y = y_k$. If $x = x_{k+1}$, the fraction becomes 1 and $y = y_{k+1}$. The formula is therefore consistent at both endpoints. (Validates the derivation.)

*(7 marks — 2 for assumptions, 3 for derivation, 2 for final formula and verification)*

---

**(b) A temperature sensor produces an ADC output in the range 0 to 4095 corresponding to a temperature range of $-20^\circ C$ to $+150^\circ C$. Design a Look Up Table with 9 entries and use linear interpolation to estimate the temperature when the ADC reading is 2750. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute the step size:**

$$\Delta x = \frac{150 - (-20)}{9 - 1} = \frac{170}{8} = 21.25^\circ C$$

**Step 2 — Construct the LUT:**

| Index $k$ | ADC value $x_k$ | Temperature $T_k$ |
|---|---|---|
| 0 | 0 | $-20.00^\circ C$ |
| 1 | 512 | $-0.75^\circ C$ (approx) |
| 2 | 1024 | $+20.50^\circ C$ (approx) |
| 3 | 1536 | $+41.75^\circ C$ |
| 4 | 2048 | $+63.00^\circ C$ |
| 5 | 2560 | $+84.25^\circ C$ |
| 6 | 3072 | $+105.50^\circ C$ |
| 7 | 3584 | $+126.75^\circ C$ |
| 8 | 4095 | $+150.00^\circ C$ |

**Step 3 — Find the index for $x = 2750$:**

$$k = \text{round}\left(\frac{2750 - 0}{512}\right) = \text{round}(5.371) = 5$$

**Step 4 — Identify bracketing points:** ADC 2750 lies between $x_5 = 2560$ and $x_6 = 3072$, with corresponding temperatures $T_5 = 84.25^\circ C$ and $T_6 = 105.50^\circ C$.

**Step 5 — Apply linear interpolation:**

$$T = 84.25 + \frac{(105.50 - 84.25)(2750 - 2560)}{3072 - 2560}$$

**Step 6 — Simplify:**

$$T = 84.25 + \frac{21.25 \times 190}{512} = 84.25 + \frac{4037.5}{512} = 84.25 + 7.886$$

**Step 7 — Final answer:**

$$T \approx 92.14^\circ C$$

*(7 marks — 2 for step size/LUT construction, 2 for index calculation, 3 for interpolation and final answer)*

---

### Question B (Choice 2): `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a) Explain with a neat block diagram how a Look Up Table is used to generate a sine wave using Direct Digital Synthesis (DDS) in a mechatronic system. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Block diagram description (textual since the diagram is in Section 4):** A DDS-based sine wave generator using a LUT consists of a phase accumulator, an address generator, the sine LUT (ROM), a digital-to-analog converter (DAC), and a low-pass filter.

**Step 2 — Phase Accumulator:** A digital counter that increments by a programmable **tuning word** $M$ on every clock cycle. The output is a phase value $\phi(n) = n \cdot M \cdot T_{clk}$, where $T_{clk}$ is the clock period.

**Step 3 — Address Generator:** The upper bits of the phase accumulator are used to compute the address:

$$k = \text{round}\left(\frac{\phi(n)}{2\pi} \cdot N\right)$$

**Step 4 — LUT Fetch:** The value $\sin(2\pi k / N)$ is fetched from the ROM-based sine LUT.

**Step 5 — DAC and Filter:** The digital sine sample is converted to an analog voltage by the DAC and smoothed by a low-pass filter to remove the high-frequency sampling components.

**Step 6 — Output frequency formula:**

$$f_{out} = \frac{M \cdot f_{clk}}{2^L}$$

where $L$ is the bit-width of the phase accumulator.

**Step 7 — Advantages in mechatronics:** Allows frequency, phase, and amplitude to be controlled digitally with micro-Hertz resolution, which is ideal for driving PWM-controlled actuators in precision positioning systems.

*(7 marks — 2 for block diagram explanation, 3 for working principle, 2 for output frequency formula and application)*

---

**(b) A sine LUT is designed with 256 entries spanning $0$ to $2\pi$ radians. Calculate (i) the step size, (ii) the memory footprint in bytes if each entry is stored as a 16-bit signed integer, and (iii) the maximum quantization error assuming $\max \vert f'(x) \vert = 1$ for $\cos(\theta)$. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Step Size:**

$$\Delta x = \frac{2\pi - 0}{256 - 1} = \frac{6.2832}{255} = 0.02464 \text{ rad}$$

**(ii) Memory Footprint:**

$$M = N \times B = 256 \times 2 = 512 \text{ bytes}$$

**(iii) Maximum Quantization Error:**

$$e_q \le \frac{\Delta x}{2} \cdot \max \vert f'(x) \vert = \frac{0.02464}{2} \times 1 = 0.01232 \text{ rad}$$

**Step 4 — Converting to a real sine value:** The error in the sine output is approximately:

$$e_{sine} \le 0.01232 \times \cos(\theta) \le 0.01232$$

**Step 5 — Engineering interpretation:** With 256 entries, the error is about 0.7% of full scale, which is acceptable for most BLDC motor commutation applications.

*(7 marks — 2 for step size, 2 for memory calculation, 3 for error analysis and interpretation)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Off-by-one error in N:** Students frequently write $\Delta x = (x_{max} - x_{min})/N$ instead of dividing by $N - 1$. This is the most common mistake in KTU scripts and costs **1 mark** every time.
> 2. **Forgetting to apply integer rounding:** The index $k$ must be an integer. Writing $k = (x - x_{min})/\Delta x$ without the `round()` function is marked as incomplete.
> 3. **Mixing up the interpolation formula:** Students often write the denominator as $x_{k+1} - x$ instead of $x_{k+1} - x_k$. The latter is the constant step size $\Delta x$, which simplifies computation.
> 4. **Unit inconsistency:** When computing memory, ensure $B$ is in bytes and $N$ is dimensionless. Showing units explicitly earns full marks.
> 5. **Forgetting the symmetry trick:** Examiners give **bonus marks** to students who mention that for $\sin/\cos$ functions, only a quarter-wave needs to be stored due to symmetry, reducing memory by a factor of 4.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- A **Look Up Table (LUT)** is a pre-stored array of function values indexed by the input variable.
- The **step size** is $\Delta x = (x_{max} - x_{min})/(N - 1)$ — note the **N − 1**, not N.
- The **index** is computed as $k = \text{round}((x - x_{min})/\Delta x)$ and must be an integer.
- **Linear interpolation** between entries $y_k$ and $y_{k+1}$ uses: $y = y_k + (y_{k+1} - y_k)(x - x_k)/(x_{k+1} - x_k)$.
- **Maximum quantization error** bound: $e_q \le (\Delta x / 2) \cdot \max \vert f'(x) \vert$.
- **Memory footprint:** $M = N \times B$ bytes, where $B$ is the bytes-per-entry.
- **Quarter-wave symmetry** can reduce sine/cosine LUT memory by 4×.
- LUTs **trade memory for speed** — critical in real-time motor control (SVPWM, FOC, BLDC).
- LUTs are **deterministic** — constant execution time, suitable for hard real-time systems.
- Common **applications**: PWM waveform synthesis, sensor linearization, DDS, inverse kinematics.
- A LUT with **no interpolation** introduces staircase errors; **linear interpolation** reduces error by ~10×.
- For KTU exams, always **state assumptions** before derivations and **verify boundary cases** after.
- The symbol `\vert` (not `|`) must be used for absolute value to avoid notation confusion in answer sheets.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Introduction to Modern Control Strategies like Fuzzy Logic and Adaptive Control
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMUT602 - MECHATRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Introduction to Modern Control Strategies like Fuzzy Logic and Adaptive Control

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Why "Modern" Control Strategies?

Classical control (PID, lead-lag, root locus, Bode) assumes a **known, linear, time-invariant (LTI)** plant. In real mechatronic systems — robotic arms, CNC spindles, autonomous vehicles, HVAC, washing machines — the plant is:

- **Nonlinear** (friction, saturation, dead-zone)
- **Time-varying** (mass changes as fuel burns, payload varies)
- **Uncertain** (parameter drift, unmodeled dynamics)

Modern control strategies relax these assumptions. Two of the most important in KTU 2024 Mechatronics (Module 4) are **Fuzzy Logic Control (FLC)** and **Adaptive Control**.

---

## 1.2 Fuzzy Logic Control (FLC)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024):**
> **Fuzzy Logic Control** is a rule-based intelligent control technique that converts a human expert's heuristic knowledge — expressed as **linguistic IF–THEN rules** — into a deterministic control signal using **fuzzy set theory**, **fuzzy inference**, and **defuzzification**, without requiring an explicit mathematical model of the plant.

### Conceptual Analogy — "The Grandma Chef"

Imagine you ask your grandmother to control the stove flame for a perfect curry. She does **not** solve differential equations. She says:

- "If the gravy is **too thick**, turn the knob **a little higher**."
- "If it is **just right**, **don't touch** the knob."
- "If it is **burning**, **shut the flame off** immediately."

These are *fuzzy rules*. Words like "too thick", "a little higher", "just right" are **linguistic variables** mapped to **membership functions**. FLC does exactly this — encodes human expertise numerically.

### Key Building Blocks of FLC

| Block | Purpose |
|---|---|
| **Fuzzifier** | Maps crisp input (e.g., error = 2.3) → fuzzy membership degrees |
| **Knowledge Base** | Stores rules + database of membership functions |
| **Inference Engine** | Fires rules and combines them (Mamdani / Sugeno) |
| **Defuzzifier** | Converts fuzzy output → crisp control signal $u(t)$ |

---

## 1.3 Adaptive Control

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024):**
> **Adaptive Control** is a control strategy that **automatically adjusts controller parameters in real time** so that the closed-loop performance remains at a desired level despite **uncertainties** or **variations** in plant dynamics. It continuously identifies plant parameters and re-tunes the controller.

### Conceptual Analogy — "The Self-Tuning Guitarist"

A classical guitarist plays one tuning (say, standard EADGBE) and assumes the instrument stays in tune. An **adaptive guitarist** keeps listening to each string and re-adjusts the pegs while playing if a string drifts. **Adaptive control is exactly this** — it listens (estimates) to the plant and retunes (recomputes) the controller online.

### Two Major Variants

1. **Model Reference Adaptive Control (MRAC):** The closed-loop system is forced to behave like a chosen **reference model**.
2. **Self-Tuning Regulator (STR):** A parameter estimator (e.g., Recursive Least Squares) identifies the plant, and a separate controller (e.g., pole-placement) is re-designed at each step.

---

## 1.4 Other Modern Strategies (Brief Context)

> [!NOTE]
> The KTU 2024 module also touches on **Neural Network Control** and **Neuro-Fuzzy Control** as extensions. They are useful when expert rules are unavailable and learning from data is preferred.

- **Neural Network Control:** Uses artificial neurons to learn the plant inverse or act as a nonlinear function approximator.
- **Neuro-Fuzzy Control:** Combines the learning of NNs with the interpretability of fuzzy rules (e.g., **ANFIS** — Adaptive Neuro-Fuzzy Inference System).

---

## 1.5 Why This Topic Matters in Mechatronics (KTU Context)

A mechatronic system = mechanical + electronic + control + computing. Modern control strategies sit at the **computing–control interface** and are the reason products like:

- **Anti-lock Braking Systems (ABS)** — fuzzy logic
- **Automatic transmissions** — adaptive
- **Drone autopilots** — adaptive + fuzzy
- **Smart washing machines** — fuzzy + neural
- **Robot manipulators with varying payloads** — adaptive

work seamlessly without retuning.

---

## 1.6 Comparative Snapshot

| Feature | Classical (PID) | Fuzzy Logic | Adaptive |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Plant model required?** | Yes (linear) | No (uses rules) | Partially (online) |
| **Handles nonlinearity** | Poorly | Excellent | Good (slowly varying) |
| **Handles time-variation** | Poor | Good | Excellent |
| **Math complexity** | Low | Medium | High |
| **Real-world tuning** | Manual | Expert rules | Automatic |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Fuzzy Logic — The Mathematics Behind It

### 2.1.1 Fuzzy Sets & Membership

A **classical (crisp) set** $A$ on universe $X$ has a characteristic function:

$$\mu_A(x) = \begin{cases} 1, & x \in A \\ 0, & x \notin A \end{cases}$$

A **fuzzy set** $\tilde{A}$ replaces the binary 0/1 with a continuum in $[0,1]$:

$$\mu_{\tilde{A}}(x) \in [0,1]$$

where $\mu_{\tilde{A}}(x)$ is the **degree of membership** of $x$ in $\tilde{A}$.

> [!NOTE]
> A membership value of **0.8** means "80% belongs to the set". This is the heart of fuzzy logic — partial truth.

### 2.1.2 Common Membership Functions (MFs)

| MF Type | Formula | Shape |
|---|---|---|
| Triangular | $\mu(x) = \max\left(0, 1 - \dfrac{\vert x - c \vert}{w}\right)$ | Triangle |
| Trapezoidal | $\mu(x) = \max\left(0, \min\left(\dfrac{x-a}{b-a}, 1, \dfrac{d-x}{d-c}\right)\right)$ | Trapezoid |
| Gaussian | $\mu(x) = e^{-\frac{(x-c)^2}{2\sigma^2}}$ | Bell curve |

> **Boundary condition:** $0 \le \mu(x) \le 1 \quad \forall x \in X$

### 2.1.3 Linguistic Variables & Rule Base

A **linguistic variable** has:
- **Name** — e.g., "Temperature"
- **Term set** — e.g., {Cold, Warm, Hot}
- **Universe of discourse** — e.g., $X = [0^\circ C, 100^\circ C]$

A typical **Mamdani fuzzy rule** is:

$$\text{R}_i : \text{IF } x_1 \text{ is } A_{1i} \text{ AND } x_2 \text{ is } A_{2i} \text{ THEN } u \text{ is } B_i$$

For a 2-input, 1-output system, an example rule base for an air conditioner:

| Rule # | Temperature (T) | Humidity (H) | Fan Speed (u) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Cold | Low | Low |
| 2 | Cold | High | Low |
| 3 | Warm | Low | Medium |
| 4 | Warm | High | High |
| 5 | Hot | Low | High |
| 6 | Hot | High | Very High |

### 2.1.4 Fuzzy Inference — Mamdani Method

For each rule, compute the **firing strength** (using min for AND, max for OR):

$$\alpha_i = \min\left(\mu_{A_{1i}}(x_1),\, \mu_{A_{2i}}(x_2)\right)$$

The output membership is **clipped** at $\alpha_i$:

$$\mu_{B_i'}(u) = \min\left(\alpha_i,\, \mu_{B_i}(u)\right)$$

Combine all rules by **union (max)**:

$$\mu_{B'}(u) = \max_i \mu_{B_i'}(u)$$

### 2.1.5 Defuzzification — Centroid Method

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **most commonly asked formula in KTU exams**.

The crisp control action is the centroid (center of gravity) of the aggregated fuzzy output:

$$u^* = \frac{\displaystyle\int_{u} u \cdot \mu_{B'}(u)\, du}{\displaystyle\int_{u} \mu_{B'}(u)\, du}$$

For discrete universes (used in microcontrollers):

$$u^* = \frac{\sum_{j=1}^{N} u_j \cdot \mu_{B'}(u_j)}{\sum_{j=1}^{N} \mu_{B'}(u_j)}$$

Other methods: **Mean of Maxima (MOM)**, **Smallest of Maxima (SOM)**, **Largest of Maxima (LOM)**.

---

## 2.2 Takagi–Sugeno (TS) Fuzzy Model

Used heavily in modern adaptive/optimal control. Rule consequence is a **linear function** instead of a fuzzy set:

$$\text{R}_i : \text{IF } x \text{ is } A_i \text{ THEN } u_i = a_i x + b_i$$

Final output is a **weighted average**:

$$u = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_i \cdot u_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_i}$$

This is computationally simpler and differentiable — ideal for **adaptive control of fuzzy systems**.

---

## 2.3 Adaptive Control — The Mathematics

### 2.3.1 Model Reference Adaptive Control (MRAC)

Consider a **first-order plant** with unknown gain $K$ and unknown time constant $\tau$:

$$G_p(s) = \frac{K}{\tau s + 1}$$

The **reference model** specifies the *desired* closed-loop response:

$$G_m(s) = \frac{K_m}{\tau_m s + 1}$$

The **adaptation law** (using MIT rule) updates the controller parameter $\theta$:

$$\frac{d\theta}{dt} = -\gamma \cdot e \cdot \frac{\partial e}{\partial \theta}$$

where:
- $e = y - y_m$ (tracking error)
- $\gamma$ = **adaptation gain** (positive scalar)
- $\frac{\partial e}{\partial \theta}$ = **sensitivity derivative**

> [!NOTE]
> The **MIT rule** is the foundational adaptive law. The negative sign drives the error toward zero.

### 2.3.2 Lyapunov-Based MRAC (Stable Adaptation)

To guarantee stability, define a Lyapunov function candidate:

$$V = \frac{1}{2} e^2 + \frac{1}{2\gamma}(\theta - \theta^*)^2$$

For $\dot{V} \le 0$, choose the adaptation law:

$$\dot{\theta} = -\gamma \, e \, \phi$$

where $\phi = \frac{\partial y}{\partial \theta}$ is the **regression vector**. This guarantees asymptotic tracking.

### 2.3.3 Self-Tuning Regulator (STR) — Recursive Least Squares (RLS)

Estimate plant parameters $\hat{\theta}$ online:

$$\hat{\theta}(k) = \hat{\theta}(k-1) + K(k)\left[y(k) - \phi^T(k)\hat{\theta}(k-1)\right]$$

$$K(k) = \frac{P(k-1)\,\phi(k)}{\lambda + \phi^T(k)\,P(k-1)\,\phi(k)}$$

$$P(k) = \frac{1}{\lambda}\left[P(k-1) - K(k)\,\phi^T(k)\,P(k-1)\right]$$

where $\lambda \in (0,1]$ is the **forgetting factor** and $P$ is the covariance matrix.

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Fuzzy set membership | $\mu_{\tilde{A}}(x) \in [0,1]$ | Definition |
| 2 | Triangular MF | $\mu(x) = \max\left(0, 1 - \dfrac{\vert x - c \vert}{w}\right)$ | MF shape |
| 3 | Gaussian MF | $\mu(x) = e^{-\frac{(x-c)^2}{2\sigma^2}}$ | Smooth MF |
| 4 | Rule firing (AND) | $\alpha_i = \min(\mu_1, \mu_2)$ | Inference |
| 5 | Rule firing (OR) | $\alpha_i = \max(\mu_1, \mu_2)$ | Inference |
| 6 | Output aggregation | $\mu_{B'} = \max_i \min(\alpha_i, \mu_{B_i})$ | Mamdani |
| 7 | Centroid defuzzification | $u^* = \dfrac{\sum u_j \mu_{B'}(u_j)}{\sum \mu_{B'}(u_j)}$ | Crisp output |
| 8 | Takagi–Sugeno output | $u = \dfrac{\sum \alpha_i (a_i x + b_i)}{\sum \alpha_i}$ | TS fuzzy |
| 9 | MIT rule | $\dot{\theta} = -\gamma e \frac{\partial e}{\partial \theta}$ | MRAC law |
| 10 | Lyapunov MRAC | $\dot{\theta} = -\gamma e \phi$ | Stable MRAC |
| 11 | RLS update | $\hat{\theta}(k) = \hat{\theta}(k-1) + K(k)[y - \phi^T \hat{\theta}]$ | STR identifier |
| 12 | Kalman gain (RLS) | $K(k) = \dfrac{P \phi}{\lambda + \phi^T P \phi}$ | STR identifier |

> **Units note:** Adaptation gain $\gamma$ is in $\text{s}^{-1}$ for first-order systems; forgetting factor $\lambda$ is dimensionless.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application | Strategy Used | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Washing machine | Fuzzy | Sensor noise + no model |
| HVAC | Fuzzy + Adaptive | Nonlinear, time-varying load |
| Robot manipulator | Adaptive | Payload changes |
| CNC feed drive | Adaptive | Tool wear, cutting forces |
| Anti-skid braking | Fuzzy | Hard to model friction |
| Drone autopilot | Adaptive + Fuzzy | Disturbances, mass change |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Centroid Defuzzification (KTU Favourite)

> **Problem (Model Question Style):** A fuzzy controller produces an aggregated output membership $\mu_{B'}(u)$ over the discrete universe $u \in \{0, 2, 4, 6, 8\}$ as:
>
> | $u_j$ | 0 | 2 | 4 | 6 | 8 |
> |---|---|---|---|---|---|
> | $\mu_{B'}(u_j)$ | 0.1 | 0.4 | 0.8 | 0.5 | 0.2 |
>
> Find the crisp control output $u^*$ using the centroid method.

### Step 1 — Identify the discrete universe and membership values

$$u = \{0, 2, 4, 6, 8\}, \quad \mu_{B'} = \{0.1, 0.4, 0.8, 0.5, 0.2\}$$

### Step 2 — Compute the numerator $\sum u_j \mu_{B'}(u_j)$

$$\sum_{j=1}^{5} u_j \cdot \mu_{B'}(u_j) = (0)(0.1) + (2)(0.4) + (4)(0.8) + (6)(0.5) + (8)(0.2)$$

$$= 0 + 0.8 + 3.2 + 3.0 + 1.6 = 8.6$$

### Step 3 — Compute the denominator $\sum \mu_{B'}(u_j)$

$$\sum_{j=1}^{5} \mu_{B'}(u_j) = 0.1 + 0.4 + 0.8 + 0.5 + 0.2 = 2.0$$

### Step 4 — Apply the centroid formula

$$u^* = \frac{8.6}{2.0} = 4.3$$

### Step 5 — Verification

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The crisp value $u^* = 4.3$ lies **within the universe** $[0, 8]$ ✓ and is biased toward the peak membership at $u = 4$ ✓. The result is **physically meaningful** as a fan speed / voltage / valve position level.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — MIT Rule Application

> **Problem:** A first-order plant has gain $K$ that is unknown. The reference model has gain $K_m = 1$. The adaptation law uses the MIT rule with $\gamma = 0.5$. At an instant, error $e = 0.4$ and sensitivity $\frac{\partial e}{\partial K} = 0.9$. Find the rate of change of $K$.

### Step 1 — Recall the MIT rule

$$\frac{d\theta}{dt} = -\gamma \cdot e \cdot \frac{\partial e}{\partial \theta}$$

Here $\theta \equiv K$ (the unknown gain being adapted).

### Step 2 — Substitute values

$$\frac{dK}{dt} = - (0.5)(0.4)(0.9) = -0.18$$

### Step 3 — Interpretation

> [!NOTE]
> The negative sign means $K$ is **decreasing** at $0.18$ units per second, **driving the tracking error $e$ toward zero** as required by the MIT rule. If $e$ had been negative (i.e., $y < y_m$), $K$ would have been **increased**.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Takagi–Sugeno Weighted Average

> **Problem:** A TS fuzzy controller has 2 rules:
>
> - R1: IF $x$ is Low, THEN $u_1 = 2x + 1$, with firing weight $\alpha_1 = 0.6$
> - R2: IF $x$ is High, THEN $u_2 = 3x - 2$, with firing weight $\alpha_2 = 0.4$
>
> For input $x = 5$, find the crisp output $u$.

### Step 1 — Evaluate each consequent

$$u_1 = 2(5) + 1 = 11$$

$$u_2 = 3(5) - 2 = 13$$

### Step 2 — Apply weighted average

$$u = \frac{\alpha_1 u_1 + \alpha_2 u_2}{\alpha_1 + \alpha_2} = \frac{(0.6)(11) + (0.4)(13)}{0.6 + 0.4}$$

$$= \frac{6.6 + 5.2}{1.0} = 11.8$$

### Step 3 — Sanity check

Since $\alpha_1 > \alpha_2$ (Low has higher activation), the output $u = 11.8$ should be **closer to 11 than to 13**, which is true ✓.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Inference Cycle of FLC (Full Derivation Flow)

The complete fuzzy control algorithm for crisp inputs $x_1, x_2$ and crisp output $u$ proceeds as:

### Step A — Fuzzification

For each input, compute the membership in every relevant fuzzy set:

$$\mu_{A_{1i}}(x_1), \quad \mu_{A_{2i}}(x_2)$$

### Step B — Rule Activation (for each of the $N$ rules)

$$\alpha_i = \mu_{A_{1i}}(x_1) \star \mu_{A_{2i}}(x_2)$$

where $\star$ is the **T-norm** (min for Mamdani, product for Larsen).

### Step C — Implication (clip / scale the output MF)

Mamdani: $\mu_{B_i'}(u) = \min(\alpha_i, \mu_{B_i}(u))$

Larsen: $\mu_{B_i'}(u) = \alpha_i \cdot \mu_{B_i}(u)$

### Step D — Aggregation

$$\mu_{B'}(u) = \max_{i=1,\ldots,N} \mu_{B_i'}(u)$$

### Step E — Defuzzification

$$u^* = \frac{\sum_{j} u_j \mu_{B'}(u_j)}{\sum_{j} \mu_{B'}(u_j)}$$

### Step F — Apply to plant

Send $u^*$ to the actuator. Measure next $x_1, x_2$. **Repeat indefinitely**.

---

## 3.5 Adaptive Control — Stability Argument

For a first-order plant $\dot{y} = -a y + b u$ with unknown $a, b$, the controller is chosen as:

$$u = \hat{\theta}_1 y + \hat{\theta}_2 r$$

where $r$ is the reference and $\hat{\theta}_1, \hat{\theta}_2$ are adapted so that $y \to r$.

Define tracking error $e = y - r$. Then:

$$\dot{e} = -a y + b(\hat{\theta}_1 y + \hat{\theta}_2 r) - \dot{r}$$

Substituting ideal values $\theta_1^* = a/b$ and $\theta_2^* = 1/b$ (so that the closed loop becomes $\dot{y} = -a_m y + a_m r$), and choosing a **Lyapunov function**:

$$V = \frac{1}{2} e^2 + \frac{1}{2\gamma} (\tilde{\theta}_1^2 + \tilde{\theta}_2^2)$$

where $\tilde{\theta} = \hat{\theta} - \theta^*$, the gradient descent adaptation law becomes:

$$\dot{\hat{\theta}}_1 = -\gamma e y, \quad \dot{\hat{\theta}}_2 = -\gamma e r$$

Since $\dot{V} \le 0$, by **Lyapunov's direct method**, the equilibrium $e = 0$ is **asymptotically stable**, proving convergence.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Fuzzy Logic Control (FLC) Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R[Reference Setpoint] --> E1[Summing Junction]
    S1[Sensor Signal] --> E1
    E1 --> ERR[Error e]
    E1 --> DE[Change in Error de]
    ERR --> FZ1[Fuzzifier Block 1]
    DE --> FZ2[Fuzzifier Block 2]
    FZ1 --> INF[Fuzzy Inference Engine]
    FZ2 --> INF
    KB[(Knowledge Base Rules and MFs)] --> INF
    INF --> AGG[Aggregator]
    AGG --> DF[Defuzzifier]
    DF --> U[Control Signal u]
    U --> ACT[Actuator]
    ACT --> PL[Plant]
    PL --> S2[Measured Output]
    S2 --> S1
```

> [!NOTE]
> The **Knowledge Base** supplies the membership functions and IF–THEN rules to the Inference Engine. The **Fuzzifier** converts crisp $e$ and $de$ into membership degrees. The **Defuzzifier** converts the aggregated fuzzy set into a single crisp value $u$.

---

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Model Reference Adaptive Control (MRAC)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R[Reference r] --> A1((+))
    YM[Reference Model Output ym] --> A2((-))
    A1 --> A2
    A2 --> ER[Tracking Error e]
    ER --> AD[Adaptation Mechanism]
    CL[Controller with Adjustable Parameters theta] --> U[Control Signal u]
    AD --> CL
    U --> P[Plant with Unknown Parameters]
    P --> Y[Plant Output y]
    Y --> A1
    R --> RM[Reference Model Gm]
    RM --> YM
    Y --> AD
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Adaptation Mechanism** receives both the **tracking error $e$** and the **plant output $y$**. It uses the MIT rule or Lyapunov law to update $\theta$ so that $y \to y_m$.

---

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Self-Tuning Regulator (STR)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R[Reference r] --> A1((+))
    Y[Plant Output y] --> A2((-))
    A2 --> A1
    A1 --> U[Error e or Control Signal]
    EST[Parameter Estimator RLS] --> TH[Estimated Plant Parameters theta hat]
    TH --> DS[Controller Design Pole Placement]
    DS --> K[Controller Gains k1 k2]
    K --> P[Plant]
    P --> Y
    Y --> EST
    U --> EST
```

> The **estimator** and **controller design** are two separate blocks re-executed at every sampling instant — hence "**self-tuning**".

---

## 4.4 Functional Architecture — Comparison Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Q{Plant Characteristics}
    Q -->|Known, Linear, Constant| CL[Classical PID Control]
    Q -->|Unknown, Nonlinear, Expert Rules Available| FZ[Fuzzy Logic Control]
    Q -->|Slowly Varying Parameters, Online Estimation Needed| AD[Adaptive Control]
    Q -->|Data Available, No Rules, Highly Nonlinear| NN[Neural Network Control]
    Q -->|Rules + Data Together| NF[Neuro Fuzzy ANFIS]
    CL --> OUT[Stable Closed Loop]
    FZ --> OUT
    AD --> OUT
    NN --> OUT
    NF --> OUT
```

---

## 4.5 Mamdani FLC — Internal Signal Flow (Detailed)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    X1[x1 Error] --> FU1[Fuzzification]
    X2[x2 Error Rate] --> FU2[Fuzzification]
    FU1 --> M1[Membership Degrees mu A1i]
    FU2 --> M2[Membership Degrees mu A2i]
    M1 --> MIN[Min T norm for AND]
    M2 --> MIN
    MIN --> ALPHA[Firing Strength alpha i]
    ALPHA --> CLIP[Clip Output MF at alpha i]
    CLIP --> AGG[Max Aggregation]
    AGG --> DEF[Centroid Defuzzification]
    DEF --> CRISP[Crisp Control u]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A — 3-Mark Questions (Short Answer / Definition)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Style]**
**Define Fuzzy Logic Control. List its four main components.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Fuzzy Logic Control (FLC)** is a rule-based intelligent control technique that converts an expert's linguistic knowledge (expressed as IF–THEN rules) into a deterministic control signal using fuzzy sets, without an explicit mathematical model of the plant.
>
> The **four main components** are:
>
> 1. **Fuzzifier** – maps crisp inputs to fuzzy membership degrees.
> 2. **Knowledge Base** – stores membership functions and IF–THEN rules.
> 3. **Inference Engine** – fires rules and combines them.
> 4. **Defuzzifier** – converts aggregated fuzzy output to a crisp control signal.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]**
**Differentiate between Fuzzy Logic Control and Adaptive Control.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Fuzzy Logic Control | Adaptive Control |
|---|---|---|
| **Basis** | Expert rules (linguistic) | Online parameter estimation |
| **Plant model** | Not required | Required (in STR) / partially (in MRAC) |
| **Best for** | Nonlinear, noisy, expert-knowledge-available systems | Slowly time-varying, uncertain parameter systems |
| **Math core** | Membership functions, inference | MIT rule, Lyapunov stability, RLS |
| **Adaptation** | Static rule base (unless adaptive FLC) | Continuous real-time tuning |

---

## Part B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — Fuzzy Logic Focus

**[KTU University Exam – Sample Paper Style]**

**(a) Explain the working of a Mamdani-type Fuzzy Logic Controller with a neat block diagram. Discuss the role of fuzzification, rule base, inference engine, and defuzzification.** **(7 marks)**

**(b) Consider a fuzzy controller for an air conditioner with two inputs — Temperature $T$ and Humidity $H$ — and one output — Fan Speed $u$. The aggregated output membership is given below. Compute the crisp control output using the centroid method.** **(7 marks)**

| $u_j$ | 0 | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $\mu_{B'}(u_j)$ | 0.0 | 0.3 | 0.7 | 0.9 | 0.4 |

#### Model Solution — (a)

**Fuzzification:**
Crisp inputs $T, H$ are mapped to membership degrees in their respective fuzzy sets (Cold, Warm, Hot for $T$; Low, Medium, High for $H$). For example, $T = 28^\circ C$ may have $\mu_{\text{Warm}}(28) = 0.6$ and $\mu_{\text{Hot}}(28) = 0.4$.

**Rule Base (Knowledge Base):**
Contains linguistic rules such as:
> IF $T$ is Hot AND $H$ is High THEN $u$ is Very High.

**Inference Engine:**
For each rule, compute firing strength $\alpha_i = \min(\mu_{T}(T), \mu_{H}(H))$ and clip the consequent MF at $\alpha_i$. All clipped outputs are aggregated by **max** to form $\mu_{B'}(u)$.

**Defuzzification:**
The crisp output is obtained via the **centroid formula**:

$$u^* = \frac{\sum u_j \mu_{B'}(u_j)}{\sum \mu_{B'}(u_j)}$$

**Block Diagram:** Refer to Section 4.5 of these notes.

#### Model Solution — (b)

**Step 1 — Compute the numerator:**

$$\sum u_j \mu_{B'} = (0)(0.0) + (1)(0.3) + (2)(0.7) + (3)(0.9) + (4)(0.4)$$

$$= 0 + 0.3 + 1.4 + 2.7 + 1.6 = 6.0$$

**Step 2 — Compute the denominator:**

$$\sum \mu_{B'} = 0.0 + 0.3 + 0.7 + 0.9 + 0.4 = 2.3$$

**Step 3 — Apply the centroid formula:**

$$u^* = \frac{6.0}{2.3} \approx 2.61$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation:**
The fan speed is set to approximately **2.61** on the discrete scale, which is biased toward the peak membership at $u = 3$ ✓.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Adaptive Control Focus

**[KTU University Exam – Sample Paper Style]**

**(a) Explain the Model Reference Adaptive Control (MRAC) scheme with a block diagram. Derive the MIT adaptation rule.** **(7 marks)**

**(b) A first-order plant $\dot{y} = -ay + bu$ has unknown parameters. The reference model is $\dot{y}_m = -a_m y_m + b_m r$. Using the MIT rule, obtain the adaptation laws for the controller parameters $\hat{\theta}_1$ and $\hat{\theta}_2$, where $u = \hat{\theta}_1 y + \hat{\theta}_2 r$.** **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — (a)

**MRAC Architecture:** Refer to Section 4.2 of these notes. The closed-loop plant output $y$ is forced to track the reference model output $y_m$.

**Tracking error:**

$$e = y - y_m$$

**Cost function (MIT rule):**

$$J(\theta) = \frac{1}{2} e^2$$

**Gradient descent** to minimize $J$:

$$\frac{d\theta}{dt} = -\gamma \frac{\partial J}{\partial \theta} = -\gamma e \frac{\partial e}{\partial \theta}$$

This is the **MIT adaptation rule**:

$$\boxed{\frac{d\theta}{dt} = -\gamma \, e \, \frac{\partial e}{\partial \theta}}$$

where $\gamma$ is the **adaptation gain** controlling the speed of parameter update.

**Block diagram:** Reference model in parallel with the plant; both fed from the same reference $r$. The error $e$ and the sensitivity derivative $\frac{\partial e}{\partial \theta}$ feed the adaptation mechanism, which updates $\theta$ of the controller.

#### Model Solution — (b)

**Step 1 — Plant and reference model:**

$$\dot{y} = -a y + b u, \quad \dot{y}_m = -a_m y_m + b_m r$$

**Step 2 — Choose controller form:**

$$u = \hat{\theta}_1 y + \hat{\theta}_2 r$$

The **ideal** values are $\theta_1^* = \dfrac{a - a_m}{b}$ and $\theta_2^* = \dfrac{b_m}{b}$ (obtained by matching the closed-loop dynamics of $y$ with $y_m$).

**Step 3 — Tracking error and its derivative:**

$$e = y - y_m, \quad \dot{e} = \dot{y} - \dot{y}_m = -a_m e - b\tilde{\theta}_1 y - b\tilde{\theta}_2 r$$

where $\tilde{\theta}_i = \hat{\theta}_i - \theta_i^*$.

**Step 4 — MIT rule applied to each parameter:**

For $\hat{\theta}_1$:

$$\dot{\hat{\theta}}_1 = -\gamma_1 \, e \, \frac{\partial e}{\partial \hat{\theta}_1} = -\gamma_1 \, e \, y$$

For $\hat{\theta}_2$:

$$\dot{\hat{\theta}}_2 = -\gamma_2 \, e \, \frac{\partial e}{\partial \hat{\theta}_2} = -\gamma_2 \, e \, r$$

**Step 5 — Conclusion:**

$$\boxed{\dot{\hat{\theta}}_1 = -\gamma_1 \, e \, y \quad \text{and} \quad \dot{\hat{\theta}}_2 = -\gamma_2 \, e \, r}$$

These laws drive $\hat{\theta}_i \to \theta_i^*$ and hence $e \to 0$ as $t \to \infty$.

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Students Make (Lose 2–4 marks per question):**
>
> 1. **Confusing the min and max operations** in fuzzy inference — min is for **AND (T-norm)**, max is for **OR (T-conorm) and aggregation**.
> 2. **Inverting the sign in the MIT rule** — students often write $\frac{d\theta}{dt} = +\gamma e \frac{\partial e}{\partial \theta}$ (positive sign). It must be **negative** to descend the error gradient.
> 3. **Forgetting to divide** by the denominator $\sum \mu_{B'}$ in centroid defuzzification — many students compute only the numerator and call it the answer.
> 4. **Writing ideal values incorrectly** in MRAC — the ideal controller parameters must be derived by matching the closed-loop plant dynamics to the reference model.
> 5. **Not stating the range of $\mu$** in fuzzy set definitions — always write $0 \le \mu(x) \le 1$.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [x] **Fuzzy Logic Control** = expert rules + fuzzification + inference + defuzzification; **no plant model needed**.
- [x] **Adaptive Control** = controller parameters **updated online** using MIT rule or Lyapunov design.
- [x] **MRAC** tracks a **reference model**; **STR** uses an **explicit online estimator** (e.g., RLS).
- [x] **Membership function** $\mu(x) \in [0,1]$; Gaussian, triangular, trapezoidal are common.
- [x] **AND** in rules → **min**; **OR** → **max**; **aggregation** → **max**.
- [x] **Centroid defuzzification**: $u^* = \dfrac{\sum u_j \mu_{B'}(u_j)}{\sum \mu_{B'}(u_j)}$ — **must appear in every FLC exam answer**.
- [x] **MIT rule**: $\dot{\theta} = -\gamma e \dfrac{\partial e}{\partial \theta}$ — **negative sign is mandatory**.
- [x] **Takagi–Sugeno** uses linear consequents and a weighted average — **faster and differentiable**.
- [x] **Lyapunov-based MRAC** guarantees stability; pure MIT rule does not.
- [x] Real-world use: **ABS = fuzzy**; **CNC tool wear compensation = adaptive**; **drones = both**.
- [x] KTU exam favourite: 14-mark question with **defuzzification numerical + block diagram** OR **MIT rule derivation + Lyapunov stability**.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Parameters to be Controlled in SI and CI Engines and in the Other Parts of the Automobile
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMUT602 - MECHATRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Parameters to be Controlled in SI and CI Engines and in the Other Parts of the Automobile

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is "Parameter Control" in an Automotive Mechatronics Context?

In modern automobiles, **Mechatronics** refers to the synergistic integration of mechanical systems, electronic sensors, micro-controller/ECU hardware, and real-time control software. A modern car contains anywhere between **60 to 100+ Electronic Control Units (ECUs)** that continuously monitor and regulate hundreds of parameters to ensure optimal performance, fuel economy, emissions, safety, and driver comfort.

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU Definition:**
> *Parameter control in SI and CI engines refers to the real-time measurement, feedback, and actuation of critical variables such as air-fuel ratio, ignition/injection timing, speed, load, and emissions by an Engine Management System (EMS) employing sensors, ECUs, and actuators in a closed-loop architecture.*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Human Body

Imagine driving a car is like **walking a marathon**:
- Your **brain** is the **ECU** (Engine Control Unit) — it makes decisions.
- Your **eyes, ears, skin** are **sensors** — they measure the world (oxygen level, temperature, pressure).
- Your **muscles** are **actuators** — they take action (fuel injector squirts, spark plug fires, throttle opens).
- Your **nervous system** is the **wiring harness / CAN bus** — it carries signals at high speed.

Just as your body automatically adjusts breathing rate, heart rate, and sweat to maintain balance, an automobile's EMS automatically adjusts fuel, air, and spark to maintain efficient, clean, and powerful combustion.

## 1.3 Categories of Parameters

| Category | Example Parameters | Why Controlled? |
|---|---|---|
| **Combustion Parameters** | AFR, Ignition/Injection Timing | Power, Fuel Economy |
| **Emission Parameters** | NOx, HC, CO, PM, EGR Rate | Pollution Compliance |
| **Thermal Parameters** | Coolant Temp, Oil Temp | Engine Longevity |
| **Kinematic Parameters** | Crankshaft/Camshaft Position, Vehicle Speed | Synchronization |
| **Driver-Assist Parameters** | Brake Pressure, Steering Angle, Yaw Rate | Safety & Comfort |
| **Auxiliary Parameters** | Battery SOC, HVAC, Lighting | Convenience & Efficiency |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:**
> For the KTU board exam, students must know:
> 1. The **list of parameters** controlled in SI vs. CI engines (with at least 6 each).
> 2. The **corresponding sensor** and **actuator** for each parameter.
> 3. A basic understanding of the **control loop** (open vs. closed loop).
> 4. Control parameters in **other parts** like ABS, EPS, Automatic Transmission, BMS.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Parameters Controlled in SI (Spark Ignition) Engines

SI engines (petrol/gasoline engines) use a spark plug to ignite a homogeneous air-fuel mixture. The ECU continuously regulates the following parameters:

### 2.1.1 Air-Fuel Ratio (AFR) Control
- **Why?** Stoichiometric AFR for petrol ≈ **14.7 : 1** (by mass). Deviation causes power loss, poor emissions, or catalyst damage.
- **Sensor:** **Wideband O₂ sensor (Lambda sensor)** placed before and after the catalytic converter.
- **Actuator:** Electronic Fuel Injector.
- **Control:** Closed-loop using **lambda (λ)** feedback, where λ = 1.0 is the stoichiometric setpoint.
- **Logic Steps:**
  1. ECU reads MAP/MAF to estimate incoming air mass.
  2. ECU computes required fuel mass = Air Mass / 14.7.
  3. ECU commands injector pulse width (IPW) to deliver exact fuel.
  4. O₂ sensor confirms exhaust λ → ECU trims IPW using **Short-Term Fuel Trim (STFT)** and **Long-Term Fuel Trim (LTFT)**.

### 2.1.2 Ignition Timing Control
- **Why?** Combustion does not occur instantly; it takes time after the spark for the flame kernel to develop. Spark must fire **before Top Dead Center (BTDC)** so peak pressure occurs at ~15°–20° ATDC for max efficiency.
- **Sensor:** Crankshaft Position Sensor (CKP), Knock Sensor (KS), MAP, Coolant Temperature Sensor (CTS).
- **Actuator:** Ignition Coil (or Distributor in older systems).
- **Control Logic:**
  - Base timing from pre-calibrated 3D MAP (RPM vs. Load).
  - **Knock control** retards timing by 1°–3° increments if KS detects detonation, then gradually advances back.
  - **Cold-start** advances timing to compensate for slow flame propagation.

### 2.1.3 Idle Speed Control (ISC)
- **Why?** Engine must run smoothly at ~700–800 RPM with no driver input on throttle.
- **Actuator:** Idle Air Control Valve (IACV) or Electronic Throttle Body (drive-by-wire).
- **Sensors:** CTS, Alternator Load, A/C Compressor Clutch, Power Steering Pressure Switch.
- **Logic:** ECU maintains target RPM by adjusting bypass air around the closed throttle plate.

### 2.1.4 Throttle Control (Drive-by-Wire)
- **Sensor:** Accelerator Pedal Position Sensor (APPS — dual redundant for safety).
- **Actuator:** DC servo motor on throttle body.
- **Modes:** Normal, Idle, Cruise, Traction Control override.

### 2.1.5 Other SI Engine Parameters
| Parameter | Sensor | Actuator |
|---|---|---|
| **Coolant Temperature** | NTC Thermistor | Radiator Fan, ISCV, Injector pulse width |
| **Manifold Absolute Pressure (MAP)** | Piezo-resistive Pressure Sensor | Injector timing/duration |
| **Mass Air Flow (MAF)** | Hot-wire / Hot-film Anemometer | Injector pulse width |
| **Camshaft Position (CMP)** | Hall-effect sensor | Variable Valve Timing (VVT) solenoid |
| **Knock / Detonation** | Piezoelectric accelerometer | Ignition timing retard |
| **Exhaust Gas Recirculation (EGR)** | EGR valve position sensor | Vacuum/Electric EGR valve |
| **Evaporative Emissions (EVAP)** | Fuel tank pressure sensor | Purge solenoid valve |

> [!NOTE]
> **VVT (Variable Valve Timing):** Modern SI engines use **VVT-i (Toyota)** / **VANOS (BMW)** / **i-VTEC (Honda)** to continuously vary camshaft phase for better volumetric efficiency across RPM.

## 2.2 Parameters Controlled in CI (Compression Ignition / Diesel) Engines

CI engines have **no spark plug** — combustion occurs due to high compression heat (~700–900°C). The mechatronic complexity is actually **higher** in diesels because of:
- High injection pressures (Common Rail = **up to 2000 bar / 200 MPa**).
- Multi-injection strategies (Pilot, Main, After).
- Stringent emission norms (BS-VI, Euro 6).

### 2.2.1 Fuel Injection Timing & Pressure
- **Sensor:** CKP, CMP, Rail Pressure Sensor, Needle Motion Sensor (in piezo injectors).
- **Actuator:** **Solenoid / Piezo injector**, **SCV (Suction Control Valve)** on supply pump, **DRV (Pressure Control Valve)** on rail.
- **Control:** ECU computes exact crank angle of injection (e.g., 8° BTDC), pressure (e.g., 600 bar for pilot, 1600 bar for main), and quantity (mg/stroke).

### 2.2.2 Multiple Injection Strategy
Modern Common Rail systems use up to **9 injections per cycle**:
1. **Pilot injection** — small dose ~2 mm³/stroke to reduce combustion noise.
2. **Main injection** — bulk of fuel for power.
3. **After injection** — for DPF regeneration (raises exhaust to 600°C).
4. **Post injection** — for SCR dosing in some systems.

### 2.2.3 EGR Rate Control
- **Why?** Recirculating 5–30% exhaust gas lowers combustion temperature → reduces **NOx** formation.
- **Sensor:** EGR cooler temperature, ΔP sensor across EGR valve.
- **Actuator:** Electric EGR valve (no vacuum in modern diesels).

### 2.2.4 Boost Pressure Control (Turbocharger)
- **Sensor:** Boost Pressure Sensor, Ambient Pressure Sensor, Turbine Speed Sensor.
- **Actuator:** **VGT (Variable Geometry Turbine)** vane actuator, **Wastegate** solenoid.
- **Control:** Closed-loop on boost pressure target. Prevents over-boost that could damage engine.

### 2.2.5 Other CI Engine Parameters
| Parameter | Sensor | Actuator |
|---|---|---|
| **Common Rail Pressure** | High-pressure sensor (up to 2000 bar) | DRV / SCV |
| **DPF Soot Load** | ΔP across DPF | Late post-injection for regen |
| **SCR (AdBlue) Dosing** | NOx sensor (upstream/downstream) | AdBlue injector |
| **Glow Plug Operation** | Cylinder head temp sensor | PTC glow plug relay |
| **Swirl Flap Control** | Swirl flap position sensor | Actuator motor |
| **Crankcase Ventilation** | ΔP sensor | Separator/PCV valve |

## 2.3 Parameters Controlled in Other Parts of the Automobile

### 2.3.1 Anti-lock Braking System (ABS)
- **Sensors:** 4× **Wheel Speed Sensors (WSS)** — typically Hall-effect or magneto-resistive.
- **Actuator:** **Hydraulic Modulator** (pump + solenoid valves — 3 per wheel: Isolation, Dump, Suction).
- **Control Law:** Classic 3-stage cycle: **Pressure Build → Hold → Release** at ~15 Hz per wheel.
- **Phases:**
  1. **Pressure Build** — both valves open, pump runs, brake pressure rises.
  2. **Hold** — isolation valve closed, pressure held.
  3. **Release** — dump valve open, pressure drops (slip decreases).
- **Target Slip Ratio:** λ ≈ 8–30% (max braking force at ~15%).

### 2.3.2 Electronic Power Steering (EPS)
- **Sensors:** Steering Torque Sensor (T-bar type), Steering Angle Sensor, Vehicle Speed.
- **Actuator:** Brushless DC motor on steering column (C-EPS) or rack (R-EPS) or pinion (P-EPS).
- **Control:** ECU reduces assist at high speed, increases at low speed / parking.

### 2.3.3 Automatic Transmission (AT/AMT/DCT/CVT)
- **Sensors:** Input/Output shaft speed, Shift fork position, ATF temperature, Throttle position.
- **Actuator:** Solenoid pack (multiple PRNDL and shift solenoids), Torque converter lockup clutch.
- **Control:** **Adaptive shift map** based on driving style, road gradient, engine load.

### 2.3.4 Suspension (Active / Semi-Active)
- **Sensors:** Body accelerometers, Wheel acceleration, Height sensors (ultrasonic).
- **Actuators:** Hydraulic cylinders (active) / MR dampers (semi-active).
- **Modes:** Comfort, Normal, Sport — adjusts damping every 10 ms.

### 2.3.5 Battery Management System (BMS) in EVs/Hybrids
- **Sensors:** Cell voltage (each cell), Pack current (Hall-effect), Temperature (NTC at multiple points).
- **Actuators:** Contactor relays (Pre-charge, Main Positive, Main Negative), Cooling pump/fan.
- **Controlled Parameters:**
  - **State of Charge (SOC)** — 0–100%.
  - **State of Health (SOH)** — degradation indicator.
  - **Cell balancing** — passive (resistor) or active (DC-DC transfer).

### 2.3.6 Other Automotive Subsystems
| Subsystem | Controlled Parameter | Sensor | Actuator |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Climate Control (HVAC)** | Cabin temp, humidity | In-car NTC, sun-load sensor, humidity sensor | Blend door motor, blower, A/C compressor clutch |
| **Lighting** | Ambient light, headlamp level | Light sensor, level sensors | Headlamp motors, LED driver |
| **Central Locking** | Door state, key fob RF | Door ajar switches, RF receiver | Door lock motor |
| **Cruise Control (ACC)** | Distance to lead car | Radar / Lidar / Camera | Throttle, Brake actuator |
| **ESC (Stability)** | Yaw rate, Lateral G | Gyro MEMS, G-sensor, SAS | Individual wheel brake, throttle cut |

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these formulas for KTU exams — they appear in derivations and numerical problems.**

| Symbol | Formula | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| $AFR$ | $AFR = \dfrac{m_{air}}{m_{fuel}}$ | Air-Fuel Ratio | — |
| $\lambda$ (Lambda) | $\lambda = \dfrac{AFR_{actual}}{AFR_{stoich}}$ | Equivalence ratio indicator (λ=1 stoich) | — |
| Stoich Petrol | $AFR_{stoich} \approx 14.7$ | Petrol stoich ratio | — |
| Stoich Diesel | $AFR_{stoich} \approx 14.5$ | Diesel stoich ratio | — |
| Brake Torque | $T_b = \dfrac{P_b \cdot 60}{2\pi N}$ | Torque from brake power | Nm |
| Brake Power | $P_b = \dfrac{2\pi N T_b}{60}$ | Power from torque | W |
| Brake Specific Fuel Consumption | $BSFC = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{fuel}}{P_b}$ | Fuel economy indicator | g/kWh |
| Slip Ratio (ABS) | $\lambda_{slip} = \dfrac{V_{veh} - V_{wheel}}{V_{veh}}$ | Tire slip | % |
| Vehicle Speed | $V = \dfrac{2\pi r N}{60}$ | From wheel speed | m/s |
| Mean Effective Pressure | $BMEP = \dfrac{P_b \cdot n_{R}}{V_d \cdot N}$ | Brake mean effective pressure | bar |
| SOC (Coulomb Counting) | $SOC(t) = SOC_0 - \dfrac{1}{C_{rated}} \int_{0}^{t} I(\tau) \, d\tau$ | State of Charge | % |
| Engine Volumetric Efficiency | $\eta_{vol} = \dfrac{m_{air,actual}}{m_{air,ideal}}$ | Breathing efficiency | % |
| Ignition Timing Equation | $\theta_{spark} = \theta_{base}(N,L) - \theta_{knock} - \theta_{coolant}$ | Final spark advance | deg BTDC |
| Lambda Setpoint | $\lambda_{target} = 1.00 \pm 0.01$ | Catalyst window | — |
| DPF ΔP | $\Delta P = K \cdot \mu \cdot \dot{m} \cdot T$ | Filter loading indicator | mbar |
| Turbo Boost | $P_{boost} = P_{ambient} \cdot (1 + 0.5(\gamma-1) M^2)^{\frac{\gamma}{\gamma-1}}$ | Isentropic compression (ideal) | bar |

> [!NOTE]
> For KTU, **Air-Fuel Ratio, Lambda, BMEP, and Slip Ratio** are the most frequently asked formulas. Memorize units carefully.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Injector Pulse Width (IPW) Calculation

The ECU must compute how long to energize the fuel injector to deliver the exact fuel mass for the desired AFR. Let us derive this step-by-step.

### Step 1: Mass of Air per Cylinder per Cycle

Let:
- $V_d$ = displacement volume per cylinder (m³)
- $N$ = engine RPM
- $\eta_{vol}$ = volumetric efficiency
- $\rho_{air}$ = intake air density (kg/m³)
- $n_{cyl}$ = number of cylinders
- $n_{R}$ = number of crank revolutions per cycle (2 for 4-stroke, 1 for 2-stroke)

$$
\dot{m}_{air} = \dfrac{V_d \cdot N \cdot n_{cyl} \cdot \eta_{vol} \cdot \rho_{air}}{n_R \cdot 60}
$$

**Conversion Logic:** $V_d \times N/60$ gives the total swept volume rate of the engine. Multiplying by $\eta_{vol}$ gives the actual air inhaled. Multiplying by $\rho_{air}$ converts volume to mass. Dividing by $n_R$ accounts for the fact that intake happens only once every 2 revolutions in a 4-stroke engine.

### Step 2: Required Fuel Mass

$$
m_{fuel,req} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{air}}{AFR_{target}}
$$

For stoichiometric petrol: $AFR_{target} = 14.7$.

### Step 3: Injector Mass Flow Rate

Let the injector be characterized by a **static flow rate** $q_{static}$ in cm³/min (cc/min) at a reference pressure drop $\Delta P_{ref}$ of **3 bar** across the injector tip.

$$
q_{actual} = q_{static} \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{\Delta P_{rail} - \Delta P_{manifold}}{\Delta P_{ref}}}
$$

**Conversion Logic:** Injector flow is proportional to the square root of the pressure differential (Bernoulli's law for orifices). If manifold vacuum is high, the differential pressure is higher → more flow.

### Step 4: Injector Pulse Width

$$
t_{inject} = \dfrac{m_{fuel,req} \cdot n_{cyl}}{\rho_{fuel} \cdot q_{actual} \cdot (N/60) \cdot n_R^{-1}}
$$

$$
t_{inject} = \dfrac{6 \cdot n_{R} \cdot n_{cyl} \cdot m_{fuel,req}}{\rho_{fuel} \cdot q_{actual} \cdot N}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> In practice, ECU adds an **injector dead-time offset** (the brief delay between energizing the solenoid and the actual needle lift, typically 0.8–1.5 ms) and applies STFT/LTFT multipliers.

## 3.2 Derivation: Slip Ratio and ABS Control Law

### Step 1: Definition of Slip Ratio

For a vehicle braking on a tire, the slip ratio is defined as the normalized difference between vehicle speed and wheel speed.

Let:
- $V_{veh}$ = true vehicle speed (m/s)
- $V_{wheel} = \omega_{wheel} \cdot r_{tire}$ = linear speed of tire contact patch
- $r_{tire}$ = effective rolling radius
- $\omega_{wheel}$ = wheel angular velocity (rad/s)

$$
\lambda_{slip} = \dfrac{V_{veh} - V_{wheel}}{V_{veh}} \quad \text{(for braking, when } V_{veh} > V_{wheel}\text{)}
$$

### Step 2: Peak Mu-Slip Curve

The tire-road friction coefficient $\mu$ is not constant; it follows the well-known **Pacejka curve**:

$$
\mu(\lambda) = D \cdot \sin(C \cdot \arctan(B \cdot \lambda - E \cdot (B \cdot \lambda - \arctan(B \cdot \lambda))))
$$

For typical dry asphalt, peak $\mu$ occurs at $\lambda_{slip} \approx 0.08$ to $0.15$ (8–15%).

### Step 3: ABS Control Strategy

The ABS ECU operates a **hysteresis controller** around the optimum slip:
- If $\lambda_{slip} > 0.30$ → **Release** pressure.
- If $\lambda_{slip} < 0.08$ → **Re-apply** pressure.
- Otherwise → **Hold** pressure.

The cycle rate is **~15 Hz** to keep the wheel in the **high-μ zone**.

### Step 4: Wheel Deceleration Threshold

Alternative ABS control uses **wheel deceleration** as the trigger:

$$
\dot{\omega}_{threshold} \approx -1.5 \, g \quad \text{(approx. 14.7 m/s}^2 \text{)}
$$

If wheel deceleration exceeds this threshold, the ECU releases brake pressure to prevent lockup.

## 3.3 Derivation: State of Charge (SOC) Update in BMS

### Step 1: Coulomb Counting Equation

Let $C_{rated}$ = rated battery capacity (Ah). The change in SOC is proportional to the net charge transferred:

$$
\Delta SOC = -\dfrac{1}{C_{rated}} \int_{t_0}^{t_1} I(\tau) \, d\tau
$$

### Step 2: Discrete-Time Update

For ECU sampling at interval $\Delta t$:

$$
SOC[k+1] = SOC[k] - \dfrac{I[k] \cdot \Delta t}{3600 \cdot C_{rated}}
$$

**Conversion Logic:** $I[k] \cdot \Delta t$ gives charge in Ampere-seconds (coulombs). Dividing by 3600 converts to Ampere-hours. Dividing by $C_{rated}$ (in Ah) yields a unitless fraction. Multiplying by 100 gives percentage.

### Step 3: SOC Closed-Loop with OCV Correction

Open-loop Coulomb counting drifts. To correct, BMS uses **Open Circuit Voltage (OCV)** lookup table at rest:

$$
SOC_{corrected} = SOC_{coulomb} + K_p \cdot (OCV_{table}(SOC) - OCV_{measured})
$$

This is essentially a **Kalman-filter-like** correction in real BMS implementations.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Engine Speed Range and Required Control

| Engine Speed | SI Engine Control Focus | CI Engine Control Focus |
|---|---|---|
| **Cranking (0–100 RPM)** | Enrich fuel 2×, advance spark, IACV open | Glow plug ON, cranking fuel map, no injection until RPM > 100 |
| **Idle (700–900 RPM)** | Closed-loop λ, ISCV active, low advance | Idle fuel map, low EGR, minimum rail pressure |
| **Part Load (1500–3000 RPM)** | Stoich AFR, MAP-based spark advance, EGR off | Pilot + Main injection, EGR ON, VGT partial |
| **Full Load (WOT)** | Power enrichment (AFR 12:1), max advance, EGR off | Full EGR OFF, max rail pressure, single main injection |
| **Overrun (Decel)** | Fuel cut-off, IACV controls dashpot | Fuel cut-off, EGR closed, brake vac maintained |
| **High RPM (Redline)** | Rev limiter cuts spark, fuel cut at +200 RPM | ECU limits rail pressure, fuel cut at redline |

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Exam Style)

**Problem:** A 4-cylinder, 4-stroke SI engine has displacement $V_d = 500 \, \text{cm}^3/\text{cyl}$, volumetric efficiency $\eta_{vol} = 0.85$, runs at 3000 RPM, intake air density $\rho_{air} = 1.2 \, \text{kg/m}^3$. Target $AFR = 14.7$. Find the fuel flow rate (kg/h).

### Step 1: Air Mass Flow Rate

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{m}_{air} &= \dfrac{V_d \cdot N \cdot n_{cyl} \cdot \eta_{vol} \cdot \rho_{air}}{n_R \cdot 60} \\
&= \dfrac{(500 \times 10^{-6}) \cdot 3000 \cdot 4 \cdot 0.85 \cdot 1.2}{2 \cdot 60} \\
&= \dfrac{(500 \times 10^{-6}) \cdot 3000 \cdot 4 \cdot 0.85 \cdot 1.2}{120}
\end{aligned}
$$

Compute numerator:
- $500 \times 10^{-6} = 5 \times 10^{-4}$
- $5 \times 10^{-4} \times 3000 = 1.5$
- $1.5 \times 4 = 6.0$
- $6.0 \times 0.85 = 5.1$
- $5.1 \times 1.2 = 6.12$

So:
$$
\dot{m}_{air} = \dfrac{6.12}{120} = 0.0510 \, \text{kg/s}
$$

### Step 2: Fuel Mass Flow Rate

$$
\dot{m}_{fuel} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{air}}{AFR} = \dfrac{0.0510}{14.7} = 0.003469 \, \text{kg/s}
$$

### Step 3: Convert to kg/h

$$
\dot{m}_{fuel} = 0.003469 \times 3600 = 12.49 \, \text{kg/h}
$$

### Answer

$$
\boxed{\dot{m}_{fuel} \approx 12.5 \, \text{kg/h}}
$$
