# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper PCMUT602]**

**List any six parameters controlled by the ECU in a Spark Ignition (SI) engine. Mention the sensor and actuator used for each parameter.**

#### Model Answer (6 × 0.5 = 3 marks):
1. **Air-Fuel Ratio** — Sensor: Wideband O₂ / Lambda sensor; Actuator: Electronic Fuel Injector.
2. **Ignition Timing** — Sensor: CKP + MAP + Knock sensor; Actuator: Ignition coil.
3. **Idle Speed** — Sensor: CKP + Coolant temp; Actuator: Idle Air Control Valve (IACV).
4. **Throttle Position** — Sensor: Accelerator Pedal Position Sensor (APPS); Actuator: Electronic Throttle Body (DC servo).
5. **Coolant Temperature** — Sensor: NTC thermistor; Actuator: Radiator fan, ISCV trim, fuel enrichment.
6. **Knock / Detonation** — Sensor: Piezoelectric accelerometer on block; Actuator: Ignition timing retard.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Sample Paper]**

**Differentiate between the control parameters in SI and CI engines. Why does CI engine require higher injection pressure control?**

#### Model Answer (3 marks):

| Aspect | SI Engine | CI Engine |
|---|---|---|
| **Ignition Source** | Spark plug | Compression heat (no spark) |
| **AFR Control** | Stoichiometric (λ=1) for 3-way cat | Lean operation, EGR heavy |
| **Combustion Noise** | Lower | Higher → pilot injection needed |
| **Injection Pressure** | 3–5 bar (port injection) | 1600–2000 bar (common rail) |
| **Key Control** | Spark timing + AFR | Injection timing + Pressure + EGR |

**Why high pressure in CI?** Diesel fuel is less volatile and injected directly into hot compressed air. To achieve fine atomization (droplet size ~10 µm) for proper mixing and rapid combustion, the fuel must be injected at **very high pressure** (typically 1600 bar in modern common-rail systems). High pressure also improves fuel economy and reduces soot/PM emissions by ensuring complete air-fuel mixing. (2 marks)

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### **Question A: [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Modified for 2024 Scheme]**

**a) Explain with a neat block diagram the various parameters controlled in a modern SI engine management system. Discuss in detail the closed-loop Air-Fuel Ratio control using the lambda sensor. (7 marks)**

**b) With a functional block diagram, describe the working of an Anti-lock Braking System (ABS). Explain the three phases of ABS control and the role of slip ratio. (7 marks)**

#### Part (a) Model Solution:

**Introduction (1 mark):**
A modern SI engine management system (EMS) integrates sensors, ECU, and actuators to control **Air-Fuel Ratio, Ignition Timing, Idle Speed, Throttle, Knock, and Emissions** in real time.

**Block Diagram (1.5 marks):** See Section 4.1 Mermaid block.

**AFR Closed-Loop Control (4.5 marks):**
1. ECU uses MAF/MAP + RPM + CTS inputs to compute the **base fuel quantity** = air mass / 14.7.
2. ECU commands **injector pulse width** to deliver this base fuel.
3. The **wideband O₂ sensor** in the exhaust measures actual λ continuously.
4. ECU compares actual λ with target (1.00):
   - If λ > 1 (lean) → ECU **increases** IPW (enrichment).
   - If λ < 1 (rich) → ECU **decreases** IPW (enrichment off).
5. Corrections are stored as:
   - **STFT (Short-Term Fuel Trim):** ±25% range, fast response.
   - **LTFT (Long-Term Fuel Trim):** ±25% range, learned average.
6. The cycle repeats at **~10 Hz**, holding λ at 1.00 ± 0.01 to keep the **3-way catalytic converter** at peak efficiency (≥98% conversion of CO, HC, NOx).

#### Part (b) Model Solution:

**Introduction (0.5 mark):**
ABS prevents wheel lockup during heavy braking by modulating hydraulic pressure, keeping the wheel in the high-friction slip range.

**Block Diagram (1.5 marks):** See Section 4.2 Mermaid.

**Three Phases of ABS Control (3.5 marks):**

1. **Pressure Build Phase:**
   - Both isolation and dump valves are open.
   - Pump runs, brake fluid pressure rises toward driver's demand.
   - Wheel decelerates; wheel speed decreases.

2. **Pressure Hold Phase:**
   - ECU detects slip ratio approaching 15–20%.
   - Isolation valve closes → pressure held constant.
   - ECU observes wheel speed to determine next step.

3. **Pressure Release Phase:**
   - Slip ratio > 30% (wheel about to lock).
   - Dump valve opens, pressure reduces.
   - Wheel accelerates back.

**Slip Ratio (1.5 marks):**
- Formula: $\lambda_{slip} = (V_{veh} - V_{wheel})/V_{veh}$
- Peak friction occurs at $\lambda_{slip} \approx 0.10$ to $0.15$.
- ABS modulates pressure in a hysteresis loop around this target.
- Result: vehicle remains steerable and stops in shorter distance.

---

### **Question B: [KTU University Exam — June 2023, Model Paper]**

**a) Discuss the parameters controlled in a modern Common Rail Diesel (CI) engine. Explain the multi-injection strategy and EGR control with neat diagrams. (7 marks)**

**b) Describe the parameters controlled in a Battery Management System (BMS) for an electric vehicle. Explain the State of Charge (SOC) estimation using Coulomb counting with a step-by-step derivation. (7 marks)**

#### Part (a) Model Solution:

**Introduction (1 mark):**
A modern CI engine uses a **Common Rail Direct Injection (CRDi)** system with pressures up to **2000 bar** and up to **9 injection events per cycle** for noise, performance, and emission control.

**Parameters Controlled (2.5 marks):**
1. **Injection Timing** — CKP + CMP + Rail Pressure → inject crank angle.
2. **Injection Pressure** — Rail Pressure Sensor → DRV / SCV.
3. **Injection Quantity** — Calculated from pedal, load, RPM.
4. **EGR Rate** — ΔP across EGR valve + NOx sensor.
5. **Boost Pressure** — VGT actuator + boost pressure sensor.
6. **DPF Regeneration** — ΔP across DPF + temp sensors.
7. **Glow Plug** — Cylinder head temp sensor.

**Multi-Injection Strategy (2 marks):**
- **Pilot injection** (2 mm³/stroke, ~10° BTDC) — reduces pressure rise rate, lowers combustion noise.
- **Main injection** (bulk fuel, ~8° BTDC) — produces torque.
- **After injection** (small, ~30° ATDC) — raises exhaust temp to 600°C for DPF regeneration.
- **Post injection** — supports SCR AdBlue dosing.

**EGR Control (1.5 marks):**
- EGR routes 5–30% exhaust back to intake.
- Lowers combustion temperature (peak 1800°C → 1500°C).
- Reduces **NOx** by ~50%.
- ECU controls electric EGR valve opening based on ΔP and NOx sensor feedback.
- At WOT / high load, EGR closes to maximize power.

#### Part (b) Model Solution:

**Introduction (0.5 mark):**
A BMS ensures safe, efficient operation of the high-voltage battery pack by monitoring voltage, current, temperature, SOC, and SOH.

**Parameters Controlled (1.5 marks):**
- Cell voltage (each cell, 0–5 V).
- Pack current (Hall sensor, ±500 A).
- Pack temperature (multiple NTCs).
- **State of Charge (SOC):** 0–100%.
- **State of Health (SOH):** 0–100%.
- Cell balancing.
- Thermal management (cooling).
- Isolation resistance monitoring (high-voltage safety).

**SOC Derivation via Coulomb Counting (5 marks):**

**Step 1:** The rated capacity of the battery is $C_{rated}$ in Ampere-hours (Ah).

**Step 2:** The net charge transferred over time interval $\Delta t$ is:
$$
\Delta Q = I \cdot \Delta t
$$

**Step 3:** The change in SOC is the ratio of charge transferred to rated capacity:
$$
\Delta SOC = -\dfrac{\Delta Q}{C_{rated}} = -\dfrac{I \cdot \Delta t}{C_{rated}}
$$

**Step 4:** In discrete-time, with sampling period $T_s$:
$$
SOC[k+1] = SOC[k] - \dfrac{I[k] \cdot T_s}{3600 \cdot C_{rated}}
$$

Conversion logic: $I \cdot T_s$ gives coulombs; dividing by 3600 gives Ah; dividing by $C_{rated}$ (in Ah) yields the unitless fraction. Negative sign because discharging reduces SOC.

**Step 5:** The continuous integral form is:
$$
SOC(t) = SOC(t_0) - \dfrac{1}{3600 \cdot C_{rated}} \int_{t_0}^{t} I(\tau) \, d\tau
$$

**Step 6:** Drift correction using OCV lookup:
$$
SOC_{corrected} = SOC_{coulomb} + K_p (OCV_{table}(SOC) - OCV_{measured})
$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where KTU Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Confusing SI and CI control parameters** — SI engines control **spark timing**, CI engines control **injection timing**. Mixing these up costs 2–3 marks.
> 2. **Forgetting units in numericals** — Air mass in kg/s, fuel flow in kg/h. Always write units in the final answer.
> 3. **Not mentioning closed-loop vs. open-loop** — KTU expects you to state whether the control is open-loop (no feedback) or closed-loop (sensor feedback). E.g., AFR is closed-loop; cold-start fuel is open-loop.
> 4. **Missing sensor-actuator pairing** — If you mention AFR control but don't mention O₂ sensor and fuel injector, you lose 1 mark.
> 5. **In ABS, forgetting to write the slip ratio formula** — The slip ratio formula $\lambda_{slip} = (V_{veh} - V_{wheel})/V_{veh}$ is mandatory.
> 6. **In BMS, omitting the Coulomb counting derivation** — KTU frequently asks for the step-by-step derivation with the discrete-time update equation.
> 7. **Diagram without arrows or labels** — Block diagrams without clear arrows (sensor → ECU → actuator) get 0 marks for diagram component.
> 8. **Confusing EGR and EVAP** — EGR = Exhaust Gas Recirculation (engine); EVAP = Evaporative emissions control (fuel tank vapor trap). Easy mix-up.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU PCMUT602 Module 4**

- [x] **SI Engine Control Parameters:** AFR (λ), Ignition Timing, Idle Speed, Throttle (Drive-by-Wire), Coolant Temp, Knock, MAP, MAF, CMP, EGR, VVT, EVAP.
- [x] **CI Engine Control Parameters:** Injection Timing, Injection Pressure (Common Rail up to 2000 bar), Injection Quantity, EGR Rate, Boost Pressure (VGT), DPF Regen, SCR AdBlue, Glow Plug, Swirl Flap.
- [x] **AFR Stoichiometric:** Petrol = 14.7:1, Diesel = 14.5:1. Lambda = 1.00 is the setpoint.
- [x] **Lambda Formula:** $\lambda = AFR_{actual} / AFR_{stoich}$.
- [x] **3-Way Catalyst:** Works efficiently only at λ = 1.00 ± 0.01.
- [x] **ABS Slip Ratio:** $\lambda_{slip} = (V_{veh} - V_{wheel})/V_{veh}$; target 8–15%.
- [x] **ABS Phases:** Pressure Build → Hold → Release, ~15 Hz cycle.
- [x] **BMS SOC Formula:** $SOC[k+1] = SOC[k] - (I[k] \cdot T_s)/(3600 \cdot C_{rated})$.
- [x] **CAN Bus:** Standard automotive communication protocol (ISO 11898) connecting all ECUs at 250 kbps to 1 Mbps.
- [x] **Drive-by-Wire:** No mechanical cable; APPS sensor + DC servo on throttle.
- [x] **VVT/VANOS/i-VTEC:** Variable valve timing for better volumetric efficiency.
- [x] **Common Rail Pressure:** Modern systems reach 2000–2500 bar.
- [x] **Pilot Injection:** Reduces diesel knock; typically 1–3 mm³/stroke.
- [x] **EPS types:** C-EPS (column), P-EPS (pinion), R-EPS (rack).
- [x] **ESC sensors:** Yaw gyro + lateral accelerometer + steering angle.
- [x] **NOx formation:** Reduced by EGR (lowers peak temp) and SCR (AdBlue dosing).
- [x] **HC, CO reduction:** Achieved by 3-way catalytic converter (SI engines).
- [x] **PM/Soot control:** DPF (Diesel Particulate Filter) with periodic regeneration.

### Final One-Line Mantra for the Exam:

> **"In SI: control SPARK and AIR-FUEL; in CI: control INJECTION TIMING, PRESSURE, and EGR; everywhere else: control FEEDBACK, SAFETY, and EFFICIENCY."**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Engine Management System (EMS): Introduction, Remapping of ECU, Cold Start and Warm Up Phases, Idle Speed Control, Acceleration and Full Load Enrichment, Deceleration Fuel Cutoff
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# ENGINE MANAGEMENT SYSTEM (EMS) - CORE FOUNDATIONS

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMUT602 - MECHATRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Engine Management System (EMS): Introduction, Remapping of ECU, Cold Start and Warm Up Phases, Idle Speed Control, Acceleration and Full Load Enrichment, Deceleration Fuel Cutoff

### Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

The **Engine Management System (EMS)** is an integrated electronic control architecture that uses an **Electronic Control Unit (ECU)** to monitor real-time engine operating conditions through sensors and precisely orchestrate actuators governing **fuel delivery, ignition timing, idle speed, and emissions**. It is the principal application of *Mechatronics* — the synergistic integration of mechanical systems, electronic sensors, microelectronic controllers, and embedded software.

The EMS implements a **closed-loop, multi-variable, time-varying control strategy** where fuel injection quantity and ignition timing are continuously recalculated based on inputs such as:

- **Manifold Absolute Pressure (MAP)** or **Mass Air Flow (MAF)**
- **Engine Coolant Temperature (ECT)**
- **Throttle Position Sensor (TPS)** / Accelerator Pedal Position
- **Crankshaft and Camshaft Position Sensors** (Hall-effect / Variable Reluctance)
- **Lambda (O₂) Sensor** (Narrowband or Wideband)
- **Knock Sensor** (Piezoelectric)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight**: The EMS is the canonical *Mechatronics case study* — it demonstrates the **Sense → Compute → Actuate** paradigm. KTU examiners expect students to identify sensor subsystems, the ECU as the brain, and actuator subsystems explicitly.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Conductor of an Orchestra:**
> Imagine an orchestra without a conductor. Each musician (injector, spark plug, idle valve) might play well individually, but the symphony collapses. The **EMS is the conductor** — the ECU reads sheet music (sensor inputs: how hard you press the pedal = TPS, how much air enters = MAF, what the engine temperature is = ECT) and instructs each musician *exactly when and how loudly* to play (actuator commands). Just as a conductor adjusts tempo during a crescendo (acceleration enrichment) or pianissimo (idle), the ECU modulates fuel and spark to maintain harmony (combustion efficiency, λ = 1, low emissions).

> [!NOTE]
> **Automotive Analogy — The Human Body:**
> The EMS mirrors the human nervous system: 
> - **Sensors** = Eyes, skin, nerves (perceive environment)
> - **ECU** = Brain (processes information)
> - **Actuators** = Muscles (execute action)
> - **Exhaust** = Breath/excretion (feedback loop via O₂ sensors)
> - **Knock sensor** = Pain receptors (detect abnormal stress)

### 1.3 Key Performance Metrics & Physical Constants

The following constants and standard metrics govern the design and analysis of any EMS:

| Parameter | Symbol | Standard Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Stoichiometric Air-Fuel Ratio (Gasoline) | AFR_stoich | **14.7 : 1** | kg air / kg fuel |
| Stoichiometric AFR (Diesel) | AFR_stoich | **14.5 : 1** | kg air / kg fuel |
| Stoichiometric AFR (CNG) | AFR_stoich | **17.2 : 1** | kg air / kg fuel |
| Universal Gas Constant | R | **8.314** | J/(mol·K) |
| Molar mass of air | M_air | **28.97** | g/mol |
| Standard air density (sea level, 25°C) | ρ_air | **1.184** | kg/m³ |
| Idle speed target (typical gasoline) | N_idle | **700 – 900** | rpm |
| Cold-start cranking speed | N_crank | **150 – 300** | rpm |
| Typical injection pressure (Port) | P_inj | **3 – 4** | bar |
| Direct Injection Pressure | P_inj | **100 – 350** | bar |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **stoichiometric ratio of 14.7:1** is the *chemical minimum* of air required to completely combust 1 kg of gasoline. It is the universal reference around which the entire fuel map is calibrated.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# SECTION 2 — DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Functional Architecture of the EMS

The EMS is structured into three operational layers that KTU examiners frequently test:

### Layer 1: Sensing Subsystem (Input Stage)
Sensors convert physical phenomena into voltage/frequency signals compatible with the ECU's Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC). 

- **Mass Air Flow (MAF) Sensor**: Hot-wire anemometer; measures *mass* of inducted air per second (kg/s).
- **Manifold Absolute Pressure (MAP) Sensor**: Piezoresistive; measures boost pressure in intake manifold.
- **Throttle Position Sensor (TPS)**: Potentiometric or non-contact (Hall-effect); 0–5 V output representing 0°–90° throttle opening.
- **Engine Coolant Temperature (ECT)**: NTC thermistor; resistance inversely proportional to temperature.
- **Crankshaft Position Sensor (CKP)**: Variable Reluctance / Hall-effect; provides engine RPM and TDC reference.
- **Camshaft Position Sensor (CMP)**: Determines fuel injection phase (sequential vs. batch mode).
- **Wideband Lambda Sensor**: Zirconia / Titania; outputs a current proportional to oxygen concentration.
- **Knock Sensor (KS)**: Piezoelectric; detects engine knock vibrations (5–15 kHz band).

### Layer 2: ECU (Processing Stage)
The ECU is a **32-bit or 64-bit automotive-grade microcontroller** (e.g., Infineon TriCore, NXP MPC5xxx, Renesas RH850) running the **AUTOSAR** real-time operating system. Its computational cycle (typically 5–10 ms for injection/ignition tasks) executes:

1. Read all sensor inputs (ADC scan).
2. Compute desired air mass → desired fuel mass.
3. Compute ignition advance angle from pre-stored 3-D maps.
4. Generate pulse-width modulated (PWM) injector and ignition coil signals.
5. Perform diagnostics (OBD-II) and adaptive learning.

### Layer 3: Actuation Subsystem (Output Stage)
- **Fuel Injectors**: Solenoid or piezoelectric; controlled by pulse width T_p (in milliseconds).
- **Ignition Coil**: Transformer; ECU controls dwell time and primary current cut-off.
- **Idle Air Control (IAC) Valve / Electronic Throttle Body (ETB)**: Stepper motor / DC motor controlling bypass air.
- **Variable Valve Timing (VVT) Solenoids**: Cam phaser actuation.
- **Evaporative Emissions (EVAP) Purge Valve**: Controls fuel vapor recovery.
- **Electronic Throttle Control (ETC) Actuator**: Drive-by-wire pedal interpretation.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Rules for LaTeX Tables:** Use `\vert` instead of `|` (pipe character) to prevent Markdown table syntax breakage.

### Master Formula Table for KTU Exam Calculations

| # | Concept | Formula | Variable Definitions | Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Lambda (Equivalence Ratio)** | $\lambda = \frac{AFR_{actual}}{AFR_{stoich}}$ | $AFR_{actual}$ = measured air-fuel ratio, $AFR_{stoich}$ = 14.7 (gasoline) | dimensionless |
| 2 | **Equivalence Ratio (φ)** | $\phi = \frac{1}{\lambda}$ | $\phi = 1$: stoich; $\phi < 1$: lean; $\phi > 1$: rich | dimensionless |
| 3 | **Required Fuel Mass** | $m_{fuel} = \frac{m_{air}}{\lambda \cdot AFR_{stoich}}$ | $m_{air}$ = mass of air inducted per cycle | kg |
| 4 | **Mass of Air per Cycle** | $m_{air} = \frac{V_{d} \cdot \eta_{vol} \cdot \rho_{air}}{n_{cyl}}$ | $V_{d}$ = displacement, $\eta_{vol}$ = volumetric efficiency, $n_{cyl}$ = cylinders | kg/cycle |
| 5 | **Injector Pulse Width (T_p)** | $T_{p} = \frac{m_{fuel}}{Q_{inj}} \cdot 1000$ | $Q_{inj}$ = injector static flow rate | ms |
| 6 | **Volumetric Efficiency** | $\eta_{vol} = \frac{m_{air,actual}}{m_{air,theoretical}} = \frac{\rho_{manifold}}{\rho_{ambient}} \cdot \frac{T_{ambient}}{T_{manifold}}$ | ratio of actual to theoretical air mass | dimensionless (0–1) |
| 7 | **Engine RPM** | $N = \frac{f_{CKP}}{N_{teeth}} \cdot 60$ | $f_{CKP}$ = crank sensor frequency, $N_{teeth}$ = teeth on reluctor (60-2 typical) | rpm |
| 8 | **Crank Angle per ADC Sample** | $\theta_{sample} = \frac{6 \cdot N \cdot T_{sample}}{N_{teeth}}$ | $T_{sample}$ = ADC sampling period | degrees |
| 9 | **Idle Speed Target (with load)** | $N_{idle,target} = N_{idle,base} + \sum_{i} K_i \cdot L_i$ | $K_i$ = compensation gain, $L_i$ = load factor (A/C, P/S, alternator) | rpm |
| 10 | **Dwell Time (Ignition Coil)** | $T_{dwell} = \frac{L \cdot I_{peak}}{V_{bat}}$ | $L$ = primary inductance, $I_{peak}$ = peak current, $V_{bat}$ = battery voltage | ms |
| 11 | **Acceleration Enrichment** | $T_{acc} = K_{acc} \cdot \frac{\Delta TPS}{\Delta t} \cdot f(ECT)$ | $K_{acc}$ = calibration constant, $f(ECT)$ = coolant temperature factor | ms |
| 12 | **Injected Air Mass (Speed-Density)** | $m_{air} = \frac{P_{map} \cdot V_{d} \cdot \eta_{vol}}{R \cdot T_{manifold}}$ | $P_{map}$ = manifold pressure, $T_{manifold}$ = manifold air temperature | kg |

### 2.3 The Fuel Map — Heart of the EMS

The ECU stores fuel quantity as a **3-dimensional map** indexed by:
- **X-axis:** Engine RPM (typically 600 – 7000 rpm)
- **Y-axis:** Load (MAP in kPa, or MAF in g/s, or TPS %)
- **Z-value:** Base injection pulse width T_p (ms)

This map is mathematically equivalent to:

$$T_{p,base}(RPM, Load) = f_{map}(RPM, Load)$$

The ECU then applies **multiplicative correction factors** to this base value:

$$T_{p,final} = T_{p,base} \cdot f_{coolant} \cdot f_{lambda} \cdot f_{accel} \cdot f_{bat} \cdot f_{air\_temp}$$

Where each correction factor is bounded between 0.8 and 1.5 under normal operation, except during cold start where it may exceed 2.0.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# SECTION 3 — STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation of Base Injector Pulse Width (Speed-Density Method)

The **speed-density method** is the canonical KTU derivation. We derive it exhaustively:

### Step 1: Ideal Gas Law Applied to Manifold Air
The air in the intake manifold is treated as an ideal gas:

$$P_{map} \cdot V_{map} = m_{air} \cdot R_{specific} \cdot T_{manifold}$$

Solving for the air mass in the manifold volume $V_{map}$:

$$m_{air,manifold} = \frac{P_{map} \cdot V_{map}}{R_{specific} \cdot T_{manifold}}$$

**Engineering interpretation:** Higher manifold pressure ⇒ more air mass available; higher temperature ⇒ lower density (less air). This is why turbocharged engines get *more fuel automatically* without MAF sensor recalibration.

### Step 2: Air Mass Trapped in One Cylinder per Cycle
When the intake valve closes, only a fraction of the manifold air is trapped into the cylinder. This is captured by the **volumetric efficiency** $\eta_{vol}$:

$$m_{air,cyl} = \eta_{vol} \cdot \frac{P_{map} \cdot V_{d}}{R_{specific} \cdot T_{manifold}}$$

**Engineering interpretation:** $\eta_{vol}$ varies with RPM and load (peaks around 80–95% at mid-RPM for naturally aspirated engines). The ECU stores $\eta_{vol}$ as a 3-D map or computes it via the **Mean-Value Engine Model (MVEM)**.

### Step 3: Required Fuel Mass for Stoichiometric Combustion
For the target air-fuel ratio $AFR_{target} = \lambda \cdot AFR_{stoich}$:

$$m_{fuel,req} = \frac{m_{air,cyl}}{\lambda \cdot AFR_{stoich}}$$

**Engineering interpretation:** For gasoline, $AFR_{stoich} = 14.7$. If $\lambda = 1.0$ (stoichiometric — normal closed-loop operation), then $AFR_{target} = 14.7$. During cold start, $\lambda \approx 0.6$–$0.8$ (rich mixture).

### Step 4: Conversion to Injector Pulse Width
An injector delivers fuel at a calibrated static flow rate $Q_{inj}$ (units: kg/s or cm³/min). The time required to inject $m_{fuel,req}$ is:

$$T_{p} = \frac{m_{fuel,req}}{Q_{inj}} = \frac{1}{Q_{inj}} \cdot \frac{m_{air,cyl}}{\lambda \cdot AFR_{stoich}}$$

Substituting Step 2:

$$\boxed{T_{p} = \frac{\eta_{vol} \cdot P_{map} \cdot V_{d}}{R_{specific} \cdot T_{manifold} \cdot Q_{inj} \cdot \lambda \cdot AFR_{stoich}}}$$

### Step 3 — Numerical Worked Example (KTU Exam-Style)

> **Given:**
> - 4-cylinder engine, displacement $V_d = 2.0$ L total
> - MAP sensor reading: $P_{map} = 60$ kPa (part throttle)
> - Manifold air temperature: $T_{manifold} = 320$ K
> - $\eta_{vol} = 0.85$
> - Injector flow: $Q_{inj} = 250$ cm³/min = $\frac{250 \times 10^{-6}}{60}$ m³/s = $4.167 \times 10^{-6}$ m³/s
> - Gasoline density: $\rho_{fuel} = 750$ kg/m³
> - Target lambda $\lambda = 1.0$, $AFR_{stoich} = 14.7$
> - $R_{specific}$ for air = 287 J/(kg·K)

**Step 1 — Air mass per cylinder per cycle:**
$$m_{air,cyl} = \eta_{vol} \cdot \frac{P_{map} \cdot V_{d}/n_{cyl}}{R_{specific} \cdot T_{manifold}}$$
$$= 0.85 \cdot \frac{60000 \cdot (0.002/4)}{287 \cdot 320}$$
$$= 0.85 \cdot \frac{60000 \cdot 0.0005}{91840}$$
$$= 0.85 \cdot \frac{30}{91840}$$
$$= 0.85 \cdot 3.267 \times 10^{-4}$$
$$= 2.777 \times 10^{-4} \text{ kg per cylinder per cycle}$$

**Step 2 — Required fuel mass:**
$$m_{fuel} = \frac{m_{air}}{\lambda \cdot AFR_{stoich}} = \frac{2.777 \times 10^{-4}}{1.0 \cdot 14.7} = 1.889 \times 10^{-5} \text{ kg}$$

**Step 3 — Injector pulse width:**
$$T_p = \frac{m_{fuel}}{Q_{inj} \cdot \rho_{fuel}} = \frac{1.889 \times 10^{-5}}{4.167 \times 10^{-6} \cdot 750}$$
$$= \frac{1.889 \times 10^{-5}}{3.125 \times 10^{-3}} = 6.04 \times 10^{-3} \text{ s} = 6.04 \text{ ms}$$

## 3.2 Analytical Decision Matrix — Operational Phases

The ECU executes a **state machine** that switches between fuel strategies based on operating conditions:

| Operating Phase | TPS Position | Coolant Temp | Lambda Target | Key Correction | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Cold Start** | Cranking | < 30 °C | 0.6 – 0.8 | +100% to +200% fuel | Compensate poor fuel vaporization |
| **Warm-Up** | Any | 30 – 80 °C | 0.85 – 0.95 | +10% to +50% fuel | Smooth transition to closed-loop |
| **Idle (Closed Loop)** | 0% | > 80 °C | **1.00 ± 0.02** | IAC valve modulation | Maintain 700–900 rpm |
| **Part Load (Closed Loop)** | 5–60% | > 80 °C | **1.00 ± 0.02** | O₂ feedback | Minimize HC, CO emissions |
| **Wide Open Throttle (WOT)** | > 80% | > 60 °C | **0.78 – 0.85** | Full Load Enrichment | Cool combustion chamber, prevent knock |
| **Acceleration** | Rapid rise | Any | Variable | Acceleration pump + wall-wetting comp | Eliminate tip-in hesitation |
| **Deceleration** | 0%, RPM > threshold | > 60 °C | **No injection (DFCO)** | Injectors OFF | Fuel economy, emissions |
| **Overrun Cutoff Re-engage** | TPS returns, RPM drops | > 60 °C | 1.00 | Restore injection | Smooth re-engagement |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# SECTION 4 — STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 EMS Top-Level Block Architecture (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    node1["Driver Input Pedal"] --> node2["TPS Sensor"]
    node3["Air Filter"] --> node4["MAF Sensor"]
    node5["Coolant Circuit"] --> node6["ECT Sensor"]
    node7["Exhaust Manifold"] --> node8["Lambda O2 Sensor"]
    node9["Engine Block"] --> node10["CKP Sensor"]
    node9 --> node11["CMP Sensor"]
    node9 --> node12["Knock Sensor"]
    node2 --> nodeECU["ECU Microcontroller"]
    node4 --> nodeECU
    node6 --> nodeECU
    node8 --> nodeECU
    node10 --> nodeECU
    node11 --> nodeECU
    node12 --> nodeECU
    nodeECU --> node13["Fuel Injectors PWM"]
    nodeECU --> node14["Ignition Coils"]
    nodeECU --> node15["IAC Valve Stepper"]
    nodeECU --> node16["Fuel Pump Relay"]
    nodeECU --> node17["EGR Valve"]
    nodeECU --> node18["Check Engine Lamp OBD"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Solid lines from sensors (left) flow into the ECU (center). The ECU executes its embedded control algorithm and outputs control signals to actuators (right). This is the canonical Sense–Compute–Actuate mechatronic loop.

## 4.2 Cold Start & Warm-Up State Transition Flow

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> KeyOn
    KeyOn --> FuelPrime: pump priming 2 sec
    FuelPrime --> Cranking: starter engaged
    Cranking --> ColdStartEnrichment: RPM detected ECT below 30C
    ColdStartEnrichment --> AfterStartEnrichment: engine fires lambda 0.65
    AfterStartEnrichment --> WarmUpEnrichment: coolant rises 30 to 80C
    WarmUpEnrichment --> ClosedLoop: ECT above 80C lambda 1.0
    ClosedLoop --> [*]: normal operation
    ColdStartEnrichment --> FloodClear: throttle wide open no start
    FloodClear --> Cranking
```

## 4.3 Idle Speed Control Closed-Loop Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["Target Idle RPM Setpoint"] --> nodeSum["Comparator Summing Junction"]
    nodeB["Actual RPM from CKP Sensor"] --> nodeSum
    nodeSum --> nodeC["PID Controller ECU"]
    nodeC --> nodeD["PWM Signal"]
    nodeD --> nodeE["IAC Stepper Motor Valve"]
    nodeE --> nodeF["Bypass Air Passage"]
    nodeF --> nodeG["Engine Intake Manifold"]
    nodeG --> nodeH["Combustion RPM Output"]
    nodeH --> nodeB
    nodeI["Load Disturbance AC PS Alt"] -.-> nodeF
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The IAC system uses a **PID controller** with gains typically: $K_p \approx 0.5$ to $2.0$, $K_i \approx 0.05$ to $0.2$, $K_d \approx 0.01$. The disturbance inputs (A/C compressor engagement, alternator load, power steering pump) are compensated *proactively* by adding a feed-forward term:

$$N_{idle,target} = N_{idle,base} + K_{P} \cdot e(t) + K_{I} \int e(t) \, dt + K_{D} \frac{de(t)}{dt} + \sum K_{i} \cdot L_{i}(t)$$

where $e(t) = N_{target} - N_{actual}$ is the control error.

## 4.4 Fuel Map Strategy (Lambda Map) — Enrichment Regions

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph S1["CLOSED LOOP STOICH LAMBDA 1.00"]
        A1["Cruise Light Load"]
        A2["Part Throttle Cruise"]
        A3["Moderate Load"]
    end
    subgraph S2["POWER ENRICHMENT LAMBDA 0.78 to 0.85"]
        B1["WOT Full Load"]
        B2["High RPM High Load"]
        B3["Knock Protection"]
    end
    subgraph S3["FUEL CUTOFF LAMBDA INFINITE"]
        C1["Decel Overrun RPM above 1500"]
        C2["Engine Braking"]
    end
    subgraph S4["COLD ENRICHMENT LAMBDA 0.65 to 0.85"]
        D1["Cold Start below 30C"]
        D2["Warm Up 30 to 80C"]
    end
    subgraph S5["LEAN BURN LAMBDA 1.05 to 1.20"]
        E1["GDI Stratified Charge"]
        E2["Homogeneous Lean Burn"]
    end
```

## 4.5 Deceleration Fuel Cutoff (DFCO) Decision Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeP["Read Sensors Each Loop"] --> nodeQ{"TPS below 1 percent"}
    nodeQ -- NO --> nodeR["Normal Injection Strategy"]
    nodeQ -- YES --> nodeS{"RPM above threshold 1300rpm"}
    nodeS -- NO --> nodeR
    nodeS -- YES --> nodeT{"Coolant above 60C"}
    nodeT -- NO --> nodeR
    nodeT -- YES --> nodeU{"No DFCO inhibit flag"}
    nodeU -- NO --> nodeR
    nodeU -- YES --> nodeV["ACTIVATE DFCO Injectors OFF"]
    nodeV --> nodeW{"RPM drops below 1100rpm"}
    nodeW -- YES --> nodeX["Resume Injection Taper in 100ms"]
    nodeW -- NO --> nodeV
    nodeX --> nodeR
```

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper 2]**
**Define Engine Management System (EMS). List any four major sensors used in a modern EMS.**

**Model Answer:**
The Engine Management System (EMS) is an integrated electronic control system that uses a microprocessor-based ECU to monitor engine operating parameters via sensors and control actuators (fuel injectors, ignition coils, idle valves) to optimize combustion, performance, fuel economy, and emissions.

Four major sensors:
1. **MAF/MAP sensor** — measures inducted air mass/pressure
2. **TPS** — measures throttle position
3. **ECT** — measures coolant temperature
4. **Lambda O₂ sensor** — provides closed-loop AFR feedback
5. (Any valid fourth sensor: CKP, CMP, Knock)

*(Examiner's Note: Award 1 mark for definition, 2 marks for listing and briefly explaining four sensors.)*

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**What is "lambda" in engine management? State its value for stoichiometric, rich, and lean conditions.**

**Model Answer:**
Lambda ($\lambda$) is the **normalized air-fuel ratio**, defined as the ratio of actual AFR to stoichiometric AFR:

$$\lambda = \frac{AFR_{actual}}{AFR_{stoich}}$$

| Condition | Lambda Value | AFR (Gasoline) |
|---|---|---|
| Stoichiometric | $\lambda = 1.00$ | 14.7 : 1 |
| Rich | $\lambda < 1.00$ (e.g., 0.85) | ~12.5 : 1 |
| Lean | $\lambda > 1.00$ (e.g., 1.10) | ~16.2 : 1 |

---

## 5.2 PART B Questions (14 Marks Each — Choose ONE)

### **OPTION A — Question A [14 Marks]**

**Q3(a). [7 Marks] [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**With a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of a typical Engine Management System. Identify the input sensors, the ECU, and the output actuators.**

**Model Solution:**

The EMS architecture follows the standard mechatronic **Sense → Compute → Actuate** paradigm.

**Block Diagram:**

```
SENSORS (Input)          ECU (Processing)         ACTUATORS (Output)
┌──────────────┐         ┌───────────────┐        ┌──────────────┐
│ MAF Sensor   │────────▶│               │───────▶│ Fuel Injector│
│ MAP Sensor   │────────▶│               │───────▶│ Ignition Coil│
│ TPS          │────────▶│   ECU         │───────▶│ IAC Valve    │
│ ECT          │────────▶│ 32-bit µC     │───────▶│ Fuel Pump    │
│ Lambda O₂    │────────▶│ + Memory      │───────▶│ EGR Valve    │
│ CKP / CMP    │────────▶│               │───────▶│ VVT Solenoid │
│ Knock Sensor │────────▶│               │───────▶│ Check Engine │
└──────────────┘         └───────────────┘        └──────────────┘
                                  ▲
                                  │
                            OBD-II Diagnostic
```

**Input Sensors (3 marks for listing and purpose):**
- **MAF Sensor**: Measures mass of incoming air (kg/s); primary input for fuel calculation in speed-density systems.
- **MAP Sensor**: Measures manifold absolute pressure (kPa); used in speed-density fuel strategy.
- **TPS**: Indicates driver's demanded load (0–100%); main axis for acceleration enrichment and DFCO.
- **ECT**: Coolant temperature; controls cold start enrichment, idle speed, ignition advance.
- **CKP**: Crankshaft position/RPM; critical for injection timing and ignition timing.
- **Lambda Sensor**: Closed-loop AFR feedback (target λ = 1.00).
- **Knock Sensor**: Detects abnormal combustion; retards ignition to prevent engine damage.

**ECU (2 marks):**
- 32-bit automotive microcontroller with embedded Flash/ROM (256 KB – 8 MB), RAM (32 KB – 1 MB), multiple ADC channels, PWM outputs, CAN/LIN communication.
- Executes real-time fuel and spark calculations every **5–10 ms** (closed-loop cycle time).
- Stores calibration maps (fuel map, ignition map, idle map) in non-volatile memory.

**Output Actuators (2 marks):**
- **Fuel Injectors**: Solenoid/Piezo; pulse-width controlled to deliver exact fuel mass.
- **Ignition Coils**: Dwell-time controlled; deliver high-voltage spark at calculated advance.
- **IAC Valve**: Bypasses air around closed throttle to control idle rpm.
- **EGR Valve**: Recirculates exhaust gas to reduce NOₓ emissions.
- **Fuel Pump Relay**: Activates electric fuel pump to maintain rail pressure.

---

**Q3(b). [7 Marks] [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Discuss the various phases of cold start and warm-up operation in a typical EMS. Explain the role of coolant temperature sensor in each phase.**

**Model Solution:**

**Phase 1 — Fuel Pump Priming (1 mark):**
When ignition is switched ON, the ECU energizes the fuel pump relay for **2–3 seconds** to pressurize the fuel rail to ~3 bar. This eliminates vapor bubbles and ensures immediate injector response.

**Phase 2 — Cranking Enrichment (1 mark):**
As the starter motor turns the engine (RPM = 150–300), fuel condenses on cold cylinder walls and intake manifold surfaces due to **poor fuel vaporization** at low temperatures. The ECU applies **cranking enrichment**:
- Pulse width multiplier = $f(T_{coolant})$: ranges from **1.0 at 80 °C to 2.0+ at -20 °C**.
- Fixed cranking schedule (lookup table indexed by ECT).

**Phase 3 — Cold Start Enrichment (After-Fire) (1.5 marks):**
Once the engine fires (RPM > 400), the ECU enters the cold start enrichment phase:
- Lambda target: **0.6 to 0.8** (very rich mixture).
- Fuel compensation decreases as RPM stabilizes and coolant warms.
- Typically lasts **0 to 60 seconds** depending on ambient temperature.

**Phase 4 — After-Start Enrichment (1 mark):**
- Enrichment factor decays exponentially from the cold-start value over **10–30 seconds**.
- The ECU uses ECT and IAT (Intake Air Temperature) as primary inputs.

**Phase 5 — Warm-Up Enrichment (1.5 marks):**
- Lambda target: **0.85 to 0.95**.
- Enrichment decays linearly from 30 °C to 80 °C coolant temperature.
- Closed-loop control via lambda sensor is *disabled* during this phase (open-loop).
- Spark advance is also retarded to aid cylinder wall heating.

**Phase 6 — Transition to Closed Loop (1 mark):**
When ECT > 80 °C and lambda sensor is hot (achieved > 350 °C via internal heater or exhaust heat), the ECU switches to closed-loop operation:
- Lambda target: **1.00 ± 0.02**.
- O₂ sensor becomes the authoritative feedback; ECT corrections are minimal.

**Role of ECT (Coolant Temperature Sensor) — Summary Table:**

| ECT Range | Lambda Target | Enrichment Factor | Closed Loop? |
|---|---|---|---|
| < 0 °C | 0.6 – 0.7 | 2.0+ | No |
| 0 – 30 °C | 0.7 – 0.85 | 1.5 – 2.0 | No |
| 30 – 80 °C | 0.85 – 0.98 | 1.05 – 1.5 | No (warm-up) |
| > 80 °C | 1.00 ± 0.02 | 1.0 (base) | **Yes** |

---

### **OPTION B — Question B [14 Marks]**

**Q4(a). [7 Marks] [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**Describe the working of an Idle Speed Control (ISC) system with a closed-loop control strategy. Explain how engine load disturbances are compensated.**

**Model Solution:**

**Working Principle (3 marks):**
The Idle Speed Control system maintains engine RPM at a calibrated **target idle speed** (typically **700–900 rpm** for a warm gasoline engine) when the throttle is closed (TPS ≈ 0%). The system controls the amount of air bypassing the closed throttle plate via an **Idle Air Control (IAC) valve**.

**Types of IAC Actuators:**
1. **Stepper Motor Type** — Bi-directional stepper motor drives a pintle valve; ECU counts steps.
2. **DC Motor Type** — Rotating DC motor with PWM control; simpler but less precise.
3. **Rotary Disc Type** — A disc rotates to vary an annular orifice area.
4. **Electronic Throttle Body (ETB)** — In modern drive-by-wire systems, the throttle itself is the actuator.

**Closed-Loop Control Architecture (3 marks):**
The ECU implements a **PID controller** with the following control law:

$$u(t) = K_{P} \cdot e(t) + K_{I} \int_{0}^{t} e(\tau) \, d\tau + K_{D} \frac{de(t)}{dt}$$

where:
- $e(t) = N_{target} - N_{actual}$ (RPM error from CKP sensor)
- $u(t)$ = IAC valve position (PWM duty cycle or step count)
- $K_P, K_I, K_D$ = tuned controller gains stored in ECU memory

**Disturbance Compensation (1 mark):**
When engine accessories engage (A/C compressor, alternator, power steering pump), they impose sudden torque loads that would reduce RPM. The ECU compensates **proactively** using a feed-forward term:

$$N_{target,adjusted} = N_{target,base} + \sum_{i=1}^{n} K_{i} \cdot L_{i}(t)$$

where $L_i$ are digital flags indicating the status of accessory loads (e.g., A/C ON, alternator load = 25A). Typical compensation values: **+50 to +150 rpm per accessory**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfall (KTU Examiner's Warning):** Students frequently forget that **idle speed is *not* controlled by fuel alone**. The IAC valve controls **air mass**, not fuel. The ECU compensates *fuel* to match the *air* — fuel follows air. Marks are lost if you describe idle control as a fuel-only system.

---

**Q4(b). [7 Marks] [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**Explain acceleration enrichment and deceleration fuel cutoff (DFCO) with their engineering significance. Show how the ECU distinguishes the two phases.**

**Model Solution:**

**Acceleration Enrichment (3.5 marks):**

When the driver suddenly depresses the accelerator pedal (TPS rate of change $> d/dt$ threshold), the manifold pressure rises rapidly, causing additional fuel to **condense on the intake port walls** (fuel "wall-wetting"). This phenomenon causes a transient lean condition, manifested as **tip-in hesitation** or **flat spot**.

The ECU computes two types of acceleration enrichment:

1. **TPS Rate Compensation:**
$$T_{acc,1} = K_{acc,1} \cdot \left|\frac{\Delta TPS}{\Delta t}\right| \cdot f(ECT)$$

2. **MAP Rate Compensation:**
$$T_{acc,2} = K_{acc,2} \cdot \frac{dP_{map}}{dt} \cdot f(ECT)$$

The total acceleration enrichment is the sum of both contributions, applied as additional injector pulse width for typically **0.5 to 2.0 seconds**, then decayed exponentially.

**Wall-Wetting Model (advanced):**
Modern ECUs use the **Wall-Wetting Model** (developed by Aquino, Hendricks, et al.) with two film parameters:
$$\dot{m}_{ff} = X \cdot \dot{m}_{fuel} - \frac{m_{ff}}{\tau_{f}}$$

where $m_{ff}$ is the fuel film mass, $X$ is the proportion deposited, and $\tau_f$ is the evaporation time constant.

**Deceleration Fuel Cutoff (DFCO) — 3.5 marks:**

DFCO is a **fuel economy and emissions optimization strategy** that completely disables fuel injection when:
- TPS < 1% (closed throttle)
- RPM > threshold (typically 1300–1800 rpm, coolant-temperature dependent)
- Coolant > 60 °C
- No fault conditions present

**Engineering Significance:**
1. **Fuel Economy**: Eliminates fuel consumption during engine braking (a fuel-injected engine returns to zero fuel delivery while coasting in gear — unlike a carburetor which still consumes fuel).
2. **Emissions Reduction**: Reduces HC and CO emissions during overrun; modern systems recover ~3–8% fuel economy in urban driving.
3. **Catalyst Protection**: Prevents oxygen contamination of the three-way catalyst from unburned fuel.

**Phase Discrimination Logic:**
The ECU distinguishes acceleration from deceleration using:
- **TPS rate of change**: positive derivative ⇒ acceleration; negative derivative ⇒ deceleration.
- **MAP rate of change**: rising MAP ⇒ acceleration; falling MAP ⇒ deceleration.
- **TPS absolute value**: 0% + high RPM ⇒ DFCO; > 5% ⇒ acceleration enrichment.

**Re-engagement:**
When RPM falls below the cutoff threshold (typically 1100–1500 rpm), the ECU restores injection with a **tapered ramp** over ~100 ms to prevent an abrupt rich spike.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Important Things to Remember — High-Density Summary:**

- ✅ **EMS = Sense + Compute + Actuate** — the canonical mechatronic loop.
- ✅ **Stoichiometric AFR for gasoline = 14.7 : 1** (diesel = 14.5; CNG = 17.2).
- ✅ **Lambda = AFR_actual / AFR_stoich**; closed-loop target $\lambda = 1.00 \pm 0.02$.
- ✅ **ECU cycle time = 5–10 ms** for fuel/ignition tasks; runs on AUTOSAR OS.
- ✅ **Speed-Density Method**: $m_{air} = \frac{P_{map} \cdot V_d \cdot \eta_{vol}}{R_{specific} \cdot T_{manifold}}$
- ✅ **Remapping of ECU**: Modifying calibration maps (fuel, ignition, boost, rev limit, speed limit); methods include OBD port flashing, chip tuning, piggyback ECUs, and standalone ECUs.
- ✅ **Cold Start Enrichment**: $\lambda = 0.6$–$0.8$; pulse width multiplier 1.5–2.0+; depends on ECT.
- ✅ **Warm-Up Enrichment**: $\lambda$ ramps from 0.85 to 0.98 as coolant rises from 30 °C to 80 °C.
- ✅ **Closed-loop operation** engages when ECT > 80 °C and lambda sensor > 350 °C.
- ✅ **Idle Speed Control**: Target = 700–900 rpm; uses PID + feed-forward for load compensation (A/C, P/S, alternator).
- ✅ **Full Load Enrichment**: WOT mode uses $\lambda = 0.78$–$0.85$ to cool combustion chamber and prevent knock.
- ✅ **Acceleration Enrichment**: $\Delta$ TPS/$\Delta$t based compensation to overcome fuel wall-wetting.
- ✅ **DFCO (Deceleration Fuel Cutoff)**: Cuts injection when TPS ≈ 0%, RPM > threshold, ECT > 60 °C; recovers 3–8% fuel economy.
- ✅ **Fuel Map**: 3-D table indexed by RPM and load; multiplied by ECT, lambda, air temp, battery voltage correction factors.
- ✅ **Wall-Wetting Model**: $m_{ff}$ film mass + time constant $\tau_f$ — required for transient AFR compensation.
- ✅ **Knock Sensor**: Piezoelectric, 5–15 kHz band; ECU retards ignition by 1–10° per knock event.
- ✅ **OBD-II**: Diagnostic standard (ISO 15765); logs fault codes (DTCs) for sensor/actuator failures.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. Confusing **lambda** with **equivalence ratio φ** (they are reciprocals: $\phi = 1/\lambda$).
> 2. Stating that idle speed is controlled by *fuel* rather than *air bypass*.
> 3. Forgetting that **cold start enrichment** is purely **open-loop** (no lambda feedback yet — sensor is cold!).
> 4. Omitting the **coolant temperature sensor** as the master variable in cold start/warm-up.
> 5. Describing DFCO as "turning off the engine" — it is *fuel cutoff while engine is still spinning* via engine braking.
> 6. Not mentioning **wall-wetting** when explaining acceleration enrichment — KTU examiners specifically test this since 2023.

---

topics.title: Fuel Control Maps
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMUT602 - MECHATRONICS
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Fuel Control Maps

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Fuel Control Map** (also termed as a *Fuel Map*, *Base Fuel Calibration Map*, or *Fuel Injection Map*) is a multi-dimensional **lookup table** embedded within the non-volatile memory (typically Flash/EEPROM) of an Engine Control Unit (ECU). It functions as the principal open-loop calibration strategy in a Port Fuel Injection (PFI) or Gasoline Direct Injection (GDI) system, mapping discretized engine operating points — defined by **Engine Speed (RPM)** and **Engine Load (MAP/MAF/TPS)** — to a corresponding **Base Injector Pulse Width (ms)** or **Injection Mass (mg/cycle)** required to maintain a target air-fuel ratio (λ ≈ 1.0 under stoichiometric closed-loop operation).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Fuel Maps form the *open-loop* backbone of Electronic Engine Management. The ECU *interpolates* between adjacent cells of the map in real-time (sub-millisecond execution) to compute the base fuel quantity, which is then post-multiplied by a chain of *compensating maps* (coolant temperature, intake air temperature, battery voltage, transient tip-in/tip-out).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The "Chef's Recipe Card"**
>
> Imagine a chef who runs a 24-hour restaurant. Every time a customer orders a dish, instead of re-inventing the spice mix, the chef consults a giant laminated **recipe card** hanging on the wall. The card is indexed by two questions:
> 1. **How hungry is the customer?** (Engine Load — small/large throttle)
> 2. **How fast are they eating?** (Engine RPM — idle/cruising/full-throttle)
>
> At the intersection of these two parameters, the card gives the exact quantity of salt (fuel) to add. The chef *interpolates* if the customer's appetite falls between two listed values. A **Fuel Control Map is exactly this recipe card**, except:
> - The customer is the **internal combustion engine**.
> - The chef is the **ECU microcontroller**.
> - The salt is the **fuel injected** into the cylinders.
> - The recipe card is **permanently stored** in ECU memory and was generated after thousands of hours of dynamometer (dyno) testing.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Stoichiometric Air-Fuel Ratio (Petrol/Gasoline):** **AFR_stoich = 14.7 : 1** (by mass)
- **Lambda (λ) Target under Closed-Loop:** **λ = 1.00 ± 0.03**
- **Alternative Fuels Stoich:**
  - **CNG:** AFR ≈ 17.2 : 1
  - **LPG:** AFR ≈ 15.5 : 1
  - **Ethanol (E100):** AFR ≈ 9.0 : 1
  - **Diesel (CI engine):** AFR ≈ 14.5 : 1 (typically λ > 1, lean operation)
- **Standard Map Axis Resolution:** Typically **16 × 16** or **20 × 20** cells for production ECUs.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Architectural Position of the Fuel Map in an ECU

The base fuel map does **not** operate in isolation. It is the **primary scalar output** that is multiplied by a series of **compensation multipliers** before the final injector pulse width command is dispatched. The closed-loop O₂ sensor trim (long-term fuel trim + short-term fuel trim) then *adaptively corrects* the result around λ = 1.

The generalized fuel calculation architecture is:

$$
FPW_{\text{final}} \;=\; FPW_{\text{base}}(N,\, L) \;\cdot\; f_{CLT} \;\cdot\; f_{IAT} \;\cdot\; f_{BAT} \;\cdot\; f_{TPS,dt} \;\cdot\; (1 + STFT + LTFT)
$$

Where each multiplicative term represents a separate calibration map or function.

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logical Decomposition of Map Generation

1. **Identification of Input Axes:** The two principal axes are *Engine Speed (N)* and *Engine Load (L)*. Speed is measured via a crankshaft position sensor (variable reluctance or Hall-effect). Load is measured as **Manifold Absolute Pressure (MAP)** via a piezo-resistive pressure transducer, or computed as a derived **Mass Air Flow (MAF)** via a hot-wire anemometer.
2. **Steady-State Cell Calibration:** On an engine dynamometer, at each (N, L) cell, the ECU calibration engineer adjusts the injected fuel until the exhaust gas analyser reads the target lambda. This pulse width is permanently written into that map cell.
3. **3-D Surface Interpolation:** Real engine operation never lands exactly on a calibrated cell. The ECU performs **bilinear interpolation** between the four surrounding cells:

$$
Z(x,y) = \begin{aligned}[t]
& Z_{00}\,(1-x')(1-y') + Z_{10}\,x'(1-y') \; + \\
& Z_{01}\,(1-x')y' + Z_{11}\,x'y'
\end{aligned}
$$

   Where $x' = (x - x_0)/(x_1 - x_0)$ and $y' = (y - y_0)/(y_1 - y_0)$ are normalized fractional positions.

4. **Transient Compensation:** During rapid throttle opening (tip-in), manifold pressure rises faster than the air actually arrives at the cylinder. The wall-wetting film model demands an *acceleration enrichment* pulse, typically generated via a **d(MAF)/dt** or **d(TPS)/dt** map.
5. **Closed-Loop Trim:** The wide-band O₂ sensor (UEGO) reports the *actual* λ. The ECU computes the **Short Term Fuel Trim (STFT)** proportional-integral (PI) error correction, which in turn updates the **Long Term Fuel Trim (LTFT)** adaptive learn table to compensate for aging, altitude, and part-to-part injector variation.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL:** Pipes inside LaTeX have been replaced with `\vert` or `\mid` to preserve markdown table integrity.

| # | Equation / Term | LaTeX Form | Engineering Use / Unit |
|---|-----------------|------------|------------------------|
| 1 | Indicated Air Mass per Cylinder per Cycle | $m_{air} = \dfrac{V_d \cdot \eta_{vol} \cdot \rho_{air}}{n_{cyl}}$ | kg/cycle — basis of speed-density fueling |
| 2 | Volumetric Efficiency Map | $\eta_{vol} = f(N,\, MAP)$ | dimensionless lookup |
| 3 | Required Fuel Mass per Cycle | $m_{fuel} = \dfrac{m_{air}}{AFR_{target}}$ | kg/cycle |
| 4 | Base Injector Pulse Width (Speed-Density) | $t_{inj} = \dfrac{m_{fuel}}{Q_{inj} \cdot \rho_{fuel}}$ | seconds |
| 5 | Lambda Definition | $\lambda = \dfrac{AFR_{actual}}{AFR_{stoich}}$ | dimensionless |
| 6 | Battery Voltage Compensation | $f_{BAT} = \dfrac{V_{nom}}{V_{bat}}$ | unitless multiplier |
| 7 | Injector Flow Compensation | $t_{comp} = t_{inj} + t_{dead}$ | adds injector dead-time |
| 8 | Wall-Wetting (Aquino) Transient Fuel | $\Delta m_{fw} = \tau \cdot \dfrac{dm_{fa}}{dt} - m_{fw}$ | kg — film dynamics |
| 9 | Manifold Fill Dynamics | $\dot{m}_{c} = \dfrac{V_{man}}{R T_{man}}( \dot{P}_{man})$ | first-order lag equation |
| 10 | Combustion Efficiency (BSFC basis) | $\eta_{b} = \dfrac{P_{brake}}{\dot{m}_{f} \cdot Q_{LHV}}$ | dimensionless |

**Boundary Conditions & Operating Limits to remember:**

| Parameter | Idle Condition | Cruise | WOT (Wide Open Throttle) |
|-----------|---------------|--------|--------------------------|
| RPM | 700 – 900 | 1500 – 2500 | 4000 – 7000 |
| MAP (kPa) | 30 – 45 | 50 – 75 | 90 – 101 |
| λ Target | 1.00 (closed-loop) | 1.00 | 0.85 – 0.95 (open-loop, MBT power) |
| FPW (ms) | 1.5 – 3.0 | 3.0 – 6.0 | 8.0 – 14.0 |

## 2.4 Real-World Utility in Engineering

Fuel control maps are the intellectual property heart of any OEM's Engine Management Software (EMS). They directly determine:

- **Driveability** (idle smoothness, throttle tip-in response, part-throttle transition).
- **Emissions Compliance** (BS-VI / Euro-7 boundaries — any calibration cell exceeding λ tolerance by > 3% triggers a CEL — Check Engine Light).
- **Fuel Economy** (every 1% lean shift at cruise saves ~0.6% brake specific fuel consumption).
- **Aftertreatment Compatibility** (TWC/GPF/DPF efficiency demands λ be held at 1.00 ± 0.01 within a narrow band around stoichiometric).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Speed-Density Base Fuel Pulse Width

The derivation below expresses the **base fuel map value** from first principles, demonstrating how the calibration engineer arrives at the numbers stored in the ECU's lookup table.

### Given:
- Engine displacement: $V_d$ (litres)
- Number of cylinders: $n_{cyl}$
- Volumetric efficiency (function of N, MAP): $\eta_{vol}(N, MAP)$
- Intake air density (from IAT sensor + speed-density equation): $\rho_{air}$
- Target Air-Fuel Ratio: $AFR_{target}$
- Injector static flow rate: $Q_{inj}$ (kg/s)
- Injector opening dead-time: $t_{dead}$ (ms)
- Battery voltage compensation: $f_{BAT}$

### Step 1 — Air Mass Inducted per Cylinder per Cycle

For a 4-stroke engine, there are $N/2$ working cycles per second per cylinder. The total air mass entering all cylinders per second is:

$$
\dot{m}_{air,total} = \dfrac{V_d \cdot \eta_{vol} \cdot \rho_{air} \cdot N}{2}
$$

Dividing by $n_{cyl}$ to obtain **air mass per cylinder per cycle**:

$$
m_{air,cyl} = \dfrac{V_d \cdot \eta_{vol} \cdot \rho_{air}}{2 \cdot n_{cyl}}
$$

*Explanation:* Every two revolutions, each cylinder completes one intake stroke. The actual air density inside the manifold (calculated via the ideal gas law $P = \rho R T$) determines how much air fills the cylinder at the closed intake valve event.

### Step 2 — Required Fuel Mass per Cycle

Applying the target Air-Fuel Ratio:

$$
m_{fuel,cyl} = \dfrac{m_{air,cyl}}{AFR_{target}}
$$

*Explanation:* If 14.7 kg of air require exactly 1 kg of gasoline, then a cylinder ingesting 0.0005 kg of air requires $0.0005 / 14.7 = 3.4 \times 10^{-5}$ kg of fuel.

### Step 3 — Base Injector Pulse Width

An injector is essentially a solenoid-controlled valve with a known flow rate $Q_{inj}$. The time required to deliver $m_{fuel,cyl}$ is:

$$
t_{base} = \dfrac{m_{fuel,cyl}}{Q_{inj}}
$$

*Explanation:* Injectors are rated in cc/min or kg/s. A Bosch EV6 injector flows ~ 264 cc/min ≈ $4.4 \times 10^{-6}$ m³/s of gasoline. Thus, the on-time for $3.4 \times 10^{-5}$ kg ≈ $4.2 \times 10^{-8}$ m³ equals approximately **9.5 ms**.

### Step 4 — Apply Compensation Multipliers

The final commanded pulse width becomes:

$$
t_{inj,final} = (t_{base} \cdot f_{CLT} \cdot f_{IAT} \cdot f_{BAT}) + t_{dead}
$$

*Explanation:*
- $f_{CLT}$: Cold engine needs rich mixture (1.3 – 1.5× at -20 °C).
- $f_{IAT}$: Cold dense air needs more fuel.
- $f_{BAT}$: Low battery voltage weakens the solenoid pull-in force, partially opening the injector; the pulse must be lengthened.
- $t_{dead}$: The mechanical latency (≈ 0.8 – 1.2 ms) before the injector needle actually lifts.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example

**Problem Statement:** A 4-cylinder, 4-stroke petrol engine with $V_d = 1.6$ L operates at 2500 RPM, MAP = 60 kPa, IAT = 35 °C (308 K), target AFR = 14.7, $\eta_{vol} = 0.85$. Injector flow rate $Q_{inj} = 4.4 \times 10^{-6}$ m³/s, $t_{dead} = 1.0$ ms, $f_{CLT} = 1.02$, $f_{BAT} = 1.05$. Atmospheric pressure $P_{atm} = 101.325$ kPa, gas constant $R = 287$ J/(kg·K).

### Solution Walk-Through

**Step A — Air Density in Manifold:**
Using the ideal gas law $\rho_{air} = \dfrac{P}{R \cdot T}$:

$$
\rho_{air} = \dfrac{60{,}000 \text{ Pa}}{287 \times 308}
$$

$$
\rho_{air} = \dfrac{60{,}000}{88{,}396} = 0.679 \text{ kg/m}^3
$$

**Step B — Air Mass per Cylinder per Cycle:**
Substituting into the speed-density expression:

$$
m_{air,cyl} = \dfrac{0.0016 \cdot 0.85 \cdot 0.679}{2 \cdot 4}
$$

$$
m_{air,cyl} = \dfrac{9.234 \times 10^{-4}}{8} = 1.154 \times 10^{-4} \text{ kg/cycle}
$$

**Step C — Required Fuel Mass per Cycle:**

$$
m_{fuel,cyl} = \dfrac{1.154 \times 10^{-4}}{14.7} = 7.85 \times 10^{-6} \text{ kg/cycle}
$$

**Step D — Base Injector Pulse Width:**

$$
t_{base} = \dfrac{7.85 \times 10^{-6}}{4.4 \times 10^{-6}} = 1.784 \text{ s?}
$$

*Correction — unit reconciliation:* Since $Q_{inj}$ is volumetric flow, we should use fuel density $\rho_{fuel} \approx 750$ kg/m³.

$$
t_{base} = \dfrac{7.85 \times 10^{-6}}{4.4 \times 10^{-6} \times 750} = 2.378 \times 10^{-3} \text{ s} = 2.378 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step E — Apply Compensations and Dead-Time:**

$$
t_{inj,final} = (2.378 \times 1.02 \times 1.05) + 1.0
$$

$$
t_{inj,final} = (2.378 \times 1.071) + 1.0 = 2.547 + 1.0 = 3.547 \text{ ms}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** A 3.5 ms pulse at 2500 RPM on a 1.6 L engine is a textbook part-throttle cruise value. Compare this to ~ 10 ms at WOT — a 3× swing driven entirely by MAP doubling and density rising accordingly.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Map Interaction During Operating Modes

| Operating Mode | Active Base Map | Active Compensation Maps | Closed-Loop? | λ Target |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Cold Start (T < 20 °C) | Cranking Fuel Map | CLT Enrichment (1.3 – 2.0×), IAT trim | NO | 0.7 – 0.9 |
| Warm Idle | Idle Cell of Base Map | Idle Air Control (IAC) trim, STFT active | YES | 1.00 |
| Part-Throttle Cruise | Mid-load cells | Light STFT + LTFT only | YES | 1.00 |
| Wide-Open Throttle (WOT) | High-load cells | Knock-sensor retard, open-loop power enrichment | NO | 0.80 – 0.85 |
| Deceleration Fuel Cut (DFCO) | N/A (zero pulse) | TPS-d/dt threshold | NO | λ → ∞ |
| Overrun / Braking | N/A (zero pulse) | RPM cut threshold | NO | λ → ∞ |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define a Fuel Control Map. What are its two principal axes in a port fuel injection system, and what is the physical quantity stored at each cell?**

**Model Answer:**
A **Fuel Control Map** is a multi-dimensional lookup table stored in the ECU's non-volatile memory that maps engine operating points to a base fuel injection command.

The **two principal axes** are:
1. **Engine Speed (RPM)** — measured by the crankshaft position sensor.
2. **Engine Load** — typically measured as Manifold Absolute Pressure (MAP) in kPa.

The **physical quantity stored** at each (N, MAP) cell is the **base injector pulse width (ms)** — the duration the injector must remain open to deliver the correct fuel mass for a stoichiometric mixture at that operating point. In modern direct-injection systems, the map stores **injection mass (mg/cycle)** instead of pulse width.

*(3 marks — 1 for definition, 1 for axes, 1 for stored quantity.)*

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**State the role of the Short-Term Fuel Trim (STFT) and Long-Term Fuel Trim (LTFT) in correcting fuel map outputs.**

**Model Answer:**
- **STFT (Short-Term Fuel Trim):** A **real-time PI (Proportional-Integral) controller** output that adds or subtracts fuel in **±10%** increments based on the instantaneous deviation of the upstream UEGO sensor reading from λ = 1.00. It oscillates rapidly (cycle-to-cycle) around the target.
- **LTFT (Long-Term Fuel Trim):** A **slowly adapting learned correction** (typically ±25%) stored in non-volatile memory, updated by averaging STFT over many cycles. It compensates for **permanent offsets** such as injector aging, intake manifold carbon build-up, altitude variation, and minor sensor drift.

Together, they ensure that even a roughly-calibrated fuel map achieves precise λ = 1.00 closed-loop control, satisfying BS-VI / Euro-7 emission norms.

*(3 marks — 1.5 for STFT, 1.5 for LTFT.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 — Module 4 Internal Choice Option A]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat block diagram, explain the **complete open-loop fuel calculation chain** in a port fuel injection system. List at least **five compensation maps** that post-multiply the base fuel map output.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A 4-cylinder, 4-stroke petrol engine has $V_d = 2.0$ L, operating at 3000 RPM, MAP = 80 kPa, IAT = 40 °C (313 K). Volumetric efficiency $\eta_{vol} = 0.88$, target AFR = 14.7, atmospheric pressure = 101.325 kPa, gas constant R = 287 J/(kg·K), injector flow rate $Q_{inj} = 5.0 \times 10^{-6}$ m³/s, $\rho_{fuel} = 750$ kg/m³, $t_{dead} = 0.9$ ms, $f_{CLT} = 1.0$, $f_{BAT} = 1.04$. Calculate the **final commanded injector pulse width** in ms.

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Block Diagram — Open-Loop Fuel Calculation Chain:**

```
RPM (CKP) ──┐
            ├──► Base Fuel Map (16×16) ──► FPW_base
MAP Sensor ─┘                                  │
                                                ▼
CLT Sensor ──► f_CLT map ──────────────────┐    │
IAT Sensor ──► f_IAT map ──────────────────┼───► × ──► FPW_compensated
Battery  ───► f_BAT map ──────────────────┘    │
TPS rate ───► f_TPS_dt map ────────────────┐    │
UEGO sensor ► STFT + LTFT ────────────────┼───► × ──► FPW_final
                                            │    │
                                            ▼    ▼
                                       Injector Driver
                                       + Dead Time Add
                                            │
                                            ▼
                                       Injector Pulse
```

**Five Compensation Maps (any five):**

1. **Coolant Temperature Compensation Map (f_CLT):** Provides rich mixture during cold start and warm-up; multiplier drops to 1.0 once engine reaches ~ 80 °C.
2. **Intake Air Temperature Compensation Map (f_IAT):** Adjusts for cold dense air vs hot thin air to maintain stoichiometry.
3. **Battery Voltage Compensation Map (f_BAT):** Lengthens pulse at low battery voltage to compensate for sluggish solenoid lift.
4. **TPS Rate-of-Change Map (f_TPS,dt):** Acceleration enrichment during rapid throttle tip-in to compensate for wall-wetting film dynamics.
5. **Short-Term Fuel Trim (STFT) Map:** Closed-loop PI correction from the UEGO sensor.
6. **Long-Term Fuel Trim (LTFT) Map:** Adaptive learned correction for permanent offsets.
7. **Barometric Pressure Compensation Map (f_BARO):** Adjusts for altitude changes.

*(7 marks — 2 for block diagram, 5 for listing and explaining five compensation maps.)*

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1 — Air Density:**
$$
\rho_{air} = \dfrac{P_{MAP}}{R \cdot T_{IAT}} = \dfrac{80{,}000}{287 \times 313} = \dfrac{80{,}000}{89{,}831} = 0.8906 \text{ kg/m}^3
$$

**Step 2 — Air Mass per Cylinder per Cycle:**
$$
m_{air,cyl} = \dfrac{V_d \cdot \eta_{vol} \cdot \rho_{air}}{2 \cdot n_{cyl}} = \dfrac{0.002 \times 0.88 \times 0.8906}{8}
$$

$$
m_{air,cyl} = \dfrac{1.5675 \times 10^{-3}}{8} = 1.9594 \times 10^{-4} \text{ kg/cycle}
$$

**Step 3 — Fuel Mass Required:**
$$
m_{fuel,cyl} = \dfrac{1.9594 \times 10^{-4}}{14.7} = 1.333 \times 10^{-5} \text{ kg/cycle}
$$

**Step 4 — Base Injector Pulse Width:**
$$
t_{base} = \dfrac{m_{fuel,cyl}}{Q_{inj} \cdot \rho_{fuel}} = \dfrac{1.333 \times 10^{-5}}{5.0 \times 10^{-6} \times 750} = \dfrac{1.333 \times 10^{-5}}{3.75 \times 10^{-3}} = 3.555 \times 10^{-3} \text{ s}
$$

$$
t_{base} = 3.555 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 5 — Apply Compensation and Add Dead Time:**
$$
t_{final} = (3.555 \times 1.0 \times 1.04) + 0.9
$$

$$
t_{final} = 3.697 + 0.9 = 4.597 \text{ ms}
$$

**Final Answer:** $t_{final} \approx 4.60 \text{ ms}$

*(7 marks — 1 each step, 1 for final answer.)*

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 — Module 4 Internal Choice Option B]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain with the help of a neat diagram the **bilinear interpolation algorithm** used by an ECU to compute the fuel pulse width between calibrated map cells. Why is interpolation necessary in real-time engine operation?

**(b) [7 Marks]** Describe the **Wall-Wetting (Aquino) Model** used to compensate fuel delivery during transient throttle operations. Why is a transient enrichment map alone insufficient?

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Bilinear Interpolation Algorithm:**

In a 16 × 16 base fuel map, the ECU receives continuous analogue inputs of RPM (N) and MAP (L) from sensors. The actual operating point (N_actual, L_actual) almost never coincides with a calibrated cell (N_i, L_j). The ECU identifies the four surrounding corner cells:

- $Z_{00}$ at (N_0, L_0)
- $Z_{10}$ at (N_1, L_0)
- $Z_{01}$ at (N_0, L_1)
- $Z_{11}$ at (N_1, L_1)

Where $N_0 \leq N_{actual} \leq N_1$ and $L_0 \leq L_{actual} \leq L_1$.

**Normalized fractional distances:**
$$
N' = \dfrac{N_{actual} - N_0}{N_1 - N_0}, \quad L' = \dfrac{L_{actual} - L_0}{L_1 - L_0}
$$

**Bilinear interpolation formula:**
$$
Z_{out} = Z_{00}(1-N')(1-L') + Z_{10}N'(1-L') + Z_{01}(1-N')L' + Z_{11}N'L'
$$

**Diagram:**

```
        MAP (kPa)
         ^
         |
   L_1 --|--------[Z01]----[Z11]
         |          |        |
         |          |        |
         |          * P_actual
         |          |        |
   L_0 --|--------[Z00]----[Z10]
         |          |        |
         +-----|----|----|------> RPM
              N_0    N_actual N_1
```

**Why Interpolation is Necessary:**
1. **Finite Map Resolution:** Production ECUs use 16 × 16 = 256 cells (or 20 × 20 = 400). A continuous input signal must yield a continuous output.
2. **Smoother Driveability:** Without interpolation, the FPW would *step* between cells, causing perceptible jerks and lambda hunting.
3. **Sub-millisecond Execution:** Modern automotive MCUs (Infineon Aurix, NXP S32K) execute bilinear interpolation in **< 10 µs**, well within the 1 ms engine cycle budget.
4. **Improved Emissions:** A continuous lambda trace prevents O₂ sensor cycle-induced emissions spikes.

*(7 marks — 3 for formula, 2 for diagram, 2 for necessity.)*

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Wall-Wetting (Aquino) Model:**

When fuel is injected onto the **back of a closed intake valve**, a fraction of it condenses into a liquid **fuel film** on the port walls. During steady-state, the film mass is constant and the ECU compensates for it. However, during a **rapid throttle opening (tip-in)**, manifold pressure rises sharply, but the fuel film *does not vaporize instantly* — it slowly evaporates over 0.3 – 1.5 seconds. Without compensation, the engine runs **momentarily lean**, causing a stumble or hesitation.

The **Aquino Model** captures this with two first-order differential equations:

$$
\dot{m}_{ff} = X \cdot \dot{m}_{fi} - \dfrac{m_{ff}}{\tau_f}
$$

$$
\dot{m}_{fv} = (1-X) \cdot \dot{m}_{fi} + \dfrac{m_{ff}}{\tau_f} - \dot{m}_{fc}
$$

Where:
- $m_{ff}$ = fuel film mass (kg)
- $m_{fv}$ = fuel vapour mass (kg)
- $m_{fi}$ = injected fuel mass (kg/s)
- $m_{fc}$ = fuel consumed in cylinder (kg/s)
- $X$ = fraction of fuel that *enters* the film (typically 0.02 – 0.10)
- $\tau_f$ = film evaporation time constant (typically 0.3 – 1.5 s)

**Why a Transient Enrichment Map Alone is Insufficient:**

1. **Time-Varying Dynamics:** The film evaporates *exponentially* over hundreds of milliseconds — a single enrichment pulse cannot model this.
2. **Operating-Point Dependence:** The film fraction $X$ and time constant $\tau_f$ depend on engine speed, load, and coolant temperature — they must be **maps**, not scalars.
3. **Reversal Effects:** During sudden throttle closure (tip-out), the manifold pressure drops rapidly, but the film *still releases* fuel vapour — causing a momentary rich spike that must also be corrected.
4. **Adaptive Calibration:** The film parameters drift with engine wear, oil consumption, and fuel composition — they require adaptive self-learning (similar to LTFT).

Thus, modern ECUs implement the **full Aquino model in real time** rather than relying on static enrichment maps.

*(7 marks — 3 for model equations, 2 for explanation, 2 for insufficiency reasoning.)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks:**
>
> 1. **Unit Confusion in the Speed-Density Formula:** Forgetting to divide by **2** for a 4-stroke engine, or dividing by **n_cyl** incorrectly (use $2 \cdot n_{cyl}$ in the denominator). Examiners *ruthlessly* deduct 2 marks for this.
>
> 2. **Forgetting Injector Dead-Time:** A common trap. The answer is incomplete if $t_{dead}$ is omitted in the final addition step. Examiners expect it explicitly.
>
> 3. **Confusing AFR and Lambda:** $\text{AFR} = 14.7$ at λ = 1.0 for petrol. Students who write $\text{AFR} = 1.0$ or confuse the two lose 1–2 marks immediately.
>
> 4. **Missing the 4-stroke Factor:** The factor of 2 in the denominator (for 4-stroke engines) is the single most-skipped step in numericals.
>
> 5. **Block Diagram Mistakes:** Drawing boxes without arrows, omitting sensor labels, or not specifying the **multiply/compensate** operation explicitly — examiners deduct 1 mark per missing arrow or label.
>
> 6. **Ignoring ρ_fuel:** When $Q_{inj}$ is given in volumetric units (m³/s), students forget to multiply by fuel density (750 kg/m³), causing the answer to be off by a factor of 750.
>
> 7. **Mixing STFT and LTFT Roles:** STFT is *fast and oscillatory*; LTFT is *slow and learned*. Examiners award full marks only when this distinction is explicit.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Fuel Control Maps**

- **Definition:** A fuel map is a multi-dimensional **lookup table** mapping (RPM, Load) → **Injector Pulse Width** stored in ECU flash memory.
- **Two Principal Axes:** **Engine Speed (RPM)** × **Engine Load (MAP or MAF)**.
- **Typical Resolution:** **16 × 16** cells for production ECUs; **20 × 20** for high-performance or research units.
- **Stored Quantity:** Pulse width (ms) in PFI systems; injection mass (mg/cycle) in GDI systems.
- **Stoichiometric AFR (Petrol):** **14.7 : 1** → λ = 1.00.
- **Diesel Lambda:** Always > 1 (lean), typically λ = 1.2 – 2.0.
- **Speed-Density Core Equation:** $m_{air,cyl} = \dfrac{V_d \cdot \eta_{vol} \cdot \rho_{air}}{2 \cdot n_{cyl}}$.
- **Volumetric Efficiency Map:** $\eta_{vol} = f(N, MAP)$ is itself a sub-map.
- **Compensation Chain Order:** CLT → IAT → Battery → TPS-d/dt → STFT+LTFT → + Dead-Time.
- **STFT:** Fast PI correction, ±10%, oscillates cycle-to-cycle.
- **LTFT:** Slow learned correction, ±25%, stored permanently.
- **Bilinear Interpolation:** Combines 4 surrounding map cells with normalized fractional weights.
- **Wall-Wetting Model (Aquino):** Two first-order ODEs for film and vapour fuel mass dynamics.
- **WOT Open-Loop λ Target:** 0.80 – 0.85 (rich, for maximum power / knock margin).
- **DFCO (Deceleration Fuel Cut):** FPW = 0 when TPS-d/dt is negative AND RPM > DFCO threshold.
- **Battery Compensation Formula:** $f_{BAT} = V_{nom}/V_{bat}$ (increases pulse when voltage drops).
- **Real-Time Execution:** Interpolation must complete in **< 100 µs** on every 1 ms engine event.
- **Aftertreatment Dependency:** Three-Way Catalytic Converter requires **λ = 1.00 ± 0.01** within a narrow band — closed-loop trim is *essential*, not optional.
- **Closed-Loop Sensors:** **UEGO** (wide-band, 0.5 – ∞ λ) for primary control; **HEGO** (switch-type, narrow-band, only useful at λ = 1.00) for trim feedback.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fuel Injection System Parameters Affecting Combustion
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMUT602 - MECHATRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Fuel Injection System Parameters Affecting Combustion

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Fuel Injection System Parameters** are the controllable mechanical, hydraulic, and electronic variables of a mechatronic fuel delivery system that directly govern the quantity, quality, timing, and spatial distribution of fuel introduced into the combustion chamber. These parameters form the core feedback-controlled variables in modern **Common Rail Direct Injection (CRDI)**, **Gasoline Direct Injection (GDI)**, and **Unit Injector (UI)** systems, and they dictate the combustion behavior — ignition delay, peak pressure, heat release rate, emissions, and thermal efficiency.

In the KTU 2024 Mechatronics syllabus context, these parameters are studied as the **actuated output variables** of the ECU (Electronic Control Unit), which processes inputs from crankshaft position sensors, camshaft sensors, rail pressure sensors, mass air flow (MAF) sensors, and lambda/oxygen sensors to deliver optimal combustion in real time.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):** The six primary fuel injection parameters that directly affect combustion are:
> 1. **Injection Timing (Start of Injection - SOI)**
> 2. **Injection Pressure (Rail Pressure)**
> 3. **Injection Duration (Pulse Width)**
> 4. **Number of Injection Events (Pilot / Main / Post)**
> 5. **Spray Pattern and Atomization Quality**
> 6. **Air-Fuel Equivalence Ratio (λ)**



## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Perfume Atomizer

Imagine you are spraying perfume from an atomizer bottle:
- **Squeezing harder (Injection Pressure)** → finer mist, more coverage, better evaporation.
- **Pressing earlier or later (Injection Timing)** → controls whether the scent arrives before, during, or after a person walks by.
- **How long you press (Injection Duration)** → controls how much perfume is released.
- **Multiple short presses (Pilot + Main + Post Injection)** → creates a layered, controlled scent profile.
- **Nozzle shape (Spray Pattern)** → narrow jet vs. wide cone determines how the mist fills the air.
- **Ventilation in the room (Air-Fuel Ratio)** → too little air and the mist hangs, too much and it disperses weakly.

In a diesel/gasoline engine, the **ECU** acts as the "hand" controlling the bottle, while the **injector** is the "nozzle," and the **combustion chamber** is the "room." Each parameter is a *knob* the mechatronic system turns in real time.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metric Definitions Used Throughout:**
> - **Injection Pressure (P):** Typically **200 – 2500 bar** in modern CRDI systems
> - **SOI (θ):** Measured in degrees **BTDC (Before Top Dead Centre)**
> - **Injection Duration (Δt):** **0.5 – 3 ms** typical range
> - **Sauter Mean Diameter (SMD):** **5 – 30 μm** for proper atomization
> - **Air-Fuel Equivalence Ratio (λ):** **0.7 (rich) to 1.3 (lean)**, with **λ = 1.0 = stoichiometric**

## 1.3 Why This Topic Matters in Mechatronics

Mechatronics is the **synergistic integration** of mechanical, electronic, control, and computer engineering. A fuel injection system is one of the most mechatronically dense subsystems in any modern vehicle. The ECU, sensors, and solenoid/piezo actuators form a **closed-loop control system**, where the controlled variable is **combustion quality**. Understanding how each parameter affects combustion is essential to designing robust controllers (PID, look-up-table based, model-based) that optimize fuel economy, emissions, and drivability.
