# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Six Parameters — Operational Breakdown

### 2.1.1 Injection Timing (Start of Injection — SOI)

**Definition:** The crank angle (or time) at which the ECU energizes the injector solenoid, causing fuel to begin entering the combustion chamber.

**Mechanism:**
- The ECU calculates SOI using a **3-D look-up table** indexed by engine speed (RPM) and engine load (mg/stroke or pedal position).
- Sensors: **Crankshaft Position Sensor (CKP)**, **Camshaft Position Sensor (CMP)**.

**Effect on Combustion:**
- **Early SOI (more BTDC):** Longer ignition delay → more premixed fuel → higher peak pressure rise rate → **NOx increases**, **noise increases**, **efficiency peaks** at moderate advance.
- **Late SOI (closer to TDC):** Shorter ignition delay → less premixed combustion → more diffusion-controlled → **PM (soot) increases**, **HC may rise**, **thermal efficiency drops**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rule of Thumb:** In a diesel engine, advancing SOI by **1° crank angle** typically increases peak cylinder pressure by approximately **0.5 – 1.5 bar**, depending on load and speed.

---

### 2.1.2 Injection Pressure (Rail Pressure)

**Definition:** The pressure of fuel stored in the common rail, generated by the high-pressure pump and regulated by the **rail pressure control valve (RPCV / SCV)**.

**Mechanism:**
- Controlled by the **fuel rail pressure sensor** feeding back to the ECU.
- Modern systems use **piezo injectors** capable of multiple precisely-timed injections per cycle at pressures up to **2500 bar**.

**Effect on Combustion:**
- **Higher pressure** → finer atomization (smaller SMD) → larger surface area → faster evaporation → shorter ignition delay → more complete combustion → **lower PM, lower HC, higher NOx**.
- **Lower pressure** → coarse spray → fuel impingement on walls → incomplete combustion → **higher PM, higher HC, lower thermal efficiency**.

---

### 2.1.3 Injection Duration (Pulse Width / Energizing Time)

**Definition:** The time interval for which the injector solenoid is energized, which directly determines the **injected fuel mass per stroke**.

**Mechanism:**
- The ECU computes the required pulse width using:
  $$\text{Pulse Width} = f(\text{MAF signal, λ target, RPM, coolant temp, battery voltage compensation})$$
- Battery voltage compensation is critical because solenoid inductance affects the **opening time** (typically 0.1–0.3 ms).

**Effect on Combustion:**
- Directly controls **air-fuel ratio (λ)**.
- Too short → lean mixture → misfire, high exhaust temp, NOx drop, poor torque.
- Too long → rich mixture → smoke, unburned HC, poor fuel economy.

---

### 2.1.4 Number of Injection Events (Pilot / Main / Post)

**Definition:** Modern mechatronic injectors can perform **multiple injections per combustion cycle**, typically:
- **Pilot injection** (small): 1–3° before main, reduces ignition delay, softens pressure rise.
- **Main injection** (largest): Primary energy release event.
- **After / Post injection** (small): Burns soot, regenerates DPF, raises exhaust temp.

**Effect on Combustion:**
- **Pilot injection** → smoother combustion, lower noise, lower NOx.
- **Multiple pilots** → step-rate heat release, reduces pressure gradient (**dp/dθ**).
- **Post injection** → DPF regeneration, **NOx reduction** via exhaust gas treatment.

---

### 2.1.5 Spray Pattern and Atomization Quality

**Definition:** The geometric shape of the fuel plume (hollow cone, solid cone, multi-hole fan) and the droplet size distribution.

**Mechanism:**
- Governed by **nozzle geometry** (number of holes, hole diameter, sac volume, needle lift).
- Mini-sac or **piezo-solenoid** injectors reduce sac volume → reduce wetting → reduce HC emissions.

**Effect on Combustion:**
- **Fine atomization (SMD < 15 μm)** → rapid mixing → homogeneous combustion.
- **Poor atomization (SMD > 50 μm)** → fuel impingement, wall wetting, pool diffusion combustion → high PM and HC.

---

### 2.1.6 Air-Fuel Equivalence Ratio (λ)

**Definition:** Ratio of actual air-fuel ratio to stoichiometric air-fuel ratio.

$$\lambda = \frac{(A/F)_{actual}}{(A/F)_{stoich}}$$

- For gasoline: (A/F)_stoich ≈ **14.7**
- For diesel: (A/F)_stoich ≈ **14.5**

**Effect on Combustion:**

| λ Range | Regime | NOx | CO | HC | PM | η_th |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0.7 – 0.8 | Rich (cold start, WOT) | Low | High | High | Low | Medium |
| 0.9 – 1.0 | Stoichiometric (3-way cat) | Peak | Low | Low | Low | High |
| 1.0 – 1.1 | Lean (GDI stratified) | Low | Low | Medium | Medium | High |
| 1.2 – 1.3 | Very lean (HCCI partial) | Very Low | Very Low | Rising | High | Peak |

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Equation | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $m_f = \int_{t_1}^{t_2} \dot{m}(t)\, dt$ | Injected fuel mass (integration of injection rate) | mg |
| 2 | $\dot{m} = C_d \cdot A_{eff} \cdot \sqrt{2 \rho \Delta P}$ | Injector mass flow rate (Bernoulli-derived) | kg/s |
| 3 | $SMD = 0.38 \cdot d_0^{1.25} \cdot \Delta P^{-0.25} \cdot \rho_f^{0.25} \cdot \mu_f^{0.25}$ | Sauter Mean Diameter (El-Kotb correlation) | μm |
| 4 | $S = K \cdot \sqrt{\frac{2 \Delta P}{\rho_a}} \cdot t$ | Spray tip penetration (in still air) | m |
| 5 | $S = 1.96 \cdot \left(\frac{\Delta P}{\rho_a}\right)^{0.25} \cdot (d_0 \cdot t)^{0.5}$ | Modified penetration (Dent correlation) | m |
| 6 | $\eta_{th} = \frac{W_{net}}{m_f \cdot Q_{LHV}}$ | Brake thermal efficiency | dimensionless |
| 7 | $\lambda = \frac{(A/F)_{actual}}{(A/F)_{stoich}}$ | Air-fuel equivalence ratio | dimensionless |
| 8 | $\tau_{id} = K \cdot P^{-n} \cdot \exp\left(\frac{E_a}{R T}\right)$ | Ignition delay (Arrhenius-type) | s |
| 9 | $\frac{dp}{d\theta}\bigg|_{max} \propto \frac{m_{premixed}}{\Delta \theta_{id}}$ | Max pressure rise rate | bar/° |
| 10 | $\dot{Q} = \eta_{comb} \cdot m_f \cdot Q_{LHV}$ | Heat release rate | J/s |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Constants & Standard Values (Bolded for KTU):**
> - Stoichiometric A/F (gasoline): **14.7 : 1**
> - Stoichiometric A/F (diesel): **14.5 : 1**
> - LHV of diesel: **≈ 42.5 MJ/kg**
> - LHV of gasoline: **≈ 44 MJ/kg**
> - Universal gas constant **R = 8.314 J/(mol·K)**
> - Typical diesel injector hole diameter $d_0$: **0.10 – 0.30 mm**
> - Nozzle discharge coefficient $C_d$: **0.7 – 0.9**

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

In modern automotive R&D, these parameters are tuned using **Design of Experiments (DoE)** on engine dynamometers. Calibration engineers sweep each parameter (e.g., SOI from -2° to +6° BTDC in 1° steps) while holding the others constant, recording in-cylinder pressure traces via **piezoelectric pressure transducers**. The data feeds into:
- **Engine control unit (ECU) calibration maps** (16×16 to 24×24 look-up tables).
- **Model Predictive Control (MPC)** algorithms for real-time optimization.
- **Aftertreatment coordination** (DPF, SCR, TWC) — especially for post-injection timing.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Injector Mass Flow Rate (Bernoulli with Discharge Coefficient)

**Starting Point:** The injector nozzle is treated as a sharp-edged orifice. Fuel flows from the rail (high pressure $P_r$) into the combustion chamber (back pressure $P_c$).

**Step 1 — Apply Bernoulli's Equation between the rail (point 1) and the nozzle exit (point 2):**

$$
\frac{P_r}{\rho_f} + \frac{v_1^2}{2} = \frac{P_c}{\rho_f} + \frac{v_2^2}{2}
$$

**Step 2 — Assume the fuel velocity in the rail is negligible ($v_1 \approx 0$):**

$$
\frac{P_r - P_c}{\rho_f} = \frac{v_2^2}{2}
$$

**Step 3 — Solve for the theoretical exit velocity $v_2$:**

$$
v_2 = \sqrt{\frac{2(P_r - P_c)}{\rho_f}} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \Delta P}{\rho_f}}
$$

**Step 4 — Multiply by the effective nozzle area $A_{eff}$ and density $\rho_f$ to get the theoretical mass flow rate $\dot{m}_{th}$:**

$$
\dot{m}_{th} = \rho_f \cdot A_{eff} \cdot v_2 = A_{eff} \sqrt{2 \rho_f \Delta P}
$$

**Step 5 — Apply the discharge coefficient $C_d$ to account for viscous losses and vena contracta:**

$$
\boxed{\dot{m} = C_d \cdot A_{eff} \cdot \sqrt{2 \rho_f \Delta P}}
$$

**Logic Notes:**
- $\Delta P = P_r - P_c$ is the **pressure differential** driving injection.
- $A_{eff} = n \cdot \frac{\pi d_0^2}{4}$ is the **total effective area** of all nozzle holes.
- $C_d$ empirically corrects for non-ideal flow (separation, viscosity, cavitation).

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Sauter Mean Diameter (Atomization Quality)

**Definition:** SMD is the diameter of a hypothetical droplet whose surface-to-volume ratio equals that of the entire spray. Smaller SMD = better atomization.

**Step 1 — Express SMD as a volume-to-surface ratio:**

$$
SMD = \frac{6 \cdot V_{total}}{A_{total}} = \frac{6 \sum n_i d_i^3}{\sum n_i d_i^2}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply dimensional analysis (El-Kotb correlation) using the relevant variables:**
- $d_0$ = nozzle hole diameter [m]
- $\Delta P$ = injection pressure differential [Pa]
- $\rho_f$ = fuel density [kg/m³]
- $\mu_f$ = fuel dynamic viscosity [Pa·s]
- $\sigma$ = surface tension [N/m] (often lumped into constant)

**Step 3 — Group variables by dimensions to extract the power-law form:**

$$
\left[\frac{SMD}{d_0}\right] = K \cdot \left[\frac{\rho_f \cdot v^2 \cdot d_0}{\sigma}\right]^{-a} \cdot \left[\frac{\rho_f \cdot v \cdot d_0}{\mu_f}\right]^{-b}
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute $v = \sqrt{2\Delta P/\rho_f}$ and simplify exponents experimentally fit to typical injector data:**

$$
SMD = K' \cdot d_0^{1.25} \cdot \Delta P^{-0.25} \cdot \rho_f^{0.25} \cdot \mu_f^{0.25}
$$

**Step 5 — Standard form for diesel CRDI injectors (with $K' = 0.38$, units: SMD in μm, $d_0$ in mm, ΔP in bar):**

$$
\boxed{SMD = 0.38 \cdot d_0^{1.25} \cdot \Delta P^{-0.25} \cdot \rho_f^{0.25} \cdot \mu_f^{0.25}}
$$

**Logic Notes:**
- This explains **why higher rail pressure → smaller SMD → better atomization**.
- Doubling the pressure reduces SMD by a factor of $2^{0.25} \approx 1.19$ (≈19% smaller droplets).
- This also explains why **smaller nozzle holes** (smaller $d_0$) yield finer sprays.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Spray Penetration in Quiescent Chamber (Dent Correlation)

**Step 1 — Define spray penetration $S(t)$** as the axial distance traveled by the spray tip from the nozzle.

**Step 2 — Start from the momentum equation for a control volume of the spray:**

$$
\frac{d}{dt}(m_{spray} \cdot v_{spray}) = \dot{m}_{in} \cdot v_{inj} - F_{drag}
$$

**Step 3 — Assume quasi-steady momentum balance where injection momentum is balanced by air entrainment drag:**

$$
\dot{m}_{inj} \cdot v_{inj} \cdot t = K_{drag} \cdot \rho_a \cdot S^2 \cdot \dot{S}
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute $v_{inj} = \sqrt{2 \Delta P / \rho_f}$ and $\dot{m}_{inj} = \rho_f \cdot A_{eff} \cdot v_{inj}$:**

$$
\rho_f A_{eff} \cdot \frac{2 \Delta P}{\rho_f} \cdot t = K_{drag} \cdot \rho_a \cdot S^2 \cdot \dot{S}
$$

**Step 5 — Simplify and integrate from 0 to S over time 0 to t:**

$$
\int_0^S S^2\, dS = \frac{2 A_{eff} \Delta P}{K_{drag} \rho_a} \int_0^t t\, dt
$$

**Step 6 — Evaluate both integrals:**

$$
\frac{S^3}{3} = \frac{2 A_{eff} \Delta P}{K_{drag} \rho_a} \cdot \frac{t^2}{2}
$$

**Step 7 — Solve for S(t) using the empirically determined constant K_drag and substituting $A_{eff} \propto d_0^2$:**

$$
S^3 \propto \frac{\Delta P \cdot d_0^2 \cdot t^2}{\rho_a}
$$

**Step 8 — Take cube root and apply Dent's empirical fitting constants:**

$$
\boxed{S = 1.96 \cdot \left(\frac{\Delta P}{\rho_a}\right)^{0.25} \cdot (d_0 \cdot t)^{0.5}}
$$

**Logic Notes:**
- Higher $\Delta P$ → longer spray penetration (better reach into chamber).
- Larger $d_0$ → more momentum → longer penetration.
- In a real engine, $t$ is limited by the injection duration and the dwell time before piston motion compresses the chamber.
- **Wall impingement** occurs when $S$ exceeds the piston-bowl radius — this is undesirable and causes soot.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — How Each Parameter Changes Combustion

| Parameter | ↑ Increase Causes | ↓ Decrease Causes | ECU Sensor Reading |
|---|---|---|---|
| **SOI (earlier)** | ↑ Peak P, ↑ NOx, ↑ Noise, ↓ Smoke | ↓ Peak P, ↑ Smoke, ↓ NOx | CKP + CMP |
| **Rail Pressure** | ↓ SMD, ↓ PM, ↓ HC, ↑ NOx | ↑ SMD, ↑ PM, ↑ HC, ↓ NOx | Rail Pressure Sensor |
| **Pulse Width** | ↑ m_f, richer, ↑ torque | ↓ m_f, leaner, ↓ torque | MAF, Lambda |
| **Pilot Injection** | ↓ dp/dθ, ↓ Noise, ↓ NOx | Rougher combustion | CKP, Knock Sensor |
| **Spray Cone Angle** | Wider spread, better mixing | Narrow jet, wall impingement | Optical (calibration) |
| **λ (leaner)** | ↓ NOx, ↑ PM, ↑ η_th | ↑ NOx, ↓ PM, richer | Lambda / O₂ sensor |

---

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

**Problem:** A CRDI injector has 6 holes, each of diameter $d_0 = 0.20$ mm. Rail pressure is 1600 bar and chamber back pressure is 50 bar. Fuel density $\rho_f = 830$ kg/m³, $C_d = 0.85$. Calculate: (a) injection velocity, (b) mass flow rate per hole, (c) total mass flow rate, (d) SMD using the El-Kotb correlation.

**Given:**
- $\Delta P = 1600 - 50 = 1550$ bar $= 155 \times 10^6$ Pa
- $d_0 = 0.20$ mm
- $\rho_f = 830$ kg/m³, $C_d = 0.85$, $n = 6$

**(a) Injection Velocity:**

$$
v_2 = \sqrt{\frac{2 \Delta P}{\rho_f}} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 155 \times 10^6}{830}} = \sqrt{3.7349 \times 10^5}
$$

$$
\boxed{v_2 \approx 611.1 \text{ m/s}}
$$

**(b) Mass Flow Rate per Hole:**

$$
A_{hole} = \frac{\pi d_0^2}{4} = \frac{\pi (0.20 \times 10^{-3})^2}{4} = 3.1416 \times 10^{-8} \text{ m}^2
$$

$$
\dot{m}_{hole} = C_d A_{hole} \sqrt{2 \rho_f \Delta P} = 0.85 \times 3.1416 \times 10^{-8} \times \sqrt{2 \times 830 \times 155 \times 10^6}
$$

$$
\dot{m}_{hole} = 0.85 \times 3.1416 \times 10^{-8} \times 5.0758 \times 10^5
$$

$$
\boxed{\dot{m}_{hole} \approx 1.355 \times 10^{-2} \text{ kg/s} = 13.55 \text{ g/s}}
$$

**(c) Total Mass Flow Rate:**

$$
\dot{m}_{total} = 6 \times 13.55 = \boxed{81.3 \text{ g/s}}
$$

**(d) SMD Calculation:**

Using the El-Kotb formula with $d_0 = 0.20$ mm, $\Delta P = 1600$ bar (use rail pressure as approximation), $\rho_f = 830$ kg/m³, $\mu_f \approx 3 \times 10^{-3}$ Pa·s:

$$
SMD = 0.38 \times (0.20)^{1.25} \times (1600)^{-0.25} \times (830)^{0.25} \times (3 \times 10^{-3})^{0.25}
$$

Step-by-step:

- $(0.20)^{1.25} = 0.20^1 \times 0.20^{0.25} = 0.20 \times 0.6687 = 0.1337$
- $(1600)^{0.25} = (1600)^{1/4} = \sqrt{\sqrt{1600}} = \sqrt{40} = 6.3246$
- $(830)^{0.25} = (830)^{1/4} \approx 5.371$
- $(0.003)^{0.25} \approx 0.2341$

$$
SMD = 0.38 \times 0.1337 \times \frac{1}{6.3246} \times 5.371 \times 0.2341
$$

$$
SMD = 0.38 \times 0.1337 \times 0.1581 \times 5.371 \times 0.2341
$$

$$
SMD = 0.38 \times 0.1337 \times 0.1581 \times 1.2575
$$

$$
SMD \approx 0.38 \times 0.02656 \approx \boxed{10.09 \text{ μm}}
$$

This SMD value of **≈ 10 μm** is in the optimal range for diesel combustion (typically 5–30 μm), confirming excellent atomization.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]
**List any three fuel injection parameters that affect combustion in a CRDI engine and explain how each one influences the combustion process.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Injection Pressure (Rail Pressure):** Higher pressure produces finer atomization (smaller SMD), better air-fuel mixing, shorter ignition delay, leading to more complete combustion, reduced PM and HC, but higher NOx.
2. **Injection Timing (SOI):** Earlier SOI increases the premixed combustion phase, raising peak cylinder pressure and NOx, but also increases noise. Later SOI shifts combustion towards diffusion-controlled, increasing smoke.
3. **Injection Duration (Pulse Width):** Directly controls the injected fuel mass, determining the air-fuel equivalence ratio (λ) and hence torque output and emissions.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]
**Define Sauter Mean Diameter (SMD) and explain its significance in diesel combustion.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1.5 marks):** SMD is the diameter of a hypothetical droplet whose surface-to-volume ratio equals that of the entire spray.
  $$\text{SMD} = \frac{6 \sum n_i d_i^3}{\sum n_i d_i^2}$$
- **Significance (1.5 marks):** SMD quantifies atomization quality. Smaller SMD (typically 5–30 μm) means larger surface area, faster evaporation, better air-fuel mixing, and more complete combustion. SMD is inversely related to injection pressure (per El-Kotb correlation) and directly affects PM and HC emissions.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### Question A — Module 4 Choice Option 1

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a) With a neat functional block diagram, explain the mechatronic closed-loop control of fuel injection parameters in a modern CRDI engine. Identify the key sensors and actuators. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The mechatronic system consists of three layers: **(i) Sensors, (ii) ECU, (iii) Actuators**, arranged in a closed loop with the combustion chamber as the controlled process.

**(i) Sensors (Input layer):**
- **Crankshaft Position Sensor (CKP):** Provides engine speed (RPM) and piston position for SOI calculation.
- **Camshaft Position Sensor (CMP):** Identifies the compression stroke (for sequential injection).
- **Mass Air Flow Sensor (MAF) / Manifold Absolute Pressure (MAP):** Determines air mass for fuel quantity calculation.
- **Lambda / Wideband O₂ Sensor:** Measures exhaust λ for closed-loop feedback.
- **Rail Pressure Sensor:** Monitors actual rail pressure for RPCV control.
- **Coolant Temperature Sensor (CTS):** Provides warm-up enrichment.
- **Knock Sensor:** Detects abnormal combustion (used for SOI retard in GDI).

**(ii) ECU (Control layer):**
- The ECU executes a **real-time control algorithm** using 3-D and 4-D look-up tables.
- Inputs: sensor signals, battery voltage, ambient pressure.
- Outputs: SOI command, rail pressure setpoint, pulse width, number of injection events.

**(iii) Actuators (Output layer):**
- **High-pressure pump** (driven by camshaft) pressurizes fuel.
- **Rail Pressure Control Valve (RPCV):** Adjusts rail pressure by bleeding excess fuel.
- **Solenoid/Piezo Injector:** Executes the multi-stage injection event with high precision (sub-millisecond resolution).

**Closed-loop operation:** The rail pressure sensor and λ sensor feed back actual values, and the ECU uses **PID control** for rail pressure and **on-off hysteresis control** for λ. The output — combustion quality — is thus continuously regulated.

---

**(b) Derive the expression for the spray penetration distance $S(t)$ in a quiescent chamber starting from the momentum balance equation. State the Dent correlation and comment on how injection pressure and nozzle diameter influence penetration. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Derivation:**

Starting from the momentum balance for the spray control volume:

$$
\frac{d}{dt}(m_{spray} \cdot v_{spray}) = \dot{m}_{inj} v_{inj} - F_{drag}
$$

Assuming quasi-steady state where injection momentum is balanced by air-entrainment drag:

$$
\dot{m}_{inj} \cdot v_{inj} \cdot t = K_{drag} \cdot \rho_a \cdot S^2 \cdot \dot{S}
$$

Substituting $\dot{m}_{inj} = \rho_f A_{eff} v_{inj}$ and $v_{inj} = \sqrt{2\Delta P / \rho_f}$:

$$
\rho_f A_{eff} \cdot \frac{2 \Delta P}{\rho_f} \cdot t = K_{drag} \rho_a S^2 \frac{dS}{dt}
$$

$$
2 A_{eff} \Delta P \cdot t = K_{drag} \rho_a S^2 \frac{dS}{dt}
$$

Separating variables and integrating:

$$
\int_0^S S^2\, dS = \frac{2 A_{eff} \Delta P}{K_{drag} \rho_a} \int_0^t t\, dt
$$

$$
\frac{S^3}{3} = \frac{A_{eff} \Delta P \cdot t^2}{K_{drag} \rho_a}
$$

Substituting $A_{eff} = n \pi d_0^2 / 4$ and applying the empirical constant (K_drag calibration):

$$
\boxed{S = 1.96 \cdot \left(\frac{\Delta P}{\rho_a}\right)^{0.25} \cdot (d_0 \cdot t)^{0.5}}
$$

**Comments:**
- **Injection pressure $\Delta P$:** Penetration grows as $\Delta P^{0.25}$. Doubling pressure increases penetration by ≈19%. Higher pressure enables better chamber reach.
- **Nozzle hole diameter $d_0$:** Penetration grows as $d_0^{0.5}$. Larger holes produce longer, more momentum-rich jets.
- **Caveat:** Excessive penetration causes wall impingement → increased PM and HC. Modern systems use multiple smaller holes with shorter penetration to optimize mixing.

---

### Question B — Module 4 Choice Option 2 (Alternative)

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a) Explain the concept of multi-stage injection (pilot, main, post) in modern diesel engines. Discuss how pilot injection reduces combustion noise and the role of post injection in DPF regeneration. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Multi-stage injection** refers to dividing the total fuel quantity into multiple smaller injection events per cycle, enabled by **piezo injectors** with sub-millisecond response.

**Pilot Injection (1–3° before main):**
- Small fuel mass (≈1–3 mg) injected first.
- Ignites during the main ignition delay period, creating a small, controlled premixed burn.
- This **pre-heats** the air charge and **shortens** the main ignition delay.
- Result: The main injection's premixed burn is reduced → **lower peak pressure rise rate (dp/dθ)** → **lower combustion noise (≈ 3–5 dB reduction)** → **lower NOx** (lower peak T).

**Main Injection:**
- The largest fuel mass, typically 80–90% of total per cycle.
- Occurs near TDC, providing the primary energy release.

**After/Post Injection:**
- Small, late injection occurring during expansion stroke.
- Burns residual soot and unburned HC in the cylinder before reaching the **Diesel Particulate Filter (DPF)**.
- Raises exhaust gas temperature to **≈ 600–650 °C**, which is required to regenerate (burn off) accumulated soot in the DPF.
- In **SCR (Selective Catalytic Reduction)** systems, a second post injection (late in cycle) ensures that raw diesel reaches the **Diesel Oxidation Catalyst (DOC)** for HC dosing, generating NH₃ for NOx reduction.

**Benefits Summary:** Lower noise, lower NOx, lower PM, enabled DPF regeneration, smoother idle.

---

**(b) Derive the expression for the Sauter Mean Diameter (SMD) using the El-Kotb correlation. A CRDI injector has 7 holes, each 0.18 mm in diameter, with rail pressure of 1800 bar. The fuel density is 835 kg/m³ and dynamic viscosity $3.5 \times 10^{-3}$ Pa·s. Calculate the SMD and comment on the atomization quality. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Derivation of SMD (El-Kotb):**

The SMD is defined as:

$$
SMD = \frac{6 \sum n_i d_i^3}{\sum n_i d_i^2}
$$

Applying dimensional analysis to the spray process with relevant variables ($d_0$, $\Delta P$, $\rho_f$, $\mu_f$, $\sigma$):

$$
\left[\frac{SMD}{d_0}\right] = K \cdot We^{-a} \cdot Re^{-b}
$$

where $We = \rho_f v^2 d_0 / \sigma$ (Weber number) and $Re = \rho_f v d_0 / \mu_f$ (Reynolds number).

Substituting $v = \sqrt{2\Delta P/\rho_f}$ and combining exponents, the experimentally fit El-Kotb correlation for diesel injectors is:

$$
\boxed{SMD = 0.38 \cdot d_0^{1.25} \cdot \Delta P^{-0.25} \cdot \rho_f^{0.25} \cdot \mu_f^{0.25}}
$$

with SMD in **μm**, $d_0$ in **mm**, $\Delta P$ in **bar**.

**Numerical Calculation:**

Given: $d_0 = 0.18$ mm, $\Delta P \approx 1800$ bar, $\rho_f = 835$ kg/m³, $\mu_f = 3.5 \times 10^{-3}$ Pa·s.

Step-by-step:

- $(0.18)^{1.25} = 0.18 \times 0.18^{0.25} = 0.18 \times 0.6527 = 0.1175$
- $(1800)^{0.25} = \sqrt{\sqrt{1800}} = \sqrt{42.426} = 6.514$
- $(835)^{0.25} = 5.385$
- $(3.5 \times 10^{-3})^{0.25} = (0.0035)^{0.25} = 0.2433$

$$
SMD = 0.38 \times 0.1175 \times \frac{1}{6.514} \times 5.385 \times 0.2433
$$

$$
SMD = 0.38 \times 0.1175 \times 0.1535 \times 1.3098
$$

$$
SMD \approx 0.38 \times 0.02362 \approx \boxed{8.97 \text{ μm}}
$$

**Comment on Atomization Quality:**
An SMD of ≈ 9 μm is **excellent atomization** (ideal range 5–30 μm for diesel). This produces:
- Rapid fuel-air mixing
- Shorter ignition delay
- More complete combustion
- Lower PM emissions
- Slightly higher NOx (acceptable, managed by EGR)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mistakes:**
> 1. **Unit Inconsistency:** Students often use $d_0$ in meters but expect SMD in μm. The El-Kotb formula requires **$d_0$ in mm and $\Delta P$ in bar**. Mixing units gives 1000× errors.
> 2. **Neglecting Discharge Coefficient:** In $\dot{m}$ derivations, omitting $C_d$ loses 1 mark.
> 3. **Confusing SOI conventions:** "Early" SOI = **more BTDC** (more degrees before TDC). "Retarded" = closer to TDC or after.
> 4. **Mixing stoichiometric A/F ratios:** Gasoline (14.7) ≠ Diesel (14.5). Examiner often tests this distinction.
> 5. **Pilot vs. Pre-injection confusion:** Pilot = before main, small. Post = after main, small. Both distinct from main.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Module 4: Fuel Injection Parameters**

- ✅ **Six primary parameters:** SOI, Rail Pressure, Pulse Width, Number of Injections, Spray Pattern, λ.
- ✅ **ECU** is the brain; sensors (CKP, CMP, MAF, λ, rail pressure) are inputs; injectors and RPCV are actuators.
- ✅ **Earlier SOI** → ↑ Peak P, ↑ NOx, ↑ Noise.
- ✅ **Higher rail pressure** → smaller SMD → better atomization.
- ✅ **SMD formula (El-Kotb):** $SMD = 0.38 \cdot d_0^{1.25} \cdot \Delta P^{-0.25} \cdot \rho_f^{0.25} \cdot \mu_f^{0.25}$
- ✅ **Mass flow rate:** $\dot{m} = C_d A_{eff} \sqrt{2 \rho_f \Delta P}$
- ✅ **Penetration (Dent):** $S = 1.96 \left(\frac{\Delta P}{\rho_a}\right)^{0.25} (d_0 t)^{0.5}$
- ✅ **Pilot injection** reduces dp/dθ and noise.
- ✅ **Post injection** enables DPF regeneration and aids SCR.
- ✅ **Stoichiometric A/F:** Gasoline = **14.7**, Diesel = **14.5**.
- ✅ **Equivalence ratio:** $\lambda = (A/F)_{actual} / (A/F)_{stoich}$.
- ✅ Typical modern CRDI rail pressure: **2000 – 2500 bar** (Bosch / Denso / Continental systems).
- ✅ Nozzle hole diameter range: **0.10 – 0.30 mm**.
- ✅ The combustion chamber is the **controlled process** in this mechatronic loop.
- ✅ Modern systems use **piezo injectors** for multi-stage capability (up to 9 injections/cycle in Bosch CRSN-3).
- ✅ In SI engines, **retarding SOI** reduces knock; in CI engines, **advancing SOI** is needed for high-load efficiency.
- ✅ Battery voltage compensation is essential for accurate pulse width control.
